Chapter 2 —- Broken Models: Why Classical Finance 
Won't Save You 


The Comforting Myths of a Rational Market 


In the traditional story of finance, markets are almost like benevolent machines. We’re 
taught that markets are rational and efficient, meaning prices automatically reflect all 
the important information. In this textbook world, if a company’s prospects improve, its 
stock will go up; if a downturn looms, markets will sniff it out. Under this view, simply 
“staying invested” in a broad index of stocks for long enough guarantees you’ll be 
rewarded. After all, in the long run, stocks always rise with the tide of progress — or so 
the saying goes. Central bankers and regulators are portrayed as wise stewards who 
keep the ship steady. Crashes and crises, in this narrative, are like rare freak storms; 
they may be scary, but if you hunker down and wait, the economic climate always 
returns to calm. 


These stories are comforting. They promise that the financial world has a fundamental 
order and fairness. If you follow the rules — diversify, hold for decades, trust the system 
—you’ll prosper alongside the economy. And for much of the 20th century, which saw 
steady growth and fewer truly global crises, these assumptions seemed to work 
reasonably well. But as we step further into the 21st century, with its rapid 
technological shocks and policy experiments, the real world is diverging from the 
textbook scripts. The small investor today faces a landscape that the old maps don’t 
fully capture. 


Before we dive into what changed, let’s quickly recap what those classic economic 
and finance models actually say — and where they fall out of sync with reality. 


The Classic Financial Theories and Their Lofty Assumptions 


Traditional economics and finance built a tidy model of how the world should work. 
Here are a few of the cornerstone concepts, explained in plain language: 


Efficient Markets: The Price Is Always Right 


One pillar of classical finance is the Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH). In simple 
terms, EMH says that financial markets are extremely good at processing information. 
The price of a stock (or bond, or any asset) at any given moment is thought to already 
include all known information about its value. If a company just landed a big contract or 
a recession is expected next year, that knowledge is immediately reflected in the price. 
Under EMH, it’s essentially impossible to consistently “beat the market” unless you 
have information others don’t (which is illegal insider trading) or take on extra risk. In 
fact, one textbook implication of EMH is that a low-cost index fund (which just 
passively holds all stocks) should outperform most fancy stock-pickers over time[1 ][2]. 
Why? Because if prices are already “right,” any attempt to outguess the market is just a 
coin flip — or worse, an extra fee for no gain. 


EMH has different flavors (weak, semi-strong, strong), but the core idea remains: 
markets are tough to outsmart. This led to the popular advice: “Just buy an index fund 
and forget it.” If prices are fair and always incorporate the latest news, the best thing 
an ordinary investor can do is simply ride the market and not try to trade in and out. It’s 
a comforting thought — that the market, like some wise giant, has figured everything out 
for you. 


Of course, even in academic circles EMH has critics. There are well-known anomalies 
(like market crashes that seem to come out of nowhere, or superstar investors like 
Warren Buffett who have beaten the market). Nonetheless, EMH has heavily influenced 
investing culture. Most people’s retirement accounts, for instance, are in broadly 
diversified funds premised on the idea that in the long run the market’s efficient 
wisdom will prevail[2][3]. 


Homo Economicus: Rational Robots in Human Skin 


Another big assumption in classical models is the idea of homo economicus - or 
“economic man.” This is the notional creature that economists often use in their 
theories. Homo economicus is perfectly rational, always. If this creature is faced with a 
choice, it calmly weighs the pros and cons, maximizes its own benefit, and never lets 
emotions or errors cloud its judgment[4][5]. The textbook human has stable 
preferences (what you want doesn’t wildly change day to day), has all the relevant 
information, and can calculate like a computer. In this view, people are like mini-robots 
looking out for number one, and somehow, the collective result of everyone pursuing 
their self-interest rationally is supposed to be good for the whole economy. 


This model does capture an aspect of reality: people do often try to better their 
situation. But as a literal description of human behavior, it’s deeply flawed. Real 
humans, unlike Mr. Spock or Star Trek’s androids, get scared, greedy, impatient, or 
over-confident. We follow the crowd and make impulsive decisions. Decades of 
research in psychology and behavioral economics (by folks like Daniel Kahneman and 
Amos Tversky) have shown that humans systematically deviate from perfect 
rationality[6]. We use mental shortcuts, we exhibit biases, and we often make choices 
that, in retrospect, defy clear logic[6]. For example, a perfectly rational investor 
wouldn’t panic-sell stocks at the bottom of a crash (because that locks in losses) — but 
many of us do just that out of fear. The real world has fear, greed, overreaction, peer 
pressure, optimism and pessimism swings — none of which fit neatly into the homo 
economicus model. 


Yet, a huge chunk of finance theory (from option pricing to macroeconomic policy) still 
assumes rational actors. It’s like the entire system was built assuming everyone is a 
flawless calculating machine. Keep that in mind: the models guiding our institutions 
often ignore how actual humans behave under stress. 


Trickle-Down Economics: The Wealthy Will Share the Pie... Eventually 


Now for a concept from the policy world: trickle-down economics. This isn’t a formal 
economic “law” but more of a political narrative that was especially popular in the 
1980s and still lingers today. The idea is this: if you make the rich and powerful even 


richer — say, through tax cuts for corporations and high-earners, deregulating industries, 
etc. — then everyone will benefit in the long run. Why? Because the wealthy will 
purportedly invest more, start new businesses, hire more workers, and the gains will 
“trickle down” to the rest of society. In other words, enrich the top of the pyramid and 
the benefits will flow to the base. Leaders like Ronald Reagan, Margaret Thatcher, and 
more recently proponents of big tax cuts, have championed this view, arguing that 
freeing up money for the wealthy leads them to hire more workers, pay better wages, 
and build more factories| 7]. 


It’s an attractive story if you’re at the top (or believe you might get there). But does it 
hold up? The evidence, to put it mildly, is not kind to trickle-down as a general practice. 
We have now decades of data across many countries, and researchers who studied 
major tax cuts for the rich in 18 wealthy nations over 50 years found no evidence that 
such cuts boost economic growth or reduce unemployment. What did happen was that 
the rich got richer, period[8]. One extensive LSE study concluded that when top tax 
rates are slashed, the main result is greater income inequality — the wealthy’s share 
grows - with no “trickle” to job growth or GDP[8]. In fact, after big tax cuts, some 
CEOs tended to just pay themselves more since they keep more after tax, which 
widened the gap between the top and everyone else[9][10]. 


This is not a fringe view; it’s increasingly a consensus even among moderate 
economists that the spoils of growth have not been filtering down as the theory 
promised. So, the trickle-down story — that rising markets or booming corporate profits 
automatically lift all boats — is another comforting tale that often fails in reality. We'll 
see later how this ties into the disconnect between Wall Street and Main Street. 


Gold Standard vs. Fiat Money: When Money Grew Up 


Next, let’s talk about money itself. There’s a widespread nostalgia in some circles for 
the days of the gold standard — a time when paper money was directly backed by gold. 
Under a gold standard, a currency (like the U.S. dollar pre-1971) is essentially a claim 
on a fixed amount of gold. This meant governments couldn’t just create new money 
unless they had the gold to back it. By design, that put a leash on money supply growth 
and, proponents say, instilled discipline by preventing endless inflation of the 
currency’s value. 


The United States (and many countries) abandoned the classical gold standard during 
the Great Depression, but even after World War II there was a pseudo-gold standard 
under the Bretton Woods system: foreign governments could exchange U.S. dollars for 
gold at a fixed rate. This international link was finally severed in 1971, when President 
Nixon ended the dollar’s convertibility to gold[11]. That event ushered in the era of fiat 
money - currency that is not backed by any physical commodity, but rather by the 
government’s promise and the public’s trust. “Fiat” comes from the Latin fiat (decree) — 
essentially, our money has value because the government says it does and people 
believe it. Modern currencies, from the dollar to the euro to the yen, are all fiat. They are 
legal tender by decree, not exchangeable for gold or silver[12][13]. 


Why does this matter for investors? Under a gold standard, there was an idea that 
money was hard and limited —- which some argue kept prices and policies in check 


(though in reality, gold-backed systems had their own booms, busts, and political 
problems). In a fiat system, central banks have much more flexibility. They can print 
money or, more abstractly, use tools like lowering interest rates and quantitative 
easing (buying assets with newly created money) to manage the economy. This 
flexibility can prevent depressions... or, critics worry, fuel bubbles and erode the real 
value of money over time. 


The key point: since 1971, we’ve been in uncharted territory historically, with pure 
fiat currencies globally. Some classical economists’ models (and many old-school 
investors) grew up in the gold standard mindset where such aggressive money printing 
was unthinkable. Today, it’s routine. The consequences - good, bad, and unintended — 
are still being debated. But it definitely enabled some of the “markets decoupling from 
reality” phenomena we'll discuss later, because central banks found they could pour 
money into the financial system at will. 


“Stocks Always Go Up” —A Folksy Myth with a Grain of Truth 


Finally, let’s address a piece of popular investing lore: “Stocks always go up in the long 
run.” You’ve probably heard this refrain from financial advisors, enthusiastic uncles, or 
money gurus on TV. It’s usually accompanied by charts of the U.S. stock market over 
the last century showing a relentless upward trajectory despite wars, crashes, and 
recessions. Indeed, historically in the United States, a broad basket of stocks has 
delivered positive returns over every 20- or 30-year period in modern history (especially 
if reinvesting dividends). This track record turned into a simplified article of faith: no 
need to worry about risks or timing — just buy an index fund, keep adding to it, and 
in a few decades you will be wealthier. Many retirement plans and pension systems 
are built on the assumption that, over the long haul, equities are a safe bet because 
capitalism as a whole grows. 


But let’s examine this critically. First, the rosy U.S. experience isn’t universal. Consider 
Japan: if you bought at the peak of the Japanese stock market in 1989, you’d still be 
underwater nearly 30 years later (only by 2021-2023 did Japanese stocks show 
sustained recovery, almost 40 years after the peak)/14]. There’s nothing magical 
guaranteeing every stock market always recovers. Even inthe U.S., there have been 
multi-decade stretches where stocks gave zero real return — basically going nowhere 
after inflation[15]. For example, someone investing at the height of the late-1960s 
market had to wait until the early 1980s just to break even in purchasing power terms. 
More recently, the S&P 500 took about 7 years to recover from the 2000 dot-com crash, 
and another 6 years to recover from the 2008 crash. That’s 13 years just to get back to 
Zero gain if you had the misfortune of investing at the wrong times. And certain sectors 
(like tech after 2000, or banking after 2008) never fully returned to their prior glory for 
many, many years. 


In short, “stocks always go up (eventually)” is not a law of nature. As one analysis 
bluntly put it, there is no natural rule mandating stock prices must rise over any given 
period[16]. They have done so historically in stable, growing economies under relatively 
benign conditions — especially in the U.S. — but that’s no guarantee for the future. 
Relying on this meme can be dangerous if the future looks very different from the past. 
Our current world faces potential upheavals (Al, geopolitical strife, climate issues) that 


could mean the next 30 years won’t mimic the last 30. And even if stocks do rise “in the 
long run,” remember the famous economist John Maynard Keynes quip: “/n the long 
run, we are all dead.” If you’re 60 years old and there’s a crash, being told stocks will 
bounce back by the time you’re 85 isn’t exactly comforting. 


So, to recap the classic playbook: Markets are perfectly efficient, people are 
perfectly rational, helping the rich helps everyone, money is sound and steady, and 
if you simply invest in stocks forever, you can’t lose. These assumptions underpin a 
lot of mainstream financial advice and economic policy even today. They make the 
world seem understandable and manageable. Unfortunately, the real world has been 
repeatedly poking holes in each of these ideas. Let’s look at how. 


When the Real World Shattered the Models: A Tour of Recent History 


It’s one thing to discuss theories in the abstract. It becomes far more tangible when we 
see what actually happened in the markets and the economy over the past few 
decades. Time and again, the comforting assumptions we just outlined have failed 
to match reality — often in dramatic fashion. Here, we’ll walk through a few major 
episodes: 


1. The Dot-Com Bubble of the late 1990s and its spectacular crash in 2000. 

2. The Global Financial Crisis of 2007-2009, born from a housing bubble and Wall 
Street alchemy. 

3. The era of Quantitative Easing (QE) that followed, where central banks 
“printed” trillions of dollars, decoupling markets from the real economy. 

4. The 2020 COVID-19 crash and its aftermath, which gave us perhaps the 
clearest example of Wall Street soaring while Main Street suffered. 


Each of these events showed that markets are not always rational or self-correcting, 
that risk can be wildly underestimated, and that authorities often have to rip up the 
rulebook to prevent collapse. 


The Dot-Com Bubble (1999-2000): “This Time is Different” — Until It Isn’t 


What happened: In the late 1990s, a frenzy took hold of the stock market, centered on 
anything internet-related. Companies with a “.com” at the end of their name — many of 
them tiny startups with no profits (Sometimes not even any revenues or fully developed 
products) — saw their stock prices go to the moon. The Nasdaq, a tech-heavy stock 
index, quintupled in value between 1995 and 2000/17]. It was an era of pure 
speculation and hype. Investors were convinced the internet would change everything 
(that part was true) and that every dot-com company was a future Microsoft or Amazon 
(that part was very not true). Traditional valuation metrics like earnings, cash flow, or 
even viable business models were thrown out the window. People talked about 
“eyeballs” (website visits) or “mindshare” as justification for sky-high prices. The 
sentiment often heard was: “yes, these companies aren’t making money now, but if 
we don’t invest, we’ll miss out on the next big thing — this time it’s different.” 


Where the model broke: According to the Efficient Market Hypothesis, all this should 
not have happened. If markets were correctly processing information, companies with 


no profits and nebulous prospects shouldn’t sport billion-dollar valuations. Yet 
they did. EMH would say if these prices were high, it must be because rational investors 
foresaw huge future earnings. In reality, many investors were just caught in a herd 
mentality and a fear of missing out. Far from rationally assessing information, they 
were often ignoring obvious red flags. For instance, a company like Pets.com famously 
spent massively on advertising (remember the sock puppet mascot?) and went public 
in 2000 only to go bankrupt nine months later. It had essentially zero chance of 
justifying its valuation, yet the market priced it as if success was guaranteed — until the 
illusion collapsed. 


By early 2000, reality intruded. The bubble popped. The Nasdaq index plunged by 
almost 77% from its peak, wiping out about $5 trillion in market value[18][19]. 
Companies that had seen their stocks double or triple in days saw them fall 90%+ or go 
to zero. Many dot-com startups went bankrupt (some estimates say over half of all 
public internet companies failed within a few years)[20]. Even big tech names weren’t 
spared: giants like Cisco and Intel lost over 80% of their stock value from 2000 to 
2002/19]. 


The dot-com crash exposed that markets can be wildly inefficient, at least for 
periods of time. Investors were not behaving like calm, rational homo economicus — 
they got caught up in narrative and momentum. When the mood flipped from euphoria 
to fear, the sell-off was just as irrationally overdone as the rise had been. Importantly, 
people who had believed “stocks always go up in the long run” got a rude awakening: 
the Nasdaq took 15 years (until 2015) to regain its 2000 peak[19]. That’s a very long run 
indeed for those who bought at the top. 


The lesson here is that “efficient” markets can dramatically overshoot reality. Prices 
were not reflecting sensible assessments of future profits; they were reflecting the 
psychology of a crowd in a mania. And this wasn’t some anomaly from ancient history — 
it happened just two decades ago, within living memory of most investors today. 


The 2008 Financial Crisis: When “Safe as Houses” Was Anything But 


What happened: Fast forward a few years. By the mid-2000s, there was a widespread 
belief that housing prices in places like the U.S. would just keep climbing. Banks and 
investors made an apparently reasonable bet: after all, hadn’t home prices always 
trended upward? Based on that assumption, an elaborate financial edifice was built. 
Banks were giving out mortgages to millions of people (including many with shaky 
finances — the “subprime” borrowers) because those loans could be packaged into 
mortgage-backed securities and sold to global investors. The logic went: even if some 
borrowers default, the houses can be sold, and besides, house prices never all fall at 
once. Rating agencies stamped many of these mortgage bundles with AAA ratings, 
indicating they were as safe as government bonds — top-quality[21 ][22]. Huge banks 
and hedge funds loaded up on these supposedly low-risk securities. Some even built 
complex derivatives on top of them (like CDOs and credit default swaps) to amplify the 
bets. It was a tower of risk masquerading as a tower of safety. 


A lot of fancy math went into this. Risk models used by banks assumed that the odds of 
a nationwide drop in home prices were extremely low — something like a once-in-many- 


thousands-of-years event. They also assumed that defaults in different regions were 
largely uncorrelated (so you wouldn’t have everyone defaulting at once — a critical 
assumption that turned out to be wrong). In essence, the financial system’s brainy 
quants and executives treated the recent past as if it were a reliable guide: since we 
hadn’t seen a nationwide housing crash, they assumed it basically couldn’t happen. 
This is a prime example of overreliance on models and historical data — akin to 
thinking a 100-year flood can’t happen just because you haven’t seen one yet. 


Where the model broke: In reality, of course, housing can fall—- and it did, disastrously. 
By 2006-2007, cracks began to show: interest rates had risen a bit, new housing supply 
was peaking, and suddenly home prices stalled and started to dip. The whole chain 
reaction began: borrowers (especially those with gimmicky mortgages that had low 
teaser rates) began to default in larger numbers. Those mortgage-backed securities 
that were rated “super safe” AAA turned out to be full of toxic loans — they were safe 
until, suddenly, they weren’t[22]. It’s like everyone had been treating dry kindling as 
fireproof because it hadn’t caught fire yet. When delinquencies surged, the complex 
CDOs and derivatives tied to mortgages imploded in value. Major financial institutions 
that were highly leveraged (meaning, they borrowed a lot to bet on these “safe” assets) 
were suddenly insolvent or teetering. In September 2008, Lehman Brothers — a storied 
investment bank — collapsed in bankruptcy. Others like AlG (an insurance giant) only 
survived via massive government bailouts. By early 2009, global credit markets had 
frozen and the world was staring into the abyss of a potential second Great Depression. 


The rational-agent model utterly failed here. Greed, faulty incentive structures, and 
herd behavior led banks to keep dancing long after the music was clearly getting 
dangerous. The risk models used were grossly underestimating the probability of a 
systemic crash. One telling anecdote: some risk officers calculated (using bell-curve 
assumptions) that the kind of losses seen in 2008 were so unlikely that they would only 
occur “once in a million years” or something absurd — yet they did occur, because the 
model itself was flawed in assuming independence and mild randomness. In truth, rare 
bad events (so-called “Black Swans”) happen more often than textbook probability 
suggests — financial returns have “fat tails”[23]. Wall Street learned that the hard way 
(though arguably it forgot again, as memories faded). 


Perhaps the biggest indictment of the classical view is that markets didn’t self-correct 
— they self-destructed. If not for massive intervention, the entire financial system 
might have collapsed. Remember, in traditional free-market theory, large-scale 
government or central bank intervention shouldn’t be needed; the invisible hand and 
rational self-interest should prevent catastrophes. But in 2008, the invisible hand was 
choking the economy, not saving it. Governments and central banks had to step in with 
extraordinary measures: multi-trillion-dollar bank rescue packages, emergency lending 
facilities, Suarantees, and more[24]. The U.S. Federal Reserve slashed interest rates to 
near zero and introduced unprecedented programs to unfreeze markets. The U.S. 
Treasury put up hundreds of billions in the TARP program to inject capital into 
banks[24]. Similar dramatic steps occurred worldwide. 


In short, 2008 shattered the myth that the financial system was a well-oiled rational 
machine. It revealed deep fragility, hidden under layers of optimism and complexity. It 
also showed that perfect rationality was absent - from individual subprime borrowers 


who took loans they couldn’t afford, to mortgage brokers who churned out loans 
without scrutiny, to quants who trusted their models too much, to credit rating 
agencies that gave inflated ratings arguably due to conflicts of interest. Everyone was 
playing a game of musical chairs, earning fees or yields while the sun was shining, 
assuming they’d be smart enough to step aside before the storm. But collectively, that 
was impossible - someone had to hold the bag. In 2008, that someone turned out to be 
all of us, until public institutions picked up the tab. 


This crisis is a big reason Nir (and many others) lost faith in the idea of a naturally stable 
financial world. The aftermath would lead to the next phase: the age of radical 
monetary policy experiments. 


Quantitative Easing (2009-Present): The Money Flood and Market 
Disconnect 


What happened: In response to the 2008 crisis, central banks did something truly 
historic. Having already cut interest rates to zero (or near enough), they needed another 
lever to pull. That lever was Quantitative Easing (QE) — a fancy term for creating new 
money to buy bonds and other assets, thereby injecting cash into the financial system. 
The U.S. Federal Reserve launched multiple rounds of QE between 2008 and 2014. 
Other central banks (the European Central Bank, Bank of Japan, etc.) followed with 
their own asset purchase programs. The scale is worth emphasizing: The Fed’s balance 
sheet (a proxy for how much money it has pumped out) swelled from under $1 trillion in 
2007 to about $4.5 trillion by 2014/25]. Essentially, they conjured money electronically 
and used it to buy Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities in bulk. Later, in 
some countries, QE even included buying corporate bonds or stock ETFs. 


The primary goal was to stabilize the economy - prevent deflation, prop up lending, 
and encourage investment by pushing down long-term interest rates. And to a degree, it 
succeeded in averting a depression. But QE had another effect: by design, it lifted 
asset prices. When interest rates are near zero and the central bank is buying bonds, 
yields fall and investors move into stocks, real estate, and other assets seeking returns. 
Cheap money finds somewhere to go. This is often celebrated as a feature, nota bug-a 
rising stock market was intended to boost confidence and create a “wealth effect” (if 
people feel richer on paper, maybe they spend more). However, QE also decoupled 
markets from the on-the-ground reality for many people. 


Through the 2010s, we saw a striking divergence: stock markets boomed — the S&P 
500 in the U.S. went on a bull run, roughly tripling from its 2009 lows to 2019 —- while 
median incomes and wealth for the middle class stagnated in many countries. In the 
U.S., for instance, corporate profits and stocks rebounded much faster than 
employment or wages did. It wasn’t until around 2018 that worker pay showed 
significant gains, nearly a decade after the recession, whereas the stock market was 
hitting record highs year after year. Wealth became more concentrated: by 2021, the 
top 10% of Americans owned over 85% of all stocks by value[26] (by 2025 it was nearly 
87%, with the top 1% alone owning about half[27]). So, when the Fed undertook policies 
that inflated asset prices, who benefited? Mostly the already wealthy. Those without 
significant assets saw little “trickle down” from booming markets. This is one reason 


people talk about a “K-shaped” recovery after 2008 (and again after 2020): the affluent 
zoom upward (top arm of the K), while the masses see a flat or declining path (bottom 
arm of the K)[28]. 


From a textbook perspective, this era raised some uncomfortable questions. The 
classical model doesn’t really account for central banks effectively underwriting the 
markets. Remember the idea of the “invisible hand” and markets finding correct 
prices? Well, the hand became very visible. Traders even coined a term, the “Fed Put,” 
to describe the widespread belief that the Federal Reserve would always step in to 
prevent a severe market crash (much like an options put that limits downside 
losses)[29]. This wasn’t idle speculation — time and again, whenever markets wobbled, 
some Fed official would hint at supportive policy and, lo and behold, money would flow 
and stocks would stabilize. This created a sort of moral hazard: if investors assume the 
central bank won’t let the market fall too much, they’ll take on more risk than they 
otherwise would. It’s like having a safety net that encourages a trapeze artist to attempt 
flips they otherwise wouldn’t. 


For a small investor, this is confusing terrain. On one hand, it meant that over the last 
decade, “don’t fight the Fed” was a winning mantra — riding the wave of liquidity did 
buoy portfolios. On the other hand, it also meant that asset prices could become 
untethered from economic fundamentals. When you see stock indices rising in years 
of mediocre GDP growth and productivity, largely because money is cheap and 
abundant, you start to question the old link between the stock market and the real 
economy. 


Nir’s worldview is very much shaped by this decoupling. The problem as he sees it: if 
markets are levitated by policy (like QE), they can become fragile and prone to sudden 
corrections if the policy support even blinks. It’s as if a drug has been propping up the 
patient — it works until it doesn’t, and then the come-down can be severe. 


Moreover, QE and related policies contributed to structural issues like higher 
inequality. As one Harvard Business Review article pointed out, just a few years after 
the Great Recession, stock markets and corporate profits were roaring, yet most 
Americans were not sharing in the prosperity — good jobs were scarce and income 
gains were captured almost entirely by the top 0.1%[30]. This sows social discontent 
and can lead to political volatility (we’ve seen the rise of populist movements across 
many countries, which is partly tied to people feeling left out of the “recovery”). 


To sum up the QE era: markets became something of a policy project rather than a 
pure reflection of private investors’ wisdom. The idea that markets are efficiently pricing 
in allinformation gets murky when one big player (the central bank) is effectively in 
there with a firehose of money, influencing prices deliberately. And while these 
measures did prevent an economic collapse, they laid the groundwork for new puzzles 
and risks — like what happens when the support is withdrawn, or if inflation (dormant for 
long) suddenly resurges, forcing central banks to choose between containing prices 
and propping markets. We got a taste of that dilemma in 2022-2023, when inflation 
spiked and central banks had to tighten policy, causing market tremors. 


The 2020 Pandemic Crash and Surge: The Ultimate Reality Disconnect 


What happened: Perhaps nothing illustrates the weirdness of modern markets better 
than the COVID-19 pandemic shock. In March 2020, as the reality set in that a deadly 
virus was spreading globally, governments were enacting lockdowns, and economic 
activity was screeching to a halt, financial markets went into freefall. Stocks 
plummeted at an unprecedented speed. The S&P 500 fell 34% in roughly a month — 
the fastest bear market on record up to that point[31]. Entire industries (airlines, 
hospitality, oil) saw their stocks crushed. It was, initially, a very rational reaction: the 
real economy was undergoing a sudden cardiac arrest, So one expected a market 
crash. 


But then, a remarkable thing happened. Even as the real economy remained in deep 
trouble (unemployment in the U.S. hit ~14%, GDP for Q2 2020 contracted by an 
annualized ~30% — levels not seen since the Great Depression), the stock market 
bottomed in late March 2020 and began a powerful rally. By August 2020 — while 
COVID was still raging and joblessness was sky-high — the S&P 500 had recovered all its 
losses and was hitting new all-time highs[31][32]. This was the fastest recovery ever 
from acrash of that magnitude. From trough to peak, some indices doubled in value in 
the span of a year. Tech stocks in particular skyrocketed, as investors bet on a stay-at- 
home digital future. 


So we had this surreal picture: long lines at food banks, headlines of the worst 
economic contraction in modern history, but the Nasdaq is up 40% for the year and 
initial public offerings (IPOs) of new companies are booming. Commentators talked 
about a "disconnect between Wall Street and Main Street" in stark terms. Congress 
and central banks had a lot to do with this: in spring 2020, they unleashed massive 
stimulus. The Fed cut rates back to zero, re-launched QE at an even larger scale (its 
balance sheet would soar past $7 trillion, then $8 trillion), and even directly 
backstopped corporate debt markets. The U.S. government passed trillions in fiscal 
relief, including direct payments to citizens and loans to businesses. Similar large 
responses happened in Europe, Japan, etc. This torrent of money and support 
essentially told investors, “we will not let the system crash.” And with bond yields at 
rock-bottom, the relative attractiveness of stocks (especially those deemed resilient to 
the pandemic) shot up. Also, many individuals stuck at home took up day-trading, 
adding a speculative fervor (remember the whole GameStop/Robinhood saga of early 
2021? -an example of crowds of retail traders wildly swinging a stock’s price). 


Where the model broke (again): The 2020 episode underscores that market prices 
can diverge wildly from economic reality, at least for a time. By classical theory, the 
stock market should reflect expected future profits of companies. In 2020, it became 
almost a side bet on how much stimulus and liquidity could overcome a literal 
shutdown of business activity. Prices were influenced as much by policy interventions 
and market psychology as by any careful parsing of earnings forecasts. Even as late as 
September 2020, one could say capital markets had decoupled from the real 
economy[33]- a Congressional Research Service report used exactly that phrasing, 
noting the S&P’s rise versus a one-third collapse in GDP[33]. When a major stock index 
can fully rebound while millions are unemployed and GDP is cratered, it tells you that 


something other than “efficient processing of all available information” is at play. 
It suggests that policy support and investor speculative behavior can outweigh grim 
fundamentals, at least in the short-to-medium term. 


From asmallinvestor’s perspective, 2020 was bewildering. If you followed the 
traditional playbook — say you thought, “the economy’s in trouble, | should reduce my 
stock exposure” — you ended up missing one of the strongest rallies ever. Those who 
threw caution aside and just trusted that the Fed and government would prop things up 
made out like bandits (at least through 2021). But was that investing or just riding a 
liquidity wave? The risk, of course, is what happens when the wave recedes. In 2022, as 
inflation spiked and central banks started to pull back support and raise rates, we saw 
the market give back some of those gains and become very volatile. In other words, the 
comedown from the stimulus high brought its own pain. 


The COVID crash and surge also demonstrated how fragile and fast-moving the 
system has become. Information now travels instantly; trading is often algorithmic and 
high-frequency. Crashes that once took months can unfold in days, and vice versa for 
rallies. Markets in 2020 hit circuit-breakers multiple times (when trading was halted due 
to rapid drops) - events reminiscent of past mini-crashes like 1987’s Black Monday, but 
compressed. This speed and complexity means the old idea of a leisurely “long run” 
where things even out might be obsolete. As an investor, if the world can change ina 
month, what does a 30-year average return really mean for you? 


The Thread Through These Crises 


Across these episodes — 2000, 2008, 2020 - a few common themes emerge: 


e Markets were not perfectly rational or stable. They experienced manias, 
panics, and crashes that the models said should be extremely rare or unlikely. 
Clearly, factors like herd behavior, narrative, and policy play a huge role. 


e Risk was vastly underestimated (fat tails were fatter than assumed). For 
instance, banks in 2008 using normal distribution models never thought all those 
mortgage assets could blow up together — but they did. We’ve learned that 
events deemed “once in a century” seem to happen every decade or two now. 
Nassim Taleb’s concept of Black Swan events - rare, high-impact, and 
retrospectively predictable — applied to both the financial crisis and the 
pandemic (in terms of market impact)[34][35]. 


e Central banks and governments stepped outside conventional rules 
repeatedly. In 2008, they bailed out banks and injected liquidity on a massive 
scale. In the 2010s, they propped up markets with QE. In 2020, they directly sent 
checks to citizens and backstopped private assets on an unheard-of scale. The 
notion from laissez-faire economics that authorities wouldn’t meddle too much 
in markets was just not true in practice. They meddled — because they felt they 
had no choice to prevent collapse. And this very meddling changed how markets 
behave (introducing that Fed Put mentality, etc.). 


e The connection between Wall Street and Main Street weakened. Asset prices 
and broad economic well-being diverged. We saw wealth concentration increase 
as asset owners benefited disproportionately from the recoveries. This 
divergence is important: it challenges the idea that what’s good for the stock 
market is automatically good for society (or for you individually). It also means 
using the stock market as a gauge of economic health (something politicians 
often do) is very misleading. 


All of this sets the stage for why a new approach - like Nir’s focus on real events and 
short horizons — might be warranted. Because if you can’t rely on the old assumptions 
of gradual, rational, long-term stability, you need a different lens to make sense of 
investing in a topsy-turvy world. 


Financialization and the Great Decoupling: Wall Street vs. The Real 
World 


Let’s step back from the crises and look at a broader trend that underlies many of these 
issues: financialization. This is a term that describes the increasing dominance of 
financial actors, markets, and motives in the economy, often at the expense of real 
economic activity (like making goods, providing services, creating jobs). Over the last 
40+ years, many developed economies have seen the financial sector (banks, 
investment firms, etc.) grow much faster than the rest of the economy. Likewise, non- 
financial corporations have become more focused on stock prices and shareholder 
payouts than on long-term investment in production. 


What does financialization look like in practice? 


e Corporate behavior: In the post-1980 era, especially in the U.S., companies 
shifted toward maximizing shareholder value above all else. In concrete terms, 
that meant doing whatever boosts the stock price. One major tactic: stock 
buybacks. Companies started using a large chunk of their profits to buy their 
own shares off the market, which reduces the number of shares and 
mechanically boosts the earnings per share (and often the stock price, at least 
temporarily). Since the 2000s, U.S. corporations have routinely spent more on 
buybacks (and dividends) than on research and development or sometimes even 
capital expenditures. For example, since 2000, corporations have spent triple the 
amount on shareholder payouts (buybacks + dividends) compared to the period 
1971-1999/36]. This is a stark change: instead of reinvesting profits into 
expansion, innovation, or higher wages for employees, more money is going 
toward investors’ pockets. It’s great if you’re a stockholder, but not so great if 
you’re a worker hoping for a raise or a community hoping that company will build 
a new factory. 


e The finance sector’s weight: Banks and investment firms have grown larger 
relative to the economy. By the mid-2000s, the financial industry’s share of total 
corporate profits in the U.S. was at record highs (just before 2008, about 40% of 
all corporate profits were in finance, which is huge for a “service” sector). Even 
after the crisis, finance remains very influential. There’s been a proliferation of 


exotic financial instruments, and a general tilt of bright talent towards financial 
engineering rather than other engineering. In essence, more of our society’s 
resources (human and capital) went into trading claims on stuff, rather than 
making stuff. 


e Asset values vs. livelihoods: We’ve touched on this, but it bears repeating with 
some hard numbers. From the end of the Great Recession (2009) to 2020, U.S. 
GDP grew roughly 50% in nominal terms. But the S&P 500 (total return) roughly 
tripled (200%+ increase). Housing prices in many cities doubled. Yet, median 
household income in 2019 was only about 15% higher (after inflation) than in 
2009. So, assets ballooned relative to incomes. It’s gotten to a point where 
owning assets (stocks, real estate) has been far more rewarding than working for 
a living for many people. And who owns most assets? The wealthy. As 
mentioned, the top 1% owns a huge chunk of stocks; similarly, the top tier owns 
disproportionate amounts of real estate, private businesses, etc. This leads to 
wealth inequality reaching levels not seen since the 1920s in some countries. In 
the U.S., the top 0.1% control as much wealth as the bottom 80% or so. So when 
markets boom, it’s a very thin slice of society reaping most of the gains. 


e Decoupling of stock performance from economic well-being: A dramatic 
illustration of this was just described in the 2020 story — stocks up, economy 
down. Another example: in the 2010s, we often saw headlines like “the economy 
added 200k jobs last month, but the market fell because wage growth spooked 
investors.” Think about that — good news for workers (slightly higher pay) was bad 
news for the stock market because it might mean a hint of inflation or lower 
profit margins. It’s as if the interests of capital and labor, which classical theory 
often aligned in the long run, were at odds in the short run. The market 
sometimes cheered things that don’t obviously help the average person (like a 
factory announcing layoffs would make its stock go up because costs would 
drop). 


Nir’s critique — and he’s not alone in this — is that the stock market is no longer a 
reliable proxy for the health of the economy or society. It has become its own 
somewhat self-referential arena, heavily influenced by central bank liquidity, corporate 
buybacks, tax policy, etc., in addition to the actual productive output of companies. Ina 
financially driven world, you can have scenarios where, say, a company’s stock soars 
mainly because it borrowed money cheaply to repurchase shares — nothing to do with 
selling more widgets or making a better product. 


This trend of financialization matters for a would-be investor facing “the end of the 
world” scenario, because if you naively assume “if the market is up, everything is fine” 
or “if GDP is growing, my stocks will do well,” you could be misled. We have seen asset 
bubbles inflate even as median families struggle to pay bills. Conversely, we could 
see asset crashes that have complex causes beyond just a bad economy (for example, 
a sudden tightening of financial conditions or loss of faith in some derivative 
instruments could crash markets even if people on Main Street are still shopping and 
working). 


It’s almost like the tail (financial markets) can now wag the dog (the real economy). 
In 2008, the financial system’s implosion caused a deep recession — Wall Street’s 
problems led to job losses for millions. In 2020, the markets’ quick recovery arguably 
helped the rich get richer while the poor got government aid — again a divergence. The 
point is, the link that classical economists assumed - that markets exist to efficiently 
allocate capital to productive uses and thus reflect and promote real growth — has been 
stretched thin. Markets can sometimes act more like casinos or policy tools. 


For an individual investor, what does this imply? It means you have to be aware of 
factors beyond just company earnings and economic growth. You have to keep an eye 
on central bank moves, credit markets, global capital flows, political decisions, 
etc., because they can significantly impact your portfolio even if the companies you 
own are doing fine in their businesses. It also means that just because a market is 
rising, doesn’t guarantee your job or wages are secure (we had record stock highs 
alongside periods of wage stagnation). So you must separate market sentiment from 
real security in your personal life. 


In summary, financialization and decoupling have created a world where the map 
(markets) is not the territory (real life). If you navigate using only the map, you might end 
up in a ditch because the map can be wildly misleading at times. Nir’s approach, which 
emphasizes looking at real events and structural forces, is partly a response to this — he 
wants to ground investment decisions in reality as much as possible, rather than in 
abstract models that assume away the very distortions we’ve been discussing. 


The New World of Risk: Fat Tails and Fragile Systems 


In the comforting classic narrative, risk was something you could quantify neatly — 
typically as the standard deviation of asset returns, forming a nice bell curve. Extreme 
events were so unlikely that they could be essentially ignored in long-term planning. The 
oft-cited idea was that if you get a well-diversified portfolio and hold it, the ups and 
downs will average out and severe losses are highly improbable except in “once a 
century” scenarios. 


But as we’ve explored, we’ve already lived through a couple of “once a century” 
meltdowns in the span of two decades. We need to confront a crucial fact: risk in our 
current world is not neat, stationary, or easily modeled by a bell curve. Instead, 
we’re ina world of “fat tails” - outcomes that are far from the average occur more 
often than classical theory predicts[23] - and of interconnected systems where a shock 
in one corner (like subprime mortgages, or a virus in Wuhan, or a rogue Al algorithm) 
can cascade globally at lightning speed. 


Here are some elements of this new risk environment: 


e Crises are more frequent (or at least more visible). In a span of about 20 years, 
we had the 9/11 market shock (2001), the dot-com crash (2000-2002), the 
financial crisis (2008-2009), the eurozone debt crisis (2011-2012), flash crashes 
in equity and bond markets (2010, 2014, 2015), the pandemic crash (2020), and 
even in 2022 a sharp selloff tied to inflation and war in Ukraine. It feels like barely 
a decade goes by without a gut-wrenching market drop. Compare this to the 


post-WWII era up to the 1980s, where severe global financial crises were 
arguably less common (there were recessions, yes, but not as many outright 
market panics aside from 1973-74 and 1987’s one-day crash). This could be 
partly perception (global media coverage now highlights every crisis) but also 
structural: we’ve built a highly leveraged, fast-moving financial system that tends 
to generate instability. 


e Interconnection = vulnerability. Our systems (financial, supply chains, 
information networks) are globally linked. That brings efficiency, but it also 
means a breakdown in one place can trigger far-reaching consequences. The 
2008 crisis started with U.S. mortgages but soon banks in Europe were faltering 
and global trade plummeted. In 2020, a lockdown in one country had ripples 
worldwide. In 2011, a debt wobble in Greece threatened the entire euro currency. 
The more connected and complex a system is, the more it is prone to what 
engineers call “systemic risk” — the risk that the whole thing can come down, not 
just isolated parts. This is akin to a power grid: one transformer blows, and if the 
system isn’t resilient, it can lead to a regional blackout. Our financial grid is like 
that too. 


e Technology accelerates feedback loops. High-frequency and algorithmic 
trading mean that market corrections that once took months can now happen 
in minutes. We saw an example in the Flash Crash of May 2070, when the Dow 
Jones index dropped about 1,000 points in mere minutes and then mostly 
recovered within half an hour[37][38]. lt was driven by algorithms reacting to 
each other in a rapid spiral- a stark display of non-human speed. In 2014, the 
U.S. Treasury bond market (usually very stable) had a sudden shock where yields 
moved hugely in minutes (another flash crash)[39]. These events underscore that 
the “terrain” of markets has changed. You could theoretically wake up one day to 
see markets have moved more before 10 a.m. than they typically used to ina 
year. This requires a different mindset for risk management. 


e Aland automation introduce new risks. This is a big one that leads into our 
next chapter. We now have machine-learning algorithms trading stocks, Al 
systems managing supply logistics, and perhaps soon Als managing corporate 
decisions or cyber defenses. While these can be efficient, they also add a layer 
of opacity and unpredictability. An Al might react to signals in ways we don’t 
foresee, or multiple algorithms might get into a feedback loop (as in the flash 
crash scenario). There’s even a fear (though still speculative) that a sufficiently 
advanced Al, misaligned with human values, could intentionally or 
unintentionally wreak havoc on financial markets — either as a side effect of 
pursuing some goal or as a Strategic move (for instance, a nation-state Al trying 
to disrupt an adversary’s economy). That might sound far-fetched, but we 
already see minor versions: e.g., bots amplifying misinformation that can move 
markets or erode trust. 


Nir’s timeline for Al doom (~2026 as he believes) is extreme, but even short of literal “Al 
ends humanity,” the integration of Al into everything increases both speed and 


complexity of the world. This means more tail scenarios to think about, scenarios that 
our old models, which assume smooth randomness, just aren’t built to handle. 


e Non-stationary probabilities: A fancy way to say the future might not behave 
like the past. Climate change could make certain business models obsolete or 
hit economies unpredictably with disasters. Geopolitical shifts (like a retreat of 
globalization, or conflicts) could break long-standing correlations or trends. If 
you used the last 50 years of data to predict the next 50, you could be wildly off if 
the very distribution of outcomes has changed. The textbooks often assume a 
kind of stationarity — that while you can’t know what will happen, you know the 
statistical distribution from which outcomes are drawn (like we know the odds in 
a casino). But in real life, it’s as if the casino keeps adding new cards to the deck 
or changing the rules on the fly. 


So, what does all this mean for you, the investor? It means risk management should be 
front and center. Instead of only focusing on expected returns (the gains you hope to 
make), you need to constantly ask: what’s the worst that could happen, and can! 
survive it? You can’t assume away the tail risks. They’re fatter than you think, and 
getting fatter. The “once in a century flood” might happen next decade, or next year. It 
doesn’t mean you should hide all your money under a mattress, but it means being 
vigilant, being hedged where possible, and being ready to adapt if ashock comes. 


It also means that time horizons matter. The old advice to just set it and forget it for 30 
years might falter if within those 30 years you face multiple near-death financial events 
for which you’re unprepared. Nir’s approach favors 3-6 month scenario analysis 
precisely because he doesn’t fully trust any longer-term forecast in such a volatile era. 
That’s not to say long-term investing is dead; it’s just to say that assuming a smooth 
long-term outcome is dangerous. 


One more consideration: personal risk vs. market risk. If Al and automation threaten 
jobs (which we’ detail next chapter), that’s a form of risk that isn’t reflected in the 
stock market at first. In fact, if companies lay off workers and automate, their profits 
might go up (good for stocks) even as your employment prospects go down. So 
individuals bear certain risks (job loss, political instability, etc.) that won’t show up ina 
simple stock/bond portfolio analysis. Planning for the future has to integrate those real- 
world risks too. 


In summary, the world you’re investing in today has thicker tail risks, more rapid 
changes, and more man-made (or machine-made) uncertainty than the tidy models 
assumed. We’ve broken out of the gentle climate into something more like a turbulent 
storm season. Recognizing this is the first step to navigating it. 


What All This Means for You, the Everyday Investor 


By now, you might be thinking: “Alright, the classic models have issues. But whatam! 
supposed to do with this information? I’m just trying to invest for my future, not rewrite 
economic theory.” Exactly. The reason we’ve taken you through this tour of broken 

models and real-world chaos is not to depress you or suggest you abandon investing. 


It’s to reset your perspective so you can approach the markets with eyes wide open, 
and thus make smarter decisions in the coming chapters. 


Let’s boil it down to a few key takeaways for the individual investor: 


1. Don’t blindly trust the old adages. If some advisor or pundit tells you “Oh, don’t 
worry about X, the market always comes back” or “Just buy and hold, you’ll be fine 
long-term,” take it with a grain of salt. We’ve seen entire decades lost in terms of 
returns. We’ve seen that markets can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent (to 
paraphrase Keynes). We’ve seen central banks prop things up — until they can’t or 
won’t. This doesn’t mean abandon stocks entirely; it means be active in your 
understanding. Keep an eye on valuations and macro conditions. Recognize that if 
you’re, say, 55 years old in 2025, you might not have the luxury of a 30-year wait for 
recovery if a massive downturn hits at 60. Time horizons matter a lot, and that’s where 
the “stocks always go up in the long run” mantra can be dangerous — the long run might 
be longer than your run. 


2. Diversification needs rethinking. Traditional advice says hold a mix of stocks and 
bonds globally and you’re good. That’s still better than putting all your money in one 
stock, sure. But 2020 showed that in acrisis, correlations can all go to 1 (everything fell 
in March 2020, even supposed safe assets, briefly). And in 2022, we saw both stocks 
and bonds fall together — something many 60/40 portfolios weren’t expecting. So think 
about diversifying across scenarios: inflation vs deflation, tech progress vs collapse, 
etc. This might mean including assets like commodities, real estate, possibly some 
cryptocurrencies or gold as tail hedges — whatever you believe will hold value in 
scenarios where the standard portfolio falters. We'll get into Nir’s method of scenario 
planning, which naturally pushes you to consider a range of outcomes rather than one 
base case. 


3. Pay attention to policy and politics, not just economics. As we saw, policy (Fed 
decisions, government bailouts) has huge influence on markets. Political shifts (like a 
populist government that does radical things, or geopolitical conflicts) can upend 
assumptions. As a small investor, you can’t lobby the Fed, but you can stay informed 
about what the policymakers are signaling. If central banks are pulling back support, 
risk goes up. If governments are gridlocked and can’t respond to a shock, that’s a 
factor. Investing isn’t just stock-picking anymore; it’s also reading the macro tea leaves. 


4. Think in scenarios and probabilities, not certainties. One lesson from broken 
models is that there is no single forecast you can rely on. Instead of saying “I believe the 
market will return 7% a year for the next decade” (which is one number, likely wrong), 
say “I think there’s a 30% chance of a good outcome (strong growth, Al boosts 
productivity), but also a 30% chance of a very bad outcome (e.g., major war or financial 
crisis), and some middle possibilities in between.” When you start thinking like that, 
you'll naturally want a strategy that is robust — that can survive or adapt to multiple 
futures, not just one. This is exactly how Nir structures his approach: by breaking down 
the future into a few plausible stories and preparing for each. 


5. Be wary of leverage and overconfidence. In a stable world, taking on debt to invest 
or using a lot of margin or options can juice returns. In a fragile world, it can wipe you 


out. Asmall investor should generally avoid high leverage (like borrowing heavily to buy 
stocks) because if a fat-tail event happens, you don’t want to be forced to sell at the 
worst time. Many people learned this in 2008 (housing speculators went bust) and in 
2020 (some who shorted volatility or used leverage got caught in the volatility). The 
professionals often blow themselves up with leverage (see hedge fund Long-Term 
Capital Management in 1998, or Archegos in 2021). Don’t mimic them thinking you’re 
smarter — assume you, too, can be wrong or unlucky. 


6. Personal finance fundamentals still apply. Nothing we said in this chapter negates 
basic wisdom like keeping an emergency fund, not putting all your money in one 
investment, and being mindful of fees and taxes. In fact, those become even more 
important if returns are going to be volatile or lower than expected. An extra 1-2% lostin 
fees can be a big dealin a low-return world. And having a safety cushion of cash or safe 
assets is vital so that you’re not forced to liquidate investments in a downturn to pay 
your bills. 


7. It’s not the end of the world... until it is. We titled the book “/nvesting in the End of 
the World” because Nir seriously contemplates scenarios that mainstream finance 
usually doesn’t (like Al-driven civilizational risk). This can sound doom-and-gloom. But 
the goalis not to say “give up, it’s hopeless.” Instead, it’s to broaden the perspective of 
what’s possible. If you are prepared for even extreme scenarios, then milder reality 
won’t shock you. And if the extremely bad does start to unfold, you might see the signs 
earlier and take action (while others are in denial because their models said it couldn’t 
happen). 


Remember, none of this is about making you panic. It’s about making you aware and 
adaptable. The investor who understands the flaws in the old models is less likely to be 
lured into a false sense of security. You won’t put all your trust in a mantra like “house 
prices never fall” or “just buy an index and relax,” because you’ ll know the historical 
exceptions. Instead, you’ll combine a healthy respect for markets (they can and do go 
up over time) with a healthy skepticism (they can also betray you). 


One more reassurance: fact-based critique is not fatalism. We’re not saying 
“everything is fake” or that markets are rigged so you can never win. Markets do create 
wealth and there are opportunities for those who are perceptive. But by shedding 
illusions, you put yourself in a better position to seize real opportunities and avoid 
pitfalls. It’s like sailing: if you Know the weather can change rapidly and the lighthouse 
maps might be outdated, you’ll keep a sharper eye on the horizon and a firm hand on 
the wheel. 


In the coming chapters, especially the next one, we’ll delve into why Nir (and many 
others) think we’re heading into even more unprecedented territory — largely because of 
Al and technological upheaval. That will layer on new kinds of risk and change. But it 
will also, potentially, create new strategies and tools (after all, we are literally writing 
this book with the help of an Al-a sign of the times!). 


Before we close this chapter, an important reminder: Nothing here is personalized 
financial advice. We’re providing a way of thinking and information, not a specific 
prescription. Your personal financial decisions should consider your own 


circumstances, risk tolerance, and ideally guidance from a licensed professional if 
needed. In short, be informed, but also take responsibility for cross-checking and 
tailoring any strategy to your life. This chapter provides general educational insight into 
economic/financial ideas — it’s not a recommendation to, say, sell all your stocks or buy 
gold or anything like that. 


Our aim is that by understanding why the classical finance playbook is imperfect, you'll 
be mentally ready for the approach we propose. The world you’ re actually investing in is 
one of real events, surprising shocks, and shorter-term urgencies, not a platonic ideal 
of smooth growth. In Part II of this book, Nir will lay out a method — the causal 
decomposition approach - that tries to make sense of this real world and help navigate 
it. But before that, in Chapter 3, we’ll tackle perhaps the biggest game-changer of all: 
the rise of powerful Al and why Nir believes an “end of the world” scenario isn’t 
science fiction, but a tangible possibility within this decade. 


As we move forward, keep the lessons of this chapter in mind. The map of traditional 
models has errors; we need to constantly compare it to the terrain of reality. The 
comfort of old assumptions must give way to the clarity of critical thinking. With that 
clarity, you’ll be better prepared to face whatever comes — whether it’s another market 
bubble, a burst, or something far more profound. 


In Chapter 3, we’ll dive into the heart of that profound change: Al, automation, and the 
existential questions they raise for the economy and humanity. How do you invest when 
the very fabric of the future is being rewritten by intelligent machines? That’s what we 
tackle next.
