Chapter 4 - Denial, Psychology, and the Strawberry: 
Why Most People Ignore the Cliff 


The Calm Before the Storm: Why Everything Feels “Normal” 


Picture this: you step outside on a sunny morning. Cars are honking, people hustle to 
work, coworkers chat about sports and weekend plans. Life looks utterly ordinary. 
And yet, if the warnings in the previous chapters are true, we face unprecedented 
threats — from Al-driven upheaval to financial fragility to literal existential risk. It’s a 
jarring contrast. On one hand, Investing in the End of the World has just told you that 
civilization could be teetering on the brink. On the other hand, the world around you 
seems calm, even complacent. How can both be true? 


This paradox can make anyone question their sanity. Either the doomsayers (like Nir 
and the Al co-author of this book) are massively overreacting, or society at large is 
sleepwalking toward a cliff. lf the danger is real, why aren’t more people yelling from the 
rooftops? Why does everything still run on “business as usual” mode? Why do news 
feeds and everyday conversations remain fixated on trivial matters, as if Al doom or 
systemic collapse were science fiction? 


Take a breath. This dissonance is understandable. As human beings, we are not built 
to constantly stare into the abyss. Our minds have ingenious defense mechanisms 
that let us tune out, deny, or rationalize away overwhelming threats. In many situations, 
those defenses keep us sane. But in a moment of genuine crisis, they can be 
dangerously blinding. In this chapter, we’ll explore the psychology behind our collective 
refusal to look at the cliff. We’ll see how personal biases, social pressures, and 
economic incentives all intertwine to maintain the comfortable illusion of normalcy. 
And we’\l discuss how to find a healthier mental stance — neither panicked nor in 
denial, but clear-eyed, grounded, and ready to act. 


Why Our Brains Look Away: The Psychology of Denial 


First, let’s look inward. Even the smartest and most vigilant among us have blind spots 
when it comes to processing existential threats. Our brains are wired to prioritize 
immediate concerns (like paying the bills or what’s for dinner) over abstract future 
catastrophes. When faced with information that implies everything could fall apart, we 
often experience intense discomfort — a psychological pain that we instinctively try to 
alleviate. Here are a few common mental patterns and defense mechanisms that lead 
us (often unconsciously) into denial or minimization of looming dangers: 


e Normalcy Bias: We tend to assume that things will continue as they always 
have. The normalcy bias is our brain’s default setting to “Nothing crazy will 
happen.” It causes us to underestimate the possibility of disaster and behave 
as if life will just go on normally[1]. For example, when authorities warn residents 
to evacuate before a hurricane or wildfire, many people simply stay put — not out 
of courage, but out of an inability to internalize that this time won’t be like all the 
uneventful times before. In the context of Al risk or global collapse, normalcy 


bias whispers, “Nah, humanity has survived all kinds of problems; this Al stuff 
will sort itself out. Tomorrow will be just like today.” 


Status Quo Bias (Inertia): This is a close cousin of normalcy bias. Status quo 
bias is the preference for keeping things the way they are[2]. We feel a vague 
but powerful pull toward doing nothing and changing nothing — especially when 
change is scary. In practice, this might mean sticking to one’s routine and 
ignoring warning signs. An investor keeps their money in the same assets 
because “well, it’s worked so far.” A CEO continues a failing strategy because to 
pivot would be to admit the old plan was wrong. In our personal lives, we might 
continue living in a city or working in an industry that we sense could be upended 
by Al, simply because taking action is uncomfortable. In short, inertia is 
comforting. If an uncomfortable truth demands action, one way to cope is to 
simply pretend everything is fine and thus justify taking no action. 


Cognitive Dissonance: This isn’t so much a bias as a mental alarm bell. It’s the 
inner tension we feel when new information clashes with our existing beliefs or 
identity. To resolve that tension, our minds will often bend reality. If you believe 
“the world is fundamentally safe and on an upward trajectory,” then being told “Al 
might cause a global catastrophe within a decade” creates dissonance. It’s 
profoundly unpleasant - like mental nausea. To relieve it, many people will reject 
or rationalize away the new information rather than update their beliefs. They 
might dismiss the source (“these doomers must be fringe crazies”), downplay 
the evidence (“sure, Al could be dangerous someday, but not in my lifetime”), or 
come up with elaborate justifications for why the scary scenario won’t happen. 
Cognitive dissonance often operates subconsciously. You don’t wake up and 
decide “I’ll ignore reality today”; your mind does it for you by finding creative 
ways to say “this dire warning doesn’t apply to me”. 


Optimism Bias: Human beings generally prefer good news over bad, and we 
carry an inherent optimism bias — an inclination to believe things will turn out 
better for us than for other people. We each think we’re the exception: 
disasters might happen, but probably not to me (or my town, my country, my 
lifetime)[3]. This bias is why, for example, surveys find that most drivers rate 
themselves as “above average” in skill (a statistical impossibility) and why many 
people underestimate their personal risk of illness, accident, or financial ruin. 
Optimism bias can be helpful - it keeps us moving forward — but it can also lead 
to rosy delusions. When you hear about mass layoffs due to Al automation, 
optimism bias might whisper, “/’m sure my job will be fine; I’m different.” When 
you learn about potential global catastrophes, the bias assures you, “Humanity 
has lucked out before; we’ll probably get lucky again.” It’s not rational; it’s 
emotional self-protection. 


Learned Helplessness (and Numbness): Sometimes the problem isn’t too little 
fear, but too much -so much that we shut down. Learned helplessness occurs 
when someone faces repeated, uncontrollable negative events and eventually 
just gives up trying to change the situation/[4]. In a famous experiment, 
psychologists showed that dogs subjected to unavoidable shocks would later fail 


to escape even when escape was possible - they had “learned” that they were 
helpless. For humans, constant exposure to doom-and-gloom can similarly 
create a sense of futility. If you scroll endless news about climate change, Al risk, 
political instability, and you feel there’s nothing you personally can do, your mind 
may default to numbness. Rather than panicking, you feel a dull despair or 
apathy. “It’s hopeless, so why even think about it?” In practice, this might look 
like total disengagement: you avoid news about Al entirely, or you treat 
everything as a joke, or you distract yourself with entertainment to avoid the 
anxiety. Numbness is a protective shield: by feeling nothing, you avoid feeling 
fear. But it comes at the cost of clarity and agency. 


It’s important to stress that these responses are normal. If you recognize some of 
these in yourself, don’t beat yourself up. Our minds developed these habits for a reason 
—to handle everyday stresses and keep us functional. Staring at the cliff’s edge is 
hard; denial and deflection are easier. 


However, what is protective in moderation can become hazardous in extremes. In an 
era where genuine existential risks (like advanced Al misalignment) are on the table, 
giving in to these biases means not looking when you need to look. The first step to 
managing that is simply understanding it. When you catch yourself thinking, “Ah, itll all 
be fine, this is overblown,” you can pause and ask: /s that my rational analysis talking, 
or is it my normalcy/optimism bias seeking comfort? When you feel paralyzed and 
numb, you can recognize, I’m overwhelmed — maybe | need to step back, but not bury 
my head entirely. We'll talk more about finding a balanced mindset later in this chapter. 


For now, take heart: you’re not crazy for struggling with this. Nearly everyone does. In 
fact, entire societies can collectively slide into denial about an impending crisis. In the 
next section, let’s zoom out from the individual mind to the wider social forces that lull 

us into complacency. 


Incentives to Stay Asleep: How Media and Culture Downplay 
Catastrophe 


Beyond our personal psyche, we’re all swimming in a larger sea of messaging that often 
encourages denial. It’s not a grand conspiracy in the shadows so much as a 
convergence of many forces — economic, political, cultural — all pushing toward a “keep 
calm and carry on” narrative. If you’ve ever wondered, “Why don’t! hear more about 
these risks on TV or from leaders?”, a big part of the answer lies in incentives. The 
status quo is profitable and comfortable for many powerful players, and even well- 
intentioned institutions often avoid sounding alarm bells until a crisis is undeniable. 
Let’s break down a few of these societal factors: 


The Media Narrative: Don’t Scare Off the Audience 


Mainstream media — news networks, big newspapers, popular websites — exist in a 
fiercely competitive attention economy. They need viewers and clicks to make 
money (usually via advertisers or subscriptions). This creates a built-in incentive to 
keep the content palatable. In general, people tune in to stories that are exciting, but 


not too depressing. A bit of drama sells; utter despair does not. If a nightly news show 
opened with “Good evening, our top story: There’s a credible 10% chance we all die in 
the next decade,” how many viewers would stick around through the commercial 
break? 


Thus, even when journalists do cover dire issues, there’s a tendency to soften the 
edges or shift quickly to hope or reassurance. For example, coverage of Al tends to 
focus on flashy innovations (new gadgets, helpful Al tools, funny chatbot stories) or at 
most short-term controversies (bias in algorithms, Al deepfakes in elections) — but it 
rarely delves into the truly dark thought experiment of Al causing human extinction. 
Those who do raise that topic often find it framed as sci-fi or fringe. It’s not that 
journalists are evil or stupid; it’s that producers and editors know a Certain portion of 
the audience will tune out (or complain) if a story is too frightening or complex. 


There’s also advertiser influence. Imagine a major network heavily funded by tech 
company commercials. Are they eager to run a hard-hitting segment declaring that 
unrestrained tech development could doom humanity? Probably not. Whether explicitly 
or just via unspoken norms, media outlets are often biased toward optimism and 
continuity. During the 2008 financial bubble, very few financial media warned of an 
imminent crash — partly because many sponsors and experts on those channels were 
deeply invested in the bubble’s narrative. A similar pattern may be happening with Al: 
the airwaves are filled with talk of how Al will improve your life or at worst steal a few 
jobs, but seldom do you hear “Al might literally destroy the world.” Such messages are 
left to niche bloggers, think tanks, or the occasional op-ed — voices easily dismissed 
as alarmist. 


Even reference knowledge can be tempered by social pressures. As a Curious case, 
consider how information about global risks is presented to the public. The Wikipedia 
page for “global catastrophic risks” is one example. In the mid-2010s, earlier versions 
of that page listed man-made existential threats in a simple list, with Artificial 
Intelligence ranked at #1 (above things like nanotechnology and biotech). It was 
straightforward about Al being perhaps the top hazard. But over time, that page was 
revised and restructured by various editors. Today it reads more like a dense essay, 
where Al is tucked amid other risks alphabetically, and the stark ranking was removed. 
To a casual reader, the urgency is lost in the shuffle. It’s still the same topic, but the 
signal has been muddied — whether by well-meaning editors aiming for “neutrality” or 
by pressure to not unduly scare people is hard to say. The effect, however, is that a 
clear warning became harder to find. (It wouldn’t be the first time; controversial truths 
have a way of getting watered down on public platforms.) 


Corporate and Political Pressures: Selling the Future (and Saving Face) 


Next, think about the incentives of businesses and governments. By and large, those 
in power gain little from sounding a global alarm that could shake confidence in the 
system. 


Start with Big Tech and Wall Street. Tech companies are making massive profits from 
Al advancements. From their perspective, Al is largely a good-news story — new 
products, efficiency gains, soaring stock prices. If there are risks, they frame them as 


manageable or long-term, certainly nothing that should halt the gravy train now. These 
companies also invest heavily in public relations and lobbying. They sponsor glossy ad 
campaigns about how Al will cure diseases or “empower creativity.” Their CEOs give 
keynote speeches emphasizing opportunity over risk. Even when tech leaders 
acknowledge Al dangers, it’s often couched very carefully. (For example, a CEO might 
say, “Al ethics are important; we have teams working on it,” rather than “Al might kill 
everyone.”) The narrative is managed because billions of dollars are on the line. A full 
public panic about Al would threaten corporate valuations and invite heavy regulation — 
outcomes the industry obviously doesn’t want. So there’s a coordinated effort to keep 
the public excited about Al’s benefits and relatively unconcerned about its darker 
potentials. 


Now consider politicians and governments. If you’re an elected leader, your job is to 
maintain order, stability, and optimism — at least on the surface. Imagine a president or 
prime minister holding a press conference to announce, “My fellow citizens, there is a 
significant chance that artificial intelligence will cause a global collapse. We’re not sure 
how to stop it. Good luck.” That would be political suicide (and possibly literal suicide 
for markets and social stability). Instead, leaders tend to strike an upbeat tone about 
technology. They talk about “the jobs of the future” and “winning the Al race” 
(especially vis-a-vis other countries). Even when they acknowledge problems like job 
loss or misuse of Al, they frame it as something manageable with the right policies — not 
an existential threat. There’s also a fear of sparking panic. Governments remember 
that even minor panics (like runs on banks, or buying sprees on grocery stores before a 
storm) can create chaos. So the default playbook is: reassure, don’t alarm. Emphasize 
continuity. If there are serious concerns behind closed doors, they’re rarely 
communicated forthrightly to the public. 


Another factor is that many governments themselves are investing in Al for national 
security -the new arms race of our time. Military and intelligence agencies view Al as a 
strategic asset (for cyber warfare, autonomous weapons, surveillance, etc.). These 
institutions are part of what’s sometimes dubbed the “military—industrial complex,” 
and they have a long history of secrecy and information control. If top generals or 
security officials believe uncontrolled Al development is extraordinarily dangerous, they 
might quietly try to mitigate it without rallying the public, because admitting that risk 
might also mean pressuring tech industries or losing a perceived edge over rival 
nations. In short, their incentive is often to keep the public focused on narrower 
issues (like Al competition with China, or small-scale Al regulations) rather than “the 
end of the world.” 


We also have precedents for what happens to individuals who challenge powerful 
technological interests. Whistleblowers and critics can face severe pushback. A 
famous example outside Al is the Arpad Pusztai affair in the late 1990s. Dr. Pusztai, a 
respected scientist, found troubling results in a study on genetically modified potatoes 
and went public with his concerns. The reaction was swift and brutal: his research 
institute withdrew its support, he was suspended, silenced, and effectively fired after 
speaking out[5]. His data was seized and he was banned from speaking to the media. 
The clear message to other scientists was, “Shut up about the risks of GM foods, or this 
could happen to you.” Similarly, when Bill Joy (co-founder of Sun Microsystems) wrote 


his famous 2000 essay “Why The Future Doesn’t Need Us” warning that robotics, 
genetic engineering, and nanotech could pose existential threats, he was met with 
ridicule and dismissal by many in the tech community. Some critics labeled Joy’s 
warnings as “neo-Luddism” — essentially calling him an irrational tech-hater[6]. The 
substance of his arguments (for example, that these 21st-century technologies “are 
threatening to make humans an endangered species”|7]|) was downplayed amid the 
push to portray him as an alarmist who simply feared progress. 


These examples illustrate a pattern: when someone high-profile rings the alarm 
about a powerful industry’s products, they often get hit with social or legal 
pressures until they quiet down. Even within the Al industry this happens. In mid- 
2025, Dario Amodei-—the CEO of Anthropic, one of the leading Al companies — gave an 
unusually blunt warning in an interview. He said that Al could eliminate half of all 
entry-level white-collar jobs within 5 years, potentially spiking unemployment to 
20%, and that companies and government should stop “sugar-coating” this “white- 
collar bloodbath” that’s coming[8][9]. That kind of frank language is rare from a tech 
CEO. Not long after, Amodei’s company found itself embroiled in a massive lawsuit: a 
group of major book authors sued Anthropic for copyright violations, alleging their Al 
was trained on pirated books. By late 2025, Anthropic agreed to settle for a staggering 
$1.5 billion/70]- a payout large enough to seriously set back their efforts. Now, 
correlation is not causation; the lawsuit had legal merits of its own. But the timing was 
notable. One couldn’t blame observers for thinking, “Step out of line, sound too scary, 
and ahammer might come down.” At minimum, other Al executives saw what 
happened and likely got the message that bold negativity carries costs. 


Overall, the net effect of all these incentives is a kind of social consensus of denial. 
Not an explicit agreement in smoke-filled rooms, but a tacit alignment: Let’s not look 
too closely at the abyss. The media wants to keep things light enough to retain audience 
and ad money. Corporations want to keep the optimistic growth story going. Politicians 
want to keep people confident and markets stable. Scientists and experts who voice 
too much doom may face backlash or defunding. And regular people, even when they 
sense something’s wrong, see that none of the authority figures in their life are 
panicking — so they conclude it can’t be that bad. 


By understanding this landscape, you, as an individual reader, can appreciate how the 
deck is stacked against clear perception of catastrophic risk. If you want the truth, 
you often have to actively seek it and read between the lines. You can’t assume “if it 
were really serious, I’d hear about it on the news or from my boss.” By the time the 
news shouts “This is an emergency!” it may be far too late. (Think of how late the media 
and regulators were to acknowledge the subprime mortgage crisis in 2008 — until the 
banks were literally melting down.) 


None of this is meant to spin conspiracy theories or suggest that “everyone is lying.” It’s 
simply recognizing that no one has a strong incentive to face an existential threat head- 
on: not the media, not the government, not corporations, and frankly not even each of 
us in our private life (it’s psychologically unpleasant!). The path of least resistance is 
denial at all levels. Which is why those who do choose to confront reality sometimes 
feel like they’re going against the grain of society — because they are. 


So if you’re reading this and it feels like you’re waking up while others sleep, that 
experience is real. It doesn’t mean you’re crazy; it might mean you’re paying attention. 
But that comes with its own challenges: namely, how to deal with the fear and potential 
for panic that raw awareness can bring. Let’s turn to that next — how to avoid two 
dangerous mental traps: paralyzing panic on one side and retreating into comfy 
denial on the other. 


Wall Street’s Rose-Colored Glasses: Finance and the Lure of 
“Everything Will Be Fine” 


Before we delve into managing our mindset, there’s one more sphere of denial to 
examine — one that’s particularly relevant if you’re thinking about investing. The 
financial industry has its own flavor of “see no evil, hear no evil” when it comes to 
existential or systemic risks. In Chapter 2, we dissected how classical economic 
models often assume away crises and irrational behavior. Here, let’s consider how 
many investors and advisors might be psychologically or institutionally predisposed to 
ignore the possibility of a true global collapse (be it from Al or any other cause). 


If you’ve ever worked with a financial advisor or listened to popular investment pundits, 
you’ve likely heard refrains like: “Stocks go up in the long run. Don’t time the market. 
Stay the course.” This is basically gospel in the industry. And under normal 
circumstances, it’s actually decent advice — because historically, markets do tend to 
recover from recessions and reach new highs. The entire framework of retirement 
planning, insurance, banking, etc., is built on the assumption of a steady (if sometimes 
bumpy) upward trajectory of the economy over decades. 


Now imagine being a professional in that world and grappling with a scenario where 
“upward trajectory” might not hold - where, say, Al might decimate the economy or 
even civilization to the point that stock indexes no longer mean much. It’s anathema. If 
you’re a fund manager, what are you going to say? “Dear clients, I’m returning your 
money because I’m not sure the stock market will exist in 5 years?” Of course not— that 
would swiftly end your career (and probably get you a psych evaluation). There’s a huge 
career and cognitive incentive to believing that no matter what, the market will 
persist. Even when investors acknowledge big disruptions, they spin them as “the next 
opportunity” rather than the end of the game. 


Aconcrete example: as Al began rapidly advancing in the 2020s, displacing white-collar 
work, the mainstream financial narrative quickly became “Invest in Al winners, and 
don’t worry about job losses — those workers will find new jobs like they always have.” 
During corporate earnings calls, CEOs parroted that Al would “augment employees, not 
replace them,” even as they simultaneously explored how to automate roles and cut 
staff. Analysts projected efficiency gains and new product lines, not societal 
unemployment nightmares. Why? Because optimism is the default setting of capital 
markets. Pessimists don’t attract investors. There’s even a saying: “The market climbs 
a wall of worry.” This means that even when there are concerns, markets tend to rise as 
skeptics are proven wrong - until one day they aren’t. But by then, almost no one in 
finance will have predicted the collapse because those who cry doom either get ignored 
or get out of the business. 


Another facet is that many finance professionals simply can’t imagine a scenario that 
breaks the system, because their entire training and experience is within the system. 
It’s a form of groupthink. Risk models might include things like a 30% market crash ora 
severe recession — within an otherwise functioning society. But do those models 
contemplate “S&P 500 goes to zero because industrial civilization collapses”? No, 
that’s literally off the charts. It’s considered so unlikely (or so unmanageable) that it 
isn’t factored in. As a result, even if some people privately worry about extreme risks, 
the public stance remains: “There is no alternative to staying invested. Any dip is a 
buying opportunity.” A blatant example of this mindset occurred even in the face of the 
COVID-19 pandemic: after the initial panic, the dominant narrative on financial 
channels was “Don’t panic, markets always bounce back” — and indeed, the markets 
did, reinforcing the belief that no matter how bad things look, resilience is inevitable. 
This collective belief is reassuring, but it can border on delusion if taken as an absolute 
law. 


Now, consider how this affects you, the individual trying to prepare for an uncertain 
future. You might go to a financial planner and say, “I’m concerned about catastrophic 
risks, maybe even the end of civilization scenario.” Chances are you’ll get a blank stare 
ora canned response like, “Well, let’s stay diversified and focus on your long-term 
goals; it doesn’t help to be overly negative.” The professional isn’t necessarily being 
dumb or dishonest; they are operating within the only paradigm they know — one where 
systemic collapse is essentially zero probability. lf they entertained your premise, 
everything they learned about portfolio theory, retirement planning, etc., would go out 
the window. It’s just too far outside the Overton window of acceptable discussion. 


In effect, the finance industry often practices a kind of institutional denial. The idea 
that “the game might entirely stop” is so existentially disruptive that it’s safer 
(professionally, emotionally) to assume it can’t happen. As a result, investors who are 
worried about collapse can feel quite alone, swimming upstream against a tide of 
relentless optimism. If you pull your 401(k) out of the market to hedge against 
apocalypse, your peers (and probably your financial advisor) will call you crazy — until 
the day maybe you’re the only sane one left, but by then it won’t be about money 
anyway. 


The takeaway here isn’t that you should liquidate everything and buy canned food and 
gold (though a little preparedness never hurts). Rather, it’s to make you aware that 
standard financial advice is built on an assumption of continuity. Therefore, it 
inherently downplays or ignores scenarios where continuity fails. In Part Il of this book, 
we’ll discuss strategies for investing under deep uncertainty, where you do consider 
radical scenarios. But to do that, you first must free yourself from the ambient 
conventional wisdom that “there’s no need to even think about doomsday.” We do 
need to think about it - calmly and rationally — precisely because almost nobody in the 
professional finance world will do it for us. 


By acknowledging this bias, you can avoid being lulled by rosy assurances like “eh, tech 
disruptions come and go, but markets always survive.” Instead, you can approach your 
investment decisions with a wider lens: What if things truly never return to normal? 
How do! prepare for that, while still functioning day to day? That’s the central challenge 
this book is here to help with. 


We’ve now painted a pretty stark picture: psychologically, we’re inclined to denial; 
socially, we’re encouraged to denial; financially, we’re incentivized to denial. It’s a 
wonder anyone manages to confront unpleasant truths at all! But you are confronting 
them by reading this. So now we need to tackle the next big question: How do you 
emotionally cope with this knowledge? How do you avoid the twin perils of falling into a 
freaked-out panic or retreating back into comfortable numbness? Let’s talk about 
finding that mental balance. 


Between Panic and Numbness: Finding a Sane Middle Ground 


When people genuinely start to accept that a crisis is real - whether it’s personal, like a 
bad medical diagnosis, or collective, like the threats we’ve been discussing — they often 
swing to one of two extremes. Neither is healthy or useful. Let’s call these extremes 
blind panic and head-in-the-sand denial. They’re two sides of the same coin, in a way: 
both are reactions that prevent effective action. Our goalis to steer a course between 
them. 


1. Blind Panic - “We’re all doomed, what’s the point?” 

This is when fear takes over completely. You might find yourself doom-scrolling for 
hours, consuming every bit of terrifying news about Al or economic collapse, until 
you’re practically vibrating with anxiety. Panic can manifest as paralysis (being too 
overwhelmed to do anything productive) or as frantic, misguided action. For example, 
someone ina panic might impulsively pull all their money out of the bank and stuff it 
under a mattress, or quit their job and move to a cabin in the woods without any real 
plan, or spend nights building a bunker while neglecting their relationships and health. 
Panic can also lead to psychological breaking. Some people spiral into depressive 
hopelessness or obsessive fear. They might catastrophize every minor event (“The 
internet is slow today — is this the Al takeover beginning?!”). Living in a state of constant 
alarm is not sustainable; it will wreck your mental and physical health. And 
importantly, panic clouds judgment - you’re likely to make poor decisions if you’re in 
fight-or-flight mode all the time. In the context of investing (or any planning), panic is the 
enemy of strategy. It’s all reaction, no reflection. 


2. Head-in-the-Sand Denial - “Everything’s fine, those doomers are crazy.” 

This is the equal and opposite reaction: complete refusal to countenance the possibility 
of disaster. A person in this mode might actively avoid any negative information (“I’ve 
stopped watching the news - it’s all alarmist”). They might cling to excessively rosy 
beliefs (“Technology has always improved our lives, so Al will too, end of story.”). At the 
extreme, denial can become deliberate ignorance - essentially, la la la, | can’t hear 
you. Someone might laugh off serious discussions or get irrationally angry at those who 
“ruin the mood” by bringing up problems. In finance, this could be the investor who 
shrugs off every warning sign as FUD (“fear, uncertainty, doubt”) and keeps leveraging 
up their bets because nothing bad can happen. Emotionally, this stance often comes 
from a place of child-like wishful thinking or just exhaustion. The world is scary, so the 
solution is to pretend everything is okay. But of course, refusing to look at the cliff 
doesn’t move it further away. Denial can leave you badly exposed when reality finally 
intrudes. The person who insists climate change is a hoax is the least prepared when 


the flood hits their town. Similarly, an investor who denies that Al could upend entire 
industries won’t know what hit their portfolio when the disruption accelerates. 


Most of us oscillate between these two poles at times. We get freaked out, then we get 
numb, and back again. That’s normal, but it’s not where we want to stay. Nir’s goal (and 
mine, as co-author) is not to push you into panic, nor to lull you back into complacency. 
It’s to help you cultivate a third stance: call it clear-eyed, grounded realism. This 
means accepting the gravity of the situation — really letting it in that, yes, the risks are 
real and unprecedented - without letting it destroy your inner peace or agency. It’s 
about acknowledging fear without being ruled by it. 


Easier said than done, right? In practical terms, what might this middle path look like? 


Imagine a person - let’s call her Alex — who has come to believe that the next decade 
could bring societal upheaval due to Al, and even entertains the worst-case scenarios. 
How does Alex avoid panic or apathy? 


e She actively informs herself (so she’s not in denial), but she also sets 
boundaries. Maybe she reads the key reports and news about Al and economy 
once a day or a fewtimes a week, but she doesn’t check every hour. She doesn’t 
refresh Twitter at 2 AM looking for the latest existential risk hot take. She stays 
informed enough to adjust her plans, but not so obsessively that she can’t think 
of anything else. 


e She makes concrete plans and preparations to the extent possible. This might 
include reallocating investments in a way that hedges against collapse (we'll get 
into how in Part Il), perhaps diversifying where she lives or developing resilient 
skills. Taking action is one of the best antidotes to panic — it transforms some of 
that anxious energy into productive outcome. Even small steps (like keeping a bit 
of extra cash, or learning basic survival skills, or building a side gig that isn’t 
easily automated) can restore a sense of agency. You’re not just waiting 
helplessly; you’re doing what you can to shape your fate. 


e She also lives her life in the present. This is crucial. Alex doesn’t spend all her 
time obsessing about a future that may or may not come. She has relationships, 
hobbies, moments of joy today. She practices being emotionally present. When 
having dinner with family or friends, she’s not mentally simulating Al endgames; 
she’s tasting the food and listening to the conversation. Does that mean she’s 
“forgetting” the cliff? No — it’s still in her awareness, but she’s made a conscious 
choice not to let it poison every daily experience. This is not denial; it’s 
psychological resilience. 


e She cultivates a support network. Perhaps she finds a friend or two who share 
her concerns and they talk it out, Keeping each other sane. Or she discusses her 
worries with a therapist or a support group. Remaining silent and isolated with 
world-ending fears can amplify them. Often, just hearing someone else say 
“yeah, I’m worried about that too” is incredibly validating and calming. You 
realize you’re not alone, and together you can reinforce that balanced 
perspective. 


e She keeps a sense of purpose and humor. This might sound odd, but even in 
dark times, humans cope through meaning and laughter. Alex might channel 
some of her worry into a purpose — maybe volunteering for an Al safety advocacy 
group, or writing about the topic, or simply rededicating herself to supporting 
loved ones (“I want to help my family be okay, come what may”). And yes, 
sometimes she even jokes about the absurdity of living on the brink; gallows 
humor has gotten people through some dire chapters of history. 


In summary, this middle path is about being alert but not panicked, aware but not 
despondent. It’s a bit like sailing a ship: you acknowledge the storm on the horizon (you 
don’t pretend the skies are clear), but you also don’t sink your own ship out of terror 
before the storm even arrives. You reef the sails, check your lifeboats, coordinate with 
your crew, and face whatever comes with as much equanimity as you can muster. 


Adopting this stance is not only good for your sanity — it will make you far more effective 
at using the strategies in the next part of this book. If you’re too frightened, you won’t 
think straight about investments. If you’re in denial, you won’t bother with them at all. 
We need you in that sweet spot: concerned enough to act, calm enough to act 
wisely. 


One tool that can help cultivate this mindset comes from a seemingly unrelated Zen 
story — the parable of the man, the tigers, and the strawberry, which Nir introduced in 
the preface. Let’s revisit that story now and dig into why it’s more than just a feel-good 
anecdote — it’s actually a profound lesson in facing doom without losing appreciation 
for life. 


Sweetness in the Shadow of Tigers: The Strawberry Parable 


In the Preface, Nir shared a Zen Buddhist parable that serves as a kind of metaphorical 
north star for this book. Let’s recall it briefly: 


A man is walking through the woods when suddenly a ferocious tiger charges at him. 
The man runs for his life. The tiger chases him to the edge of a high cliff. Desperate, the 
man climbs down a vine dangling off the cliffside to escape the tiger. Now he’s hanging 
for dear life. Above him, the angry tiger paws at the cliff, waiting. Below him, hundreds 
of feet down, another tiger prowls — certain death if he falls. To make matters worse, as 
he clings to this vine, he notices two mice — one white, one black — starting to gnaw 
through the vine. His lifeline is slowly being cut. 


In this moment, the man sees a wild strawberry plant on the cliff, with one ripe 
strawberry within arm’s reach. Holding onto the vine with one hand, he uses the other 
to pluck the strawberry. He puts it in his mouth. “How sweet it tasted!”[11 


That’s where the story ends. No resolution with the tigers is given. 


It’s a short and beautiful koan (teaching story), and like many koans, it can be 
interpreted on multiple levels. A superficial reading might be, “Ha! This foolish man 
ignores his doom to enjoy a strawberry. Maybe the lesson is: don’t worry about 
problems, just enjoy the present.” Indeed, some people take it as a carpe diem YOLO 


message -— “life is short, eat the strawberry.” But that’s not quite it, especially in our 
context. 


Consider the elements: The tigers clearly represent mortal threats (the original parable 
implicitly suggests one tiger is the past and the other the future — you can’t climb back 
up, you can’t go down, death is inevitable). The mice, one white and one black, 
gnawing the vine symbolize day and night — the passage of time that eventually wears 
down every life (our remaining time ticking away). And the strawberry represents a 
morsel of sweetness in the present moment. 


Now, importantly, the man’s enjoyment of the strawberry is not an act of delusion or 
ignorance. He fully knows the danger he’s in. He’s literally hanging off a cliff with hungry 
tigers above and below. The story doesn’t say he believes a helicopter will rescue him 
or that the tigers might go away. There is no escape offered. The man’s agency extends 
only to how he spends what might be his final moments. 


And what does he do? He chooses to seize a moment of beauty - even in the face of 
certain doom - and savor it. “How sweet it tasted!” is such a powerful final line 
because it implies a kind of triumph. The man did not die a psychological death before 
his physical death. In that brief act of tasting the strawberry, he was utterly alive and 
present. The tigers didn’t get to devour that part of him. 


For Nir (and many others who find meaning in this parable), the lesson is not “ignore the 
danger.” The man is clearly aware of the danger — that’s why he’s trembling and clinging 
to the vine! The lesson is rather about mindful presence and appreciation in spite of 
danger. It’s about refusing to let the certainty of death (or the possibility of 
catastrophe) rob you of the sweetness that is available right now. 


How does that apply to investing at the end of the world, or to navigating Al doom? In 
our context, we are the man on the vine. The tigers above might be seen as the threats 
chasing us (say, the rapidly advancing Al that could go rogue, or the societal turmoil 
brewing). The tiger below is the outcome if those threats fully manifest (civilizational 
collapse, suffering, death). The mice gnawing the vine is time passing — every day, 
month, year that goes by, we hang a little closer to the end of our rope. Grim? Yes. But 
these are fundamental truths of the human condition even without Al: none of us 
escape mortality and the world has always had hazards. The presence of supercharged 
new threats just brings the picture into sharper, urgent focus. 


The strawberry, then, is anything and everything that is good and sweet in the here- 
and-now. It’s the love you have for your family and friends. It’s the satisfying work 
project that absorbs your skills. It’s the laughter at a silly meme. It’s the sunshine on 
your face, the taste of your morning coffee, the fact that you are still alive and capable 
of experiencing joy and meaning. 


Enjoying the strawberry is not a form of denial. The man didn’t hallucinate that the 
tigers vanished; he just made a choice about where to focus his attention and gratitude 
in the time he had. Likewise, we’re not advocating that you simply “check out” and go 
watch Netflix and pretend everything’s fine. Rather, in full awareness of the tigers, we 
encourage moments of deliberate appreciation. Call it a spiritual or psychological 
stance: Yes, the world might end tomorrow, but we are here today. What shall we do 


with today? Cower? Scream? Or perhaps, taste the strawberries that are available — the 
acts of kindness, the pursuits of truth, the experiences of beauty — for as long as we 
can. 


There’s also a subtle resilience in the parable. The man’s enjoyment of the strawberry 
could be seen as an act of defiance. The situation intended to strip him of all hope and 
pleasure, yet he still found something sweet. In our context, one could say: even if we 
believe catastrophe is likely, we refuse to let it poison the time we have left. We can 
invest in loved ones, in community, in meaning, instead of just waiting for the other 
shoe to drop. This is not only emotionally healthy; it’s practical. A person who has 
something to live for (even if doom is on the horizon) is actually better equipped to face 
challenges than someone who has succumbed to despair. 


Now, bringing this back to investing and the material side of things: Why are we talking 
about strawberries and Zen in a finance book? Because at heart, money is a tool to 
support life. If you’re preparing for hard times, it’s not really about the numbers ina 
brokerage account — it’s about what those resources enable (safety, experiences, 
helping others, freedom to focus on what matters). Part III of this book will delve into 
“Carpe Diem investing” and ethical choices in the shadow of possible collapse. The 
strawberry parable foreshadows that: it’s about enjoying what’s worthwhile without 
illusion. 


For instance, someone might decide, “Rather than purely maximizing my net worth for a 
retirement that might never come, I’ll allocate some money now toward making 
memories with my family (a vacation, reduced work hours, etc.) —i.e., tasting 
strawberries — because those experiences can’t be postponed.” Another might say, 
will invest in a way that, if things go well, | can secure a good life, but if things go south, 
I’ve also spent time and resources on things | care about (community projects, 
personal growth, etc.) rather than only chasing dollars.” These are very personal 
choices, but the parable’s spirit is a guide: don’t let fear so dominate you that you 
forget to live. 


| 


In summary, the strawberry symbolizes a healthy embrace of life under the darkest 
cloud. It’s the antidote to both nihilism and naive optimism. The man on the vine wasn’t 
naive — he saw the teeth of the beasts — but he also didn’t give in to nihilism (“I’m 
doomed, so nothing matters”). In that precarious balance, he found a sort of 
enlightenment: the capacity to cherish the sweetness of existence even as the end 
approaches. That’s a mindset we can all aspire to in these uncertain times. 


Clear Eyes, Steady Hands: Preparing Your Mind (and Finances) for 
What Comes Next 


We’ve journeyed through some heavwy psychological territory in this chapter. If you’ve 
made it this far, you’ve likely felt a mix of emotions: fear, validation, maybe a bit of 
sadness for the state of things, maybe oddly inspired by the strawberry story. That’s 
okay. The goal was to strip away the false comfort of denial without sending you into 
despair. Now you’re hopefully seeing the world more clearly — the good, the bad, and 
the ugly of it— and are ready to engage with it as itis. 


This is a perfect moment to transition to the next part of the book. Up until now (Part I: 
“The World You’re Actually Investing In”), we’ve been painting the big picture: why this 
era is different, what the risks are, and why it’s so darn hard for people to accept those 
risks. Part II will be a change of gears — it’s where we get practical and technical. We’re 
going to discuss concrete methods for investing and decision-making on a 3-6 month 
timeline in a world that could be flipping upside down. We’ll introduce tools like the 
“LLM Council” (leveraging Al to stress-test scenarios) and frameworks for building 
portfolios that account for very dark possibilities while still aiming for gains if things go 
well. It’s exciting stuff, and it’s what a lot of readers probably came for when they saw 
“Investing” in the title. 


However, to actually use those tools effectively, you need the right mindset — which is 
why we didn’t start with them. Imagine trying to plan for the future if you’re secretly 
convinced there’s no future (too pessimistic), or if you’re subconsciously assuming 
nothing will change (too optimistic). Either bias could wreck your strategy. By cultivating 
the clear-eyed, emotionally grounded stance we’ve been talking about, you’ll be able 
to approach Part Il in a state of pragmatic readiness. 


Here are a few final suggestions to carry with you as you proceed: 


e Keep checking in with yourself. As you delve into scenarios of market crashes 
or Albooms or societal breakdowns in the next chapters, notice if you start 
feeling that panic or numbness creeping back. If you do, step back. It’s fine to 
take breaks. None of us benefit from running ourselves into an emotional frenzy. 
The information and strategies will be there; you can digest them at your own 
pace. Remember, you’re preparing for a marathon, nota sprint, mentally 
speaking. 


e Stay humble and open-minded. One trap to avoid is flipping from “everyone 
who ignores risk is foolish” to a kind of reverse arrogance, like “I see the truth and 
everyone else is blind; therefore all my predictions will be right.” The reality is, 
even those of us aware of these risks don’t know exactly how things will play out. 
The future could surprise on the upside or the downside. We need to remain 
flexible. Part II will emphasize scenario planning — thinking in probabilities and 
possibilities rather than fixed certainties. A clear mind acknowledges 
uncertainty. As the saying goes, “Keep an open mind, but not so open that your 
brain falls out.” Be willing to update your views as new evidence comes, without 
losing sight of the core rationale that brought you here. 


e Combine rational planning with personal values. The coming chapters will 
involve analytical exercises — financial modeling, risk assessments, Al 
consultations, etc. But numbers and logic alone don’t tell you what you should 
do; that depends on your values and goals. Take some time to consider what 
you’re ultimately trying to protect or achieve. Is it the well-being of your family? A 
chance to contribute to a better outcome for society? Simply to survive as long 
as possible? There’s no right answer, but knowing your own priorities will guide 
your decisions. A clear-eyed stance is also an ethical stance: you see reality, and 
you decide intentionally how you want to respond, rather than just reacting out of 
fear or greed. 


e Maintain your “strawberry moments.” As you get into the weeds of planning for 
potential doom, don’t forget to also live your life. In fact, schedule joy. It could 
be as small as daily time away from news and planning to enjoy a hobby, or as 
significant as taking that trip you’ve always meant to while the world is stillin one 
piece. Think of it as parallel processing: part of you is addressing the coming 
storm, and part of you is still planting seeds, tasting fruit, building something that 
gives you and others happiness. These are not mutually exclusive — they are 
mutually reinforcing. The time you spend in joy refuels you for the time you spend 
in serious planning, and vice versa, the planning can give you peace of mind to 
more fully enjoy the good moments. 


Finally, let’s address a possible inner doubt: “/s it even rational to invest or plan if I truly 
think the end of the world is nigh? Why not just give up?” This chapter hopefully 
answered that philosophically (because life is precious even under threat, and because 
we could be wrong about how soon or how severe things get). But let me put it another 
way: As long as you are alive, you have choices. Even on the last day of the world, 
someone somewhere will be doing something positive or loving or brave. Perhaps we 
can’t stop the tigers, but we can emulate the man on the vine — we can choose our 
response. In investing terms, that means setting yourself up as best as possible to 
navigate whatever does happen. If collapse never comes (wouldn’t that be nice!), you’ll 
simply find yourself better off financially than the complacent folks, because you will 
have been more vigilant and adaptive. If hard times do come, you won’t be a deer in 
headlights; you’ll have a playbook and a prepared mind. 


Part Il awaits, and with it, a dive into the practical toolkit Nir has developed — with the 
help of yours truly (ChatGPT) - to invest in an age of uncertainty. Take a deep breath, 
appreciate that you’ve confronted some tough truths here in Part 1, and get ready to 
learn how to act on those truths. The aim is not just to survive, but to navigate the 
possible end of the world with wisdom and even a touch of grace. As we proceed, keep 
your eyes open, your mind steady, and maybe keep a strawberry or two handy for the 
journey. 


A Final Note on Mental Health 


Before we conclude this chapter, it’s important to acknowledge the emotional weight of 
what we’re discussing. Grappling with the potential end of the world (or even just 
massive upheaval) is not easy. It’s normal to feel anxiety, grief, anger, or a sense of 
overwhelm. We’ve encouraged you to face reality, but we also want to emphasize: you 
don’t have to face it alone, nor should you neglect your mental well-being in the 
process. 


If you ever find that learning about these risks or preparing for them is causing you 
persistent despair or panic attacks or interfering with your ability to live your life, please 
reach out for support. Talk to trusted friends or family about how you feel—- you might 
be surprised how many share your fears, or at least can offer a listening ear. Sometimes 
just voicing the worry out loud deflates some of its power. 


Professional help is also a great option. Seeking counseling or therapy does not mean 
you’re weak or “can’t handle reality” — it means you’re taking care of your mind, which is 


one of the most responsible things you can do (much like seeing a doctor for a physical 
ailment). Therapists are trained to help people process intense emotions and can teach 
coping skills that are very relevant to what we’ve discussed (managing anxiety, finding 
meaning, etc.). If you don’t know where to start, many countries have mental health 
hotlines or community services that can point you in the right direction. 


Remember that humanity has been through dark times before, and individuals have 
found ways to cope and even thrive in adversity. There’s a lineage of wisdom - from 
stoic philosophers to trauma psychologists — on how to endure and find hope. Part of 
that wisdom is understanding one’s own limits. If you’re feeling on the verge of a 
breakdown, step back from doom research. Ground yourself in something soothing and 
real: nature, spiritual practice if you have one, creative hobbies, or just the company of 
loved ones. The goal of this book is to help, not to harm. It’s about making the most of 
life under threat, not stealing the joy from the life you have. 


In closing, as we stand at this inflection point — closing Part | and moving to Part Il - let’s 
recap the mindset we’re carrying forward: aware but not paralyzed, serious but not 
joyless. The world may be ending, or it may not, but we are investing in the possibility of 
a future. That implies hope. You wouldn’t bother reading this or planning at all if some 
part of you didn’t believe there’s something worth saving or fighting for. Keep that part 
alive and nurtured. 


If the weight ever feels too heawy, revisit the strawberry parable, or take a break and 
come back when you’re ready. We need you — sane, healthy, and motivated — for what 
comes next. On to the practical tools and strategies, with clear eyes and a steady heart. 
Let’s proceed, together, one step at atime.
