Chapter 5 — Reading Stock Charts Like a Physicist, Nota 
Day Trader 


How Most People Get Hypnotized by Charts 


Many investors — especially newer ones — find themselves hypnotized by stock charts. 
It’s an almost trance-like state: staring at those flickering lines and numbers, heart 
racing with each uptick or downtick. You might recognize the feeling. Imagine watching 
a stock’s price wiggle on a 1-day chart: it’s up a little, now it’s down, now up again. 
Each movement triggers a tiny spike of emotion — excitement on the green bumps, 
anxiety on the red dips. Before long, you’re overinterpreting every little squiggle as if it 
were a meaningful omen. This is how most people end up reading charts in a confused 
way, reacting to short-term noise that, in truth, says almost nothing about the stock’s 
real value. 


Why does this happen? Part of the reason is information overload and our brain’s 
tendency to see patterns everywhere — even in randomness. A rapidly updating chart 
can feel like a heart monitor for your money: every blip seems life-or-death. News 
headlines and social media amplify the effect, pouring in a constant stream of 
commentary that makes minute-by-minute price moves feel significant. But as we’ll 
see in this chapter, most of those tiny moves are meaningless noise[1 [2]. The real 
story of a stock — the signal - only emerges when you step back and look at a bigger 
timeframe. We want to help you break out of that hypnotic fixation on the immediate 
twitching of prices. Instead, you’ll learn to read stock charts with a calm, discerning 
eye, more like a scientist observing data than a gambler glued to a slot machine. 


The Day Trader and the Physicist: Two Ways of Seeing the Same Line 


Let’s illustrate two very different mindsets for looking at a stock chart. We call them the 
Day Trader and the Physicist. They could both be looking at the exact same price 
graph, but you’d think they were on different planets by how differently they interpret it. 


e Day trader mindset: The day trader lives for the short term. Picture someone 
with multiple monitors, 5-minute and 1-hour charts flashing, maybe even tick- 
by-tick price quotes. Every tiny move must mean something. A modest jump in 
the morning? The day trader imagines a hot trend and feels the rush of FOMO 
(fear of missing out). A sudden dip at lunch? Panic —- maybe an ominous pattern 
foreshadowing a crash. This mindset treats every small wiggle as meaningful, 
jumping in and out of trades to capture quick profits. Emotions run high: joy from 
a quick win, stress from a quick loss, dopamine hits from the action. It’s almost 
like playing a video game or gambling — constant feedback, constant reaction. 
Day traders often rely on very short-term technical patterns and may believe they 
can predict the next tick by sheer screen-watching prowess. In essence, the day 
trader sees a stock chart as a source of immediate signals — patterns to act on 
right now — and tends to ignore the fundamental context. It’s all waves with no 
sense of the tide. 


e Physicist mindset: Now imagine the physicist — not literally a physics PhD, but 
an investor who thinks like one. This mindset steps back and looks at the longer- 
term patterns in the data. Where the day trader sees a crazy, squiggly line, the 
physicist sees a time series of measurements. Every stock price is a data point 
produced by underlying forces (we’ll dig into those forces soon). A physicist- 
minded investor knows that any measurement (like a stock price) has noise - 
random short-term fluctuations — and signal —- the true underlying trend or 
pattern[1]. Instead of zooming in to every minute, they zoom out to observe a 
stock’s behavior over months or years. They ask questions like: “What broad 
trend do! see here? Is the stock generally rising, falling, or flat over the past year? 
What events correspond to the big moves?” The physicist is comfortable 
ignoring short-term wiggles as mostly random noise. Like a scientist smoothing 
out experimental data, they might mentally filter out those intra-day jumps and 
drops that the day trader obsesses over. In emotional terms, this mindset is 
patient and analytical. There’s less adrenaline involved. A physicist-minded 
investor can look at a volatile day and say, “Meh, that’s just a ripple on the 
surface,” whereas the day trader might have felt compelled to buy or sell three 
times before lunch. 


In short, the day trader sees a stock chart as a thrilling short-term battleground, while 
the physicist sees it as a record of underlying forces playing out over time. One is 
looking for quick wins in the noise; the other is looking for meaningful signals in the 
bigger picture. Our approach in this book leans heavily toward the physicist’s way. Even 
though we’re not literally doing physics, adopting that calmer, data-driven perspective 
will help you see what really matters on a chart. 


Analogy time: Think about ocean waves. A day trader fixates on the little waves and 
chop on the surface — reacting to every crest and trough as if it’s important. A physicist 
notices the tide and the current. Yes, there are many small waves (Some caused by 
random wind gusts), but underneath, there might be a strong tide pulling in or out. If 
you’re standing at the beach, the difference is huge: if you only watch the ripples, you 
might not notice that slowly but surely the water level is rising. The physicist-minded 
investor pays attention to that slower rise or fall —- the tide of the market or the stock’s 
trajectory — rather than each little splash. In investing terms, the “tide” might be a multi- 
month upward trend due to, say, a company’s sales growth or a new technology wave. 
The “random ripples” are the daily price quirks that don’t leave a lasting mark. 


Another analogy: consider measuring the temperature. If you stare at minute-by- 
minute temperature readings on a random day, you’ll see it jump a bit up or down - 
maybe a cloud passed by or a breeze cooled things momentarily. If you plot those 
minute changes, it might look wild. But zoom out to the daily scale and you see the 
clear pattern: it was cool at night, then the sun came up and it got warmer, then cool 
again after sunset. Zoom out further to monthly averages over a year, and you see an 
even clearer signal: summer is hot, winter is cold. The small fluctuations average out; 
the big seasonal trend stands out. Stocks are similar. Day traders are glued to the 
minute-by-minute “weather”; physicist-style investors focus on the “climate” of the 
stock’s performance. 


Noise vs. Signal: What Actually Matters in a Stock’s Path 


We’ve mentioned noise and signal, so let’s dig a bit deeper into those. These terms 
come from science and engineering (imagine static on a radio vs. the music you want to 
hear), but they apply perfectly to stock charts. 


Noise in the stock market refers to all the random, short-term, often insignificant price 
movements that constantly occur[3][2]. It’s the day-to-day (or minute-to-minute) 
volatility that doesn’t reflect any meaningful change in the company’s value. This noise 
cancome from all sorts of trivial or transient factors: a flurry of algorithmic trades, a 
piece of gossip on Reddit, a minor analyst quote, or just the normal ebb and flow of 
traders buying and selling for no profound reason. Signal, on the other hand, means the 
movements that do reflect something real - a genuine change in the market’s view of 
the company’s worth, driven by substantive events or shifts (strong earnings, anew 
product, arecession, etc.). 


How can you tell noise from signal? Often it’s a matter of time and magnitude. A 
classic saying (often attributed to Benjamin Graham) is that “in the short run, the 
market is a voting machine, but in the long run it’s a weighing machine.” In other words, 
short-term prices are like opinion polls (noise), whereas long-term prices 
eventually reflect true value (signal)/4]. Let’s make that concrete: 


e lfastockis up or down 1-2% in a day, that’s usually noise. Stocks wiggle a 
percent or two all the time without any real news. It could just be random trading 
noise or the market’s mood that day. Nothing fundamental at most companies 
changes in a single day - a company doesn’t magically become 2% more 
valuable by lunchtime and then 1% less valuable by the close. As an investor, 
you typically shouldn’t get excited or alarmed by a 1-2% daily move; it’s normal 
“breathing” of the market. In fact, even moves up to around 5% in a day might be 
just routine volatility or a reaction to a small news item that will be forgotten next 
week[5]. 


e |fastock moves, say, 30% or 50% over a few months or a year, that is almost 
certainly signal. A change of that magnitude usually means something real 
happened. Perhaps the company’s profits grew significantly, or it launched a hit 
product, or conversely it lost a big lawsuit or its sector went into a slump. Big, 
sustained moves don’t happen by pure chance; they reflect investors collectively 
re-evaluating the company’s future earnings or risk in light of new information. 
For example, when a company’s stock climbs 40% over six months, you can bet 
there were real forces at work — maybe a whole industry trend lifting it or 
excellent quarter results each period. Likewise, a 40% drop in a year might 
correspond to a major setback or economic downturn affecting the business. 


Put simply: noise is the tiny squiggles, signal is the broad wave. One useful mental 
trick is to expect noise. Know that on any given day, a stock can bounce around for no 
good reason — and that’s okay. It doesn’t require action. As Investopedia succinctly 
puts it, “the shorter the time frame, the more difficult it is to separate meaningful 
movements from the noise”, and “the price of a security can vary widely in a day, but 
almost none of this movement represents a fundamental change in value”[5]. 


Conversely, “a longer time frame can provide a clearer picture of a trend”[6]- zooming 
out filters out a lot of the random noise and lets the real signal emerge. 


Consider an example of noise vs. signal in action. Think about earnings 
announcement day for a company. The stock might swing wildly that day— up 5% in the 
morning, then down 3%, then up again, as traders react to the report and conference 
callin real-time. That intra-day chaos is mostly noise. Now step back: if, over the next 
three months, the stock ends up 25% higher in a steady climb, that’s signal -— it tells us 
that those earnings (and perhaps subsequent developments) convinced investors the 
company is more valuable than before. The initial frenetic reaction contained a lot of 
noise, but the eventual trend reveals the signal. 


Another vivid illustration was the COVID-19 pandemic’s effect on different stocks. 
When lockdowns began in early 2020, almost the entire market crashed — a lot of it was 
signal (a real economic shock). But within that, some stocks had exceptional moves 
based on real-world changes: for instance, shares of Zoom Video Communications 
exploded upward in 2020 because suddenly everyone needed to work and socialize via 
Zoom. In a matter of months, Zoom’s stock soared on the order of 700% from its pre- 
pandemic levels|7]- a massive move that clearly signaled a fundamental surge in 
demand for its service. By late 2020, Zoom was trading at prices many times higher than 
at the start of the year, reflecting real revenue growth and usage that went through the 
roof. That wasn’t random noise; that was a signal of a new reality for the company’s 
business. Of course, later on, as life normalized and competitors caught up, Zoom’s 
stock tumbled more than 85% from its peak([8]- again, a very meaningful multi-month 
move indicating that the pandemic-fueled boom was tapering off. If you zoom in (no 
pun intended) to the daily chart during those times, you’d see wild volatility - huge 
swings on individual days when news hit. But the overarching picture (Zoomed out to a 
year or two) shows a clear rise and fall linked to major real-world developments. 


The key takeaway: Don’t let short-term noise hijack your focus. We want to 
concentrate on the longer-term signals — the moves that actually matter for your 
investments. In practice, that means training ourselves to step back from the day-to- 
day gyrations and spend more time looking at multi-month and multi-year trends. In the 
next section, we'll get very practical about how to do that using common chart tools. 


Timeframes: Why 1 Day Lies and 5 Years Tells the Truth 


Take a look at any stock charting tool - whether it’s Google Finance, Yahoo Finance, or 
a brokerage app — and you'll notice you can change the timeframe of the chart. 
Usually, you have buttons like 1D (1 day), 5D (5 days), 1M (1 month), 6M (6 months), 
YTD (year-to-date), 1Y (1 year), 5Y (5 years), and MAX (max history). These are like 
zoom lenses on a camera. And just like a camera zoom, what you see (and how it looks) 
can change dramatically depending on your zoom level. 


One of the simplest habits you can develop is to ignore the ultra-short timeframes 
and focus on the longer ones. Specifically, in our investing method we almost never 
bother with the 1D, 5D, or 1M views of a stock. Those views are dominated by noise 
and are essentially lying to you about the bigger picture. A 1-day chart can make a 
stock look extremely volatile — it might show a price zigzagging up and down, maybe a 


3% range that day, which visually could look like a mountain range. But that same day’s 
movement on a 1-year chart might appear as a barely noticeable blip. The short chart 
exaggerates the significance of that day’s action. 


In contrast, the 6M, 1Y, 5Y, and MAX charts tend to tell the truth about the stock’s 
trajectory. They filter out the day-to-day static and show you the meaningful trends. 
Let’s break down each briefly: 


6M (Six Months): This view is great for seeing the stock’s recent trend over 
roughly half a year (which nicely aligns with our 3-6 month trading horizon). Ona 
6M chart, noise gets smoothed out a bit. You can see if the stock has been 
generally rising, falling, or flat since, say, last season. Important moves - like a 
20% climb after a product launch, or a 15% drop during a market sell-off — will be 
very evident in a 6M view. But the little zigs and zags from single days will mostly 
blend into the curve. If nothing big happened, the 6M chart will look relatively flat 
or gently sloping, whereas a 1M chart might have looked choppy even if it 
ultimately went nowhere. 


1Y (One Year): The 1Y chart goes further in separating signal from noise. With a 
full year in view, you’ll clearly spot any major event impacts. Did the stock crash 
last autumn? Did it soar in spring? Those will show up as distinct moves on the 
1Y timeline. The daily volatility within each week gets compressed, so you see 
the forest, not the trees. For example, if you look at a one-year chart of a travel 
company, you’d easily see the big drop when a new COVID variant caused 
lockdowns, and the recovery later — even if that drop took a couple of weeks, it 
stands out at the 1Y scale. A1D or 5D chart around that event might have been 
absolute chaos, but a 1Y chart makes the overall effect crystal clear. 


5Y (Five Years): The 5-year chart is powerful for revealing long-term cycles and 
the truly massive signals. On a 5Y or MAX chart, only very significant events 
register. Minor ups and downs get ironed out almost completely. What shows 
up? Things like the 2020 pandemic crash and recovery, or the 2022 bear market 
in tech stocks, or a big bull run in 2017. If a company introduced a revolutionary 
product in 2019 that doubled its stock by 2021, the 5Y chart will highlight that 
growth. If another company slowly declined over 5 years due to fading business, 
you'll see that long descent —- something that no 1-month chart would ever tell 
you. Essentially, the 5Y/MAX view acts as a noise filter, leaving mostly the 
structural trend and the major turning points. 


MAX (All available data): Hitting “Max” usually shows you the stock’s entire 
trading history (or at least many decades if available). This is where you see the 
really big picture. For a well-established stock, the MAX chart might show 
multiple booms and busts: maybe the dot-com bubble of 2000 as a blip, the 
2008 financial crisis dip, etc., all the way to today. The MAX chart is great for 
context — it reminds you that, for example, even huge crashes can be just 
chapters in a longer story. On a MAX chart of the S&P 500, the crash of 1987 is 
just a quick V-shape, 2008 is a larger dip, and 2020 is another dip — but the long- 
term trend is up (until it isn’t, if the “end of the world” truly comes!). Now, in our 
framework, we don’t invest for 30-year horizons, but it’s still useful to know the 


lifetime context of a stock or sector. The MAX view filters out almost everything 
except the most important events. If a company’s MAX chart shows one spike 
and crash that dwarfs all else (like many meme stocks do), that tells you 
something about how unusual that event was. 


Let’s apply this practically with an example using a real stock and a common chart tool 
(Google’s stock chart). Suppose we’re looking at Nvidia (NVDA) — a large tech company 
many readers might have heard of. Nvidia’s stock was relatively flat or modestly up and 
down for a couple of years, and then in late 2022 into 2023, it exploded upward due to 
excitement around Al (artificial intelligence) demand for its chips. If you pull up a 6M 
chart for Nvidia covering early 2023, you’ll see a dramatic climb — a clear signal of 
something big. In fact, in the first five months of 2023, Nvidia’s stock surged roughly 
180% thanks to an Al boom driving demand for its technology[9]/[10]. On the 6M chart, 
that looks like a steep mountain; you can’t miss it. Now imagine if you only looked at 
Nvidia’s 1-day chart on a random day during that period. One day might show, say, a 
4% jump or a 2% drop, and you might not grasp how significant the overall rise has 
been. The one-day view would have kept you focused on daily volatility, while the 
six-month view shouts the real story: “something fundamental changed; this stock is 
ona tear!” 


Nvidia’s stock price over a six-month span in 2023. The chart (from Google Finance) 
shows NVDA rising sharply - a ~138% gain — amid an Al-driven rally. On a multi-month 
view like this, the signal of a massive upward trend is clear, whereas intraday noise 
barely registers. 


Likewise, if you look at a 5-year chart of Nvidia, you’d see not only the 2023 boom but 
also other key signals: a dip in 2018 (when crypto-mining demand fell, hurting Nvidia 
briefly), a sharp fallin early 2020 (pandemic panic), a huge rise through 2020-2021 
(tech boom), another dip in 2022 (tech slump), and then the 2023 Al surge. Each of 
those multi-month or multi-quarter moves corresponds to real events or cycles. By 
contrast, a 1M chart at any given point in those years might have just looked like a mess 
of ups and downs with no context. 


So our rule of thumb is: use 6M and 1Y charts for tactical insight, and use 5Y/MAX 
charts for big-picture context. Ignore 1D, 5D, 1M for our purposes — they will tempt 
you to act on noise. One exception: if you’re timing an entry or exit today, you might 
glance at an intraday chart to not place an order at an oddly high or low moment in the 
day (some people check that to avoid “buying the intraday top”). But generally, all 
important decisions in our method should be driven by what you see in the multi-month 
views, not the daily gyrations. 


A quick exercise you can try (we’ll give a more detailed “homework” at the end of this 
chapter): open a stock chart and deliberately click each timeframe from 1D to MAX. 
It’s almost funny how different the story of the stock looks at each setting. The 1D might 
say: “This thing is unpredictable! It’s all over the place!” The 1Y might say: “Actually, it’s 
been steadily rising, with one big dip in the middle.” The 5Y might say: “Overall, this 
stock doubled in five years, but with two rough patches.” It’s the same stock —- same 
reality - but you need the right lens to see the aspects of reality that matter for your 


investing. We care about 3-6 month outcomes, so we need lenses that reveal trends 
and signals in that range, and not lenses that drown us in irrelevant detail. 


Why Our System Lives in the 3-6 Month Window 


You might be wondering, why do we emphasize a 3-6 month investment horizon in 
this book? It’s a bit unorthodox — traditional investing books often talk about holding for 
years or decades (long-term retirement investing) or, at the other extreme, very short- 
term trading. Our approach sits in a sort of middle zone, and it’s very much by design 
given the worldview we’re working with (recall: Nir’s view that there’s a significant 
chance of an Al-driven collapse around the near future, perhaps ~2026). Let’s break 
down the reasoning: 


Less than 3 months is mostly noise. In very short horizons (days or weeks), stock price 
movements are dominated by randomness and short-lived events. As a small, 
individual investor, trying to consistently predict what a stock will do in the next two 
weeks is incredibly difficult - you’re essentially competing with high-speed algorithms, 
insider information (that you don’t have), and sheer luck. For example, say you buy a 
stock hoping it goes up this month — and then some totally unpredictable headline (a 
war, a virus outbreak, a CEO scandal) knocks it down next week. There’s just too much 
chaos in the short term. Moreover, frequent trading (daily/weekly) incurs more costs, 
more chances to make mistakes, and tends to start resembling gambling rather than 
strategic investing. We are not running a day-trading or swing-trading scheme here; 
those require constant attention and carry high risk of being whipsawed by noise. So we 
avoid the ultra-short game. 


Beyond 6-12 months, the crystal ball gets hazy (especially if the world might end!). 
In aconventional setting, you might invest with a 5, 10, or 20-year horizon, counting on 
the idea that good companies will grow over long periods. And historically that’s often 
been true. But our book’s context is unusual: we’re contemplating the “end of the 
world” scenario driven by Al and other factors. If one genuinely believes there is a 
high probability of societal or economic collapse in, say, the next 2-3 years, then 
planning for 10 years out doesn’t make sense. Even outside of a doomsday context, 
projecting anything beyond a year or two gets very uncertain in today’s volatile world. 
Technology is changing fast, geopolitics are tense, etc. We don’t want to rely ona 
strategy that requires a decade of stability to pay off, because we might not have a 
decade of stability! In a sense, long-term investing becomes a luxury we can’t afford 
if we think the long term might not arrive in the usual way. Therefore, we cap our primary 
horizon around 6-12 months. We still acknowledge things could take a year to play out, 
but we’re not planning on 5+ year horizons. 


Three to six months is a sweet spot for meaningful moves. Why? Because real, 
fundamental events tend to unfold over a few months and become reflected in stock 
prices over that timeframe. If you think about major story arcs for companies or the 
economy, a lot of them play out in quarters: an earnings beat or miss might cause a 
jump or drop, but then over the next 2-3 quarters the company’s trend becomes Clear. 
Or consider macro trends: a recession fear can send stocks down for several months, 
and then a policy response or recovery can send them up in the next few months. These 
things aren’t usually fully reflected in a single day, nor do they necessarily require 5 


years. For example, in an Al-boom scenario, a stock like Nvidia saw its huge rerating 
within half a year in 2023 (as we discussed) — nobody waited 5 years to price in that Al 
demand; it happened over a few months once evidence showed up. Similarly, during 
the COVID crash, the market fell roughly 35% in a matter of weeks in March 2020 and 
then bottomed and recovered within months after stimulus actions. If you had a 
thesis about a recovery, a 3-6 month trade on an index or stock could capture that. 
Waiting 5 years wasn’t necessary; in fact, by 5 years later, that specific opportunity was 
long gone and replaced by different issues. 


Reducing exposure to long-term unknowns: Another reason for our timeframe choice 
is risk management. Holding any investment for a very long time exposes you to all sorts 
of unknown future events — some good, some catastrophic. Normally, diversification 
and patience are used to weather those. In our case, however, we intentionally are 
saying: “We think the farther-out future is extra uncertain (because of potential 
collapse scenarios, etc.), so we’re going to focus on nearer-term opportunities that we 
have a higher confidence in and that can materialize before things potentially go 
haywire.” Think of it this way: if you genuinely believed there’s a significant chance the 
stock market as we know it might not exist 5 years from now, you’d probably not lock 
your money away in a 30-year index fund plan. You’d try to make something happen 
sooner and then maybe convert to cash or other assets for safety when the storm 
comes. Our 3-6 month window aligns with “make hay while the sun shines, because we 
don’t know how long this sun will last.” 


It’s a flexible, rolling window. We’re not saying you must sell exactly at 6 months or 
that you can’t hold longer if conditions are still favorable. The idea is that when we pick 
an investment, we’re looking for something that has a catalyst or thesis likely to play 
out in the next quarter or two, maybe up to two quarters (which is ~6 months). We 
monitor as it goes. If after 6 months the thesis is still strong and the world hasn’t ended, 
one could choose to hold a bit longer — but likely by then either the opportunity has 
been realized (and we take profit), or something changed (and we cut loss or switch 
strategy). We’re not doing “day trading”, but also not doing “buy and forget for 10 
years.” It’s an active investing approach with a medium horizon. 


Not too short, not too long - just right. In a way, 3-6 months is a “Goldilocks zone” 
for this book’s strategy. Shorter than that, and we’re in the realm of frantic trading and 
mostly noise — not suitable for most people, and not aligned with focusing on 
fundamental forces. Longer than that, and we risk running out of time given our grim 
outlook, and we expose ourselves to big-picture disruptions that could nullify our 
investment (for instance, even if you picked a great company, if a global collapse hits in 
2 years, a 5-year plan gets ruined). So we aim to capture meaningful moves that 
happen over a few months, and then reevaluate. 


One more practical point: many readers might have accounts around the size of $10k 
(give or take). With a $10k portfolio, you’re not an institution; you have the advantage of 
being nimble. You can move in or out of positions without moving the market. That’s 
perfect for a 3-6 month strategy. If you had $10 billion, you couldn’t reposition that fast 
— but with $10k, you absolutely can adapt every quarter as needed. So we’re tailoring 
the approach to what a small investor can realistically do to maximize their chances in 
a possibly short-window world. 


To be clear, we are not “guaranteeing” profits in 3-6 months or claiming that every 3- 
6 month period will have a great opportunity. We’re simply choosing this horizon as the 
most practical and philosophically consistent one for our situation. It’s long enough 
that we can focus on real signals (real events driving stock moves), and short enough 
that we’re not banking on a rosy distant future. It’s investing in the now (or the very 
near future) given that the long term is, frankly, in question if you buy our “end of the 
world” premise. 


Seeing Price as a Trace of Forces, Not Random Squiggles 


Now we get to a really important mindset shift: looking at a stock chart and seeing 
traces of underlying forces rather than just a random squiggly line. This is a bit 
philosophical, but also very practical for analysis. It’s also literally how a physicist 
might approach data from an experiment — by thinking about the contributing factors 
behind the observations. 


When you see a stock’s price history, imagine that price being determined by the sum 
of several different forces pushing and pulling on it. Each force is something in the 
real world or the market psyche. For example, some of the major forces could be: 


e The company’s own performance: Is the company growing earnings? Did it 
launch a new product or make a big profit surprise? This “internal” force pushes 
the stock up if positive, or pulls it down if the company stumbles. Think of this as 
the company’s fundamental trajectory. 


e The overall market conditions (market sentiment): Sometimes all stocks 
move together due to broad forces. If there’s a bull market (investors optimistic, 
economy growing), that’s like a rising tide lifting boats — a force pushing many 
stocks up in unison. In a bear market or crash, a general fear or liquidity crunch 
drags most stocks down regardless of their individual merits. 


e Interest rates and finance conditions: This is a big one in modern markets. 
When interest rates go up (or other financial conditions tighten), it’s a force that 
often pushes stock prices down, especially for high-growth stocks (because 
future profits are less valuable when discounted back at higher rates). When 
rates go down or stimulus happens, it’s a force pushing stocks up. It’s almost 
like gravity —- changing the incline of the hill that all stocks are trying to climb. 


e Economic cycles (recession vs expansion): If people expect a recession, 
that’s a force pulling many stocks down (especially those dependent on 
economic growth, like retailers or manufacturers). In an expansion or recovery, 
it’s a tailwind pushing them up. This overlaps with market sentiment but is more 
tied to concrete economic indicators (employment, GDP, etc.). 


e Geopolitics and external shocks: Think of things like wars, trade policies, 
pandemics, new regulations, etc. These can be significant forces for certain 
sectors or even the whole market. For instance, an outbreak of war might push 
oil prices up (helping energy stocks) but pull airline stocks down. A trade ban on 
a certain tech might hurt one company but help a competitor. These forces are 
often one-time or episodic events that create noticeable spikes or slumps on 
charts. 


e Investor psychology and hype cycles: Sometimes a force is basically “mass 
psychology.” If a narrative catches fire (like “electric vehicles are the future” or 
“this meme stock will go to the moon”), it can push a stock well above 
fundamentals for a while. This is less rational, but it’s real as a force. Eventually, 
reality reigns (when hype deflates, that force reverses into a downward push). 
You might consider this a part of market sentiment, but specifically focused on 
stories and trends that investors fixate on. 


When we combine all these, the stock’s price is like the resultant line from adding up 
these influences. This might sound abstract, but you can often point to a chart and 
decompose it in plain language. For example, let’s say a stock had a gentle rise for a 
year, then a sharp plunge, then a partial recovery. The narrative might be: “The gentle 
rise was due to the company’s solid earnings growth (company performance force 
upward) plus a generally bullish market (market sentiment upward). The sharp plunge 
happened when an unexpected event hit - perhaps the CEO resigned amid scandal or a 
product failed (company shock force downward), plus at the same time the whole 
market was pulling back due to interest rate hikes (macro force downward). The 
recovery after that plunge could be the company finding a new footing (maybe they 
addressed the problem, so company fundamentals stabilizing) and maybe an overall 
market bounce (macro sentiment turning positive again).” 


In fact, major stock chart movements almost always correlate with real-world 
developments. A famous example: in 2018, Facebook’s stock (now Meta) had a steady 
climb, then plunged around 20% in a single day in July —- that was a signal, not noise, 
tied to a disappointing earnings and growth forecast (a real fundamental force) and 
concerns about user data scandals. Over the subsequent months, the stock drifted 
down further - reflecting that underlying concern. Or consider Apple’s stock: zoom out 
ona 15-year chart, and you’ll see a big upward trajectory with some noticeable dips. 
Those dips align with events like the 2008 crisis (everyone fell) or perhaps a period 
around 2015-2016 when iPhone sales plateaued (company performance concern). The 
big upward stretches align with successful product cycles (iPhone, iPad, services 
growth) and bull markets. The point is, there is a rhyme and reason to the big picture 
moves. 


Thinking this way sets you up perfectly for Chapters 6 and 7, where we will get more 
technical in breaking down a stock’s movements into components. We'll essentially 
be asking: how much of this stock’s movement was due to the market at large? How 
much was due to interest rates? How much was specific to the company? That’s the 
physicist approach — identify and measure the forces. In this chapter, we just want you 
to start imagining those forces when you look at a chart. Instead of seeing a mystery 
squiggle, you’ll start asking, “What could have caused this section of the graph to go 
up? Why did it dip here? What was happening in the world or in the company?” Even if 
you don’t know the answers yet, that habit of thought is incredibly useful. It keeps you 
focused on reality (events, fundamentals) rather than chart superstition. 


One caution: sometimes people try to find patterns in charts (like certain shapes or 
technical indicators) and treat them as magical predictors on their own. That’s not what 
we advocate. While technical analysis can have its uses, our emphasis is 
understanding the real forces, not just pattern-spotting. A physicist-minded investor 


uses Charts as a tool to visualize data, not as crystal balls. For us, a chartis the 
starting point to ask why, not the final answer. For example, if a stock is at an all-time 
high on a 5-year chart, we’d ask, “What force is propelling it? Ils the company killing it 
with a new Al product? Is the whole sector euphoric?” That leads to analysis: is that 
force sustainable or will it fade? Conversely, if a stock is near multi-year lows, what’s 
pushing it down - and might that force abate or reverse (creating an opportunity)? 


By seeing price moves as traces of forces, you become less mystified and less 
intimidated by charts. They’re no longer these arcane squiggles you have to “decode” 
by some secret method - they are simply records of what has happened to a business 
(and the world around it). And crucially, you don’t get as emotionally caught up in the 
chart itself; you stay grounded in thinking about the business and world events. 


How to Look at a Chart Without Losing Your Mind (or Your Shirt) 


Let’s talk about the emotional side of looking at stock charts. It’s one thing to 
intellectually know about noise vs. signal, or to vow to focus on long-term forces — but 
when you’re actually watching your portfolio day to day, it’s easy to slip back into a 
reactive mindset. So here are some practical tips for maintaining emotional hygiene 
and discipline while looking at charts: 


1. Zoom Out to Calm Down: Whenever you feel anxious or overly excited by a recent 
price move, force yourself to zoom out to a 1Y or 5Y chart of that stock (or the S&P 
500 if it’s a market move). This literally puts things in perspective. A 5% drop today 
might look scary on a 1D chart (a big red vertical line!). But on a 1Y chart, you might see 
that the stock is still up 30% versus last year, and today’s drop barely dents that. This 
helps counteract the recency bias that plagues us — the tendency to think that 
whatever happened today is hugely important. By zooming out, you visually and 
mentally remind yourself that today is just one day out of many, and you’re less likely 
to make a rash decision. As one portfolio manager put it, “The highs and lows in the 
market are normal and temporary. Over the long term, stock prices track the earnings 
power of businesses.”[4] In other words, volatility is part of the journey and zooming 
out shows that the journey has ups and downs, but it’s the overall direction that 
matters. 


2. Limit the Frequency of Checking: This is easier said than done in our smartphone 
era, but it’s worth trying. If you’re not a day trader, you don’t need to check prices every 
minute. Constantly watching the chart can create a false sense of urgency and prompt 
you to act when you really shouldn’t. It’s like watching a pot of water on the stove — it 
feels like it’s taking forever to boil and you might be tempted to crank the heat (or think 
something’s wrong) when in fact it’s fine. Consider setting specific times to review your 
stocks, maybe once a day or even a couple of times a week, rather than reacting to 
every intraday wiggle. Some experienced investors deliberately avoid looking at their 
portfolio on extremely volatile days unless they plan to trade, because they know it 
could spook them into a bad move. Find a balance that keeps you informed but not 
obsessive. 


3. Develop a Narrative for Your Positions: One way to stay calm is to always anchor 
your holdings to a clear reason or thesis. If you bought a stock because, say, you 


believe in its new Al product boosting earnings next quarter, write that down for 
yourself. Then, when you see the stock chart dip for a couple days, refer back to your 
reason. Ask: Has anything changed about my thesis? lf not, probably that dip is just 
noise and you can calmly hold on. Having a narrative turns the chart from an enemy into 
just feedback. If something has fundamentally changed (say, a force you identified 
reversed — e.g., the company’s Al product got delayed), then you can make a reasoned 
decision to adjust. The idea is to respond to changes in the story, not changes in the 
stock squiggle per se. 


4. Avoid Being “Hypnotized” by Rapid Moves: In the heat of a fast-moving market, 
there’s a temptation to stare at the screen tick by tick —- which often results in panic 
selling or greed-driven buying without proper analysis. If you find yourself doing this, 
step away for a moment. Remind yourself: the market will be there tomorrow. Missing 
a few minutes or hours of action is rarely going to make or break your 3-6 month 
strategy. In fact, many of the worst investor mistakes happen from over-trading — selling 
in a panic during a sudden drop, or chasing a stock after it’s already spiked (buying high 
from FOMO). By maintaining a physicist’s detachment, you can watch even a wild chart 
without losing your cool. Think of a doctor in an emergency room - they see a heart 
monitor racing or dropping and they have to stay calm and assess Cause and treatment, 
not scream or jump around. Similarly, you as an investor should aim to be the calmest 
person in the room when achart goes crazy. Often the best move is to do nothing until 
you’ve analyzed the situation fully. 


5. Remember the End Goal: Our end goal isn’t to “win” every day or to brag about 
catching the exact tops and bottoms. It’s to make it through what might be the final 
years of “normal” markets with some gains and sanity intact, focusing on big moves 
that matter. Keeping that in mind can help you ignore the trivial swings. If you truly 
believe a collapse or major upheaval is coming in a year or two, the daily noise is even 
less relevant - what matters is positioning for those truly big events (and maybe 
enjoying some profits before things go south). It’s a somewhat somber motivation, but it 
does clarify your focus. You’ll find it easier to ignore a meaningless 2% dip when you’re 
hunting for a possible 50% move on a thesis or protecting against a potential 100% 
down event in acrash scenario. We have bigger fish to fry than today’s micro- 
fluctuation. 


6. Use Tools and Data, Not Gut Feeling, to Decide: One emotional trap is when you 
start imagining patterns in the noise just because you’ve been looking too closely. For 
instance, you might convince yourself “the stock has gone down every afternoon this 
week, so! should sell before afternoon comes” —- that’s likely just random variation, not 
a real pattern. A physicist-minded approach would be: verify if there’s any real cause 
(did something systematically happen each afternoon? Probably not). Whenever 
possible, lean on actual analysis (like the kind we’ll do in later chapters) — look at 
earnings, read news, check if similar stocks are moving (maybe it’s sector-wide). This 
grounds your interpretation of the chart in evidence rather than gut superstition. It also 
slows you down, which is good for emotional control. 


7. Embrace Boringness: Finally, don’t be afraid to let chart-watching be a bit boring. A 
calm, almost dull approach to monitoring your investments can actually mean you’re 
doing it right. If you find it’s too exciting too often, you might be effectively gambling. The 


best investors often say their investing process is not adrenaline-pumping; it’s 
methodical. For example, legendary investor Warren Buffett famously said that if you’re 
investing, “activity” (constant buying/selling) is not indicative of success; often 
inactivity is, because you made a good choice and just let it play out. Now, we’re not 
holding for decades like Buffett, but the principle stands: you should be spending more 
time thinking and researching, and relatively less time trading. When looking at charts, 
you might sometimes need to tell yourself: “Relax. It’s just data. Let’s see what it’s 
telling me —if anything — and not project my fears onto it.” 


In essence, looking at a stock chart without losing your mind comes down to 
maintaining the physicist’s perspective even when emotions flare. Noise is 
expected; volatility is normal[11]. If you internalize that, you won’t feel compelled to 
react to every twitch. You’ll treat a price drop not as a personal threat but as a puzzle: 
does this drop represent a real change (signal) or is it just temporary fluctuation (noise)? 
That analytical pause is often enough to prevent a costly knee-jerk reaction. 


A Small Homework: Zoom Out on a Stock You Know 


Let’s put these ideas into practice with a simple exercise. Think of this as homework — 
not the tedious kind, but a quick activity that can really solidify what you’ve learned in 
this chapter. The goalis to train your eyes and mind to automatically seek the big 
picture in stock charts and to be aware of how different timeframes affect your 
perception. 


1. Pick a stock you know and care about. It could be a company whose products or 
services you use regularly (like a favorite tech company, a retail brand, etc.), or maybe a 
stock you’ve considered investing in. It’s more engaging if you’re somewhat familiar 
with the company’s story. 


2. Go to a free chart website or app (Google Finance is very easy: just search the 
company name and the word “stock” on Google, and a chart will usually appear). 
Yahoo Finance or others are fine too. 


3. Start with the 1-Day (1D) chart. Note what you see. How much did the price move 
today (or in the last trading day)? Is it jumping around? How does the chart make you 
feel? Jot down a quick impression: e.g., “Today the stock zig-zagged between \$X and 
\$Y, | feel a bit anxious watching it because it looks unpredictable.” It’s okay if the 
market is closed as you do this — you can still see the day’s range or the last price bar. 


4. Click to the 5-Day (5D) chart. Now you see roughly a week’s worth of trading. What’s 
your impression now? Maybe you see one day was up big, another down, etc. Does it 
look like noise? Write a note: e.g., “Over the past week, the stock moved within, say, a 
5% range. There’s no clear trend, just Some ups and downs.” 


5. Click 1-Month (1M). At one month, do you see any emerging pattern? Perhaps the 
stock is slightly up or down over the month, or maybe it had a dip in the middle. Note 
how much of that chart is just squiggles versus a direction. How useful (or not) is the 1M 
view for understanding the stock? Jot it down. 


6. Now click 6-Month (6M). This is where things get more interesting. Over half a year, 
many stocks will show a more defined story. Is the stock higher than it was six months 
ago, or lower? Are there any big spikes or drops on the way? Mark roughly when those 
happened. For example, you might see a sharp drop around three months ago - that 
could correspond to, say, an earnings release or a market-wide dip. Write your 
observations: e.g., “Over 6M, the stock is up about 10%. It had a big drop in Month 2 
(maybe something happened then) and recovered after Month 4.” 


7. Click Year-to-Date (YTD) or 1-Year (1Y) (whichever is available, or both). This gives 
you the near-full-year perspective. Compare the start and end points: how big is the 
overall change? Also, identify the major movements. Perhaps “It climbed steadily in the 
first quarter, then gave up those gains in the summer, and now it’s slightly above where 
it started.” Try to correlate: do you recall any news about the company or economy in 
those times? Even if not, just note that you can see distinct phases in the year. 


8. Click 5-Year (5Y). Step back and admire the broader landscape. Does the stock have 
an obvious long-term uptrend (like many big tech stocks do)? Or has it been cyclical, up 
then down then up? Identify at least one big rise or fall on this 5-year chart. For 
instance, you might see “Wow, in 2020 it really plunged and then skyrocketed after — 
likely the pandemic effect.” Or “It steadily grew from 2018 to 2021, then dropped a lotin 
2022.” These are signals; think about what forces might have caused them. You don’t 
need the exact answer — just make an educated guess: “This big dip was around March 
2020, probably the COVID crash. This big rise in 2021 could be the recovery plus some 
specific company success.” If the stock is company-specific, maybe recall company 
events: “This spike corresponds to when their blockbuster product launched,” etc. 


9. Finally, if available, click MAX (or All). This might stretch back a decade or more. 
What’s the overall trajectory? Sometimes this is very enlightening. A stock like Amazon 
or Apple shows a decades-long massive rise (with occasional short downturns barely 
visible). Some older companies might show multiple cycles (ups in the 90s, downs in 
2000s, etc.). Note how tiny the day-to-day volatility looks on this scale — often you just 
see a smooth curve or a few notable big moves. This is truth in filtering: only the really 
important stuff is left. Jot how the MAX view feels compared to the 1D: probably the 
MAX view feels almost calm or inevitable in its trend, whereas the 1D felt frantic. 


10. Reflect on your feelings at each stage. Which view made you feel the most 
nervous or impulsive? (Likely the 1D or 5D, if it was volatile.) Which view made you feel 
the most focused or understanding of the stock’s situation? (Likely the multi-month or 
year views, where you could say “oh | see what’s going on overall.”) This little self-check 
is important. It demonstrates in real time how zooming out can change your 
emotional reaction from “I need to do something now!” to “Okay, | see the overall 
pattern, and | know why I’m in this.” 


11. (Optional) Investigate one big move: If you identified a major spike or drop on the 
5Y/MAX chart and aren’t sure what caused it, you might take a moment to find out. A 
quick Google search like “[Company Name] stock [Month Year]” often brings up news 
from that time. For example, “Netflix stock April 2022” would immediately show news 
of Netflix losing subscribers that month (which indeed led to a huge drop in its stock). 
This step isn’t required, but it can be eye-opening — it cements the idea that big chart 


moves have real reasons. You could even use our companion website’s deep research 
reports if the event is related to a sector theme; for instance, if it was an Alboom ora 
crypto crash that moved the stock, we likely have an analysis of that force on prophet- 
of-ai.com. But the main point here is to connect at least one chart movement to a real 
event, reinforcing the habit to always seek the why behind the chart. 


12. Conclude which timeframe is most useful for a 3-6 month investment 
approach. After doing this, answer for yourself: /f /’m planning a trade or investment to 
hold for the next few months, which chart view should | rely on most? You'll probably 
conclude that the 6M and 1Y charts give you the best sense of recent momentum and 
key support/resistance levels (if you’re into technicals at all), while the 5Y/MAX gives 
you context for major forces at play. The 1D/5D likely won’t factor much into your 
decision anymore, except perhaps to fine-tune an entry point on a given day (and even 
that might not be necessary). 


By completing this exercise, you’ll have experientially learned the core lesson of this 
chapter: short-term charts are full of noise that can mislead and stress you out, 
whereas longer-term charts reveal the true signals and help you stay focused on 
what matters. You’ll also start to develop the habit of interpreting charts in light of 
underlying events (“reading like a physicist”) rather than as isolated squiggles or, 
worse, a casino graph. 


Take a moment to celebrate this shift in perspective. Reading stock charts the right way 
—the smart, calm way - is a foundational skill for any investor, and you’re building it. In 
the next chapters, we’ll equip you with more tools to dissect those big forces we talked 
about (so you can, for example, separate a stock’s move into “this part was the market, 
this part was the company’s own doing”). But none of that would help if you were still 
glued to the 1-day chart freaking out over noise. By adopting the physicist mindset and 
a long-range view, you’re now prepared to delve into the more technical analysis 
without losing sight of the forest for the trees. Remember, the goal is to invest wisely 
in the short time we might have, and that means focusing on the signal of meaningful 
moves, not the distracting noise of daily flux. Keep this chapter’s lessons handy 
whenever you feel the pull of those hypnotic squiggles — a quick zoom-out can work 
wonders for both your financial results and your peace of mind. Happy (and calm) chart 


reading![1][5
