Chapter 6 — From Big Falls to Real Causes: Building 
Stock-Specific Parameters 


When Crashes Are Clues, Not Nightmares 


Most investors dread seeing their stock take a nosedive. It’s natural—- a big crash in 
price feels like a nightmare. But what if we told you that, in our approach, crashes are 
actually clues? In this chapter, we’ll flip the script: instead of panicking at big drops (or 
getting dizzy with big surges), we’ll treat these extreme moves as valuable information. 
Each major fall or rally in a stock chart is like the market underlining something in bold: 
“Pay attention, something real happened here!” 


Nir’s investing philosophy, which we’ll call Causal Decomposition Investing (CDI), is 
all about digging into those big moves to find the real-world causes behind them. We’re 
going to use the short time we have (remember, Nir envisions we might not have 
decades of status quo left) to focus on what truly moves a stock. If the world is ending 
(or dramatically changing) soon, we don’t have time for 30-year theories — we need to 
know now what matters. In the 3-6 month trading horizon we’re focused on, a crash or 
a rally isn’t just a wild rollercoaster swing; it’s a chance to learn which specific factors 
are driving that stock. In other words, big moves separate the signal from the noise. Our 
job is to interpret that signal. 


So, when a stock you own (or are considering) plunges 30% in a few months, don’t just 
cover your eyes. Ask why. Treat it like a detective scene. The stock chart is giving you a 
clue that something significant — a real cause —is in play. Maybe it’s a disastrous 
earnings report, a CEO scandal, a regulatory shock, or a global crisis. Whatever itis, 
identifying it will make you a smarter investor going forward. Likewise, if a stock rockets 
upward, we ask the same question: what real-world event is behind this? 
Understanding these causes will help us build a shortlist of “parameters” for the stock 
—the handful of things that actually matter for its price. Those parameters become our 
compass for navigating the stock in future turbulent times. 


Why Short-Term Charts Lie to You 


Open any financial app or website and you’ll likely see options to view a stock’s 
performance over various timeframes — 1 day, 5 days, 1 month, etc. The temptation is 
to obsess over these short-term charts. After all, they show what’s happening right 
now, minute by minute. But here’s the hard truth: for our purposes, short-term charts 
mostly lie. They are full of noise and very little signal. 


What do we mean by noise? In markets, noise is the flurry of random, short-lived price 
moves that don’t reflect any lasting or fundamental change. A stock might dip 1% in the 
morning because a trader took profits, then jump 2% by afternoon because of a vague 
rumor on social media, and fall again by evening over an unrelated news headline. By 
next week, those wiggles are forgotten and the stock might be right back where it 
started. If you zoom in on a 1-day or 5-day chart, these wiggles loom large. They can 


trick you into thinking something hugely important is happening every hour. But most of 
the time, itisn’t. It’s just noise. 


Nir’s approach focuses on a roughly 3-6 month horizon for trades. Why? Because 
anything shorter is usually noise that’s impossible to reliably trade on (unless you’re a 
high-frequency robot, which we are not), and anything much longer might run us into 
the extraordinary scenario of Al-driven collapse that this book assumes could be 
looming. We’re in a sweet spot: a few months to maybe a year. In that context, the day- 
to-day zigs and zags are distractions we can ill afford. If the world might end (or 
radically change) in the near term, do we really care about a 1% swing that lasted an 
afternoon? No —- we care about bigger moves that we can potentially act on inthe 
coming months. 


Short-term charts “lie” by exaggerating the importance of short-term moves. Looking at 
a 1-month chart might show a steep drop last Tuesday, but if by Friday the price 
recovered, that drop was just noise. If you reacted to it in panic, you might have sold at 
the bottom for no good reason. Many small investors fall into this trap: they see a stock 
dipping over a few days and assume something must be wrong, when in fact it’s normal 
volatility or rumor-driven fluctuation. To avoid this, we need to zoom out and geta 
clearer perspective. In the next section, we’ll do exactly that. 


Zooming Out: The 5-Year and MAX View 


The antidote to misleading short-term charts is simple: zoom out. Instead of a 
microscope, grab a telescope. Most chart tools (like Google’s stock charts, Yahoo 
Finance, etc.) let you view a stock’s performance over longer periods — 6 months, 1 
year, 5 years, or the MAX (from inception to now). These are our preferred lenses. 


Why 5-year and MAX? Because at those scales, only truly important events show up. 
Minor blips get smoothed out. You’re left with the mountain ranges, not the molehills. A 
5-year view will show you the medium-term landscape: the major peaks and valleys 
that a stock traversed in recent memory. The MAX view goes further, showing the entire 
history of the stock (which is great for seeing once-in-a-decade type events). 


Let’s illustrate this with a quick exercise. Go to Google (or your favorite finance site) and 
type in a stock ticker — say, “TSLA” for Tesla. By default, Google might show you a 1-day 
or 5-day chart. Ignore that. Click on the “5Y” (5-year) option. What do you see? The 
chart likely has a few big hills and drops. Now click “MAX”. The chart will redraw to 
show Tesla’s whole journey from its IPO (in 2010) to today. On this MAX chart, you’ll 
notice something interesting: until around 2019 or 2020, Tesla’s price line was relatively 
flat or modest, and then it skyrockets and becomes very volatile. That visual alone tells 
a story - something changed in 2019-2020 that made the stock far more valuable and 
more volatile. Indeed, Tesla’s market capitalization and public profile exploded during 
that period. 


By zooming out, we accomplish a few things: 


e We filter out short-term noise: Those little daily swings are now tiny squiggles 
you can barely see. The irrelevant chatter fades away. 


e We focus on major movements: If a 30% crash in a week happened, it will 
clearly appear as a sharp dip on a 5Y chart. If a stock doubled in six months, 
you'll see that spike unmistakably. 


e We set the context: You can see, for example, whether a stock is still up over 5 
years despite a recent drop, or vice versa. This guards against recency bias 
(overweighting recent events). 


Crucially, each major peak or valley on a 5Y or MAX chart begs a question: “What 
happened here?” That’s where our detective work begins. The chart gives us the when 
—we must find out the why. And because we’re looking at big moves, the why is likely to 
be something real and significant (as opposed to the 1-day chart where “why” might be 
“someone sneezed and algorithms went wild”). 


In summary: /gnore the 1-day, 5-day, even 1-month views -— they lie. They’ll have you 
overreacting to shadows. Instead, zoom out to find the solid objects casting those 
shadows. In the long view, only the events that truly matter — the real signals — will 
stand out. And those are what we care about for Causal Decomposition Investing. 


The Idea of Decomposition: One Messy Line, Many Hidden Causes 


You’re looking at a stock chart — one curvy line going up and down. It seems like a single 
thing, right? One stock, one price line. But here’s a different way to think about it: that 
single line is actually a mixture of many different forces. The art of our approach is to 
decompose that messy line into its hidden components, at least conceptually. 


This idea is a bit like how engineers or scientists use “Fourier analysis” or signal 
processing. Don’t worry — no math here, just an analogy. Imagine you’re listening to a 
song. It sounds like one continuous piece of music, but it’s actually made up of drums, 
guitar, vocals, maybe some electronic beats — multiple elements combined. If you had 
the right software, you could separate the tracks and realize, ah, there’s a bass line, 
here’s the drum pattern, etc. Similarly, a radio signal sends out a carrier wave that’s 
modulated with the actual music or voice; a receiver demodulates it, separating the 
message from the carrier. 


In the stock market, a single stock’s price is like that combined song or radio signal. It’s 
influenced by a bunch of different “tracks” playing at once: - Company-specific 
performance: How is the company doing? (E.g., Are its sales growing? Did it launch a 
hit product? Did the CEO do something crazy?) - General market mood: Is the overall 
stock market bullish or bearish? (In a market-wide crash, even great companies’ stocks 
fall. In a roaring bull market, even meh companies get bid up.) - Sector trends: Is the 
company’s industry hot or in trouble? (For example, if oil prices crash, oil company 
stocks fall regardless of their individual merits. If Al is hyped, any Al-related stocks 
might rise together.) - Economic factors (macro): Interest rates, inflation, recessions, 
booms. These cast a big shadow. High interest rates, for instance, can push down 
growth stock valuations (we saw this in 2022 for many tech stocks, including Tesla[1]). 
A recession fear can drag most stocks down, whereas easy monetary policy (low rates, 
money printing) can lift stocks broadly. - Geopolitics and crises: Wars, pandemics, 
political upheavals. These can cause market-wide shocks. The COVID-19 pandemic in 
March 2020, for example, caused a sharp decline in virtually all stocks, Tesla included 


(Tesla fell roughly 60% from its February 2020 peak during the COVID crash[2)). - 
Random noise: And yes, there’s pure noise — random fluctuations, technical trading 
quirks, one-off events that don’t have lasting significance. 


Causal Decomposition Investing (CDI) means we consciously think of these separate 
components. We’re effectively saying, “This stock’s price = market effects + industry 
effects + company’s own fundamentals + unique shocks + ... etc.” In practice, we won’t 
make a formula out of it, but we approach analysis with this mindset. Instead of seeing 
one inexplicable squiggly line, we see the layered influences: part of that dip was the 
market tanking, part was the company’s earnings miss, part was the CEO’s dumb 
tweet, and part was just noise. 


For this chapter, our focus will zoom in on one of those layers: company-specific 
shocks and events. Why? Because that’s where we often find the unique parameters 
for a stock. Market-wide factors (like interest rates) affect many stocks similarly —- those 
are important, but they’re not specific to, say, Tesla vs. another tech stock. We will 
account for those macro factors in our big picture, but the goal here is to find what 
unique storyline drives a particular stock. In other words, what makes Tesla’s price 
move differently than, say, Ford’s or Apple’s? 


Think of it like tuning an old radio. First you get a lot of static (noise). Then you start 
hearing some music (market and sector trends — a general broadcast). Finally, you fine- 
tune to get the clearest signal of the specific song playing (the company-specific story). 
By focusing on big crashes and rallies in a stock’s history, we effectively tune into the 
strongest signals —- the moments where one of those hidden “tracks” in the mix got 
really loud. Perhaps it was the drumbeat of a CEO scandal, or the high note of a 
successful product launch, or the bass drop of a financial crisis. Each big move lets us 
identify one major component of the stock’s story. 


We won’t be doing literal signal processing, but the mindset is: separate the mixed 
signal into its components. With that mindset, let’s do a deep-dive case study to see 
this in action. 


Tesla Case Study: From Squiggly Line to Real Events 


Let’s apply our method step by step with a real example: Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). Tesla is an 
ideal case study for this chapter because its stock history is full of dramatic swings and 
headline-making events. Even if you’re not a “Tesla fan,” you’ve likely seen it in the 
news over the years. By dissecting Tesla’s big rises and falls, we’ll demonstrate how to 
go from a confusing chart to a clear list of real causes and key parameters. 


Step 1: Get the Big Picture Chart. Open a 5-year or MAX chart for Tesla. On a 5-year 
view (say, mid-2020 to late-2025), you’ll immediately notice some huge moves. The 
MAX view (2010-2025) shows Tesla’s humble beginnings and then an exponential rise 
and increased volatility in the later years. Jot down a few time periods where the stock 
had major inflection points — peaks or troughs that stand out. 


For Tesla, some major chart features might include: - Early 2020: a sharp drop around 
February—March 2020. - Late 2020: a steep rise toward the end of the year. - Late 2021 
into 2022: a peak in late 2021 followed by a big decline through 2022. - 2023 recovery: a 


significant rebound in 2023. - Late 2024: a new peak (all-time high). - 2025: some 
renewed volatility. 


Now, Step 2: Investigate “What happened?” for each big move. This is where we dig 
into news and history around those dates. I’ll walk you through these for Tesla: 


March 2020 Crash: On the chart, Tesla’s price plunged in March 2020. If you 
recall, that’s when the COVID-19 pandemic went global and markets crashed. 
This was not Tesla-specific — nearly every stock was pummeled in that 
timeframe. Tesla, being a high-growth tech-heavy stock, actually got hit extra 
hard: it fell about 60% from its February 2020 high to the March 2020 low[2]. The 
cause? Pandemic-induced economic fears, factory shutdowns, and a broader 
market panic. In Tesla’s case, its factory in Fremont had to close temporarily due 
to lockdowns, which added to the concern. Key point: This event tells us that 
macro crises (like a pandemic) can overpower everything. It’s a cause for the 
crash, but not a Tesla-unique cause — it was a universal market shock. 


Late 2020 Surge: After the pandemic crash, Tesla’s stock started climbing 
dramatically and by late 2020 it went into overdrive, reaching what was then 
record highs. What caused this big rally? Two big real-world events stand out: 


S&P 500 Index Inclusion: In November 2020, it was announced that Tesla would 
be added to the S&P 500 index effective December 21, 2020. This was a huge 
milestone. Why does it matter? Because all the funds that track the S&P 500 
(and there are many, including big index mutual funds and ETFs) were forced to 
buy Tesla stock to include it in their holdings. Instantly, there was massive 
demand for shares. Indeed, Tesla’s stock jumped over 10% the day after the 
announcement[3]. Analysts estimated that index funds would need to buy 
roughly $10 billion worth of Tesla shares to rebalance, creating a substantial 
short-term boost in demand|/4]. This index inclusion is a one-time event, but it 
caused a frenzy of buying through late 2020, contributing to Tesla’s steep rise. 


Product and Profit Breakthroughs: Around the same time, Tesla was proving 
that its business was for real. 2020 was the year Tesla started delivering the 
Model Y (a crossover SUV) in volume, and it continued to sell its Model 3 sedan 
like hotcakes. Tesla also posted consistent quarterly profits in 2020, which was a 
big deal—it helped qualify Tesla for that S&P inclusion (the index requires 
sustained profitability). In short, Tesla transitioned from a niche automaker to a 
mainstream one that year. There was also excitement about future products (like 
the Cybertruck unveiled in late 2019, and Tesla’s big ambitions in software and 
self-driving). All this created a hype cocktail: Tesla was riding technological 
success, investor euphoria, and technical factors like index inclusion all at once. 
Result: The stock price skyrocketed. (Fun fact: Tesla’s stock rose nearly 8-fold in 
2020/2] if you measure from January to the end of the year. That’s not normal - 
clearly, extraordinary things were priced in.) 


Late 2021 Peak and the 2022 Collapse: The next huge inflection is around late 
2021. Tesla stock hit an all-time high in November 2021 (after a big run that year) 


and then went on to decline significantly over the course of 2022. Let’s break that 
into cause and effect: 


Fall 2021 Rally (and Peak): What pushed Tesla to a peak in November 2021? 
One catalyst was a headline-grabbing deal: in October 2021, rental car giant 
Hertz announced an order of 100,000 Tesla vehicles for its fleet. This news was 
seen as a watershed moment for EV adoption — if Hertz is buying Teslas by the 
hundred-thousand, EVs are going mainstream. The stock jumped on that news, 
contributing to Tesla’s market cap sailing past $1 trillion for the first time. In fact, 
Tesla hit a series of record highs around that time, triggered in part by the Hertz 
order[5]. By early November 2021, Tesla’s share price (adjusted for splits) was at 
its zenith. 


Musk Sells Stock (Nov 2021): Then came a plot twist from the company’s iconic 
CEO, Elon Musk. In early November 2021, Musk suddenly polled his millions of 
Twitter followers asking if he should sell 10% of his Tesla stake (ostensibly to pay 
taxes). The poll said “yes,” and Musk proceeded to sell a large chunk of stock 
over the next few weeks. This was huge news — the CEO unloading billions worth 
of shares is not taken lightly by markets. The result: Tesla’s stock plunged about 
12% in a single day after the selling started[6], and it kept dropping for a while. 
Over a few days, over $100 billion of Tesla’s market value was wiped out[7]. This 
event is a classic company-specific cause: one person’s action (Musk’s sale) 
directly caused a selloff. It had nothing to do with the economy or Tesla’s car 
sales — it was about share supply and investor confidence. 


2022 Decline — Multiple Factors: After Musk’s sales, Tesla stock didn’t 
immediately bounce back to new highs; instead, 2022 turned into a rough year. 
By the end of 2022, Tesla’s stock was down roughly 70% from that November 
2021 high[1] - an extremely large drawdown. What happened here? Several 
factors piled on: 


o Macro Storm (Interest Rates & Economy): In 2022, inflation surged and 
the Federal Reserve hiked interest rates aggressively. High-growth stocks 
like Tesla are very sensitive to interest rates (because a lot of their 
valuation is based on future earnings; higher rates discount those future 
earnings more harshly). So the entire tech sector and growth stock 
universe fell in 2022. Tesla was no exception; this created a bearish 
backdrop. 

o Supply Chain and Demand Worries: 2021-2022 saw global supply chain 
issues — chips were in short supply, shipping was snarled. Tesla managed 
better than many automakers, but not without headaches. Additionally, 
as interest rates rose, consumers could less afford pricey electric cars, 
and competition from other EV makers was growing. By late 2022, there 
were reports of Tesla having to cut prices in China and the U.S. to 
stimulate demand. All this contributed to the stock’s decline (investors 
feared Tesla’s growth might be slowing). A Motley Fool analysis summed 
it up: supply chain problems and weak demand amid rising rates 
contributed to Tesla’s 2021-2022 drop/1]. 


o Elon Musk’s Twitter Saga: Perhaps the most unique and contentious 
factor was Elon Musk’s acquisition of Twitter (rebranded as X). In April 
2022, Musk announced plans to buy Twitter, and the deal closed in late 
October 2022. Why would that affect Tesla’s stock? Two reasons: money 
and focus. To finance the $44 billion Twitter deal, Musk sold tens of 
billions worth of his Tesla shares throughout 2022 (beyond the late 2021 
sales) — this increased the supply of shares on the market and signaled to 
investors that Musk was willing to cash out a big part of his stake[8]. 
Additionally, once he owned Twitter, Musk started spending a lot of time 
(and reputation) on it. He was running Twitter day-to-day, making 
controversial decisions, and getting entangled in political debates on the 
platform. Tesla investors grew worried that Musk was “distracted” and 
not devoting enough attention to Tesla’s challenges. Even some long- 
time Tesla bulls publicly lamented that Musk needed to refocus on 
Tesla[9][10]. There was also a fear that Musk might have to sell even more 
Tesla shares to fund Twitter’s losses, since Twitter was struggling 
financially/8]. All of this cast a negative shadow over Tesla’s stock in late 
2022. One prominent Tesla investor described the situation as Musk 
“abandoning Tesla,” which, while hyperbolic, shows the sentiment 
then[11]. 

o Puttogether, 2022 was a “perfect storm” for Tesla’s stock: 
macroeconomic pressure, industry competition, and a very idiosyncratic 
issue of Musk’s Twitter adventure. The result was that 2022 became one 
of Tesla’s worst years on record, stock-wise (Tesla was one of the worst 
performing big-cap stocks that year, down more than 50% year-to-date by 
Dec 2022/12)). 


2023 Rebound: If you continue along the chart, you’ll see that in early to mid- 
2023, Tesla’s stock made a considerable comeback. From its low point in 
January 2023 (when pessimism was peaking), the stock roughly doubled by mid- 
2023. What caused this rally? 


Technical Rebound & Sentiment Shift: First, after such a huge drop in 2022, 
Tesla’s stock was likely oversold (too beaten down). Savvy investors saw a 
bargain around the ~$100 per share level (split-adjusted) in early 2023 and 
started buying. Sometimes, absence of new bad news is good news -— by early 
2023, the rate hikes were expected to slow down and Musk had stated he would 
stop selling shares. In fact, Musk even said in late 2022 that he wouldn’t sell 
Tesla stock for a while, trying to reassure investors. 


Positive Developments: There were also bullish developments: Tesla’s 
business continued to grow (2023 deliveries were on track for record highs). 
Critically, something interesting happened in the EV industry: Tesla opened up 
its charging standard (the Tesla Supercharger network) to other car 
manufacturers. In mid-2023, companies like Ford and GM struck deals to adopt 
Tesla’s charging plug (the NACS standard) for their future EVs. This was seen as 
a huge win for Tesla’s ecosystem - essentially, Tesla’s technology becoming an 
industry standard, which could mean new revenue streams and a validation of 
Tesla’s lead. The stock jumped on these announcements in the summer of 2023. 


Broader Tech Rally: 2023 also saw a resurgence in tech stocks in general (the 
NASDAQ had a strong first half of 2023). There was buzz about artificial 
intelligence, and while Tesla is primarily an automaker, Elon Musk frequently 
touted Tesla as much a tech/Al company (with its self-driving tech and evena 
humanoid robot project). Tesla sometimes traded in tandem with high-growth 
tech names, so the overall market optimism helped lift Tesla too. 


By late 2023, Tesla hadn’t fully returned to its 2021 peak, but it had recovered a lot of 
ground from the 2022 trough. This taught us that some factors from 2022 had eased: for 
example, inflation was coming under control (so rate hike fears eased), Musk had 
refocused somewhat on Tesla (even saying he’d ensure Tesla shareholders would 
benefit from Twitter long-term[13]), and Tesla was making strategic moves to 
strengthen its business. 


Late 2024 All-Time High: Fast forward on the chart to roughly the end of 2024. 
Tesla’s stock does something many doubted it would do again: it reached a new 
all-time high, around $480 per share in December 2024/14]. This is higher than 
the peak of 2021. What on earth fueled this? 


The context here includes both company-specific and macro elements. By late 
2024, investors had digested the interest rate situation (and possibly anticipated 
that rate cuts or an easier monetary environment was on the horizon, which 
would be positive for stocks). Tesla, for its part, continued to post solid growth. 
Perhaps there were expectations of new models (there were rumors of a cheaper 
“Model 2” or “Model Q” for the mass market) and the long-delayed Cybertruck 
finally starting deliveries (Tesla’s Cybertruck launched in late 2023 in small 
numbers, with hopes of scaling in 2024-2025). 


Another factor that anecdotally played in late 2024 was political. AU.S. 
presidential election took place in November 2024. The outcome (which, 
according to some analyses, was the reelection of a certain figure or 
administration perceived as business-friendly) led some to speculate that Tesla 
might benefit from a more favorable political climate. One report noted that 
Tesla’s stock hit record highs shortly after the election, implying investors 
believed Elon Musk’s relationship with the incoming administration could help 
Tesla[14]. Whether or not that’s true, the key is that market sentiment turned 
very bullish on Tesla by the end of 2024. 


Hitting a new high told us that the worries of 2022 were firmly in the rearview 
mirror by then, and new optimism had taken hold. However, as always in 
markets, new challenges would emerge... 


2025 and Musk’s Big Buy: The chart in 2025 shows some ups and downs. After 
the euphoria of late 2024, Tesla stock in 2025 faced a bit of a reality check. 
Growth was still there but slowing, competition in EVs was heating up (many new 
models from competitors), and some investors feared that the stock was 
overvalued at its new highs. By mid-2025, Tesla’s stock had actually pulled back 
from the peak and was relatively flat or slightly down for the year — making it an 
underperformer among big tech names up to that point[15]. 


e Thencame Elon Musk’s surprise move in September 2025: he bought a huge 
chunk of Tesla stock on the open market. This was notable because, aside from 
participating in some planned capital raises, Musk hadn’t done open-market 
buying of Tesla in a long time (his last big open purchase was in early 2020). In 
September 2025, Musk spent about $1 billion of his own money to buy roughly 
2.6 million Tesla shares[16][17], at prices around $372-$395 per share. This was 
disclosed through regulatory filings and immediately caught investor attention. 
The CEO buying that much stock is generally interpreted as a major vote of 
confidence. And indeed, Tesla’s stock jumped ~6% on the news of Musk’s 
purchase[18]. Commentators said this was a clear signal that Musk was “all in” 
on Tesla’s future, recommitting after the earlier distractions[19][20]. It also 
coincided with Tesla pushing big initiatives in Al and robotics (Musk’s purchase 
came soon after the board proposed a massive new compensation plan tied to 
ambitious goals, indicating Musk wanted a bigger stake as Tesla enters new 
frontiers[21 ][22]). 

e Musk’s big buy in 2025 is an example of a positive company-specific catalyst: 
unlike his sales which drove the stock down, his purchase boosted confidence 
and drove it up. It highlighted how much influence Musk’s personal actions have 
on Tesla’s stock —a key insight for our parameters list. 


Now, stepping back, we identified and explained several major Tesla stock moves and 
their causes: - March 2020 crash > Cause: COVID-19 (macro crisis)[2]. - Late 2020 
surge > Cause: S&P 500 inclusion (technical market event)[3], plus product success 
and profits (fundamental improvements). - Late 2021 peak > Cause: Big Hertz order 
(market validation of EV demand)[5]. - Late 2021 into 2022 decline > Cause: Elon Musk 
selling 10% stake (company-specific)[6]. - 2022 further crash > Cause: Rising rates & 
recession fears (macro), supply chain & demand issues (industry), Musk distracted with 
Twitter and selling more stock (company-specific)[1|[8]. - 2023 rebound > Cause: 
sentiment reversal, easing of rate fears, Tesla tech partnerships (industry), no new 
Musk sell-offs and business growth (company fundamentals). - Late 2024 new high > 
Cause: optimism on business and maybe favorable policy environment (mix of 
company and macro)/14]. - 2025 Musk buy bump > Cause: CEO buys $1B of stock, 
signaling confidence (Ccompany-specific)[18][17]. 


Notice how each of these was a real-world event or trend. None of these said “just 

random fluctuation.” When you zoom out, the big moves usually have real causes. By 
doing this exercise, we’ve essentially demodulated Tesla’s price signal into distinct 
“stations”: one station is “Elon Musk’s actions,” another is “broader market cycles,” 
another is “Tesla’s product milestones,” another is “macro/interest rates,” and so on. 


Now it’s time to take those insights and formalize them into Tesla’s specific 
parameters. 


Turning Events into Parameters: Tesla’s Personal “Control Panel” 


From the Tesla case study, we identified a handful of factors that came up over and 
over as drivers of big price moves. These are what we'll call stock-specific parameters 
—the dials and gauges that, when tweaked, seem to move Tesla’s stock the most. Think 


of it like a control panel for Tesla’s stock price. Here’s Tesla’s “dashboard” as we’ve 
derived from history: 


Elon Musk’s Stock Trades and Actions: This one is huge for Tesla. When Elon 
Musk sells a large chunk of Tesla stock, it has historically pushed the price 
down sharply[6]. Conversely, when he buys stock or signals he won’t sell, it 
boosts confidence[18]. Musk’s personal stake management is unusually 
important for Tesla because he’s not just a CEO — he’s a celebrity leader and 
major shareholder whose moves are closely watched. Not many companies 
have this factor (e.g., you don’t see Tim Cook publicly trading Apple shares ina 
dramatic fashion), so this is a very Tesla-specific parameter. Key takeaway: If 
news hits that “Musk is selling shares” or “Musk is buying more shares,” expect 
Tesla’s stock to react accordingly — it’s a critical parameter. 


Major Product Launches and Milestones: Tesla’s big product events have often 
moved the stock. For example, the rocky rollout of the Model 3 in 2017-2018 
(production hell) coincided with a big stock dip[23], whereas achieving volume 
production by 2019 and unveiling the Cybertruck preceded a stock recovery and 
rally. The launch of the Model Y in 2020 gave Tesla access to the booming 
crossover market and helped boost growth. Looking ahead, events like the 
Cybertruck’s success or failure, or a potential launch of a cheaper mass- 
market Tesla, are likely to be key stock drivers. This parameter generalizes to: 
delivery and production numbers - if Tesla significantly beats or misses 
production targets for new models, the stock moves. For our purposes, we can 
summarize this as “Product launches/successes vs. production 
problems/failures.” When a new Tesla modelis a hit, or a big technology (like Full 
Self-Driving software) makes strides, it’s bullish. If there are delays, recalls, or 
flops, it’s bearish. 


Large Contracts or Deals (Market Validation): When Tesla scores a big victory 
in the marketplace - like a huge order from a company (e.g., the Hertz deal) or a 
major partnership — it can cause a rally[5]. These events signal that Tesla’s 
products are in high demand or that its technology is becoming standard (as with 
other automakers adopting Tesla’s charging network in 2023). Essentially, 
anything that validates Tesla’s market position in a big way is a key parameter. 
If tomorrow Tesla announced a deal to supply batteries to all of Volkswagen, you 
can bet the stock would jump. So keep an eye on major business development 
wins. 


Index Inclusion/Exclusion and Technical Market Factors: We saw how getting 
added to the S&P 500 index was a one-time rocket boost for Tesla[3]. That 
specific event won’t repeat (it’s in the index now), but similar technical factors 
could matter. For instance, if Tesla were ever (hypothetically) removed from a 
major index or if huge index rebalancing rules changed, that could affect it. Also, 
stock splits can be in this category (Tesla’s 5-for-1 split in 2020 and 3-for-1 split in 
2022 didn’t change fundamentals but did coincide with optimistic sentiment and 
rallies, as splits often attract retail investors). So while this isn’t an everyday 


parameter, it’s worth noting: structural market events (index changes, stock 
splits, inclusionin ETFs, etc.) can create significant short-term moves. 


e Regulatory and Policy Environment: Tesla operates at the intersection of tech 
and autos, which means regulation can be a big deal. Government policies on EV 
incentives, emissions standards, or energy can boost or hurt Tesla. For instance, 
if a country increases electric vehicle subsidies or mandates phasing out gas 
cars sooner, that’s a tailwind for Tesla. On the flip side, if there are regulatory 
crackdowns - say, stricter safety regulations on self-driving systems (imagine a 
regulator banning Tesla’s Full Self-Driving beta until certain criteria are met), or 
higher costs due to tariffs — those could cause stock drops. While we didn’t focus 
on a single past event here, we can infer its importance. (One could argue the EV 
tax credits in the 2022 U.S. Inflation Reduction Act indirectly helped Tesla by 
making their cars more affordable, or how China’s EV incentives being reduced in 
2019 affected Tesla’s China sales somewhat.) The key parameter is “regulatory 
support or hurdles for EVs and renewable tech.” Keep an eye on legislation or 
rulings that specifically impact Tesla’s industry. 


e Competitive Landscape & Supply Factors: This is a bit broad but very relevant. 
Tesla doesn’t exist ina vacuum; competition is rising. When a competitor makes 
a big move -e.g., a rival launches a compelling EV at a lower price point —it can 
sway Tesla investors. Similarly, if critical supplies (like lithium for batteries or 
chips for its cars) become scarce or pricey, that can squeeze Tesla’s margins and 
spook the stock. In our historical recap, we saw supply chain issues and rising 
competition contributed to Tesla’s 2022 decline[1]. So a parameter for Tesla is 
“industry competition and supply chain health.” Are other automakers 
catching up? Is Tesla able to secure enough batteries? These questions matter 
for the stock’s trajectory in a major way when they hit critical points (for example, 
if Tesla’s growth slows because of supply limits or competition, the stock reacts). 


e Elon Musk’s Other Ventures and Public Persona: Like it or not, Tesla’s brand 
and stock are tightly linked to Musk’s persona. When Musk’s other ventures (like 
Twitter) or his public behavior (tweets, media appearances, politics) create 
controversy, Tesla often feels some impact. We saw that investor sentiment 
soured when Musk was preoccupied with Twitter and posting polarizing 
content[24][10]. If Musk were to, say, dive heavily into another side project 
(SpaceX doesn’t hurt Tesla since it’s separate, but if he started spending half his 
time on, say, anew Al startup), or if he gets embroiled in a scandal, it could affect 
Tesla. This parameter is a bit fuzzy, but essentially “Musk’s focus and behavior” 
is a factor. It’s unique to Tesla (most companies aren’t so dependent on one 
person’s image), which is why we include it. Many Tesla investors literally gauge 
the stock’s prospects by their trust in Musk’s leadership at any given time. 


To sum up Tesla’s parameters in a quick list: 1. CEO’s Stock Moves (Insider trading 
signals) - Musk’s buying or selling [6][18]. 2. Product Success/Failure — Launches, 
production milestones, new models (e.g. Model 3/Y, Cybertruck)/23]. 3. Major Deals & 
Orders - Big contracts, partnerships, customer orders (like Hertz)[5]. 4. Market 
Structure Events — Index inclusion, stock splits, etc. (e.g. S&P 500 entry)[3]. 5. 


Policy/Regulation — EV tax credits, environmental laws, self-driving legal framework 
(anything government does that helps or hinders Tesla). 6. Competition/Supply — EV 
competitors’ moves, battery supply, chip shortages, demand swings|1]. 7. Musk Factor 
—Elon’s attention and antics outside Tesla (Twitter, public image) and how they reflect 
back on Tesla[8][10]. 8. Macro factors — (This one isn’t unique to Tesla, but we note it) 
interest rates, economic cycles, and market crashes (Tesla is sensitive to these, as 
seen in 2020 and 2022)/[2][1]. 


You might notice that some of these parameters are broader (macro environment) 
while others are very specific (Musk’s stock sales). That’s fine - a stock’s key drivers 
can include one or two macro items if the stock is particularly sensitive to them. The 
important thing is, our Tesla list is much shorter and more understandable than a 
laundry list of 100 possible factors. We’re focusing on the high-impact, proven ones. 


With Tesla’s case study done, you can see how we went from a crazy squiggly charttoa 
clear set of factors that explain those squiggles. Now, the exciting part: you can 
generalize this method to any stock. Let’s talk about how to do that. 


Try This at Home: How to Analyze Any Stock This Way 


The process we followed for Tesla can be applied to any large, liquid stock (or even ETFs 
or indices). It’s a straightforward investigative approach. Here’s a step-by-step guide or 
checklist you can use: 


1. Pick a Stock You Care About. It could be one you own, one you’ve heard about, or 
just any company you want to analyze. Maybe something stable like Coca-Cola, or 
another tech high-flyer like Apple, or a bank, or an energy company — anything. 


2. Open the Long-Term Chart (5Y and MAX). Use Google’s stock chart or another free 
tool. Type the company name or ticker, and switch the view to 5-year (and also glance 
at MAX for full history). Resist the urge to obsess over the 1-day or 1-week moves. We 
want the bird’s-eye view. 


3. Identify Major Peaks and Valleys. Look for the prominent highs and lows on the 
chart: - When did the stock price clearly drop a lot (say 20% or more) in a relatively short 
period (weeks or months)? - When did it rise significantly or hit a peak? Mark those 
periods mentally or jot them down. For example, you might note: “Stock X had a big 
drop around Q4 2018, another in early 2020, and a big spike in mid-2021,” etc. 


4. Investigate Each Big Move - Find the Cause. This is the research part. For each date 
or period you noted: - Do a quick news search: e.g., “Why did [Stock X] drop in [month 
year]?” or check financial news archives for that time. - Often, Wikipedia’s page for the 
company might mention major events or controversies by year. Or simply googling 
“[Company name] stock [Month Year]” will surface news articles. - Look for specific 
events or explanations. Maybe the company missed earnings badly, maybe the CEO 
resigned, maybe there was a scandal, maybe a competitor launched something, maybe 
a natural disaster struck a key factory, maybe an acquisition was announced — anything 
concrete. Also note if the whole market was down (e.g¢., if it’s March 2020, the cause 
was likely the COVID market crash which affected everyone). - Avoid accepting vague 
reasons like “due to market conditions” without details. Dig for the real story. For 


example, instead of settling for “Stock X fell due to unfavorable market sentiment,” try 
to find what triggered that sentiment specifically for Stock X —- was it an analyst report? A 
product failure? Investors don’t just feel gloomy for no reason; usually something 
sparks it. 


5. Categorize the Causes (Company vs Sector vs Macro). Once you have a list of 
events that caused big moves, label them: - Company-specific: originating within the 
company. (Examples: CEO actions, earnings results, product launches/failures, 
mergers and acquisitions involving the company, scandals, lawsuits against the 
company, etc.) - Sector/Industry: an event or trend affecting the whole industry. 
(Examples: a commodity price change for oil companies, a new regulation for all banks, 
a technology shift like all phone makers being affected by anew component shortage.) - 
Macro/Market-wide: broad economic or market events. (Examples: recessions, 
interest rate changes, pandemics, geopolitical events, stock market crashes that hit 
nearly everything.) - Some events will have overlap. That’s okay. For instance, a 
pandemic is macro, but if you’re analyzing an airline stock, the pandemic is both macro 
and very company-specific in effect because it directly hits their industry. Use your 
judgment on what bucket it primarily falls in. 


6. Look for Patterns and Recurring Themes. Now, step back and see if certain types of 
events show up repeatedly for your stock: - Does the company often rise or fall based 
on what the CEO says or does? (CEO-centric stock) - Does it move a lot on earnings 
reports? (Maybe the stock is very earnings-sensitive, so that’s a parameter.) - Are most 
of the big moves tied to one particular product or segment? (For example, a pharma 
company might always spike or crash based on drug trial results —so trial outcomes are 
a key parameter.) - Does it seem to mostly move with macro trends? (E.g., perhaps a 
utility stock mainly swings with interest rate changes and not much else.) - By spotting 
patterns, you zero in on what really matters. You might find, say, 3 big drops in the last 
decade all happened when oil prices fell, for an oil company — that’s a strong hint that 
oil price is a key parameter for that stock. 


7. List the Key Parameters for the Stock. This is your end product-—a short list 
(perhaps 5 to 10 items max) of the main factors that move the stock. These should be 
phrased as real-world things not just stock technicals. For example: - A retail 
company’s parameters might be: consumer spending levels, fashion trend 
hits/misses (product inventory success), management changes, store expansion 
pace, e-commerce competition. - A bank’s parameters could be: interest rate 
changes, loan default rates/economic cycles, regulatory fines or rule changes, 
CEO credibility, major acquisitions or crises. - Make sure these are rooted in the 
events you observed. If you noticed the bank stock drops every time interest rates fall, 
then “interest rate environment” is a key parameter. If the retail stock crashed when 
they missed a fashion trend and had to discount heavily, then “product trend success” 
is a parameter. 


8. Use and Update the List. Going forward, use this parameter list as your guide for 
that stock. When you see news about the company or the world, you can better gauge, 
“Is this likely to affect my stock meaningfully?” If it matches one of your key 
parameters, then it probably will. If it doesn’t, maybe it’s noise you can ignore. 
Periodically update the list if a new type of event moves the stock in a big way that you 


hadn’t considered. (For example, if suddenly cyber-security becomes a huge issue for a 
company after a big hack, and previously it wasn’t on your radar, you might add “cyber- 
security incidents” as a parameter for, say, atech company.) 


Let’s try a quick hypothetical example for practice: The Boring Utility Co. Suppose you 
chart a big electric utility company over 5+ years. You might see a dip in late 2018, a dip 
in early 2020, and not much huge movement otherwise (utilities are often more stable). 
Researching: - Late 2018 had a broad market sell-off (Fed was raising rates, etc.) -—the 
utility fell maybe 15%. Cause: macro. - Early 2020, COVID crash — utility fell 30% with 
the market, then rebounded. Cause: macro (pandemic) and maybe people worried 
about commercial electricity demand. - Perhaps you see a spike in early 2021 —- maybe 
that utility became a takeover target or there was talk of a merger (just an example). - 
And a slow decline in 2022 - likely because interest rates rose (utilities often trade like 
bond-proxies; when rates rise, their stocks fall because the dividend is less attractive). 


From this, the parameters for “Boring Utility Co.” might shape up as: interest rate 
trends, broad economic cycles, regulatory changes in its service area, and major 
M&A (merger) news. Notice, we didn’t say “CEO tweets” — probably irrelevant for a 
boring utility. Nor would we say “product launches,” since utilities don’t launch new 
products every year like a tech firm. But we included regulation, because maybe one of 
those dips coincided with a state utility commission denying a rate increase or 
something (just as an example — utilities are very subject to regulators). 


Another example, say you do this for Apple (AAPL): - Big moves to investigate might be: 
the 2008 crash (macro), the 2010-2012 period of iPhone explosive growth (company- 
specific, product success), a drop in 2013 (maybe a stumble after Jobs’ passing or a 
product miss), huge rise in 2019-2021 (services and 5G iPhone cycle, plus macro bull), 
etc. - Likely parameters would include: iPhone sales cycles, major product category 
launches (like Apple Watch, potential AR glasses), supply chain disruptions (Apple 
is sensitive to production issues, like if factories in China shut down), 
leadership/vision (less of an issue now, but back in 2011, Steve Jobs’ health was a 
parameter), broader consumer spending and macro (since if people tighten budgets, 
high-end smartphones might see less demanda). 


The beauty of this approach is that it makes you a kind of mini-expert on the stock’s 
story. Instead of feeling like the stock moves randomly, you’ll start saying “Oh, Stock Y 
is down 10% this month - that aligns with [one of its key parameters].” For instance, an 
Apple investor might shrug off a random analyst downgrade (not a key factor in the long 
run), but pay very close attention to news that a key supplier factory caught fire (supply 
chain hit - that is one of Apple’s critical factors). 


One more thing: this method can also highlight when a stock is very sensitive to macro 
vs. when it’s mostly marching to its own drummer. Some stocks (like Tesla or high- 
growth names) might swing wildly based on their own news but also get yanked by 
macro tides. Other stocks (like utility or consumer staples) might be almost entirely 
driven by macro/sector and have relatively few company-specific dramas. In those 
cases, you might find that your “parameters” list leans more on external factors. That’s 
okay. The goal isn’t to force every stock into having 10 unique internal drivers — it’s to 
find whatever the big drivers are, even if they’re macro. 


Summary and a Small Homework Assignment 


Summary: In this chapter, we learned that big crashes and rallies are our friends — 
they are clues that point to real causes, not just nightmares to fear. By zooming out to 
5-year and MAX charts, we cut through the short-term noise and identify the truly 
significant moves in a stock’s history. Using a mindset borrowed from signal 
processing, we treat the stock’s price as a mix of different factors and attempt to 
decompose it into its key drivers. We introduced Nir’s concept of Causal 
Decomposition Investing (CDI), focusing especially on the company-specific causes 
that make a stock move. 


Through a detailed case study of Tesla, we saw this process in action: - We identified 
Tesla’s major stock moves (crashes and surges) and traced them to real-world events: 
from Musk’s share sales and purchases[6]/18], to index inclusion[3], to product 
launches and production issues[23], to macroeconomic swings and Musk’s Twitter 
saga[1|[8]. - We then distilled those into a list of stock-specific parameters for Tesla — 
essentially the “control panel” of factors an investor should watch (Musk’s actions, 
product milestones, big orders, regulatory context, competition, etc.). When one of 
those lights blinks, you know Tesla’s stock is likely to react. 


Finally, we outlined how you can generalize this method to any stock. The steps are: 
zoom out, spot big moves, research the causes, categorize them, and extract the key 
parameters that recur. By doing so, you transform yourself from a passive observer of a 
squiggly line into an active analyst who understands the story behind the stock’s 
movements. 


This approach has a few powerful benefits: - lt keeps you focused on what matters. 
You won’t be as easily thrown by every random headline because you’ll know which 
categories of news have historically mattered for your stock. - It makes the investing 
process more rational and less emotional. Big drop? You’ll investigate the cause 
calmly instead of panic-sell, because you know it’s either a key factor (in which case 
maybe there’s a real problem to evaluate) or it’s not (in which case it might be an 
opportunity). - It respects the fact that in our possibly shortened investing horizon (given 
the “end of the world” premise), we need to be efficient. We don’t have decades to wait 
out downturns, so we focus on immediate causes and actionable knowledge, but we 
also ignore short-term noise that’s just distracting. 


Now, here’s a small homework assignment to solidify what you’ve learned: pick one 
stock and do this yourself. Ideally, choose a stock that you either own or are 
considering investing in. It could even be an ETF or index if you want, though with a 
broad index the “company-specific” events will actually be macro or sector events. 


Your task: 1. Zoom Out on the Chart: Look at 5Y and MAX for that stock. 2. Mark the 
Big Moves: Note a few dates/periods of big rises or falls. 3. Find the Causes: Use 
Google, news articles, Wikipedia timelines — whatever helps — to identify what 
happened around those times. Write down a brief cause (e.g., “Early 2018 drop - CEO 
resigned unexpectedly,” or “2021 surge —- passed major tax reform that helped, plus 
blowout earnings,” or “2020 crash - COVID lockdowns, entire sector down”). 4. List the 
Key Parameters: Based on those causes, list the main factors that seem to drive the 


stock. Keep it to perhaps 5-7 items that you think are the big ones. If you find yourself 
writing down 15 things, try to consolidate —- remember, we want the short list of high- 
impact parameters, not every possible influence. 


5. (Optional) Check Your Work: If you’re comfortable, discuss it with a friend or on 
an investment forum to see if others agree. For instance, “I think the key drivers 
for Company Z are A, B, and C —- does that align with what you’ve seen?” 
Sometimes seasoned investors in that stock will chime in, or you might discover 
something you missed. Just be wary of people who give very generic answers — 
you’ve done the specific homework, so trust your analysis. 


By doing this exercise, you’ll not only understand that particular stock better, you’ll also 
practice the skill of causal decomposition. After a few times, you’ll start doing this 
mentally whenever you look at a new stock chart. Instead of seeing chaos, you’ll see 
stories. That’s a superpower in investing — it turns abstract price movements into 
concrete narratives you can reason about. 


As we proceed in the book, we'll apply this thinking to other scenarios and discuss how 
to act on these insights (e.g., making a trade when you anticipate one of your key 
parameters is about to flash). But for now, mastering the ability to filter signal from 
noise and identify real causes is a huge leap forward in how you approach investing. 


Remember: This is a framework, not a crystal ball. It won’t tell you the future with 
certainty, but it will help you focus on the right questions. Instead of asking “What will 
the stock do next month?” you’ll ask “What factors are likely to change next month, and 
how might they affect the stock?” That’s a smarter question. And as the world (possibly) 
careens toward disruptive events, being able to tell the difference between trivial price 
noise and meaningful signal will be invaluable. So, embrace the big falls as clues, 
follow the trail to the real causes, and you’ll be well on your way to practicing Causal 
Decomposition Investing like a pro. 


Happy investigating —- and don’t forget to do your homework! Your future self (however 
long that future may be) will thank you for it.
