Chapter 7 —- Why Your Stocks Don’t Live in a Vacuum 


Imagine waking up to find your portfolio down 5% overnight. You scan the news and see 
nothing specific about your companies. What happened? Often, the answer is that 
your stocks were caught in a market-wide storm rather than any company’s own 
doing. In investing, individual stocks move not only on their own merits (like earnings or 
CEO actions) but also under the influence of big background forces that affect many 
stocks at once. These forces are what we call the “macro layers” — the ocean of factors 
in which every company ship sails. When a stock plunges, sometimes it’s because that 
company hit an iceberg; other times, it’s because the whole ocean was stormy, 
dragging everything down together. In practice, it’s usually a mix. To truly understand a 
company’s performance, you need to separate how much of a price swing came from 
company-specific news versus how much came from macro forces in the 
background. 


Nir’s approach, Causal Decomposition Investing, is about doing exactly that. We want 
to decompose (or break down) a stock’s price moves into different causes. In Chapter 
6, we focused on company-specific factors — things like a bad product launch or a 
CEO’s mistake. Now, in Chapter 7, we add the macro layers: the overall market’s 
trend, interest rates, recession risk, and geopolitical shocks. By the end of this chapter, 
you'll see how those layers interact with individual stocks, and you’ll learn a simple 
routine to keep an eye on macro risks. Remember, our worldview here assumes the 
next few years could be extremely turbulent (Nir worries about Al-driven collapse 
around 2026), so macro risk isn’t an abstract long-term idea — it’s something that could 
hit hard within months. We won’t debate that doom scenario here; instead, we’ll just 
equip you to track the big forces that might crash into your investments on a 3-6 
month horizon. 


The Market as One Giant Stock: S&P 500 Equal Weight 


When investors talk about “the market,” they often mean a major index like the S&P 
500. Think of an index as a basket of many stocks — a proxy for the market’s overall 
mood. The S&P 500, for example, includes 500 large U.S. companies. But the standard 
S&P 500 index is market-cap weighted: bigger companies (by market value) count 
more in the index. In mid-2023, Apple alone made up about 7.6% of the S&P 500, and 
the top 10 stocks together were around 30% of it[1]. That means if Apple’s stock has a 
bad day, it can pull the whole S&P 500 down noticeably. Conversely, the hundreds of 
smaller companies in the index barely move the needle individually. 


For a more balanced view, many investors also look at the S&P 500 Equal Weight 
Index. This version contains the same 500 companies but gives each stock the same 
weight - roughly 0.2% each[2]. In an equal-weight index, Apple counts no more than a 
small tech firm in the S&P. Why use equal weight? Because it acts like “the market” 
with every company treated equally, which can better reflect what’s happening to a 
“typical” large stock without a few giants distorting the picture. In 2023, for instance, 
the regular S&P 500 was heavily lifted by a handful of tech megacaps (“The Magnificent 
Seven”), while many other stocks lagged. The equal-weight S&P told a more subdued 
story about the average stock’s performance. For our purposes — understanding macro 


forces — the equal-weight index can be thought of as one big “meta-stock” 
representing the broad market’s movement. If this meta-stock is tanking, it’s a signal 
that some macro storm is underway. 


Let’s take a brief tour of the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index’s history, and tie major drops 
to real-world events. Each of these events was like a giant wave hitting all stocks at 
once: 


e 2008-2009: Global Financial Crisis — This was one of the worst market crashes 
in history. In 2008 alone, the S&P 500 (equal weight or not) dropped nearly 40%, 
and by early 2009 it had lost over half its value from the 2007 peak[3]. What 
happened? In short, a housing bubble burst and big banks started failing. The 
crisis hit full force when Lehman Brothers collapsed in September 2008, 
triggering panic in financial markets worldwide. Credit froze up, a recession took 
hold, and virtually every stock plunged because the entire system was in 
trouble. This crash wasn’t about one company’s bad luck — it was about systemic 
failure. Even solid businesses saw their stock prices cut in half simply because 
the background ocean (the financial system) was experiencing a once-in-a- 
century storm. 


e 2011: Eurozone Debt & U.S. Credit Downgrade - In August 2011, global 
markets were rocked by fears that European countries like Greece, Italy, or Spain 
might default on their debts (the Eurozone debt crisis). Around the same time, 
the U.S. credit rating was downgraded by S&P from AAA to AA+ due to fiscal 
concerns. Investors suddenly worried about a “double-dip” recession. The result 
was a sharp, sudden drop in stocks worldwide — an 11% slide in about two 
weeks in late July/early August[4][5]. Basically, fear was contagious: trouble in 
Europe and a hitto U.S. financial credibility sent investors running from stocks 
broadly. Even though this turmoil wasn’t caused by any specific company, nearly 
every stock on the S&P 500 got dragged down in the chaos. 


e 2015-2016: China’s Slowdown and Global Jitters — In the summer of 2015, 
China’s stock market burst out of a bubble and crashed hard, sending 
shockwaves globally. In August 2015, the Chinese central bank also devalued 
the yuan, which surprised markets|[6]. These events raised fears that China’s 
economy (a major engine of global growth) was stalling. On “Black Monday” 
August 24, 2015, the Shanghai index fell about 8.5% in one day, and U.S. stocks 
tumbled in sympathy (the Dow Jones index fell over 1,000 points intraday). Again 
in early 2016, China’s market melted down, oil prices were crashing to multi-year 
lows, and recession fears spiked. This January 2016 global rout sent the S&P 
500 down roughly 10-15% as investors worldwide sold off risky assets[7]. 
Notably, nothing was fundamentally wrong with many U.S. companies then — it 
was the macro fear (China slowing, global growth faltering) that caused a 
mini-panic across all stocks. 


e 2018 Q4: Fed Tightening and Trade War - 2018 started strong, but by the end of 
the year the market hit a rough patch. The U.S. Federal Reserve had been 
gradually raising interest rates (more on rates soon), and by late 2018 investors 
feared the Fed might “over-tighten” and choke economic growth. At the same 


time, the U.S. and China were engaged in an escalating tariff war — tit-for-tat 
tariffs on hundreds of billions of dollars of goods. By October 2018, these worries 
led to a volatile skid in stocks[8]. Tech and other growth stocks were sold off 
hard, and by Christmas 2018 the S&P 500 (equal weight) was down about 15- 
20% from its peak, briefly touching bear market territory (a 20% decline). The 
headline causes were clear: the combination of trade war tensions and rising 
rates made investors cautious about all stocks. One news piece from that time 
noted how uncertainty over tariffs and higher interest rates “would slow the 
economy, hurting corporate profits,” and this sent traders rushing out of 
stocks[8]. Essentially, a macro one-two punch - policy risks - knocked down the 
whole market. 


2020: The COVID-19 Crash - This was an extraordinarily fast and brutal market 
collapse. In February-March 2020, as the COVID pandemic led to global 
lockdowns, the S&P 500 plunged 34% in barely a month/9]. It was the fastest 
bear market ever. Why did everything crash together? Because the world 
suddenly faced a severe recession (the deepest downturn since the Great 
Depression) with businesses closed and unemployment soaring. Investors didn’t 
wait to see which companies would suffer most —- they dumped nearly 
everything. This was a true “end of the world” scenario feeling for markets: 
airlines, banks, tech, retail, you name it, all sank in unison because the entire 
economy’s engine had stalled at once. Of course, huge stimulus efforts by 
governments and central banks soon rescued the market, and by mid-2020 
stocks bounced back. But for those frantic weeks in March 2020, the macro 
shock of a pandemic dominated every stock’s fate (even companies that later 
thrived, like Zoom or Amazon, saw initial drops as the panic set in). 


2022: Inflation and War Bear Market — After recovering from COVID, the market 
hit another major macro squall in 2022. Inflation in the U.S. and Europe spiked to 
40-year highs, and central banks (especially the Fed) turned aggressively 
hawkish, raising interest rates at the fastest pace in decades to fight rising 
prices. In early 2022, Russia also invaded Ukraine, a war that shocked 
commodity markets by sending oil and gas prices soaring. By mid-2022, the S&P 
500 had fallen into a bear market (over 20% down). The slide was “fueled by 
worries over sky-high inflation, a hawkish Federal Reserve and future 
economic growth,” as Reuters noted[10]. The war in Ukraine added to the 
volatility, causing a spike in oil and other commodity prices|11] that threatened 
global growth and fed inflation further. Virtually every sector felt the squeeze. 
High-growth tech stocks were hammered (they’re very sensitive to rising interest 
rates), but even stable stalwarts declined because investors were repricing 
everything for a world of higher rates and geopolitical strife. In short, macro 
factors (inflation, rates, war) drove the market down broadly — it wasn’t that 
hundreds of companies all coincidentally had bad earnings at the same time; it 
was the background economy and world events forcing a reset. 


Late 2023: War Jitters Again — In October 2023, a new conflict erupted when 
Hamas attacked Israel, leading to a war in Gaza. Markets initially wobbled on 
fears the war could widen into a broader Middle East conflict. Oil prices jumped 


over 5% in a week (the biggest weekly leap that year) and European natural gas 
spiked nearly 40%[1 2], reflecting worries that an escalation might involve major 
oil producers like Iran. Investors reacted with a “risk-off” stance: many moved 
money into safe havens like gold, which had its best week in months[13], and 
government bonds, rather than stocks. In the U.S., the equal-weight S&P 500 
index dipped amid these war nerves, though interestingly the damage was 
limited — partly because around the same time, there were hopes that interest 
rate hikes were finally ending, which provided some offsetting relief to 
stocks[14]. Still, this episode showed how geopolitical shocks can suddenly 
inject fear into the whole market. Even companies with no direct link to the war 
(say, a U.S. retail chain) saw their stock gyrate because big investors were 
broadly reducing risk exposure until the picture cleared. 


Those are just a few examples. We could list more (like the Eurozone crisis around 
2012, or the Brexit referendum in 2016 which briefly rattled global stocks, or various 
emerging-market scares). The key point is: the overall market itself has booms and 
busts, and those waves lift or sink nearly all boats together. If you hold any individual 
stock, part of its ride is determined by this “meta-stock” of the market. When the equal- 
weight index is surging (a bull market), even mediocre companies might see their share 
prices float up. When the equal-weight index is tanking (a bear market or correction), 
even excellent companies can see their share prices dragged down for a while. As an 
investor, you should conceptually treat “the market” as an entity — like one big stock 
that you need to monitor, because it’s the backdrop for everything. In practice, this 
could mean regularly checking how the S&P 500 equal-weight index is performing. Is 
that meta-stock in an uptrend, a downtrend, or choppy? Are we near all-time highs or 
coming off a deep trough? Knowing this context will help you judge whether your stock’s 
recent move is mainly about the company or largely about the market. 


Interest Rates: The Invisible Hand on the Scale 


If the overall market is like the ocean’s tides, interest rates are like the moon that pulls 
those tides. Interest rates (particularly set by central banks like the U.S. Federal 
Reserve) have a powerful, if somewhat invisible, influence on stock prices. You might 
not see a headline every day about your stock and interest rates, but in the background, 
the level of rates is constantly affecting how investors value future profits and how 
money flows between assets. 


Let’s break it down simply. Interest rate in this context usually means the benchmark 
policy rate (for the U.S., the federal funds rate that the Fed controls, or yields on 
government bonds). Here’s a quick history of U.S. interest rates in this century to 
illustrate their macro impact: 


e Early 2000s: Coming off the dot-com bust and 9/11, the Fed cut rates to very low 
levels (around 1% by 2003) to stimulate the economy. Low borrowing costs 
helped fuel a rebound in economic activity — and incidentally, a housing boom 
(cheap mortgages) and a stock market recovery by mid-2000s. Low rates 
generally act like easy, cheap money: businesses can borrow cheaply to invest, 
consumers can spend more, and stocks often enjoy higher valuations because 


future earnings are not heavily “discounted” when interest rates (the discount 
rate) are near zero. 


2004-2006: As the economy heated up (housing was on fire), the Fed gradually 
raised rates up to about 5.25% by 2006. This tightening eventually helped prick 
the housing bubble. By 2007-2008, the cracks in the financial system appeared 
(as we saw with the 2008 crisis), and the Fed slammed rates back down. 


2008-2015: In response to the Great Recession, the Fed cut rates effectively to 
0% (and did extraordinary things like quantitative easing — buying bonds — to 
pump money into the system). This was an era of ultra-low rates to revive growth. 
Indeed, the stock market from 2009 onward had an epic bull run. It’s nota 
coincidence: near-zero interest rates made stocks look attractive because 
bonds yielded little, and low rates boosted economic recovery. It also meant that 
the present value of long-term future earnings (for growth stocks) was very high — 
there wasn’t much penalty for money coming in later, since the discount rate 
was near zero. 


2016-2018: With the economy recovered, the Fed started to “normalize” rates, 
slowly hiking from 0.25% up towards ~2.5% by late 2018. The market took these 
hikes relatively calmly at first (they were well-telegraphed and gradual). But by 
late 2018, enough hikes had accrued that investors grew anxious that higher 
borrowing costs would hurt growth. Recall from the previous section: late 2018 
saw a stock correction. Part of that was the Fed’s rate increases biting. There’s a 
clear logic — higher rates tend to negatively affect earnings and stock prices in 
general[15]. Why? Because when companies face higher interest costs on loans 
and bonds, they have less money for profits; consumers pay more for mortgages 
and credit, so they spend less; in short, the economy can slow, hitting corporate 
revenues. Additionally, when analysts value stocks, they use discounted cash 
flow models - the higher the interest rate, the lower the present value of future 
earnings. Growth stocks (which expect big profits many years out) especially get 
hurt by rising rates, because a higher discount rate makes those future dollars 
less valuable today[15][16]. We saw this in 2018: high-flying tech stocks 
stumbled as rates climbed. 


2019: Sensing that maybe they had tightened a bit too much and that global 
trade tensions were weighing on growth, the Fed pivoted and cut rates slightly in 
2019. This gave a bit of relief to markets. It’s a general pattern: when the Fed 
signals easier policy, stocks often breathe a sigh of relief (all else equal). Lower 
rates are like a tailwind — they’re “catalysts for growth,” making borrowing 
cheaper, encouraging spending, and thus boosting corporate earnings and stock 
prices[17]. In 2019, those modest cuts helped fuel a stock uptick, until... 


2020: COVID hit, and the Fed dropped rates straight back to 0% in emergency 
meetings in March 2020. They also unleashed trillions in stimulus. This flood of 
cheap money and support was a huge factor in why the market, after crashing, 
rebounded so incredibly fast and strong in 2020. Investors had confidence that 
with zero rates and monetary stimulus, they could “look past” the ugly current 


earnings and focus on the eventual recovery. In essence, zero rates made stocks 
extremely attractive relative to bonds (which paid almost nothing). This period 
reinforced a modern investor lesson: “Don’t fight the Fed.” When the Fed is 
easing (cutting rates, printing money), it tends to lift most stocks — almost like 
gravity being turned off. 


e 2022-2023: As mentioned, in 2022 inflation forced the Fed (and other central 
banks) to hike rates rapidly. The U.S. went from 0% to around 4-5% ina single 
year — an extraordinarily fast move. This was like the tide turning: suddenly, 
money had a cost again. Borrowing became expensive for companies and 
consumers, and safe bonds started offering yields (4-5%) that could compete 
with stocks. The result: stock valuations compressed, especially for speculative 
or high-growth names. The broad market struggled; indeed 2022 was the worst 
year for stocks since 2008 in many respects. It happened exactly for the textbook 
reasons: higher interest rates made investors pull back from stocks. As one 
summary put it, 2022’s bear market was driven by surging inflation and a hawkish 
Fed, and higher yields “dull the allure” of growth stocks whose cash flows lie in 
the future[18]/19]. In 2023, rates peaked around their highest in 15+ years. Later 
in 2023 and into 2024, if inflation abated, the Fed paused hikes. (By late 2025, 
hypothetically the Fed might even be easing again, but that depends on the 
inflation path and if any recession hit —- those specifics change with time.) The 
big-picture takeaway is clear: rising rates = headwind for stocks; falling rates = 
tailwind for stocks, more often than not. It’s not a perfect 1:1 correlation 
(sometimes stocks and rates rise together if the economy’s booming), but 
generally, if you plot major stock struggles, you’ll often find an interest rate 
squeeze at the same time. 


It’s crucial for an investor to at least know the current interest rate environment. You 
don’t need to be a bond trader or predict every Fed meeting, but do pay attention to the 
broad trend: Are rates rising or falling? Is the central bank in tightening mode or easing 
mode? Also, realize different sectors react differently. For instance, higher rates tend 
to hurt sectors like technology or real estate (which rely on cheap debt and have 
long-term cash flows), while sometimes benefiting banks (they can earn more on 
loans)[20][21]. Lower rates do the opposite — they especially help assets like growth 
stocks and high-dividend stocks (since bonds no longer provide high income, investors 
look to dividend stocks, etc.)[22]. 


Think of interest rates as the price of money over time — when that price changes, it 
shifts the balance of favor between stocks, bonds, and other assets. For example, 
when you can get 5% virtually risk-free in a government bond, some investors will sell 
stocks and just take the safe yield, putting pressure on stock prices. In contrast, when 
bonds yield near 0%, investors are almost forced to buy stocks (or other risky assets) to 
get any return, which pumps up stock prices (sometimes into bubbles). So, in our 
macro layer model, always consider: What are interest rates doing, and how might that 
be pushing my stocks up or down? In a high-rate, tightening environment, be more 
cautious and expect volatility. In a low-rate or cutting cycle, stocks might have a wind at 
their back (though one must ask why rates are low —- often because the economy is 
weak). 


To sum up, interest rates are a macro layer you can’t ignore. You don’t need a PhD to 
track it: simply follow the news on central bank decisions and be aware of the prevailing 
rate level. It’s one number (the Fed funds rate or 10-year Treasury yield) that 
encapsulates a lot of the financial climate. As we’ll detail in the checklist later, 
checking the direction of interest rates should become a routine part of your 
investment approach. It’s like checking the weather before a trip — you want to know if 
you'll face headwinds or tailwinds. 


Recessions: When the Engine Stalls 


Stocks ultimately represent businesses, and businesses rely on a growing economy to 
thrive. A recession is the economic equivalent of the engine stalling — overall output 
(GDP) shrinks, demand falls, and unemployment rises. Not all recessions are as epic as 
2008 or 2020; some are mild. But any recession tends to hit many stocks at once, 
because when consumers buy less and companies earn less, the default reaction is 
that share prices drop across the board. 


Recession risk is a crucial macro layer to monitor, especially for our short horizon 
approach. We don’t care about whether GDP grows 2% or 3% in the long run (we’re not 
doing 30-year plans here); we care if in the next few quarters there’s a serious chance of 
a downturn that could sink most stocks temporarily. Fortunately, there are some 
relatively simple indicators that even a non-economist investor can watch to gauge 
recession risk. 


One such indicator is the Sahm Rule, named after economist Claudia Sahm. The Sahm 
Rule is elegant and straightforward: it says a recession is likely underway if the 3-month 
average unemployment rate rises by at least 0.5 percentage points above its low of 
the past 12 months/[23]. In plain English, if unemployment starts jumping quickly, 
that’s a bad sign. Why use this? Because historically, the unemployment rate is very 
steady in expansions and only spikes when a recession hits (companies start laying off 
workers when business declines). The Sahm Rule was designed to give early 
confirmation that “we’re in a recession now” so that policymakers could respond faster 
with stimulus. For example, in 2001 the U.S. unemployment rate started from around 
4% and then rose past 4.5% — that triggered the Sahm Rule right as the 2001 recession 
began. In 2008, unemployment was in the mid-4% range in spring 2008, then by that fall 
it jumped above 5% and kept shooting up — a clear recession signal (indeed the Sahm 
Rule triggered in early 2008, basically coincident with the recession and just before the 
worst of the market crash)[24]. In 2020, unemployment exploded from ~3.5% to over 
14% in two months with the pandemic — an obviously severe recession signal[25]. The 
beauty of the Sahm Rule is its simplicity: one data series, easy threshold. As a reader, 
you don’t need to calculate it yourself; there are websites (like the St. Louis Fed’s FRED 
database) that track the Sahm recession indicator in real time, or you can just watch 
the news on unemployment rates. 


Why does recession matter so much for stocks? Because in a recession, corporate 
earnings drop across the economy. Even companies that seemed unstoppable find 
that it’s hard to sell stuff when customers are struggling or credit is tight. Also, investor 
psychology changes: people flee to safety (like cash or Treasury bonds) and dump 
stocks due to fear of the unknown. Historically, every U.S. recession has coincided with 


a significant stock market pullback. For instance, the early 2000s recession (after the 
dot-com bust, worsened by 9/11) saw the S&P 500 fall roughly 50% from 2000 to 2002 - 
a slow-motion collapse as earnings declined and the internet bubble deflated. The 
Great Recession of 2008-2009, as discussed, cut stocks in half or more[26]. Even 
shorter recessions like 2020’s came with dramatic, if brief, sell-offs (34% down ina 
month)[9]. Why nearly all stocks? Because a recession is systemic: if GDP is falling and 
unemployment is rising, almost every business has a harder time making money. 
Consumers tighten belts, businesses delay investments — so from retail to 
manufacturing to tech, revenues shrink. 


There are other popular recession indicators too. You might have heard of the yield 
curve inversion —- when short-term interest rates are higher than long-term rates, which 
has often preceded recessions. (The logic: bond traders foresee a downturn and expect 
rate cuts, etc.) For example, an inverted yield curve flashed warning in 2019 and indeed 
by early 2020 a recession hit (albeit due to an unforeseeable pandemic). However, the 
yield curve can invert well before a recession and is a bit wonky to track if you’re not 
into finance (you’d have to watch the spread between 2-year and 10-year Treasury 
yields). It’s useful but beyond what a casual investor may want to parse regularly. 


Another simple thing to watch: job market data and consumer confidence. Often, 
you'll hear news like “job openings are drying up” or “consumer spending is 
weakening.” Those can foreshadow recession. But rather than chase many variables, 
I’d suggest focusing on one or two solid signals. The unemployment/Sahm Rule 
approach is great because unemployment is a broad, fairly reliable indicator. If big 
layoffs are making headlines and the unemployment rate ticks up significantly, be on 
guard. 


So how do you use this knowledge? Not by day trading on each jobs report, but by 
maintaining a sense of where we stand in the cycle. If recession risk is low 
(unemployment still falling or very low, yield curve normal, etc.), then one macro worry 
is off the table. If recession risk becomes high (unemployment jumping, yield curve 
inverted, news of many layoffs, etc.), then you might expect broad market weakness 
and you might adjust your strategy — maybe avoid very cyclical stocks, maybe hold a bit 
more cash, etc. Remember, in a recession, even the best companies’ stocks can drop 
simply because big funds are selling out of equities generally or because those 
companies’ earnings will likely dip a bit. It’s often said that the stock market is not the 
economy — which is true in the short run (stocks can fall even when the economy’s fine, 
and vice versa) — but in a hard recession, the link becomes very tight: the economy’s 
tanking and stocks will usually follow. 


One more nuance: by the time a recession is official (declared by economists or clearly 
seen in data), stocks may already have fallen a lot. Markets anticipate. That’s why the 
Sahm Rule is valuable - it catches the recession early. Many investors try to predict 
recessions in advance (good luck!), but for us, it’s more about recognizing when the risk 
is sharply rising. It’s like seeing storm clouds gathering: you don’t know if it’ll directly hit 
you, but you grab an umbrella just in case. In our context, if macro indicators point to a 
storm (recession), we become more cautious with stock bets or demand a bigger 
margin of safety. 


In summary, keep an eye on recession risk as a macro layer. It’s about the engine of 
the economy - when it sputters or stalls, a lot of cars (stocks) on the road will slow 
down no matter how well they were driving individually. We’ll incorporate recession 
awareness into our routine soon, but first, let’s cover one more layer: wars and shocks. 


Wars, Tariffs, and Shocks: When History Punches the Market 


Geopolitical events — wars, terrorist attacks, trade wars, political upheavals — are by 
nature unpredictable, but they form another background layer that can move all stocks 
together, at least briefly. These are the “history-making” events that suddenly change 
risk perceptions or economic conditions. As investors with an eye on the “end of the 
world” (even if metaphorical), we have to acknowledge that sometimes history intrudes 
on markets violently. 


How can wars and politics affect stocks? There are a few channels: 


e Fear and Uncertainty: War is the ultimate wild card. If anew conflict erupts, 
investors often respond with fear of the unknown. Will it spread? Will 
superpowers get involved? Could it lead to something catastrophic? Even if 
those worst fears don’t materialize, the initial reaction can be sell stocks, buy 
safe havens (like gold, dollars, or Treasury bonds). We saw this on asmall scale 
with the Israel-Hamas war outbreak in 2023: initially stocks fell and oil jumped 
as investors contemplated worst-case scenarios[12], even though the conflict 
was regional. When the U.S. invaded Iraq in 2003 or during the Gulf War in 1991, 
there were also bouts of market volatility tied to the news of battle successes or 
setbacks. Generally, geopolitical crises = higher volatility, because markets hate 
uncertainty. 


e Energy and Commodities: Wars often disrupt commodity supplies. Think about 
oil: a huge portion of the world’s oil comes from geopolitically sensitive regions 
(Middle East, Russia, etc.). When war risk flares in those regions, oil prices can 
leap. In 1973, an oil embargo (not war, but geopolitical) caused a market crash 
and recession. In 1990, Iraq’s invasion of Kuwait sent oil up and spooked stocks. 
In 2022, Russia’s invasion of Ukraine propelled oil over $100 and caused natural 
gas shortages in Europe - that acted like a tax on the global economy, hurting 
corporate margins and consumer spending power, and it contributed to global 
stocks falling|11]. For individual stocks, an oil spike may help some (oil 
companies, obviously, and defense contractors if war means more military 
spending), but it hurts many others (airlines, consumer goods, etc.). Butina 
broad index sense, a sharp commodity shock due to war usually drags the whole 
index down because it raises costs and inflation. So one war can indirectly 
hammer many industries. 


e Trade and Tariffs: Not all “wars” involve bullets. The U.S.-China trade war in 
2018-2019 was essentially an economic conflict with tariffs as weapons. When 
tariffs were announced or increased, the market often dipped broadly. Why? 
Tariffs disrupt supply chains, raise costs for businesses, and slow down global 
trade — effectively a headwind for many companies at once. For example, when 
new tariffs were unveiled in mid-2018, or when talks broke down, the whole S&P 


500 often had down days. Investors worried that escalating trade barriers would 
hurt worldwide growth (which they did, modestly). A JPMorgan report in late 
2018 warned that further U.S.-China penalties could weaken global economic 
activity[27]. We saw how those macro fears contributed to the late 2018 
correction. So “war” in a broader sense, including trade wars, is a macro layer: 
companies might be perfectly well-run, but if suddenly they face a 25% tariff on 
key imports or exports, their outlook dims and their stock falls — along with many 
others facing the same shock. 


e Political Crises and Policy Shocks: Geopolitics includes things like elections, 
revolutions, sanctions, and treaties. For instance, a surprise election outcome 
might swing markets (e.g., Brexit’s victory in 2016 initially tanked European 
stocks and hit U.S. markets for a couple of days until things stabilized). 
Sanctions on a major country (like sanctions on Russian oligarchs and 
companies in 2018 or 2022) can have spillover effects - some European banks 
got hit because they had ties to Russia, etc., pulling down bank stocks generally 
on those days. Even domestic political showdowns, like a U.S. government 
shutdown or debt ceiling standoff, can become macro events if they threaten 
economic damage or credit defaults. The longer such uncertainty drags, the 
more it weighs on the broad market. 


The lesson here is not that you must predict wars or political events (good luck with 
that), but that you should always factor in a layer of “global risk.” Especially in a world 
that seems to be getting more volatile, with great power tensions rising, the chance of 
shock events is high. In our “end of the world” mindset, we practically expect some 
kind of big geopolitical or societal shock in the near future. So how do we incorporate 
this without living in constant fear? By being aware and prepared. 


Being aware means when you hear news of geopolitical escalation (say, North Korea 
missile tests, or U.S.-China naval encounter in the South China Sea, or a sudden coup 
ina major oil-producing nation), recognize that this could move markets broadly. It 
might not always —- sometimes markets shrug off events if they seem contained — but 
you should perk your ears up. For a smallinvestor, this doesn’t mean you have to trade 
on every headline. It does mean if you’re considering a new stock position and you 
notice, for example, that overall market sentiment is turning risk-averse due to some 
international crisis, you might choose to wait for things to settle rather than jump in ata 
potentially volatile moment. 


Also, think about which sectors are most exposed to current geopolitical risks. Is 
there talk of restrictions on tech exports to certain countries? Then tech stocks might 
carry an extra macro risk layer. Is there a war that could disrupt oil? Then any company 
with heavy fuel costs (airlines, trucking, even consumer goods shipping) has that macro 
layer to consider. Conversely, defense contractors or renewable energy firms might 
benefit from certain geopolitical trends (war boosts defense spending; trade conflicts 
might boost local production, etc.). Being macro-aware doesn’t only mean gloom — 
sometimes it means identifying beneficiaries of world events. But our focus in 
decomposition is mostly on the risk side: if something bad in geopolitics happens, how 
much of my stock’s movement is that versus company-specific? 


Let’s recount a couple of examples to cement this. In early 2022 when Russia invaded 
Ukraine, energy stocks (like Exxon, Chevron) shot up, and defense stocks (like 
Lockheed Martin) also surged. Meanwhile, the overall market fell for the reasons given 
(inflation, uncertainty, etc.). If you held, say, an airline stock then, it might have dropped 
15% in a short time. Not because the airline did anything wrong, but because oil prices 
spiked (jet fuel cost surge) and people feared war-related travel disruptions. That was a 
macro layer hitting the airline stock. Another example: in October 2023, some defense 
stocks jumped when the Middle East conflict began (anticipation of higher demand), 
whereas most others were flat or slightly down. If you held a generic tech ETF at that 
time, any wobble in its price could partly be attributed to “war fear” across the market. 
If you held a defense ETF, its rise could be attributed to that same war fear (but 
benefiting one sector). So macro events can have broad or targeted effects, but they 
certainly can drive market-wide sentiment. 


Lastly, remember that macro shocks often cause sharp, short-lived market 
reactions. The pattern often is: a shock happens (war, attack, surprising election), 
markets drop suddenly for a day or a week, then stabilize once the immediate 
uncertainty is priced in. Sometimes they even rebound if worst fears don’t materialize. 
For example, the initial Brexit shock sent the FTSE (UK stocks) down, but within days it 
recovered and then rose, because the actual economic impact unfolded slowly. As a 
short-term trader (8-6 month horizon), you might actually find opportunities because of 
these overreactions — but only if you can distinguish macro fear from fundamentals. 
That’s exactly why we decompose causes. If a great company’s stock falls 20% 
overnight due to, say, a Surprise geopolitical event, and you judge that its own business 
is not truly harmed long-term by that event, that could be a chance to buy at a discount 
once the macro dust settles. Conversely, if a stock is falling and you know the macro 
environment is deteriorating (war spreading, economy tanking), you might not want to 
“catch the falling knife” yet because the macro layer will keep weighing it down. 


In summary, wars and geopolitical shocks form the “wildcard” macro layer. We 
cannot predict them with precision, but we can acknowledge their power to move the 
entire market or whole sectors at once. The way to handle this is to stay informed 
(follow global news at a high level) and always ask: “Could there be a macro shock 
affecting this stock right now?” If yes, it might not be the best time to solely blame or 
credit the company itself for its price moves. 


Putting It Together: Separating Background Pain from Company Pain 


We’ve identified four major macro layers: the overall market trend, interest rates, 
recession risk, and geopolitics (wars/shocks). How do we use this knowledge alongside 
the company-specific analysis from Chapter 6? Think of it as building a mental equation 
for a stock’s movement: 


Observed Stock Price = Company’s own performance + market factor + rates factor+ 
recession factor + geopolitical factor *(+ any other layers we identify). 


This isn’t a precise formula you can plug numbers into, but a conceptual framework. 
The goal is to roughly attribute a stock’s move to these components. Let’s illustrate 
with an example: 


Suppose Tesla shares fall 30% over two months. We want to dissect why. Using our 
framework, we might reason as follows: During that period, the overall market (S&P 
500 equal weight) fell maybe 10% (perhaps due to some macro jitters). So one part of 
Tesla’s drop — say one-third — could just be it getting pulled down with the market tide 
(many stocks fell ~10%). Next, we notice interest rates spiked in those two months — 
the yield on 10-year Treasuries jumped, which tends to hurt growth stocks like Tesla. 
Maybe that could account for another, say, 5% of Tesla’s decline (investors applying a 
higher discount rate to Tesla’s future cash flows, since Tesla is seen as a growth 
company, thus lowering its valuation). Perhaps around that time, recession fears also 
ticked up — not a full recession, but let’s say consumer spending was looking shaky. If 
Tesla is considered somewhat cyclical (big purchases like cars depend on consumer 
confidence), that recession risk could add to its drop, maybe another 5%. And finally, 
Tesla had some company-specific news — suppose Elon Musk made a controversial 
acquisition or there were concerns about demand in China. That company-specific 
issue might be the remaining 10% of the drop. 


In this hypothetical breakdown: out of Tesla’s 30% decline, roughly half (15%) was 
macro (market + rates + economy fears), and half was Tesla-specific. The exact 
percentages don’t matter; what matters is understanding the mix. If you erroneously 
thought “Tesla’s down 30% solely because Musk screwed up,” you’d be misreading the 
situation — a good chunk of that fall was actually not about Tesla per se but about the 
backdrop that hit all growth stocks and the market generally. On the flip side, if Tesla 
fell 30% while the market was flat and rates were steady, then most of that would likely 
be Tesla-specific (something is wrong with the company’s story). 


This separation helps in decision-making. If a lot of a stock’s pain is from macro layers, 
and you believe those macro conditions will eventually normalize, then the stock might 
rebound when the background improves (assuming the company itself is okay). But if 
the stock’s pain is above and beyond the macro effect, that signals company-specific 
trouble you need to investigate. For example, if the whole market is down 10% from a 
war scare and your stock is down 25%, then maybe 10% of that is macro and an extra 
15% is something wrong with the company — that’s a red flag about the company. 
Conversely, if the market’s down 10% and your stock is down 12%, that’s largely just 
macro and maybe a tiny company issue; the stock might be fine once the macro fear 
passes. 


In practice, you won’t always quantify it neatly. But try to build a narrative for any major 
move in your portfolio: “X% of this move was because of market sentiment, Y% 
because of interest rates, 2% because of the company’s earnings” etc. This is exactly 
what Nir means by decomposition. It’s like an investigator assigning causation to 
different suspects. 


Another example: Apple stock in 2022 fell from its highs by something like 20-25%. 
Why? One could say: the whole S&P was down ~20% (macro market factor), that’s a big 
chunk right there. Apple also is sensitive to consumer spending, so recession fears 
contributed. And interest rates rising compressed valuations even for big stable 
companies - another portion. Company-specific issues (supply chain problems, maybe 
slower iPhone sales in China) were relatively minor in that case. So Apple’s drop was 
mostly macro-driven. And indeed, when the macro environment improved (inflation 


easing, Fed slowing hikes), Apple’s stock recovered strongly even before any dramatic 
company news - validating that it wasn’t Apple’s business that was broken, it was the 
macro tide pulling it down and then lifting it up. 


One more scenario: a defense contractor stock in 2022. Say it went up 15% while the 
market was down. How come? Macro layers: war (geopolitical) boosted it by increasing 
defense spending outlook, market layer tried to pull it down but war effect overpowered 
that, interest rates maybe had little negative effect because defense earnings are often 
government-backed and less growthy, recession risk didn’t hurt because defense is 
counter-cyclical (wars happen irrespective of economy). So in that case, macro layers 
(specifically war) added positive performance that outweighed the general market 
decline. If you held that stock, you’d attribute its movement largely to geopolitics rather 
than the company’s own doing (unless the company also got new contracts, etc.). 


The overarching point: Don’t lazily attribute 100% of a stock’s move to the nearest 
news headline. A big move usually has multiple causes. Our job as causal investors is 
to parse those causes. This gives us a clearer view of reality. It prevents us from 
panicking about the wrong thing (selling a good stock because the market fell, or 
clinging to a bad stock thinking it’s just macro). It also sets the stage for making better 
predictions: If we know what portion of a stock’s performance was macro-driven, then 
our outlook for that stock should consider our outlook for those macro factors. For 
instance, if we think interest rates will drop soon, and that was a major drag on our 
stock, then we can anticipate a possible lift just from that macro relief. 


One caution: We are not doing precise math or assigning exact percentages in reality. 
This is a qualitative, conceptual exercise. It’s sometimes obvious (e.g., COVID 
shutdown - clearly macro; or a CEO fraud scandal -— clearly company-specific). Other 
times it’s murkier. Don’t get hung up on precision. The goal is to roughly separate 
“background noise” from “the company’s own signal.” 


Now we have the full picture of layers: Company fundamentals (Chapter 6 stuff like 
earnings, competitive advantages, management actions) and Macro layers (market 
mood, rates, economic cycle, world events). In subsequent chapters, we’ll talk about 
how to incorporate this into picking and managing investments (for example, how to 
adjust if you expect a recession or if you want to bet on a certain macro trend). But 
before that, let’s translate this awareness into a simple practical routine. 


A Simple Macro Checklist for Humans (Not Hedge Funds) 


You might be thinking, “Alright, | get these macro factors are important, but how on 
earth do | keep track of all this easily? I’m just one person with a day job, not a team of 
economists.” Fear not. You can stay macro-aware without drowning in data or 
spending hours a day. Here’s a simple checklist you can run through periodically (say, 
once a week or whenever big news hits). It’s a way to cover your bases on the macro 
layers in maybe 15-30 minutes of effort: 


1. Market Pulse: Check the overall market trend. The easiest way is to look at an index 
chart. | recommend glancing at the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index (there are free chart 
websites or an ETF like RSP that tracks it). Look at its recent performance: is itin a 


drawdown (down significantly from recent highs), in arally, or moving sideways? Also, 
check its major support/resistance levels if you’re chart-inclined, but at minimum note: 
“We’re up 5% this month” or “We’re down 15% from the peak” or “We just hit a 52- 
week low.” This gives you context. For example, if you see that the equal-weight index is 
down 15% over the last quarter, you know the market layer is one of broad pessimism — 
a lot of stocks likely got dragged down by that. That might make you more cautious or at 
least more forgiving of your stocks’ poor performance (since it’s happening to almost 
everyone). Conversely, if the market index is near all-time highs, euphoria might be 
high, or maybe only a few big stocks are driving it (check equal-weight vs normal S&P 
disparity). You then know to be a bit skeptical of over-exuberance. 


In practice, just writing one line in a notebook each week like “Market mood: e.g. ina 
correction, down ~10% from peak, volatility moderate” will keep you attuned. It’s like 
taking the market’s temperature. 


2. Interest Rates Update: Know the level and trend of interest rates. You don’t need to 
follow every bond auction, but do keep track of what the Fed is doing. Are they currently 
raising, cutting, or pausing rates? What’s the Fed funds rate roughly (e.g. ~5% in 2025)? 
A quick way: read the summary of the latest Fed meeting or a news article about 
interest rate decisions. Also note the direction of bond yields (10-year Treasury yield is 
a good barometer). For instance, if you hear “10-year yield hit 5% this week, highest 
since 2007,” that’s notable — it means borrowing costs are up and could be pressuring 
stocks. If you hear “Fed signaled it may cut rates next year,” that’s also big —it could 
provide relief to stocks. 


In your weekly check, you might jot: “Rates rising, Fed still hawkish — caution for growth 
stocks,” or “Rates flat, Fed on hold — neutral macro,” or “Rates falling, Fed likely to cut — 
bullish tailwind potentially.” This helps you adjust your strategy: if rates are rising, 
maybe you avoid highly leveraged companies or those valued solely on future profits; if 
rates are falling, maybe you can be a bit more aggressive with growth plays, etc. 
Crucially, when interpreting your stocks’ moves, you’ll recall “oh, yeah, part of that 
drop was because yields jumped last week.” 


3. Recession Watch: Gauge recession risk with a simple indicator or two. As 
discussed, the unemployment rate (and Sahm Rule) is a great one. Each month, the 
government reports unemployment. Pay attention if it starts ticking up. A rule of thumb: 
if unemployment is at multi-decade lows and still falling or stable (like it was in 2019 or 
mid-2023, for example), recession risk in the near-term is low. If unemployment starts 
rising meaningfully, recession risk is elevated. Also watch things like major layoff 
announcements in the news, or consumer sentiment surveys if you come across them. 
Another accessible indicator: the yield curve (10-year minus 2-year yield). If you read 
that it’s deeply inverted (negative by a significant amount), it’s a warning of recession in 
the next year or so (though timing is uncertain). 


You don’t need to obsess daily, just note the general trend: “Unemployment still low, 
job market strong — recession not imminent” versus “Unemployment up from 3.5% to 
4.5% in six months — caution, could be early recession signal.” If you see clear signs of 
recession on the horizon, you might tighten your stop-losses, or be more selective 
(maybe favor some defensive stocks or those with stable earnings), or just be mentally 


prepared for your portfolio to possibly take a hit. And when your stocks dip, you’ll better 
understand if it’s because “everyone is pricing in a recession” rather than something 
you or the company did wrong. 


4. Geopolitical Scan: Stay aware of major global news that could affect markets. You 
don’t have to follow every twist in foreign policy, but it’s wise to at least keep an eye on 
headlines for wars, trade disputes, or major political events. Is there a war or conflict 
ongoing that’s escalating? (e.g., Ukraine-Russia, Middle East, South China Sea). Any 
major terrorist attack or geopolitical shock? Any new tariffs or sanctions being 
announced between big economies? Also political uncertainty like elections in major 
countries, especially if they could change economic policy (for instance, markets may 
react to a U.S. election if drastically different economic agendas are at stake). 


Your weekly note could be: “Geopolitics: moderate — Ukraine war continues but no new 
escalation, U.S.-China tensions simmering (watch tech export news), nothing earth- 
shattering this week.” Or if something big happened: “Geopolitics: High tension —- new 
war started in X region, oil prices spiking, could roil markets.” By articulating it, you 
calibrate your sensitivity. When high geopolitical risk is present, maybe you don’t 
leverage up or you accept that volatility will be higher. When it’s calm on that front, you 
know your stocks will more likely trade on fundamentals or other factors. 


5. Company Sensitivity Mapping: Connect the macro to your specific stocks or 
watchlist. This step is about thinking: for each stock or sector I’m invested in (or 
interested in), how sensitive is it to each macro layer? Make a little mental (or written) 
map. For example: 


e lf you have a bank stock: sensitive to interest rates (higher rates can help banks 
up to a point, but too high can cause defaults — nuance needed), sensitive to 
recession (loan losses if economy tanks), maybe not very sensitive to geopolitics 
unless extreme. 


e lf you have a consumer discretionary stock (retail, apparel): sensitive to 
recession (people cut back spending), somewhat to rates (if it affects consumer 
credit costs), not directly to war unless war affects consumer confidence or 
input costs. 


e lf you have an oil company: very sensitive to geopolitical events (oil price 
moves), somewhat to interest rates (higher rates can slow economy thus oil 
demand), somewhat inversely to recession (recession hurts oil demand). 


e lf you have a tech growth stock: very sensitive to interest rates (as we 
discussed), moderate to overall market (tech often moves with risk appetite), 
maybe not much to war unless it involves cybersecurity or supply chains, 
sensitive to trade policy if it sells internationally. 


Do this for whatever you own. It doesn’t have to be perfect. The aim is to know where 
each holding might get hit from a macro punch. Then, when a given macro event 
happens, you can quickly say “Ah, this will likely affect my Stock A more than Stock B.” 
For instance, if tomorrow interest rates spike 1%, you’d know your bank stock might 
actually rise but your tech stock might fall. If a war starts, your defense stock goes up, 
your airline stock goes down. This proactive mapping will also inform your 


diversification: if you realize all your holdings are highly sensitive to, say, recession, you 
might want to add something that can hold up or even benefit in a downturn (maybe a 
utility stock or a healthcare stock, which tend to be more defensive). 


6. Regular Reflection: /ntegrate macro into your buy/sell decisions and risk 
management. This is less a checklist item and more a habit. When evaluating a stock’s 
prospects for the next 3-6 months (our typical timeframe), always ask: “What macro 
environment will this company be swimming in?” If you expect the overall market to 
struggle, maybe you size your positions smaller or set wider stop-losses. If you expect 
interest rates to drop soon, perhaps you lean more into quality growth names that were 
beaten down. If a major geopolitical risk looms (say you think next year’s election or 
international conflict could shake things), maybe you keep some cash to deploy after 
any big shock, or you hedge slightly. 


A practical mini-routine: whenever a stock of yours moves more than, say, 5% in a day 
or 10% in a month, pause and attribute that move using our layers. Was it market? 
(Check if the whole market or sector moved similarly.) Was it rates? (Did yields jump or 
fall that day, hitting rate-sensitive stocks?) Was it recession news? (Did we get a bad 
economic report or major layoff news?) Was it geopolitical? (Any world news that day?) 
Or was it something specific the company did? Force yourself to explain the move in 
those terms. This reinforces your understanding and keeps you from knee-jerk 
reactions. 


7. Keep it Simple and Consistent: The above may seem like a lot written out, but in 
practice it condenses nicely. Many investors naturally do some of this: reading news, 
checking the market, etc. The key is to do it consistently and coherently. Instead of 
doom-scrolling or catching random snippets, have a structured check-in. Some people 
do it Sunday evening to prep for the week, or Friday afternoon to recap. Whenever suits 
you, try to touch on each macro layer in your review. It’s like a pilot’s pre-flight checklist 
— once you get used to it, it’s quick and you rarely forget a step. And it ensures you’re 
not blindsided by, say, ignoring interest rates until it’s too late. 


One thing to avoid: overreacting to every macro headline. Our goal is awareness, not 
hyperactivity. If you rebalanced your portfolio on every slight change in macro 
indicators, you’d churn yourself to death. Instead, think of macro knowledge as setting 
the background risk level. If macro indicators are all green and calm, you might be ina 
“risk-on” mode (more comfortable holding stocks). If macro indicators are flashing red, 
you shift to “risk-aware” mode (maybe tighten stops, maybe hold a bit more cash or 
defensive positions, expect volatility). You still base your individual stock picks on their 
merits, but you adjust weightings or timing based on macro. 


By following this routine, even a small investor with $10k (our assumed reader) can feel 
on top of the big picture. It should not take more than a half-hour to read some market 
summary, glance at a chart or two, and update yourself on key economic numbers. 
Think of it as reading the daily news, but specifically filtered for investing relevance. And 
if you miss a week, it’s fine - macro trends usually evolve over months, not days (except 
sudden shocks, which you’ll hear about anyway). 


Summary and a Small Homework Assignment 


We’ve covered a lot in this chapter. Let’s briefly recap: Macro layers — the market, 
interest rates, recession risk, and geopolitical shocks — profoundly influence stock 
prices. A savvy investor doesn’t just ask “What did this company do?” when a stock 
moves; they also ask “What was happening in the world?” We saw how treating the 
entire market as one big stock via the S&P 500 equal-weight index helps us gauge the 
background trend that lifts or sinks all stocks. We explained interest rates as the “price 
of time” — when that price rises, it generally weighs on stocks (especially those banking 
on future growth)/[15], and when it falls, it can buoy valuations and economic 
activity[17]. We introduced the concept of recession indicators like the Sahm Rule, 
which uses a spike in unemployment as a clear signal of a recession starting[23] —- when 
the economy’s engine stalls, expect most stocks to sputter. And we discussed wars 
and geopolitical turmoil as sometimes delivering sudden punches to markets: oil 
shocks, panics, or widespread risk aversion that can drop indexes overnight[12][13]. 
Crucially, all these layers interact with each other and with company-specific factors. 
In the short term, stock prices are a cocktail of these influences. 


The purpose of decomposing these factors is not academic — it’s practical. By 
understanding why a stock moved, we become better at predicting what might move it 
next and at avoiding costly misinterpretations. Instead of being the investor who says 
“Ugh, XYZ fell, must be a bad company,” you’ll be the one saying “XYZ fell mostly 
because the Fed hiked rates and the whole sector fell. The company itself is fine, so this 
might be a buying opportunity.” Or conversely, recognizing when a drop is not just 
macro and requires action (e.¢g., “This fell more than the market, something’s wrong 
with the company”). 


As aco-author with Nir in this journey, I’ll emphasize: this method doesn’t require 
genius, just a systematic approach. Nir operates with an assumption that huge macro 
disruptions (maybe even “the end of the world” scenarios) are on the horizon, which 
makes being macro-aware even more important. But even if you’re less pessimistic, the 
method holds — macro factors always matter, doomer scenario or not. By incorporating 
a macro check into your investing process, you elevate yourself from a stock picker to a 
portfolio manager — one who manages not just companies, but context. 


Now, a small homework assignment to cement these ideas: Create your own macro 
diary for the last big market move. Here’s how: pull up a chart of the S&P 500 equal 
weight (or a broad index of your choice) for a volatile period — for example, 2020, or 
2022, or even a shorter window like Oct-Nov 2023. Identify at least three major drops 
or spikes on that chart. For each, do a quick bit of research (a Google search or 
recalling headlines) to answer “What was happening in the world at this time?” Jot 
down those events. You might find, say, “March 2020 —- COVID lockdown announced, 
recession fear” or “June 2022 — inflation report higher than expected, Fed accelerated 
hikes” or “October 2023 - war in Middle East, oil up, yields up.” Keep it simple, just link 
event to market move. Then reflect: how quickly did markets recover after each event? 
Which were short-term panics vs. longer downturns? This exercise will reinforce how 
macro events align with market movements. It also gives you historical perspective: 


you’ll see that macro storms come and go, and markets eventually find footing — useful 
to remember during the next panic. 


Feel free to share your macro diary or discuss it on our book’s companion website 
(prophet-of-ai.com, where we host deep-dive reports). But even privately, this diary will 
make you more confident. You’ll start noticing patterns: e.g., “Huh, every time 
unemployment jumped, we were already in a bear market” or “Oil shocks often cause 
quick drops but then markets adjust,” etc. Those insights become part of your intuition 
in the future. 


By completing this chapter, you’ve effectively added a powerful set of lenses to your 
investing toolkit. You won’t see stocks in isolation anymore; you’ll see them with the full 
backdrop of the market ocean, the rate climate, the economic cycle, and the world’s 
news. In the coming chapters, we’ll use these insights to refine how we pick stocks for 
short-term trades and how we decide when to enter or exit positions. We’ll also discuss 
hedging and what to do when our “end of the world” thesis seems to be materializing. 
But no matter what comes, remember: knowledge reduces fear. When you 
understand why your portfolio is moving, you gain control - or at least the sense of 
control - which is the first step to profiting even in chaos. 


So, keep this chapter’s checklist handy. Keep decomposing causes whenever 
something shakes the market. Over time, you’ll get faster and more intuitive at it. Macro 
awareness will become second nature. And that means you’ll be the investor who stays 
calm inthe storm, sees the opportunities in the panic, and navigates the end-of-the- 
world (or just a nasty bear market) with a steady hand. After all, if you can invest at the 
end of the world, you can invest through anything. Happy macro tracking, and see you 
in the next chapter![18]/[12

Chapter 8 — Sector Deep Dives: Using Deep Research as 
Your Atlas 


Why Sector Analysis Matters 


Investing isn’t just about picking individual stocks. Entire sectors — groups of 
companies in the same industry — often move together when big events hit. Anew 
breakthrough or a policy change can lift or sink a whole sector at once, not just a 
single company. For example, a revolutionary battery technology could boost all 
electric vehicle makers, while a sudden regulation might hurt every social media stock 
at the same time. Understanding sectors gives you a broader lens: instead of only 
zooming in on one company, you see the wider industry landscape. This matters 
because many of the forces shaping our near-future market (from Al disruptions to 
climate policies) play out at the sector level. 


In Chapter 6, we learned to identify company-specific parameters (the vital signs of an 
individual stock). In Chapter 7, we layered on the macro factors — market trends, 
interest rates, recessions, even wars - that affect all investments. Now, in Chapter 8, 
we add the third dimension: sector dynamics. By combining company, macro, and 
sector perspectives, we get a 3D view of the investment terrain. Think of it this way: if 
company analysis is like studying the trees, and macro analysis is the climate, then 
sector analysis is understanding the forest ecosystem. You need all three to navigate 
effectively, especially in an uncertain world where entire industries can surge or 
collapse together. 


Another reason to care about sectors is the practicality of investing via sector ETFs or 
baskets. If you believe a theme (like robotics or renewable energy) will take off but 
aren’t sure which single stock will win, you can invest in an ETF that covers that whole 
sector. This diversifies your bet across many companies riding the same trend. For our 
typical reader with around $10k to invest and a 3-6 month horizon, sector-level moves 
can offer opportunities without the need to gamble on one make-or-break stock pick. 
It’s a way to align your investments with big-picture technological and economic 
shifts —- which is exactly what we’ll explore in this chapter. 


Deep Research Reports: Maps, Not Crystal Balls 


By now, you might be wondering how to actually map out a sector’s future. This is 
where Nir’s Deep Research reports come in. These reports (around 30 of them on the 
companion site prophet-of-ai.com) are like detailed atlases for various tech-driven 
sectors — from autonomous vehicles to hydrogen energy. Each Deep Research report 
provides a structured look at a specific sector or emerging technology, typically 
including: 


e The current state of the sector (as of 2024-2025): key players, technologies, 
market stats. 


e Abaseline trajectory: essentially, “if things continue as they are, here’s where 
this sector might go in the next few years.” This is not a guaranteed prediction, 
but a reference scenario. 


e Key parameters and drivers: the handful of factors that matter most to that 
sector’s progress (for example, for EVs these might be battery cost, charging 
infrastructure, and government subsidies). 


e Flip factors (or wildcards): events that could dramatically speed up or derail the 
sector. Nir often calls these the things that could “flip the verdict” of a sector 
outlook. They include breakthroughs, disasters, policy changes, etc. 


e Upside and downside scenarios: how the sector could perform in a best-case 
or worst-case situation if those wildcards come to pass. 


It’s important to stress that these reports are not crystal balls. No one can say exactly 
what will happen or when. Instead, think of them as maps and weather forecasts. A 
map shows you the terrain (the major factors and players in a sector), and a weather 
forecast highlights possible storms or tailwinds ahead (the flip factors). When you’re 
investing in turbulent times — say, “the end of the world” scenario this book entertains — 
having a good map and forecast is invaluable. It doesn’t guarantee you won’t hit a 
storm, but at least you’ll know it’s a possibility and have an idea which direction it might 
come from. 


You can use Nir’s deep-dive reports if you have access to them, but you can also seek 
out similar high-quality sector analyses elsewhere (industry reports, expert blogs, etc.). 
The key is to adopt that same structured thinking: know the baseline, know what could 
change it, and stay alert for those changes. In the rest of this chapter, we’ll apply this 
approach to several example sectors. These were chosen because they’re both high- 
impact (each could shape the world in the next few years) and illustrative of the 
method. By examining these, you’ll learn how to extract sector-level parameters and 
keep an eye on the flip factors that turn those sector narratives upside down. 


Key Parameters and Flip Factors Across Sectors 


We will now dive into six sectors that Nir and our Al team have researched deeply, using 
them as case studies: 


6. Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) - self-driving cars and trucks 

7. Robotics —- both industrial robots and service robots 

8. Edge Computing - pushing compute power closer to users/devices 

9. Electric Vehicles vs Oil-the EV revolution and its impact on oil demand 
10. Renewable Energy - solar, wind, and the quest to power the grid cleanly 
11. Hydrogen Economy - the potential of hydrogen as a clean energy carrier 


For each sector, we’ll break down: a quick overview of what it is and its baseline 
outlook, the key parameters to watch, the flip-factor events that could accelerate or 
crash the story, and what all this means for 3-6 month investing opportunities. As you 
read, note how we translate big-picture understanding into practical moves like “ifX 
happens, that’s probably good for stocks A, B, C and bad for D”. This is the essence of 
combining Deep Research with real-world investing. 


Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) 


1. Overview & Baseline: Autonomous vehicles refer to cars, taxis, or trucks that can 
drive themselves without human intervention. As of 2025, AV technology is finally 
moving from flashy demos to early commercial services. In cities like San Francisco, 
Phoenix, and others, companies such as Waymo (Alphabet/Google’s AV unit) and 
Cruise (GM’s AV arm) have rolled out limited robotaxi services. These vehicles use 
combinations of sensors (cameras, lidar, radar) and Al to navigate city streets. The 
baseline narrative for AVs is gradual expansion: more pilot programs in more cities, 
incremental improvements in the software, and cautious steps through regulatory 
hoops. For example, Waymo has over 1,500 driverless taxis in its fleet and in late 2025 
began offering fully autonomous ride-hailing in multiple U.S. cities[1][2]. The 
expectation is that robotaxi services will roll out city-by-city, and autonomous trucking 
will see trials on highways, over the next couple of years. AV tech is largely here, but it’s 
constrained by safety validation and public acceptance. Most cars on the road are still 
human-driven, and even Tesla’s so-called “Full Self-Driving” requires constant human 
oversight. So, baseline: exciting progress, but mainstream adoption is slow and 
patchy (with maybe a fully self-driving geo-fenced taxi service in a few major cities, and 
early autonomous trucking routes by 2026). 


2. Key Sector Parameters: What really determines how fast AVs spread? A few key 
factors stand out: 


Regulatory Approval & Safety — Self-driving cars won’t go anywhere without the 
green light from regulators. Every city/state has its own rules. Approval often 
depends on safety data: how many miles between accidents, how AVs handle 
emergency scenarios, etc. A steady drop in AV accident rates or a clear 
regulatory framework (e.g. standardized federal guidelines) would accelerate 
deployment. 

Technology Maturity (Reliability) — The Al “driver” needs to handle not just 
normal conditions but weird, edge-case scenarios (crazy pedestrians, sudden 
road work, snowstorms). The better the tech gets at these, the more viable AVs 
become. This includes the cost and capability of sensors and compute: high- 
performance lidar and Al chips were extremely expensive, but costs are coming 
down. Cheaper, better sensors mean AVs can be produced at scale without 
costing a fortune. 


Public Perception & Demand - Do people actually want to ride in a driverless 
car? Early adopters find it novel, but surveys show many are still nervous about 
AV safety. In one New York City poll in 2025, two-thirds of respondents said 
they’d prefer a human driver over a free robotaxi ride, mainly due to safety 
concerns[3]. Public trust will grow if AVs build a track record of safety (and 
convenience/affordability). On the flip side, a few well-publicized mishaps can 
scare people off quickly. 


Competitive Progress & Partnerships — There are different players: tech 
companies (Waymo, Baidu, Intel’s Mobileye), automakers (GM’s Cruise, Tesla, 
Mercedes partnering on autonomy), and startups. Their strategies differ 
(robotaxi networks vs selling autonomous features to consumers). If one player 


achieves a clear tech lead or a successful business model, others will race to 
catch up, possibly through partnerships. For instance, ride-hailing companies 
like Uber and Lyft are partnering with AV firms rather than developing tech in- 
house — Lyft has pilot programs with Waymo, and Uber has deals to potentially 
deploy autonomous fleets in the future[4]. The sector could consolidate around 
a few winners or remain fragmented; that will affect how investment flows. 


Labor and Economic Pressures - This is more of a societal factor: if there’s a 
severe driver shortage or rising costs for human drivers (like truck driver 
shortages or higher minimum wages for taxi drivers), it makes the case for AVs 
stronger economically. Conversely, if human drivers organize and push back 
(through unions or political pressure), they might slow down AV rollouts in 
certain cities. Already, taxi and Uber driver groups in some cities have protested 
AV testing, fearing for their jobs[5]. 


3. Flip Factors - What Could Change the Game: Given those parameters, what kind of 
wildcards could flip the AV sector’s trajectory dramatically? 


Major Safety Incident or Scandal (Negative Flip): The nightmare scenario for 
AV proponents is a tragic, high-profile accident caused by a driverless car — for 
example, an AV running over a pedestrian or causing a multi-car crash and it’s 
clearly the Al’s fault. If something like this happens and gains national attention, 
we could see a public and regulatory backlash. Regulators might halt testing or 
impose strict new rules overnight. This actually happened in a limited way in 
2023-2024: after some collisions and odd incidents in San Francisco, officials 
temporarily curbed Cruise and Waymo’s operations there. A more serious 
incident could set the whole sector back by years (similar to how early airplane 
crashes grounded nascent aviation for a time). Investors in AV companies 
would likely see stock drops in such a scenario. 


Regulatory Green Light (Positive Flip): On the other hand, a government could 
fully endorse AVs. Imagine a major city or state formally legalizes widespread 
robotaxi services because the data shows they’re safe —- or a country like China 
pushes a big rollout as a matter of policy. This kind of clear green light (especially 
if coupled with incentives, like permission to operate without certain costly 
restrictions) could accelerate deployment. It might also prompt other regions to 
follow to stay competitive. A related flip factor is liability clarity: if laws get 
updated to clarify who’s liable in an AV crash (the manufacturer? the software 
provider? the owner?), it removes a big uncertainty cloud hanging over the 
industry. 

Tech Breakthrough or Cost Collapse (Positive Flip): If there’s a sudden leap in 
Al capability — say a new algorithm or chip that significantly boosts driving 
reliability -AVs could quickly outpace human drivers in safety. For example, if an 
AV system proves it can drive 10x safer than a human reliably, insurance 
companies and consumers might demand AVs. Similarly, if the cost of equipping 
a car with full self-driving drops to just a few thousand dollars (from maybe 
~$100k per vehicle a few years ago), then the economics of robo-taxis and 
autonomous trucks become very compelling. Companies might deploy fleets 
much faster when it makes financial sense. 


Labor or Political Pushback (Negative Flip): We touched on driver protests; a 
flip factor would be if that turns into concrete action — e.g. a truckers’ union 
lobbying successfully for a law that bans driverless trucks on highways for the 
next decade, to “protect jobs.” Or local laws that limit AVs (like requiring a safety 
driver in the vehicle, which defeats the purpose of full autonomy). These kinds of 
interventions could significantly slow adoption in certain jurisdictions. Ona 
national scale, if a major election swung toward an anti-AV stance (not likely to 
be acentral issue, but possible as part of a broader labor platform), that could 
introduce legislation hindering AV development or deployment. 


Infrastructure and Data: Another flip factor could be something like a 
cyberattack or widespread hack on AV systems (imagine multiple AVs ina 
network getting hijacked). That would scare the public and regulators, similar to 
a safety incident. Conversely, a positive infrastructure move would be if cities 
upgrade roads to be more AV-friendly (special lanes or sensors that 
communicate with AVs). While not as dramatic as the above, infrastructure 
improvement could accelerate safe AV rollout, whereas a cybersecurity incident 
could pause it. 


4. 3-6 Month Investing Implications: How might an investor trade or position around 
this sector in the short term? A few scenarios: 


Positive News Build-Up: Suppose over a single quarter, we see a pattern of 
good news - e.g. “California expands AV operations statewide,” “Waymo reports 
5 million driverless miles with zero fatalities,” “Major ride-hail company orders 
1,000 autonomous Cars.” In such a case, sentiment on AVs could turn very 
bullish. You’d likely see stocks like Alphabet (GOOGL) (due to Waymo) or 
General Motors (GM) (due to Cruise) get a bump, as well as smaller players 
purely focused on AV tech. There are also some ETFs that cover autonomous 
tech (for instance, ETFs that bundle EV and autonomous themes). An investor 
who anticipates this chain of events could build a position in some of these 
names or the ETF ahead of time. Even chipmakers that enable AVs (think Nvidia, 
which provides Al chips to many self-driving programs) could benefit from 
bullish AV news. 


Negative Shock: On the other hand, consider the sudden negative flip — say an 
AV causes a fatal accident that goes viral. In a 3-6 month window, that could 
hurt the entire AV theme. Stocks of companies heavily invested in AV (again, 
Alphabet, GM, maybe Tesla to some extent because of its self-driving promises) 
might dip on the news. An investor might react by reducing exposure to those 
names or hedging (if they’re sophisticated, though we are avoiding complex 
hedges in this book). Conversely, who might benefit from an AV setback? 
Potentially ride-hailing companies like Uber, if investors think “well, looks like 
human drivers won’t be replaced as soon — Uber/Lyft can breathe easier for a 
while.” It’s also possible that traditional auto companies that had been seen as 
behind in AV could get a relative boost (“the playing field stays level”). Short- 
term traders often re-price such narratives quickly. 


Watch the flip factor calendar: Many flip factors for AVs don’t come ona 
predictable schedule (accidents, breakthroughs — those are surprises). But 


some things are known events: regulatory hearings, scheduled safety reports, or 
product launch announcements. For example, if Tesla says it will roll out a true 
robotaxi service by a certain date (Subject to approval), that date is one to watch 
— success or delay will move the stock. If the NHTSA (the U.S. auto safety 
regulator) has an announced date for new autonomous vehicle guidelines, that’s 
a calendar item. As an investor, having these on your radar means you won’t be 
caught off guard. In a 3-6 month horizon, even one or two such events can be 
significant. 


In summary, the AV sector is a high-stakes bet on technology and trust. The deep 
research view tells us what to monitor (safety data, regulations, tech costs, public 
sentiment), and as investors we translate that into watching specific stocks and ETFs 
and being ready to act when the big news (flip factors) hits. 


Robotics (Industrial and Service Robots) 


1. Overview & Baseline: This sector covers robots used in factories (industrial robots) 
as well as robots used in services (from warehouse logistics bots to hospital assistant 
robots to those cute burger-flipping robots). The baseline narrative here is that robot 
adoption is steadily rising, especially wherever there’s a need to automate repetitive 
tasks or address labor shortages. In factories worldwide, robot installation is at record 
highs. By 2023, there were over 4.2 million industrial robots operating globally —- a new 
record, and 10% more than the year before[6]. These robots are welding cars, 
assembling electronics, etc., mainly in places like China, Japan, South Korea, Europe, 
and the U.S. Meanwhile, “service robots” — the ones outside manufacturing — are also 
booming. In 2024, nearly 200,000 new professional service robots were sold, a 9% 
increase on the year, with logistics and cleaning robots leading the way|7][8]. We’ve 
also seen an explosion of consumer robots like robot vacuums (tens of millions sold 
annually)[9]. 


The baseline is that industry 4.0 keeps advancing: warehouses and retail are 
increasingly using autonomous guided vehicles and shelf-scanning robots; hospitals 
use delivery robots; hotels experiment with robot concierges. Many fast-food joints 
even trial fry-cooking robots. It’s not sci-fi or “every home has a C-3PO” yet — but behind 
the scenes, robots are becoming common. Growth might pause during a recession 
(companies cut capital spending) but then continues as technology gets cheaper and 
better. An important baseline factor: we have an aging global population and often 
labor shortages in key jobs (like caregivers, warehouse pickers). Robots are a solution 
to fill those gaps, so there’s a secular tailwind here. 


Operational stock of industrial robots worldwide (2013-2023). The installed base 
surpassed 4.28 million in 2023, up 10% from 2022/6]. Robot installations hit ~541,000 
units in 2023, near the all-time high[10]. 


2. Key Sector Parameters: 


e Labor Costs and Shortages: One big driver for robotics is the cost and 
availability of human labor. In countries or industries where wages are rising or 
it’s hard to find workers, robots become more attractive. For example, if 


warehouse worker wages jump or there aren’t enough applicants to fill roles, a 
company is more likely to invest in automation. So the unemployment rate and 
wage inflation in manufacturing/service jobs is a key parameter. Demographics
