Modern Al can do a decent job summarizing the flood of news and data. Of course, 
you'll be the final judge on decisions (Al can analyze, but it doesn’t have skin in the 
game -you do). 


We’ll delve more into how to practically set this up in later chapters. The takeaway here 
is that Deep Research + Al tools = a powerful combination. Deep Research gives you 
the structured map (as we did for each sector), and Al can act like your scout, 
constantly checking that map against the real world and pointing out where things are 
ahead or behind schedule. 


4. Know When to Act vs. Observe: Not every insight requires an immediate trade. 
Some sector analyses might tell you “this is a promising trend, but not ripe yet” or 
“there’s too much uncertainty; let’s wait for one of the flip factors to resolve.” That’s 
fine. The goal is to be prepared. For instance, you might not invest in hydrogen this year 
at all, but by doing the research now, you have a clear idea what to watch. Maybe you 
set an alert: “If any company produces green hydrogen below $2/kg, start looking 
seriously at hydrogen stocks.” Or “Once a major airline commits to hydrogen-based 
fuels, consider fuel cell makers.” Patience is part of the game. Your sector atlas tells 


you where treasure might lie; you don’t have to rush in until the path becomes clear or 
the odds improve. 


Finally, remain nimble. Sectors that look hot can cool off and vice versa. Always loop 
back: update your deep research notes when new information comes (did a flip factor 
actually happen? Does that change the base case’7). Investing with a sector mindset is 
dynamic — but it also prevents you from being blindsided, because you’re not just 
seeing one tree, you’re seeing the forest. And as the proverb goes, “forewarned is 
forearmed.” 


A Practical Workflow for Sector Analysis 


Let’s cement this with a practical step-by-step approach. The idea is to integrate what 
we’ve learned into an ongoing routine that keeps you on top of relevant sectors without 
it becoming a full-time job. Here’s a simple workflow you can start using: 


Step 1: Pick a Few Sectors to Focus On - You don’t need to track everything under the 
sun. Choose 2 or 3 sectors or themes that interest you and are investable. Ideally, these 
are areas where you see big potential or risk in the next couple of years. Maybe you’re 
passionate about clean energy, or you work in tech so you have insights into 
semiconductors or Al. Focusing helps you go deep where it counts. For this example, 
let’s say you pick EVs vs Oil, Renewables, and Robotics. 


Step 2: Do a Mini Deep Research Dive - For each chosen sector, gather a high-quality 
overview. This could be one of Nir’s Deep Research reports if available, or a reputable 
industry analysis or white paper. Read it and distill the key points: - What’s the baseline 
story (in plain language)? - What are the 3-5 key parameters driving this sector? - What 
are the 3-5 flip factors that could drastically change the outlook? 


Write these down in bullet form. Congratulations, you’ve just made yourself a one-page 
“atlas” for that sector. Don’t worry if you’re not an expert; even a basic map is better 
than none, and you can refine it over time. The earlier parts of this chapter serve as 
examples — you can model your notes similarly. 


Step 3: Identify Related Investments — As we discussed, list out relevant stocks and 
ETFs. lf you already own some, mark those. If not, pick a few you might consider in the 
future. For EVs vs Oil, for instance, you might list Tesla, GM, ExxonMobil, and an oil ETF. 
For Renewables, maybe First Solar, NextEra Energy, and a clean energy ETF. You’re not 
committing to buy all these right now; you’re building a watchlist. 


Set up this watchlist in whatever tool you use (a trading app, a spreadsheet, etc.). It’s 
good to have them in one place so you can easily monitor price moves and news. 


Step 4: Track the Signals — This is where your work becomes iterative. Dedicate a small 
block of time, maybe once a week or every two weeks, to update yourself on each 
sector: - Skim the news for anything related to your key parameters or flip factors. For 
example, if one parameter for renewables is “interest rates & financing,” note if there 
were any central bank decisions or big moves in bond yields that week. Or if a flip factor 
for EVs is “battery breakthrough,” check if any battery company made a big 
announcement. - Check in on your watchlist stocks: did any report earnings or 


significant developments? How did they react? - Jot down any notable changes in your 
one-page sector summary. Over time, these notes will highlight trend changes (e.g., 
“Lithium prices have fallen 30% from last year — relieving a pressure on EV costs[19].” 
Or “Waymo got approval in another city — regulatory momentum building.”). 


You can leverage Al assistants here: for example, ask an LLM “summarize this week’s 
major news for the robotics industry and any impacts on key companies.” It can save 
you time sifting through articles. Just remember to verify important facts from trusted 
sources (and you’ve seen how we cite sources — you can ask Al to give sources too). 


Step 5: Review and Adjust Your Positions (or Watchlist) as Needed — If one of your 
flip factors looks like it’s about to trigger or has triggered, that’s a call to action. 
Suppose the EU decides on a big hydrogen subsidy package (flip factor for Hydrogen 
sector — positive policy flip). If you had hydrogen on your watchlist, you might decide 
“Okay, time to take a small position in that hydrogen ETF or stock because the 
landscape just improved.” Or if a negative flip hits - say, an AV safety scandal occurs — 
and you hold an AV-related stock, you might choose to reduce exposure or at least be 
prepared for volatility. 


If nothing dramatic happens, that’s fine too. This process is as much about staying 
informed as it is about trading. Sometimes the best move is to sit tight with conviction 
until evidence tells you otherwise. 


Step 6: Iterate and Expand — Over months, you’ll get more comfortable and perhaps 
add another sector to follow, or swap one out if it’s no longer appealing. Each time, go 
through the deep dive step and keep building your knowledge base. You’ll also refine 
your ability to spot which news really matters. Initially, you might flag too many things; 
with experience, you’ll focus on the signals not noise. 


Homework: Create Your Own Sector One-Pager 


Let’s put this into practice right now in a small way: - Choose one sector or theme that 
intrigues you (it could be one we covered or something completely different - maybe 
biotech, cybersecurity, space industry, whatever). - Research for one hour (set a timer) 
about that sector’s outlook. Use reliable sources — if you find a recent industry report or 
even acomprehensive Wikipedia article, that’s a start. - Write down: the baseline 
narrative, key drivers (parameters), and flip factors, in your own words. Aim for at most 
one page or a few paragraphs. Don’t worry if it’s rough. - List a few related 
stocks/ETFs. Even if you’re not ready to invest in them, know who the main players are. 


This exercise does a few things: it makes you synthesize information (solidifying your 
understanding), and it creates a reference you can update. It doesn’t have to be perfect 
—in fact, you might identify gaps where you’re unsure (“I don’t actually know how fast 
this technology is improving each year”). That’s good, because it gives you specific 
questions to dig deeper on later or to ask an expert/LLM. 


The important part is to start. Once you have version 1.0 of your sector atlas, you can 
iterate. Investing, especially in fast-changing times, is an iterative learning process. By 
doing this work, you are effectively becoming your own research analyst, guided by 
curiosity and structured thinking rather than market hype. 


In the next chapters, we’ll explore how to turbo-charge this process with Al co-pilots 
and how to form concrete 3-6 month game plans using scenarios and probabilities. But 
the foundation you’ve built - mindset (Chapter 5), stock fundamentals (Chapter 6), 
macro awareness (Chapter 7), and now sector deep research (Chapter 8) — will ensure 
that those game plans are robust and reality-checked. 


Good luck, and happy researching! You now have an atlas in hand; the next step is 
learning how to navigate with it as the world unfolds.

Chapter 9 —- The LLM Council: Using Multiple Als 
Without Blind Faith 


In previous chapters, we explored how to read stock charts like a physicist (Chapter 
5), analyze company-specific events (Chapter 6), understand the macro forces 
shaping markets (Chapter 7), and map out sector trends and flip factors (Chapter 8). 
Now we turn to a new kind of tool to glue all of this together: Large Language Models 
(LLMs). In this chapter, we will learn how a small investor can use multiple general- 
purpose Als together as a “council” of advisors. The goal is to support our 3-6 month 
investment decisions with Al insights — without treating any single Al as a magical 
oracle, and without relying on opaque finance-specific black boxes. By the end of this 
chapter, you’ll know how to harness a council of Als to analyze a stock or ETF, compare 
their perspectives, interrogate their reasoning, and ultimately make a more informed 
decision (or decide to take no action) with your own judgment in the loop. 


Why Not Just Trust a Black-Box Finance Al? 


Before diving into LLMs, you might wonder: Why not use one of the specialized “Al stock 
picker” tools that are popping up? Services like Kavout’s Kai Score or Danelfin’s Al 
Score promise to boil down huge amounts of financial data into a single rating (e.g. “7 
out of 10” or “B+” for a given stock). These systems ingest mountains of information — 
hundreds of fundamental metrics, technical indicators, sentiment signals, and more — 
and then use machine learning to output a predictive score. For example, Danelfin’s 
platform analyzes 10,000 features per stock every day, drawing on over 600 technical 
indicators, 150 fundamental indicators, and 150 sentiment indicators to calculate 
the probability a stock will beat the market in the next 3 months[1]. The result is 
condensed into one Al Score, often derived from combining hundreds of decision trees 
and “alpha signals” behind the scenes/[2]. Kavout’s Kai Score is similar: it distills 
complex analysis into a simple 1-9 rating system, updated continuously as new data 
comes in[3]. 


Such tools can be impressive in their data-crunching power. However, there are 
several reasons this book’s method favors general LLMs over black-box finance Als: 


e Lack of Transparency: A black-box model might tell you “Stock X = Score 8/10,” 
but it rarely explains why. The reasoning — which could involve hundreds of 
factors — is not clearly communicated to the user. Even the developers might not 
fully understand the specific combination of signals that led to a particular score 
on a particular day. As an investor, you’re left in the dark about the rationale. It’s 
hard to trust a recommendation if you can’t audit the reasoning behind it. 


e Too Many Factors to Interpret: These finance Als consider everything and the 
kitchen sink —- from price momentum to obscure accounting ratios to social 
media chatter. While that breadth is a strength for pure prediction, it means the 
output isn’t tied to a coherent story you can reason about. You can’t easily 
combine their output with your own knowledge of, say, an upcoming product 
launch or a CEO change, because the model’s reasoning (and any human 


reasoning) gets drowned out by hundreds of other statistically-derived features. 
In short, you get a number or grade, but integrating it with causal understanding 
(the core of our approach) is difficult. 


e Black-Box Risk: When you rely on a proprietary Al score, you’re effectively 
outsourcing your decision to an algorithm that might pick up spurious 
correlations or be overfit to past data. If the model is wrong, you won’t know why 
it was wrong. There’s a risk of blind faith in “the Al said so,” which is dangerous — 
especially in a volatile near-term scenario like the one this book envisions. 


e = Inflexibility: Black-box scores are one-size-fits-all. They don’t account for your 
personal risk tolerance or the specific context we care about (e.g. the possibility 
of an Al-driven collapse in 2026). They won’t tell you, “This stock scored 8/10 
because it’s historically strong in recessions, but note that if the Fed raises rates 
again, this could flip.” You just get the 8/10. In contrast, we want tools that we 
can ask questions, that can consider “if/then” scenarios, and that we can prod 
to explain their assumptions. 


To be clear, we are not dismissing these Al stock rating systems as useless — they may 
well have some predictive value. But for our purposes, they don’t fit the philosophy of 
Investing in the End of the World. Here, we prioritize understanding scenarios and 
catalysts over accepting a mysterious score. We need tools that can converse with us, 
incorporate our knowledge of real-world events, and help us reason through 
uncertainty. This is where general-purpose LLMs come in. 


General LLMs as Advisors, Not Oracles 


General large language models like ChatGPT, Claude, Google’s Gemini (and others) 
are incredibly powerful text-processing engines. They’ve read billions of words of 
books, articles, and websites, and learned to detect patterns and generate human-like 
responses. How can they help a small investor? 


What LLMs Can Do Well: 


e Summarize and Synthesize: LLMs can read and digest huge amounts of text 
quickly. For example, they could summarize a 100-page earnings report or pick 
out the key points from 20 different news articles about a company. They can 
connect dots between pieces of information. This helps you get up to speed on 
a stock’s situation much faster than reading everything yourself. 


e Explain and Brainstorm: Ask an LLM “Why might stock X be going up despite 
bad news?” and it can propose several theories (e.g. “perhaps investors 
anticipated worse results, or maybe a new partnership was announced”). It can 
articulate cause-and-effect chains in plain language. Essentially, an LLM can 
serve as avery knowledgeable sounding board, helping you brainstorm 
scenarios or interpret signals that you find confusing. 


e Scenario Analysis: LLMs are good at imaginative but structured thinking. You 
can prompt them to come up with possible futures for a stock: best-case, worst- 


case, and middle-of-the-road scenarios, along with what events would lead to 
each. They won’t know the future (no one does!), but they can lay out the logical 
possibilities in a clear way. This is extremely useful for contingency planning. 


e Risk Identification: Because they have a vast knowledge base, LLMs might 
recall historical parallels or seldom-discussed risks. For example, an LLM might 
warn you, “Company Y relies on a key supplier in Taiwan — any escalation of 
conflict there could hurt its supply chain.” That’s a risk factor you might not have 
been aware of. In essence, they can function as a very diligent analyst who 
always asks, “But what if X happens?” 


Crucially, we use LLMs as advisors, not oracles. An Al model does not have a crystal 
ball. It predicts outcomes based on patterns in its training data and inputs, but it 
doesn’t truly know what will happen next quarter. Treating an LLM’s answer as gospel - 
especially in a complex domain like stock investing — would be a big mistake. As we’ve 
learned, even the smartest models can occasionally produce confident nonsense (in 
Al jargon, hallucinations). They might misquote facts or cite events that never 
happened. They can also be biased or inconsistent: phrase the question differently 
and you might get a different answer. Therefore, our stance is to never trust a single 
Al’s output blindly. 


Instead, think of a general LLM as a very knowledgeable but somewhat fallible research 
assistant. It’s like having an intern who has read everything but doesn’t always filter 
truth from fiction perfectly. One personal finance blogger described modern Als as “a 
really smart, lazy intern” — quick and broadly knowledgeable, but prone to cutting 
corners|4]. This intern can deliver great insights, but you (the human) are the supervisor 
who must double-check and guide their work. As Bob Lai, an investor who uses 
ChatGPT, Claude, and other Al tools, put it: “/ want to make sure a human (i.e. me) is 
behind the final decision-making process.”[5]| In practice, this means using Al to inform 
and challenge your thinking, while you remain responsible for the judgment calls. 


One of the best ways to guard against the limitations of any single Al model is simple: 
consult more than one. This brings us to the idea of the LLM Council. 


The LLM Council: Many Als, Many Perspectives 


If one Al model is like an opinionated expert, multiple Al models are like a panel of 
experts. Each has its own “personality” and knowledge biases — shaped by different 
training data and algorithms. By using several in tandem, we can compare their 
answers and see where there’s consensus versus controversy. This method takes 
inspiration from ensemble learning in machine learning and even the age-old wisdom of 
getting a “second opinion.” 


Tech visionary Andrej Karpathy (former Al director at Tesla) recently demonstrated a 
system called LLM Council that embodies this principle. In his prototype, instead of 
asking one modela hard question, you ask a group of models and let them debate. The 
process works in three stages: First, your query is sent to multiple LLMs in parallel, and 
you receive all their individual answers side by side[6]. Next, the models are shown 
each other’s answers (anonymously) and asked to review and rank the responses, 


critiquing which seems most accurate or insightful[7]. Finally, a designated “chairman” 
model takes all the inputs and compiles a single consensus answer for you[8][9]. It’s 
like having a committee where everyone speaks, they all double-check each other’s 
work, and then the chair summarizes the meeting. 


For our purposes, we don’t need to set up complex software to get the benefit of an Al 
council. We can do a manual LLM Council that’s almost as powerful: 


e Choose 2 or 3 LLMs to be your council members. For example, you might use 
ChatGPT (GPT-4.0 or newer), Claude, and Google’s Gemini. These are all general 
models with broad knowledge. Many are accessible through web interfaces or 
apps. (If you only have access to one Al model, you can even simulate a 
“council” by running multiple separate chats with it and prompting it to take 
different perspectives or by using different temperature settings. However, using 
different models is better for diversity of thought.) 


e Give them the same brief. You’ll prepare a structured prompt (we’ll show you 
how) describing the investment situation and what you want to know. Then you’ll 
copy-paste that prompt to each model in separate chat windows or browser 
tabs. Think of it as interviewing three candidates for the role of your financial co- 
pilot. 


e Compare their answers. Once all models have responded, you can read them 
side by side. You’ll notice areas where they agree (those are likely points of high 
confidence) and areas where they diverge (those highlight uncertainty or 
differences in interpretation). The differences are actually very valuable — they 
pinpoint where you should focus your own critical thinking or further research. 


e Foster a debate (optional). If you want to take it a step further, you can literally 
have the Als comment on each other’s answers. For instance, if Model A said 
something that Model B didn’t mention, you can ask Model B, “What do you think 
about the concern Model A raised about regulatory risk? Do you agree or 
disagree, and why?” By cross-examining them, you emulate that Stage 2 of 
Karpathy’s system - each Al reviewing the others. This often reveals even more 
insights, or it might cause one model to catch an error made by another. In 
essence, you’re making the Als explain and defend their thinking. 


The LLM Council approach ensures that you never rely on one Al’s opinion in 
isolation. Just as you wouldn’t build an investment thesis based solely on one analyst’s 
view, you shouldn’t do so with one Al model’s output either. With a council, it becomes 
much easier to spot Al mistakes or hallucinations: if one model fabricates a fact, 
usually the others will omit or contradict it, raising a red flag. Likewise, if all three 
independently highlight the same key factor (say, interest rates or anew competitor), 
you can be fairly confident that factor is important. 


In summary, multiple Als > one Al for complex decision support[10][11]. By 
synthesizing the “best thinking” of several models, we get a richer and more reliable 
perspective[12][13]. And importantly, the process remains interactive and 


transparent - you get to see the reasoning play out in plain language, and you can dig 
deeper into any part of it. 


Next, let’s talk about how to actually prepare the material and questions for your Al 
council to chew on. 


Building Your Stock Dossier for the Council 


Before convening your Al council, you need to provide it with the context and data 
about the investment decision at hand. Remember, an LLM (even GPT-5 or Claude 4.5) 
doesn’t innately know what stock you’re interested in or what’s been happening with it 
recently — you have to tell it the relevant info (or give it sources to read). This is where 
creating a concise “dossier” on the stock or ETF is invaluable. Think of it as the briefing 
packet you’d give to a group of human analysts before a meeting. 


Your dossier can be in plain text form, and it should include the following components: 


12. Basic Information: Start with the essentials — the company or ETF name, its 
ticker symbol, and a one-liner on what it does. For a stock, note the 
industry/sector and any unique roles (e.g. “Tesla (TSLA) - an electric vehicle and 
clean energy company, and a leader in autonomous driving R&D”). For an ETF, 
describe its focus (e.g. “ARKK — an innovation-focused ETF with heavy exposure 
to tech and biotech”). If it’s important, mention the company’s size (mega-cap 
blue chip vs. small-cap) or global reach (US vs international sales). 


13. Key Price History & Chart Highlights: Summarize the major movements in the 
stock’s price over the last several years (or relevant period) and tie them to 
events. You don’t need to recite every quarterly move — focus on the big swings 
and why they happened. This is applying your Chapter 5-6 skills. For example: 
“Stock X doubled in 2079 after launching its flagship product, crashed 50% in the 
2020 pandemic selloff, then rose 300% from 2020-2021 during the stimulus- 
fueled tech boom. It came back down 60% in 2022 due to rising interest rates 
and slowing growth.” By doing this, you are essentially briefing the Al on what the 
major “parameters” and past catalysts for this stock have been. Include any 
famous episodes: a big merger, a scandal, a leadership change, inclusion ina 
major index, etc., along with the stock’s reaction to each. This helps the Al 
understand the context and volatility of the asset. 


14. Current Macro Environment: Briefly describe the backdrop against which this 
stock is trading (from your Chapter 7 insights). Is the overall market in a bull 
phase, a bear phase, or uncertain? What’s happening with interest rates, 
inflation, or recession risk? Mention relevant macro factors: e.g. “We’re in late 
2025, interest rates are high (~5%) as central banks fought inflation, and there’s 
concern about a mild recession in the next 6 months. The broader market is 
choppy, with tech stocks recently under pressure due to geopolitical tensions.” 
Also note any major geopolitical or global issues that form the backdrop (wars, 
trade disputes, pandemics, etc. — unfortunately there’s always something). This 
macro layer is important because it influences all stocks to some degree, and 
the Al should factor it in when imagining scenarios. 


15. Sector and Industry Outlook: Zoom in one level from the macro to the sector or 
theme of this stock (drawing on Chapter 8’s “atlas of the future”). Summarize 
how the sector is doing and what the narrative is. For example, if it’s a 
semiconductor stock: “The semiconductor sector saw a boom in 2021, a glut in 
2022, and is now rebounding due to Al-driven chip demand. Key sector drivers 
include data center expansion and automotive chips, but there’s a risk from US- 
China tech restrictions.” If it’s an EV stock: “EV adoption continues to grow, but 
competition is fierce and profit margins are thin due to price wars. Battery costs 
and government subsidies are key factors.” List sector parameters (the ongoing 
forces) and flip factors (events that could change the trajectory). For instance: 
“A potential sector flip factor: a breakthrough in battery technology (bullish) or a 
new environmental regulation that hurts EV credits (bearish).” This sector context 
helps the Al not view the stock in isolation, but as part of a bigger story. 


16. Your Hypothesis (Optional): If you have a tentative thesis or leaning about this 
investment, you can include a brief note about it. For instance: “/’m considering 
buying because | think the market underestimates the company’s upcoming 
product launch,” or “I’m worried and considering selling because the CEO’s 
recent controversy might damage the brand.” This is optional - sometimes you 
might prefer not to “lead” the Al and instead see what it concludes on its own. 
But adding your angle can make the Al address your specific concerns or 
expectations (it will often explicitly confirm or refute your hypothesis in its 
analysis, which is useful). If you do include this, keep it to one or two sentences 
so it doesn’t overly bias the Al’s analysis. 


Your dossier doesn’t have to be long — aim for about a paragraph on each of the above 
points, maybe a bit more for the price history if there’s a lot to cover. In total, perhaps 

half a page to a page of text. The act of writing this dossier is partly for your own clarity 
too: it forces you to articulate what you know about the investment in a structured way. 


Tip: You can compile this dossier manually from your own knowledge, notes, 
and a bit of research. Alternatively, you can ask one LLM to help draft it (e.g. 
“Summarize the key events in Stock X’s history and its current situation”), but 
be sure to fact-check anything it produces. Often, a hybrid approach works: 
gather raw info yourself (from news or the company’s filings) and then have an 
LLM help summarize or organize it. 


Once the dossier is ready, you are prepared to write the Council Prompt that will be 
given to each Al model. 


Crafting a Structured Council Prompt 


To get the most useful output from your Al council, it’s important to ask your questions 
inaclear, detailed, and structured way. A good prompt will ensure the models 
understand the context and know exactly what kind of analysis you want. Here, we’ll 
outline a reusable template for a council prompt that you can adapt for any stock or 
ETF. 


Key elements to include in your prompt: 


e Context Information: Start by feeding the Al your dossier. You can literally copy 
the key points from the dossier into the prompt. It often helps to prefix this with 
something like, “Here is background information about [Stock/ETF]” to signal 
that you’re providing context. Use bullet points or short paragraphs for each 
aspect (basic info, price history/events, macro, sector, etc.), so it’s organized and 
easy for the Al to parse. 


e The Task/Questions: After the background, explicitly ask the Al to perform the 
analysis. Break the request into numbered steps or questions. This helps the 
model structure its answer. We recommend asking for something along these 
lines (you can tweak the wording): 


Council Prompt Template: You can copy and customize this for each of your Al 
models: 


Background: 

— Asset: [Name, Ticker]. [One-line description of the company or ETF]. 

— Price History & Key Events: [A summary of major price movements and the 
events that drove them, over the past few years. ] 

— Macro Context: [Current market/macro conditions relevant to this asset.] 

— Sector Context: [Sector trends, key drivers, and potential flip factors for the 
industry. ] 

— My Perspective (optional): [Your own hypothesis or concern, if any, e.g. 
“?’m bullish because... / I’m worried about...”] 


Questions: 

1. Restate & Verify Understanding: Please summarize the current situation 
of [Asset] in your own words, to ensure you understand the context. (Include 
the company’s recent performance and the macro/sector backdrop in a few 
sentences.) 

2. Scenario Analysis (3-6 months): Develop three possible scenarios for 
[Asset] over the next 3-6 months - one bullish (upside case), one base case 
(most likely or status quo), and one bearish (downside case). For each 
scenario: 

— Describe the key events or factors that would drive that scenario (e.g. 
specific catalysts, macro conditions, company actions, etc.). 

— Give a rough probability for the scenario (e.g. low/medium/high likelihood, or 
a percentage if you must — but keep it approximate). 

— Estimate the potential price change or range for the stock in that scenario 
(e.g. “up ~20%” or “down to around $X per share”). 

3. Key Risks and Flip Factors: List the main risks or wildcards that could 
change the outlook for [Asset]. These might be things that could make a bull 
case fail or a bear case avoidable. Essentially, what variables should an 
investor watch most closely? 

4. Reasons to Avoid Action: Are there any reasons an investor might want to 
stay on the sidelines and neither buy nor sell [Asset] right now? (For example, 
high uncertainty, lack of confidence in the analysis, or better opportunities 
elsewhere.) 

5. Conclusion - Your Recommendation: Based on the above, if you (the Al) 


had to make a call, what would you suggest a small investor do regarding 
[Asset] for a ~3-6 month horizon? Choose one of: “Buy (or add) a position,” 
“Hold (no action),” or “Sell (or reduce exposure)”, and explain your 
reasoning in a couple of sentences, considering the scenarios and risks. 
Please be clear and use plain language for this part. 


A promptlike this might seem long, but it ensures the Al provides a thorough, 
structured analysis rather than a shallow answer. We are basically asking the model to 
write an analyst-style report covering understanding, scenarios, risk assessment, anda 
reasoned conclusion. 


A few tips when using the prompt: 


e Use the same prompt for each model. Consistency helps you compare their 
answers fairly. If you tweak the prompt for one model because you forgot 
something, make the same tweak for the others. 


e Be aware of token limits: Very long prompts (thousands of words) might get cut 
off by some Al interfaces. If your dossier is extremely long, you might need to 
summarize it a bit more. However, the suggested length (a page or so of 
background + the questions) is usually fine for modern models. 


e Clarity and Neutrality: Notice we asked for bull, base, bear scenarios — this 
forces the Al to consider multiple outcomes, not just whichever way the wind is 
blowing. We also explicitly ask for reasons one might avoid doing anything — this 
invites the model to critique the whole idea of trading that stock, which can be 
healthy if enthusiasm is blinding us to risks. The prompt is written to be neutral 
and exploratory, not biased toward a bullish or bearish answer from the start. 


e Ask for plain language in the conclusion: This is to make sure the Al doesn’t 
give you a totally hedged, jargon-filled non-answer. We want a clear 
recommendation, but backed by the nuanced scenario discussion above. (Of 
course, we don’t blindly follow the recommendation — but it’s useful to see what 
the Al would do if forced to pick.) 


Once you’ve crafted this prompt with your specific asset’s info, you’re ready to run your 
Al council session. 


Running the Council: Analyzing and Comparing Al Advice 


Now comes the exciting part: seeing what the Al models have to say. Suppose you have 
opened three Al tools — say, ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini (as an example trio). You 
paste the structured prompt into each and await their responses. You will then have in 
front of you three analyses of the same situation. Here’s how to make the most of them: 


1. Read each response fully and take notes. Treat each Al’s answer like the report of a 
different human analyst. You might even label them in your notes as “Analyst A, B, C” 
(or by model name). Note the scenarios they described and the rationale behind their 
conclusions. Jot down key points: What did each identify as the main bullish drivers? 


Main risks? What probabilities or confidence did they express? Also note the tone: is 
one model clearly more optimistic or more pessimistic than the others? 


2. Identify Areas of Agreement. If all (or 2 out of 3) models converge on a similar 
outlook, that’s significant. For example, if every model’s bearish scenario mentions the 
same risk -— say, “a recession causing demand to drop” — then you know that risk is 
widely recognized and should be taken seriously. Or if both Model A and Model B’s base 
case see modest growth ahead, there’s a tentative consensus on the direction. 
Agreements can tell you where the “middle of the road” likely lies. Often the council will 
agree on broad things (e.g., “Macro conditions will heavily influence this stock” or 
“Competition is a key factor”). These common points are usually the obvious but 
important ones — the things any informed observer (Al or human) would notice. 


3. Identify Areas of Disagreement. Pay close attention to where the models diverge, 
because there lies the opportunity for deeper insight. For instance, Model A might say: 
“Regulatory approval of the company’s new drug is likely, so I’m bullish,” whereas 
Model B might say: “/’m not convinced the new drug will get approved; that’s a 
downside risk.” Or one model might highlight a factor the others glossed over: “Model C 
brought up a recent management scandal as a risk, whereas A and B didn’t mention it.” 
These discrepancies highlight uncertainties in the situation —- aspects where 
reasonable analysts (or Als) can come to different conclusions. Each disagreement is a 
prompt for you to investigate further or to question the models more. 


4. Cross-Examine the Models (the Debate): Once you see an interesting disagreement 
or unique point, you can loop back to the models for clarification. For example, you 
could go to ChatGPT and say: “Another Al analysis suggested that a new competitor in 
China could steal market share from this company — you didn’t mention competition in 
your answer. Do you see that as a risk? Why or why not?” A good Al will then reflect and 
perhaps say, “Yes, that’s a fair point, Chinese competition (e.g., BYD in EVs) could 
indeed pressure margins, even though! focused more on tech factors earlier.” 
Similarly, you could ask the more bearish model: “Model X was more optimistic about 
this factor — is there evidence to support that optimism, or do you think they’re 
overlooking something?” By doing this one-on-one with each model, you essentially 
create a dialogue. It’s like you went back to each analyst and said, “Hey, Bob said 
something different — care to respond?” This often leads the models to refine their views 
or acknowledge conditions under which the other’s point would matter. Sometimes, a 
model might double down and provide counter-evidence for why it disagrees — which is 
also useful. Through this process, you’re drilling down into the contentious points to 
see if they are deal-breakers or just differences in emphasis. 


5. Synthesize the Insights — Find the “Meta-Consensus.” After any follow-up rounds, 
step back and consider everything you’ve learned. What’s the worst-case scenario 
that at least one model made you aware of? What’s the best-case scenario that was 
painted? Do you personally feel one is more plausible? What are the common risks 
everyone noted (those should perhaps be weighted more heavily, since multiple 
sources flagged them)? And importantly, did the exercise reveal any unknowns you 
need to research more before acting? For example, if one model’s argument hinges on 
an upcoming product launch date that you’re unsure about, you might check the news 
or company filings to confirm that detail. 


At this stage, you might notice patterns like: 


e All three models think the stock is likely to be flat-to-up modestly (base case of 
slight growth), and none presented a really convincing bull case beyond that. 
This suggests expectations should be tempered — maybe the upside is limited 
unless new information comes. 


e One model (say, Claude) was extremely pessimistic, predicting a 30% drop in the 
bear case due to a specific risk, whereas the others’ bear cases were milder. You 
need to judge: is Claude onto something real (maybe a niche issue the others 
underplayed)? Or is it being too dour relative to mainstream opinion? You might 
decide to dig more into that risk or simply note it as an outlier viewpoint. 


e ModelA and B both recommended “hold/no action,” citing high uncertainty, 
whereas Model C said “buy a small position” citing a specific catalyst. That tells 
you most “opinions” lean toward caution, but there is a bull argument if a certain 
catalyst pans out. You could decide to wait until that catalyst (e.g., an earnings 
report or a product release) gives more clarity. 


The beauty of this council method is that even when it doesn’t give a clear “Yes, buy 
now!” answer (and often it won’t), it clarifies the picture. It surfaces the key variables 
and disagreements. In investing, clarity about what you know and don’t knowis half the 
battle. Many times, the council might actually convince you not to trade a particular 
stock because the situation is too uncertain or balanced — and that is a perfectly valid, 
often wise outcome. Remember, “no action” is a decision too. 


6. Make a Decision (or Decide Not To). Finally, using the council’s input plus your own 
judgment, you’ll choose an action: buy, hold, sell, or skip. The Al models will have given 
their notional recommendations, but you weigh those along with your personal 
conviction and risk tolerance. For example, if all models were cautiously optimistic and 
you also feel positive, you might initiate a small position, knowing what the downside 
risks are. If they were split and you’re unsure, you might hold off —- perhaps you’ll say, 
“Pll wait a month and see if some of these uncertainties resolve (like that regulatory 
decision or earnings release) before | reconsider.” If they all flashed warning signs and 
your gut was bullish, you need to seriously ask yourself if you’ve been blinded by bias — 
maybe stand back and cool off, or at least reduce how much you were going to invest 
until more data comes in. 


To illustrate how this works in practice, let’s walk through a concrete example of an 
LLM council session for a well-known stock. 


Example: A Tesla Council Session 


Imagine an investor (maybe you) is considering what to do about Tesla (TSLA) in the 
current environment. Tesla is a company many people are familiar with, and it’s a stock 
known for its volatility and passionate bulls and bears — making it a great example to 
test an Al council. 


Step 1: Prepare the Dossier. Here’s a condensed version of what our Tesla dossier 
might look like: 


Asset: Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). A leading electric vehicle (EV) manufacturer and 
renewable energy company, led by CEO Elon Musk. Market cap is large-cap, and 
it’s one of the most followed tech stocks. 


Price History & Key Events: Tesla’s stock has been on a roller coaster. It 
famously soared throughout 2020 (up roughly 700% that year[14]) as EV hype 
built and Tesla was added to the S&P 500 index (December 2020). In 2021 it hit 
all-time highs amid a tech bull market. However, 2022 was brutal - the stock 
price collapsed by about 65-70% from its peak by the end of 2022/15]. Reasons 
included: Elon Musk selling shares and focusing on his Twitter acquisition, rising 
interest rates hurting growth stocks, and concern that Tesla’s growth was 
slowing. In 2023, the stock rebounded significantly from its early-2023 lows 
(~$100) to around $250-300 by late 2023, as the market recovered and Tesla 
announced strong sales and an Al angle (optimism about self-driving and 
robotics). It’s still below the absolute peak, but volatility remains high. Big recent 
events: the launch of the Cybertruck (late 2023, with production ramp in 2024), 
ongoing price cuts on Tesla models to defend market share, and Musk’s ever- 
erratic public persona which sometimes impacts the stock. 


Macro Context: It’s late 2025. The global economy is uncertain — there’s been 
talk of a mild recession as central banks kept interest rates high to tame 
inflation. Interest rates are indeed high (the U.S. Fed rate in the ~5% range), 
which makes car loans more expensive and can dampen auto sales, especially 
for pricier EVs. Oil prices have fluctuated, but there’s a general transition to EVs 
still underway. Geopolitically, there’s tension (trade issues between the U.S. and 
China, war risks) that could affect supply chains or market sentiment at any 
time. Overall stock market sentiment is cautious; we’re notin a raging bull 
market like 2021, but it’s not a full bear crash either - it’s choppy and news- 
driven. 


Sector Context (EV/Auto/Al): The EV sector is competitive and dynamic. Tesla is 
the market leader in EVs by profits, but competitors like traditional automakers 
(GM, Ford, VW) and newer players (Rivian, Lucid, and especially Chinese 
companies like BYD) are all vying for market share. Key sector parameters: 
battery costs (lithium and other material prices), government policies (EV 
subsidies, emissions regulations), and technology (range and performance 
improvements, autonomous driving tech). In 2023-2025, we’ve seen price wars — 
Tesla cut prices on its cars to stoke demand, which pressured margins but also 
put strain on competitors. A major theme is autonomous driving and AI: Tesla’s 
Full Self-Driving (FSD) software is in beta, and Musk claims robotaxis are coming 
“soon” (though he’s missed such timelines before). Tesla also has an emerging 
energy storage business and even a humanoid robot prototype (Optimus), which 
tie into the broader Al/robotics narrative. Sector flip factors: a breakthrough in 
battery technology (could greatly lower costs — bullish for Tesla if they lead it), or 
conversely a battery supply shortage or new tariff (could hurt production — 
bearish). Another flip factor: regulatory changes — e.g., if a government mandates 
EV adoption faster (bullish for all EV makers), or if there’s a safety recall or 
regulation on self-driving tech (could be bearish, especially for Tesla which 


pushes autonomy aggressively). Also, macro flip: if interest rates drop quickly, it 
could reignite growth stock fervor (bullish for Tesla); if a severe recession hits, 
auto sales could plummet (bearish). 


My Perspective: (Hypothetical investor’s stance) I’m on the fence. | feel Tesla 
has long-term potential in Al and energy, but in the next 6 months | worry that 
high interest rates and increasing competition might hurt its sales. Essentially, 
I’m not sure if | should buy more on dips, or stay away due to the risks. 


Now we take this dossier and plug it into our prompt structure for each model. 


Step 2: Ask the Council. We give the structured prompt (as outlined earlier) to 
ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini, each in their own chat. Let’s summarize what each one 
comes back with (for brevity, we’ll outline their key points rather than full verbatim 
responses): 


Model A (ChatGPT) Response: After restating the context (it correctly notes 
Tesla’s huge 2020 rise, 2022 fall, and the current macro environment), ChatGPT 
gives scenarios: 


Bullish scenario: It imagines that Tesla exceeds delivery expectations in the 
next two quarters, the Cybertruck rollout is successful and has a waitlist, and 
perhaps FSD gets a positive regulatory nod or tech breakthrough. It also 
assumes the economy avoids recession. Probability it gives is maybe ~30% 
(medium-low). Outcome: it speculates Tesla’s stock could rise by ~20-30% (for 
example, from say $250 to around $320) in this scenario. 


Base case: Tesla performs decently but not spectacularly. Deliveries grow 
modestly, margins are a bit squeezed from price cuts, and no major surprises. 
The macro stays status quo (sluggish but not collapsing). Probability ~50% 
(ChatGPT calls it likely). Stock might stay roughly range-bound or up slightly 
(maybe +10% over 6 months, hovering in the $250-$280 range). 


Bearish scenario: Acombination of disappointments — perhaps a recession hits 
cutting car demand, or Tesla has production issues ora recall. It also mentions 
competition, e.g. “Chinese EV makers or anew model from a rival undercuts 
Tesla’s market share in key segments.” Probability maybe 20% (low, but not 
negligible). It suggests the stock could drop by -30% or more in that case 
(perhaps down to ~$180 or below). 


Key risks/flip factors: ChatGPT lists: (1) macro recession or high rates hurting 
demand (agrees with our concern), (2) competition intensifying (mentions BYD, 
etc.), (3) Elon Musk’s actions (if he does something like sell more stock or gets 
into a scandal, that could hurt sentiment), (4) regulatory issues (like if there’s an 
accident that leads to self-driving restrictions). It also notes positive flip factors 
like a tech breakthrough or sudden policy boost for EVs. 


Reasons to avoid action: It says there is high uncertainty — the stock is known to 
swing widely, so timing a 3-6 month trade is tricky. It notes the valuation is still 
relatively high, so if the growth story wavers, downside could be large. Also, it 
mentions that so much hinges on unpredictable factors (Musk’s behavior, 
macroeconomy) that an investor might just wait for clarity. 


Conclusion: ChatGPT’s tone is moderately cautious. It concludes something 
like: “If | had to choose, | would hold (no immediate action). The upside scenario 
exists but is not guaranteed, and there are significant risks in the short term. It 
might make sense to keep a small position if you already believe in the long term, 
but not go heavy until there’s more certainty on the macro outlook and how 
Tesla’s new products perform.” In plain language, it’s basically saying: don’t rush 
in right now. 


Model B (Claude) Response: Claude restates the situation too (interestingly, it 
emphasizes Elon Musk’s role and the competitive landscape a lot in the 
summary). Its scenarios: 


Bullish: Claude imagines a stronger bull case than ChatGPT did. It posits 
something like: “Global EV adoption accelerates faster than expected in early 
2026, Tesla’s sales in China and Europe jump after a new subsidy, and its Al 
initiatives (maybe the Tesla Robot or FSD) impress investors.” It also speculates 
Elon Musk might announce a stock buyback or something financially positive. 
Probability it gives might be around 20% (lowish). In this scenario, Claude thinks 
the stock could soar, maybe +40% (given Tesla’s volatility, it’s not shy to suggest 
a big move if optimism returns — e.g., from $250 to $350+). 


Base case: Tesla keeps on trucking with steady growth but also significant 
spending. Claude notes margins might keep shrinking due to competitive 
pricing, but volume sales grow. It also mentions that investor sentiment might 
remain mixed. Probability ~50-60%. It sees the stock perhaps roughly flat or 
modestly up (like 0 to +15% range) by mid-2026. 


Bearish: Claude highlights one particular risk: “Tesla could face a serious recall 
or reputational hit if any issue arises with the new Cybertruck or FSD accidents.” 
It also underscores the possibility that Musk’s involvement in other ventures 
(Twitter/X or whatever new thing) could distract him further. Additionally, it says 
if the Fed doesn’t cut rates and auto loans stay expensive, demand could fall off. 
Probability ~30%. Outcome: stock down by 20-30% or more, maybe even 
returning to ~$170s. 


Key risks/flip factors: Claude’s list has overlap with ChatGPT (economy, 
competition, Musk), but it adds a nuanced one: battery supply and raw 
materials. It notes that if lithium prices spike again or if there’s a supply chain 
disruption, Tesla’s production could be constrained or costs up. It also mentions 
a flip factor: if a major competitor (like Apple, hypothetically, or a big traditional 
automaker) launches a very compelling EV or autonomous system, it could 
change the game. 


Reasons to avoid: Claude explicitly says Tesla’s stock is very volatile and “priced 
for perfection,” meaning a lot of good news is already expected. It warns that the 
next 6 months could be a “coin flip” and that an average investor might not have 
an edge here. It also mentions the broader market risk —if the market corrects, 
Tesla tends to drop more than average because it’s high-beta. 

Conclusion: Claude leans slightly more optimistic than ChatGPT, interestingly. It 
might say: “/ would lean towards a small buy or add a bit to a position, only if you 
have high risk tolerance and believe in Tesla’s long-term vision. Otherwise, 


holding is reasonable. The council of factors considered indicates moderate 
upside potential but with high volatility.” In other words, it’s a bit more bullish that 
the upside scenario (or at least some upside) will materialize, but it couches that 
with many conditions. 


Model C (Gemini) Response: Gemini’s style is concise. It summarizes Tesla’s 
context focusing on numbers (it mentions, for example, Tesla’s delivery growth 
percentage and the fact that 2022 drop was linked to Musk’s Twitter saga). 
Scenarios: 


Bullish: Gemini emphasizes macro improvement: it says if inflation drops and 
interest rates start coming down sooner than expected, growth stocks like Tesla 
could rally broadly. It also notes Tesla might unveil better-than-expected 
earnings or a surprise new product. Probability ~25%. Stock could go up ~25%. 


Base: lt assumes things go as currently projected: Tesla grows ~+30% in 
deliveries (just an example number it gave) which is decent but not shocking, 
and margins are stable. Probability 50%. Stock perhaps +10% or so. 


Bearish: Gemini’s bear case is heavily macro-driven: “/f the economy enters a 
recession, discretionary purchases like cars (especially expensive EVs) could 
drop sharply. Tesla might have to cut prices further, hurting profits.” It also notes 
any geopolitical shock (like tariff on U.S. cars in China or similar) could hurt. 
Probability 25%. Stock down maybe -20%. 


Risks/flip factors: Gemini’s list is straightforward: (1) Economy/recession, (2) 
Competition (it names a couple of competitors, let’s say it mentioned BYD and 
Ford’s EV division), (3) Execution issues (factory shutdowns, production delays), 
and (4) Musk’s leadership (if he does something like unexpectedly leaves CEO 
role or gets into trouble). A flip factor it adds: currency exchange rates — since 
Tesla sells globally, a strong dollar could hurt overseas revenue (a detail neither 
AnorB brought up). 


Reasons to avoid: Gemini points out that Tesla’s valuation is high relative to 
traditional automakers (like its P/E ratio is much higher), which could be a 
reason to avoid if one is value-conscious. It also notes the political risk: Tesla 
(and Musk) occasionally attract political scrutiny, which could introduce 
headline risk. It basically says if you’re not comfortable with high volatility, you 
might avoid trading Tesla on a short horizon. 


Conclusion: Gemini’s conclusion is balanced: “/f / had to decide, | would hold off 
on big moves. The next 3-6 months carry as much downside risk as upside 
potential for Tesla. A prudent approach might be to keep only a modest position 
(if any) until we see clearer signs of economic trend or company performance.” 
Essentially a neutral stance. 


Step 3: Compare the Council’s Answers. In this Tesla scenario, what do we see? 


General Agreement: All three models acknowledge high uncertainty and high 
volatility. None of them gave a slam-dunk “you must buy now” or “sell 
immediately” — they allhad nuances. Common threads: 


Macro matters: Every model made the macroeconomic situation (interest rates, 
recession risk) a central part of the analysis, especially for the downside. So 
clearly, the council agrees that how the economy goes in the next few months 
will heavily influence Tesla’s stock. As an investor, that tells us: keep an eye on 
Fed decisions, inflation reports, etc., because they’re as important as Tesla’s 
own news. 


Competition and execution are risks: All models mentioned the competitive 
landscape or execution (production, recalls) as concerns. This reinforces that 
Tesla doesn’t operate in a vacuum; what rivals do could impact it, and Tesla’s 
own ability to execute new launches (like the Cybertruck) is crucial. 


Musk factor: Interestingly, each model in some way mentioned Elon Musk — 
either his distractions (Twitter) or if he sells more shares or does something 
unpredictable. This is a real-world factor (we know Musk’s antics have affected 
Tesla stock in the past) and the Als picked up on it. So that’s another area of 
agreement: the human element at the top could swing investor sentiment. 


Bull/Base/Bear spread: All models see a plausible bull case and a plausible 
bear case — meaning the range of outcomes is wide. None said “the worst that 
can happen is just a small dip” — they all see substantial downside in a bad 
scenario (20-30% drop). Similarly, all see some upside in a bull case (20%+ 
gain). So they agree it’s a high-beta situation with a wide cone of outcomes. 
That’s a caution sign if you’re risk-averse. 


Key Differences: There were some differences in tone and emphasis: 


Optimism vs. Caution: Claude was a tad more optimistic about a bullish 
outcome than the others (it even flirted with the idea of adding a position, albeit 
cautiously). ChatGPT and Gemini were more on the fence or leaning 
hold/neutral. This could reflect differences in training data or personality of the 
models: maybe Claude “believes” more in Tesla’s tech narrative (or simply 
rolled a bit more optimistic in that run). As an investor, | note: one voice said 
“maybe buy a bit,” two said “probably hold.” It’s like two analysts advising 
caution and one saying “a speculative buy.” Depending on my own leaning, | 
might weigh one more, but it’s valuable to see that divergence. 


Specific Risks Highlighted: Claude brought up battery raw materials 
specifically; Gemini mentioned currency and political risks; ChatGPT 
emphasized regulatory/accident risk in self-driving. Each added a unique point. 
That gives me a checklist of things to consider: e.g., “Do | know how much China 
sales matter? Perhaps | should check Tesla’s revenue breakdown by region. 
What if China tensions rise?” Or “How dependent is Tesla on lithium pricing? 
Perhaps not short-term, but if | were holding long-term it’s a factor.” These 
details enrich my understanding. 


Catalyst focus: ChatGPT and Gemini had fairly mainstream catalysts 
(deliveries, economy, etc.), whereas Claude imagined a bit more of a dramatic 
catalyst (like an Al breakthrough or a stock buyback). This reminds me to weigh 
how likely each catalyst is. The more dramatic, the less likely perhaps. So | might 
internally downweight scenarios that require, say, Musk doing a buyback (he 
historically hasn’t done that yet) or a sudden huge tech leap. 


e Numbers and Probabilities: All gave rough probabilities but they weren’t 
identical. ChatGPT gave bull ~30%, Claude ~20%, Gemini ~25% (just from our 
summary). These are all ballpark and not scientific, but if one model had said 
“Bull case 60% likely!” that would stand out. None did that here; they all 
signaled bullis possible but not the base case. If one had dramatically different 
odds, that’s a case where I’d dig in and ask it why it’s so confident. In our case, 
they were roughly in line, which actually increases my confidence that base- 
case (moderate outcome) is indeed the most probable scenario. 


Step 4: Follow-up Questions (Debate): We decide to ask a couple of targeted follow- 
ups to each model based on differences: - We ask ChatGPT: “Claude’s analysis 
mentioned the risk of battery metal prices or supply affecting Tesla. You didn’t discuss 
that. Do you consider supply chain/raw material costs a significant factor for Tesla in 
the next 6 months?” ChatGPT responds acknowledging that lithium and other materials 
are indeed a factor for EV makers, and while it might not dominate the 3-6 month 
outlook as much as demand-side factors, a spike or shortage could certainly hurt 
Tesla’s production or costs. It thanks the prompt for raising that point and integrates it, 
saying something like “/ agree that if battery materials become a bottleneck or more 
expensive, it would tilt things bearish.” This follow-up confirms Claude’s point is valid; 
now ChatGPT and Claude both agree on it. Good to know. - We ask Claude: “Gemini 
pointed out Tesla’s high valuation relative to other car companies as a reason to be 
cautious. How do you view Tesla’s valuation? Is it justified by growth, or is it a risk 
factor?” Claude then elaborates that Tesla indeed has a much higher P/E than legacy 
automakers because investors price in growth and tech potential. It might say “/f Tesla 
stumbles or growth slows, the stock could fall a lot because the valuation leaves room 
for disappointment. This is a risk.” So Claude basically concurs that valuation is 
something to watch — if the company doesn’t live up to high expectations, the stock can 
drop disproportionately. Again, consensus building: now all models have effectively 
acknowledged that as well (since ChatGPT probably implicitly knew it, and Gemini 
stated it). - We ask Gemini: “ChatGPT was relatively concerned about Elon Musk’s 
focus (like the Twitter distraction) as a risk. Do you see Musk’s behavior or focus as a 
major factor for Tesla’s stock performance near-term?” Gemini replies that Musk’s 
leadership and statements certainly influence investor sentiment. It might say “Yes, if 
Musk were to, say, announce he’s selling stock or if he gets entangled in a controversy, 
it could hurt the stock in the short run. Conversely, if he refocuses on Tesla and delivers 
on promises, that could help.” So Gemini acknowledges the “Musk overhang.” Now 
we’ve cross-checked all these unique points. 


After these follow-ups, we’ve essentially gotten the models to converge a bit more or 
at least we fleshed out that none of them strongly disagrees with the others’ main 
concerns once asked. No model said, “No, that factor is irrelevant.” They mostly said, 
“Yes, that’s also a valid consideration.” This often happens - the initial answers differ in 
emphasis, but when probed, a holistic picture emerges that is richer than any single 
answer. 


Step 5: Decision Time in the Example. Given all this, what might our investor do about 
Tesla? In our hypothetical scenario, the council leaned towards caution. No one gave a 
green light without reservations. The potential upside exists but comes with significant 


risks and relies on positive macro conditions and flawless execution by Tesla. The 
downside scenarios, while not guaranteed, could be nasty (and two out of three models 
basically said “uncertainty is high, maybe hold”). 


If las the investor was leaning bullish initially (say | was a Tesla fan thinking “itll go up 
for sure”), the council’s tempered outlook might save me from overconfidence. | might 
decide: “Alright, I’ll not make a big buy now. Maybe I'll even trim a little of my position to 
reduce risk, or just hold what | have but not add more until | see things like the next 
earnings or macro data.” The council in this case serves as a reality check, highlighting 
the risks | might have downplayed. 


If | was leaning bearish (thinking to short or sell), the council did present a bull 
scenario that is plausible (maybe new subsidies or simply the market staying strong). 
So l’d be cautioned that betting against Tesla is also risky. | might instead decide: “/ 
won’t short it; ’ll just avoid taking a big position either way. Perhaps I'll revisit after the 
next Fed meeting or Tesla’s quarterly report clarifies some uncertainties.” 


In essence, the council session might lead to a “no action” or a very small tactical 
move, which is often prudent given the mixed outlook. The important outcome is that | 
now feel | have a much clearer grasp of the situation: | know exactly what to watch 
(Fed decisions, Tesla’s delivery numbers, competitor moves, Musk’s behavior, etc.), 
and | know the range of outcomes to mentally prepare for. 


This example underscores that the goal of using an Al councilis not to geta 
deterministic prediction, but to sharpen your own analysis. Whether the stock 
ultimately goes up or down, you’ll be less surprised because you’ve already 
contemplated those possibilities. 


Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them 


While the LLM Council approach is powerful, we must be mindful of pitfalls — both 
those inherent to Al models and those in how we use them. Here are some common 
issues and strategies to mitigate them: 


Pitfall 1: Hallucinations (Al Fabricating Information). LLMs sometimes make stuff 
up. They might confidently present a “fact” or reasoning that is just wrong. For example, 
an Al might erroneously say, “Tesla’s CFO resigned last month,” even if no such thing 
happened, simply because it misremembered or mixed up information. If you act ona 
non-existent piece of news, that’s dangerous. To guard against this: - Provide the Al 
with accurate data (your dossier) so it doesn’t have to guess. - Ask for sources or 
specifics: If an Al cites a fact that you didn’t provide (like a regulatory change ora 
financial metric), double-check it. You can literally ask, “When did that happen? Could 
you provide a source or more details?” Often, the Al might correct itself when pressed. - 
Cross-verify among models: One advantage of multiple models — if only one says 
something bizarre and the others don’t mention it, flag that item for verification. In our 
council example, if only one model claimed “Tesla’s CEO announced stepping down” 
and the others didn’t, you’d certainly investigate that before believing it. - Quick 
manual fact-check: Use the internet or a trusted data source to confirm key facts (like 


major news, P/E ratios, etc.) if they play a big role in the analysis. Don’t let a juicy- 
sounding tidbit go unchecked. 


Pitfall 2: Over-Focusing on Recent News (Recency Bias). Als are often trained on data 
up to a certain point and might overweight whatever recent info you give them, 
especially if your dossier heavily features the latest headline. Similarly, humans do this 
too — it’s easy to give too much importance to a recent earnings miss or yesterday’s 
news story. The risk is that short-term noise can cloud the bigger picture. - Safeguard: 
Always include a bit of historical perspective in your dossier/prompt (that’s why we 
include multi-year history of price moves). This reminds both you and the Al of longer- 
term patterns. - If you suspect the Al is latching onto one recent event too much, ask a 
follow-up like, “Do you think that [news item] has long-term significance or could it be a 
short-term overreaction?” This forces reflection. - Conversely, if the Al answers seem 
oblivious to something very recent (maybe their training cutoff missed it), you might 
need to explicitly mention it in your prompt (“Note: last week the company missed its 
sales target”) or check an updated source. Keep the context current. 


Pitfall 3: Prompt Bias (Leading the Witness). How you ask questions can bias the 
answers. If your prompt is too leading, you might just get your own opinion echoed 
back. For example, asking “Don’t you think Tesla will struggle because of rising 
competition and Musk’s issues?” will almost guarantee the Al paints a bearish picture 
(because you’ve framed it that way). This defeats the purpose of an objective analysis. - 
Safeguard: Keep the initial prompt as neutral and open-ended as possible. That’s why 
our template asks for bull and bear scenarios explicitly, to ensure both sides are 
considered. - If you have a hypothesis, it’s fine to mention it (as we did), but also ask, “/s 
this view justified?” or “What could prove this wrong?” so the Al examines it critically. - 
When doing follow-ups, don’t reveal your personal bias too strongly. Instead of “Model 
Ais clearly too optimistic, right?”, ask “Model A had a more optimistic take — what do 
you think about their points?” This way you’re not pushing the second model to just say 
“Yes you’re right, Ais wrong” without analysis. - Using multiple models inherently helps 
here too —- each model’s different style acts as a counterbalance to bias in the phrasing. 
If one model was subtly influenced by phrasing, others might give a counterpoint. 


Pitfall 4: False Precision and Overconfidence. Als can output nice-looking numbers 
or odds (e.g. “there’s a 72.5% chance of X happening”) which gives a misleading sense 
of certainty. In reality, such numbers are rough guesses at best. Also, the models might 
sound sure about things that are very uncertain. - Safeguard: Treat all numbers as 
educated estimates, not exact predictions. In our prompt, we specifically asked for 
rough probabilities like low/medium/high. It’s better to have the Al say “low chance” 
than “13.2% chance” which implies more precision than it can justify. - If an Al does 
give very specific figures, you can ask how it arrived at them. Often it’s just an intuition 
or based on past data patterns, not any hard calculation. - Remind yourself that even if 
all models say “we think there’s a high chance stock goes up 10%,” that doesn’t ensure 
it will happen. Reality can and will deviate. - Use the council’s output to understand 
directional risk/reward (e.g., upside vs downside potential) rather than to set exact 
price targets or probabilities. 


Pitfall 5: Over-Reliance on Al (Human in the Loop Failure). The biggest pitfall is if you 
start letting the Al council make your decisions for you. The whole ethos of this method 


is that the human remains the final decision-maker. If you ever find yourself saying 
“Well, | didn’t understand why, but all the Als said buy, so! bought,” then you’ve fallen 
into the trap we set out to avoid. - Safeguard: Always do a sanity check with your own 
knowledge and values. Ask yourself, “Do these Al-generated scenarios make sense to 
me? Is there something | know that the models didn’t mention?” If something feels off, 
investigate further. - Maintain healthy skepticism. Even if the council reaches a 
consensus, remember they could all be missing the same factor (garbage in, garbage 
out). For instance, if all models were trained largely on past data where a certain 
scenario never happened, they might collectively underestimate it. Your human 
imagination and awareness can sometimes see outside the Al box. - Diversify your 
input beyond Al: The council shouldn’t replace reading actual news, company reports, 
or expert opinions. It’s an assistant, not a guru. Use it to augment, not substitute, your 
research. For example, if the council heavily leans on a certain thesis, go verify that with 
a real-world source (maybe an article or the company’s press releases) before betting 
onit. - Keep position sizes and risk in check. If you’re trying something somewhat 
guided by Al insights, treat it like an experiment. Don’t bet the farm. Remember that 
even professional analysts and traders —- humans and algorithms alike — get things 
wrong often. With a short 3-6 month horizon especially, luck and unforeseeable events 
play a big role. 


Bonus Pitfall: Confirmation Bias via Al. This is a subtle one: If you really want to take a 
certain action, you might unconsciously manipulate the Al or interpret its words to 
reinforce your desire. For example, if you’re itching to buy Tesla, you might focus only 
onthe bullish parts of the council’s output and ignore the bearish parts, or you might 
keep re-prompting until you get a model to give you a strong “Yes, buy!” 
recommendation. This is dangerous — it’s basically using the Al as an echo chamber. - 
Safeguard: Be honest with yourself about your biases. If you notice you’re fishing for a 
certain answer, step back. Sometimes deliberately ask the Al to play devil’s advocate to 
your view: “Give me reasons my inclination to buy might be wrong.” Make sure you hear 
the uncomfortable side, not just what feels good. - In our example, if someone really 
wanted to buy, they could find an Al or a prompt phrasing to justify it - but that defeats 
the purpose. The council method works only if you allow it to challenge your 
assumptions. 


By being aware of these pitfalls, you can use the LLM council method in a way that truly 
enhances your decision-making rather than leading you astray. It’s about striking a 
balance: leverage the superhuman strengths of Al (breadth of knowledge, speed, 
scenario generation) while compensating for its weaknesses with your own human 
judgment and external verification. 


Step-by-Step Workflow Recap 


Let’s consolidate the process into a straightforward workflow you can follow whenever 
you want to analyze a potential investment using an Al council: 


1. Pick Your Asset: Choose the stock or ETF you want to evaluate. It should be 
something liquid and within the scope of your strategy (as discussed in earlier chapters, 
we’re focusing on larger stocks/ETFs, not penny stocks or ultra-complex derivatives). 


2. Create a Brief Dossier: Write down the key points about this asset (Company basics, 
price history with events, macro conditions, sector outlook, and your initial thoughts). 
Aim for a few paragraphs covering the essentials. This is your “briefing document.” 


3. Formulate the Council Prompt: Using the dossier, fill in the structured prompt 
template. Essentially, you will be giving the Al the background and asking for scenarios, 
risks, and a recommendation. Make sure the prompt is clear and neutral in tone. 


4. Run the Prompt by Multiple Als: Open two or three LLM interfaces (ChatGPT, 
Claude, Gemini, Bard, etc.). Paste the same prompt into each and submit. Label or 
note which response came from which model (to avoid confusion later). 


5. Analyze Each Al’s Response: Read them carefully. Perhaps even highlight or 
underline key parts: scenario outcomes, specific risks mentioned, the final 
recommendation. Make a quick list of common points vs unique points. 


6. Compare and Synthesize: On a sheet of paper or a note app, make two columns: 
“Agreements” and “Differences.” Fill these in based on the council’s answers. What did 
all models agree on (e.g., “macro risk high,” “bull case requires X”)? What were the 
discrepancies (“Model A: optimistic about tech, Model B: skeptical about tech”)? This 
step will clarify the consensus and the debate. 


7. Do Follow-up Queries (if needed): If there are differences that you want clarified, go 
back to each model and ask targeted questions. For instance, “Model B said something 
you didn’t mention — what’s your view on that?” This step is optional but very 
enlightening for complex situations. It can often bring the models’ views closer together 
or at least explain the rationale behind them. 


8. Consider an Action (Including No Action): Now, take a breath and ask yourself: 
given all this information, what should I do (if anything)? There are a few possible 
outcomes: - Strong Council Alignment (all leaning one way): Example: all models 
suggested the stock looks pretty good and risks seem moderate. While not guaranteed, 
that alignment might give you confidence to take a small position or at least put the 
stock on a “potential buy” list, assuming it fits your criteria. Conversely, if all screamed 
danger, you’d likely stay away or maybe even reduce any holdings you have. - Mixed 
Opinions / Uncertainty: If the council is split or heavily caveated (whichis common), 
the prudent move is often to hold off active trading. Consider simply keeping the stock 
on watch. Sometimes the best decision is no decision until more clarity emerges. You 
can note what key future events (earnings, economic data, etc.) might resolve the 
uncertainty and plan to revisit after those. - Insights Trigger Another Idea: 
Occasionally, the council might highlight a different opportunity or risk. For example, 
maybe in analyzing one stock, the Als mention that a whole sector is bullish. That might 
shift your focus to an ETF or another stock. Or you realize you should do a council 
session on acompetitor stock as well, to compare. - Document Your Decision 
Rationale: It’s good practice (for learning) to write a short paragraph summarizing: 
“Based on the Al council: Model A/B/C said XYZ. They mostly agreed on [positive 
factors] and [negative factors]. l’ve decided to [action] because [reasons].” This is like a 
mini investment journal entry. It will help you later evaluate how this process worked (or 


if something unexpected happened, you can see whether the council had considered 
that possibility). 


9. Monitor and Update: If you do take a position (or even if you just remain interested), 
keep an eye on the key factors the council identified. If a few months pass, you can 
even run an updated council session with new information. Perhaps one of the risks 
materialized — how do the Als update their scenarios now? The method is repeatable. 
Over time, you might not need to ask all the detailed questions again, maybe just 
specific follow-ups as new data comes. 


Finally, always integrate this tool within your broader investment plan. The council is 
one input. You might also be considering portfolio allocation, personal financial needs, 
etc., which the Als don’t know. Ensure your final decisions align with your overall risk 
management. 


Try It Yourself - A Hands-On Exercise 


To cement this knowledge, consider doing a practice run with the LLM council method 
on a stock or ETF of your choice (with play money or just as a thought experiment). For 
example: 


1. Pick a stock you’re interested in —- maybe one that’s been in the news or a big 
name you’ve considered investing in. 

2. Write a short dossier about it, covering the points we discussed. 

3. Use at least two different LLMs (if you only have one, use it twice with slightly 
varied prompts or styles) to analyze the stock with the structured prompt. 
Compare their answers. Note where they agree or differ. 

5. Formulate your own conclusion. Would you buy, sell, or do nothing? And why? 


Even if you ultimately do nothing, you’ll have practiced how to engage with Alina 
structured decision process. You'll likely learn something new about the asset, or even 
about how the Al thinks versus how you think. 


By doing this exercise, you’ll also get a feel for which models you trust more or find 
more useful. Each has its quirks —- one might be better at numerical analysis, another at 
qualitative insight. Using them together covers blind spots. 


Final Thoughts 


The rise of accessible Al tools is transforming how individuals can approach investing. 
Just a few years ago, having an on-demand panel of “experts” to brainstorm investment 
ideas with was unimaginable for the average person. Now, with a laptop or phone, we 
can summon multiple informed perspectives in seconds. This chapter showed you how 
to harness that power responsibly — by treating Als as advisors to debate and critique, 
not oracles to obey blindly. 


By using an LLM council, we strive to get the best of both worlds: the vast knowledge 
and pattern-recognition of Al, and the critical thinking and values of a human investor. 
This method aligns with the philosophy of this book: expecting the unexpected (“the 


end of the world” as a provocative metaphor for disruption), and preparing for it with 
clear-eyed analysis rather than hopeful guesses. Al won’t save us from market risks or 
make decisions for us — but if used wisely, it can help us see more clearly through the 
fog of uncertainty. 


As you integrate this into your investment routine, keep iterating and learning. You’ll get 
better at crafting prompts, at discerning when an Al answer seems off, and at 
synthesizing multiple inputs into a coherent view. In doing so, you’re not just potentially 
improving your investments; you’re also building a skill set that will be increasingly 
valuable in an Al-driven future: the skill of collaborating with artificial intelligence 
effectively. 


And finally, a gentle reminder: No matter how sophisticated our tools, investing always 
involves risk. Markets can prove any prediction wrong. Use the council to illuminate 
possibilities, but always make sure you are comfortable with the decision and the 
potential downside if things go against you. This book, and this chapter, aim to educate 
and guide, but they are not personalized financial advice. Always consider your own 
financial situation and, if needed, consult with a professional advisor before making 
major investment moves. 


By staying inquisitive, skeptical, and informed — with a team of Als by your side but not 
running the show - you’ll be well-equipped to navigate whatever the financial future 
holds, even if it feels like “the end of the world.” 


Happy (and savvy) investing, and welcome to the new age of the Al-augmented investor.
