investment idea comes along, you need cash to buy in. If you’re 100% invested 
all the time, you might be forced to sell something (possibly at a bad time) to free 
up money for the new idea. In a fast-moving “end times” market, you want to be 
able to act quickly on new information or scenarios. 


e Emotional buffer: Holding some cash can make it easier to stick with your 
strategy on the invested portion. If you know you have a safety cushion, you’re 
less likely to be terrified by each dip in your portfolio. That means you can think 
more rationally and not make panicky decisions. In times of crisis or uncertainty, 
peace of mind is a valuable asset in its own right. 


Cons of holding cash: 


e Inflation and lost potential gains: In normal times, people caution against 
holding too much cash because inflation slowly eats away at it. For example, if 
inflation is 5% per year and you hide cash under your mattress, in a year that 
cash buys 5% less stuff. Over just a few months, this is a small factor, but it’s not 
zero — especially if inflation is running hot. Also, if markets take off upward while 
you sit in cash, you miss those gains. There is a cost to being out of the market. 


e No growth: Cash might keep its face value, but it doesn’t grow onits own. A 
diversified investment might earn dividends or interest or just appreciate in 
price. Cash just sits there. In a short-horizon strategy, this is fine - we’re not 
expecting to leave things for decades — but it’s worth remembering that cash is 
more of a parking spot than an engine of returns. 


e False safety if the system fails completely: This is a bit philosophical, but in an 
absolute worst-case scenario (like a complete financial system collapse), cash 
in the bank might not save you — its value could evaporate or you might not be 
able to access it. However, our strategy isn’t about doomsday prepping for total 
collapse; it’s about navigating markets in a tumultuous time. For our 3-6 month 
purposes, cash is still king for safety and optionality. 


Guidance for using cash: We recommend always keeping a cash buffer as part of your 
allocation. The exact percentage can vary with your comfort level and the scenario -— it 
might be 5% for an aggressive stance or 20% (or more) if you’re very uneasy about the 
near future. The key is, don’t feel like you must always be 100% invested. Ina 
potentially fast-changing world, having some money on the sidelines is not a drag —it’s 
a tactical advantage. When you do invest in stocks or ETFs, you’ll do so with more 


confidence knowing that not all your money is riding on those bets. And if nothing in the 
market looks attractive for a while, there’s nothing wrong with holding extra cash until 
clarity emerges. In short, cash is your flexibility fund. 


Peripheral Asset Classes: Bonds and Commodities 


Beyond stocks, ETFs, and cash, there are other asset classes people often consider. In 
a traditional investing book, you’d likely see chapters dedicated to bonds, real estate, 
commodities, and perhaps alternative assets. In our end-of-world oriented framework, 
however, these play a secondary role at best. We'll discuss them here to explain why 
they are not central in our strategy. It’s not that they’re “bad” investments universally — 
it’s that given our assumptions (short horizon, high uncertainty, small investor), they 
aren’t as useful for implementing the kind of strategy we’re focusing on. 


Bonds and Fixed Income 


What they are: Bonds are essentially |OUs issued by governments, municipalities, or 
companies. When you buy a bond, you are lending your money to the issuer for a set 
period of time, in exchange for regular interest payments and the promise that your 
principal will be returned at maturity. “Fixed income” is a broader term that covers 
bonds and similar instruments that pay a set interest. Bonds come in many flavors: 
government bonds (considered relatively safe in stable times), corporate bonds (loans 
to companies), short-term bills, long-term bonds that can span decades, and so on. 


How they’re usually used: In classic financial advice, bonds are a key component of a 
diversified portfolio. They are generally viewed as more stable than stocks. Many 
advisors suggest that as you get older or as you want to protect your money, you should 
increase your allocation to bonds. The reason is that bonds often behave differently 
from stocks — for instance, in a recession or stock market crash, central banks might 
cut interest rates, which can make existing bonds (with higher locked-in rates) more 
valuable. Also, if a company goes bankrupt, bondholders (as lenders) have a higher 
claim on assets than stockholders, which can mean bonds are a bit safer in dire 
situations. In other words, bonds are typically the steady, lower-risk anchor of a 
portfolio, balancing out the volatility of stocks. 


Challenges in our worldview: Nir’s worldview, which underpins this book, is that we 
might not have a normal multi-decade future for markets to slowly play out. We’re 
looking at a near-term horizon where Al-driven disruptions or other crises could lead 
to systemic collapse or radical change. In such a scenario, the usual appeal of bonds 
(“hold this piece of paper for 10 or 20 years and you’ll get your money back with 
interest”) starts to wobble. If we seriously question whether the world as we know it will 
function normally 10 years from now, a 10-year bond becomes a very uncertain bet. 
Will the government or company even be around to pay you back in full? Even if they 
are, will the currency you get paid in hold its value? These are extreme questions, but 
they illustrate why long-term promises may not comfort someone with an end-of-world 
mindset. 


Even setting aside apocalyptic thoughts, bond prices are heavily influenced by factors 
that might be tricky to navigate on a short horizon: 


e Central bank policy: Bond values move inversely to interest rates. If central 
banks jack up rates to fight inflation, existing bond prices drop; if they slash rates 
inacrisis, bonds jump. Predicting central bank moves over 3-6 months can be 
as hard as predicting the crisis itself. 


e Inflation: A big spike in inflation (not uncommon in turbulent times) can erode 
the value of fixed interest payments. If you’re holding a bond paying 2% and 
inflation jumps to 8%, you’re effectively losing buying power each month. 


e Credit risk: In shaky times, there’s a real question of whether certain issuers 
(especially companies or heavily indebted governments) might default on 
payments. Credit concerns can cause some bonds to tank in price 
unexpectedly. 


Why we don’t focus on bonds in this system: Our method in this book is built around 
analyzing events and their impact mostly on equities (stocks) and sectors. It’s not that 
bonds don’t react to events — they do — but the analysis is somewhat different, and 
frankly, more specialized. For a small investor with limited capital, trying to become an 
expert in bonds on top of everything else is probably not the best use of time, given our 
horizon. If you have $10k and you’re trying to navigate a wild few years, learning how to 
pick the right stock or sector ETF likely offers more opportunity than delving into, say, 
the nuances of corporate bond credit spreads or yield curve dynamics. 


Use case - short-term “cash-like” bonds: That said, there is a corner of the bond 
world we should acknowledge: very short-term government securities or high-quality 
bond funds that behave almost like cash. For example, in the U.S., something like a 3- 
month Treasury bill or a money market fund can be a safe place to park cash and earna 
bit of interest with very low risk. These instruments can be considered an extension of 
your cash holding (a way to get a small return on cash without taking much more risk). If 
you’re comfortable with them and have access, there’s nothing wrong with using those 
instead of literally holding all your money in a checking account earning zero. However, 
for simplicity, this book doesn’t dive deep into those, and they’re not essential to the 
core strategy — they’re more of a personal preference for cash management. 


Bottom line: Bonds are not a focus in our scenario-investing toolkit. We don’t build our 
sample portfolios around bonds, because the goal here is to either be in dynamic, 
event-responsive assets (stocks/ETFs) or safe on the sidelines (cash). We’re not trying 
to eke out a 4% annual yield from bonds while waiting for retirement — we’re trying to 
capture bigger swings in a short period or sit things out safely. If you personally have 
some bond holdings (like in a retirement account or a government savings bond), that’s 
fine — just recognize that for the specific strategy we’re discussing, bonds play a minor 
role. 


Gold and Other Commodities 


The appeal of “hard assets”: Gold, silver, oil, and other commodities often come up 
when people talk about crisis investing. Gold in particular has an almost mythical 
status — for thousands of years it’s been seen as a store of value. You’ll hear arguments 
that in times of high inflation or when trust in governments falls, gold is the ultimate 
safe asset because it’s tangible and finite. Other commodities, like oil, can also spike in 


value during wars or supply shocks, making them potentially interesting if you foresee 
such events. Intuitively, in an end-of-the-world scenario, a lot of people think “I should 
own gold (or even bullets and canned food) because those will hold value if paper 
money becomes worthless.” 


Why we consider them peripheral: There’s nothing inherently wrong with owning a bit 
of gold or other real assets as a hedge. In fact, many long-term investors do keep a 
small percentage of their portfolio in gold as an insurance policy. However, for our 3-6 
month investment approach, direct commodity investing is not a central tool, for 
several reasons: 


e Lack of cash flow or growth: Gold just sits there; it doesn’t produce earnings or 
innovation. Its price can go up, but it’s driven largely by sentiment, inflation 
expectations, or fear. That makes it a bit different to analyze compared to a 
stock where you can point to earnings or a new product. You can certainly form a 
scenario around gold (e.g., “Inflation will spike, so gold will rise”), but it’s a 
narrower bet. 


e Trading complexity: If you wanted to invest directly in commodities like oil, 
wheat, or copper over short periods, you’d likely be dealing with futures 
contracts or specialized ETFs. Those instruments come with quirks like 
contango and roll yield (technical terms that basically mean you can lose 
money even if the commodity’s spot price doesn’t move, due to the way futures 
contracts are priced over time). For gold, there are ETFs that track the price of 
gold, which are simpler — but even those can have costs and tracking error over 
time. 


e Volatility and timing: Commodities can be very volatile, sometimes even more 
than stocks. And they often move on global macro news that can be hard to 
predict. One day, a peace treaty might send gold down; another day, a banking 
crisis sends it up. If you think stock investors are skittish, commodity traders are 
ona hair-trigger alert for any news. That makes short-term trading in 
commodities risky if you’re not deeply familiar with those markets. 


How they might fit for some people: We’re not dogmatically against gold or 
commodities. If you personally feel more secure having, say, 5% of your portfolio in gold 
(or a gold ETF) as a hedge against disaster, that’s okay. It can provide some 
diversification — gold often holds value when stocks are tanking. The key is to treat it as 
a peripheral holding: it’s not there to make you rich in 3-6 months; it’s there as a form of 
insurance. Similarly, if your scenario research strongly suggests a certain commodity 
will jump (maybe you’ve studied energy markets deeply and foresee an oil price spike), 
you could tactically play that via a commodity ETF. Just understand that this is an 
advanced move and comes with its own learning curve. For most readers, sticking to 
companies and sectors (even in areas like energy or mining, where commodity prices 
are a factor) will be more straightforward than trading the raw commodities directly. 


Bottom line: Gold and commodities are on the sidelines of our strategy. They’re 
available if you have a strong reason or comfort with them, but the core method and 
examples in this book will not hinge on owning gold bars or oil futures. Our focus 


remains on equities and cash, where we can more directly apply our event-driven 
analysis. 


What This System Avoids (and Why) 


Equally important to knowing what’s in your toolbox is knowing what’s not in it— and 
why we left those tools out. In designing the investing approach for this book, we made 
a conscious decision to exclude certain asset types and tactics. These are things 
that, while popular in some corners of finance, do not fit well with our philosophy of 
keeping things clear, manageable, and aligned with a short time horizon. Let’s go 
through the main “do not use” list: 


Options and Leverage 


What are options? Options are financial contracts that give you the right (but not the 
obligation) to buy or sell an asset at a specific price before a specific date. For example, 
a call option might let you buy 100 shares of a stock at $50 per share anytime in the next 
three months - if the stock goes to $70, your option becomes very valuable; if the stock 
stays below $50, the option expires worthless. Options can be used for hedging or 
speculation, and they introduce a nonlinear payoff (small moves might not matter 
much, big moves can matter a lot). They’re a bit like side bets on the market. 


What is leverage? Leverage means using borrowed money or specialized financial 
instruments to amplify the effect of your own money. For instance, if you have $100 and 
you borrow another $100 to invest $200 total, a 10% gain gives you $20 (which is 20% of 
your own $100 — double the return). A 10% loss, however, also doubles to a $20 loss 
(20% down on your money). Leverage can also be embedded in products — for example, 
some ETFs are designed to give 2x or 3x the daily performance of an index (using 
derivatives to achieve that effect). Margin trading (borrowing from your broker against 
your stocks) is another common way to leverage. 


Why people use them: The appeal is obvious — options and leverage can 
supercharge your gains. If you’re really confident in a move, an option can turn a small 
investment into a big win, and leverage can boost a 5% up-move into a 10% profit for 
you. Additionally, options can be used more conservatively to hedge (protect against 
downside) or to generate income (selling options for premium). They are powerful tools 
in the right hands. 


Why we avoid them: In our context — a small investor, navigating a possibly chaotic 
market, with limited time — these tools are more dangerous than beneficial. Here’s why: 


e Magnified losses: Yes, leverage and options can magnify gains, but they equally 
magnify losses. And losses, especially big ones, are much harder to recover 
from. If you’re wrong on a stock and it drops 10%, you can recover from that. But 
if you were leveraged 3-to-1, that 10% drop wipes out 30% of your capital. A few 
bad moves like that, and you’re out of the game entirely. Options can even expire 
worthless, meaning you lose 100% of what you paid, even if the stock only 
moved slightly against you or simply didn’t move as much as you hoped by the 
expiration date. 


Complexity and time sensitivity: Options come with expiration dates and a lot 
of jargon (strike price, implied volatility, delta, gamma, etc.). Successfully 
trading options requires paying attention to more variables than just the stock 
price — you have to consider time decay (options lose value as they approach 
expiration, all else equal) and how volatility affects pricing. For leverage, you 
have to monitor that your account doesn’t fall below required levels (or you can 
get margin-called and forced to sell). In a fast-moving market, these add layers 
of stress and complexity that can distract from the core strategy. Remember, 
our approach is all about understanding real-world events and their effects — 
layering in options means now you also have to predict when something will 
happen (the timing) with precision, because if it happens too late, your option 
might be worthless even if you were directionally right. 


Emotional strain: Being leveraged or holding options can amplify emotions. 
Knowing that one big move can double your money can lead to euphoria or 
greed; watching an option’s value evaporate can cause panic or despair. It’s 
hard enough to keep a level head in volatile times — these tools turn the dial up to 
11 on the emotional rollercoaster. For most people, that leads to bad decisions 
(like selling at the worst time, or doubling down irrationally to “make back” 
losses). 


No need in a high-volatility world: Here’s a key point — if we’re right that we’re 
heading into extremely turbulent times, the plain vanilla stocks and ETFs are 
already going to have large moves. You won’t need leverage to see significant 
returns; the environment itself provides leverage in a sense. If a stock can swing 
30% in a few months because of world events, trying to make it swing 60% with 
leverage is just adding risk unnecessarily. We’d rather size our positions 
appropriately and sleep at night. 


Nir’s stance: As co-authors, we firmly believe that small investors are better off 
without options and leverage in this kind of scenario-focused approach. The 
potential reward is not worth the potential ruin. This system is designed to help you 
think clearly about cause and effect, not to encourage ultra-fast trading or gambling 
behavior. So, for the purposes of this book: 


We will not use options or margin in any examples or suggested portfolios. 


Any scenario can be played sufficiently with the combination of stocks, ETFs, 
and holding some cash. If you feel an urge to use leverage because you’re 
certain about something, consider that it’s better to just invest a bit more of your 
cash into that high-conviction idea (while still staying within sane risk limits) 
rather than borrowing or using complex derivatives. 


There’s an old saying: “There are old traders and there are bold traders, but there 
are no old bold traders.” We want you to have a chance to survive and thrive 
even if one or two calls go wrong. Avoiding leverage and options is part of that 
survival strategy. 


Cryptocurrency and Speculative Tokens 


What is crypto: Cryptocurrency refers to digital assets that use cryptography and 
decentralized networks (like blockchain) to secure transactions. The most famous 


example is Bitcoin, but there are thousands of other tokens (“altcoins”). Crypto assets 
can serve various purposes: some aim to be digital money, others are used in 
decentralized finance platforms, and some are basically digital collectibles or utility 
tokens for projects. Over the past decade, crypto has grown from an obscure tech 
curiosity to a massive, global speculative arena. 


The allure of crypto (especially for doomsday scenarios): Crypto enthusiasts often 
argue that coins like Bitcoin are a hedge against the collapse of the traditional financial 
system. The idea is that because Bitcoin isn’t controlled by any government and has a 
hard cap on supply, it could hold value if people lose faith in fiat currencies (like the 
dollar or euro). We’ve seen periods where crypto prices skyrocketed, making early 
investors incredibly rich. The stories of turning a few hundred dollars into millions with 
the right coin at the right time are all over the media. This naturally grabs the attention 
of anyone looking for big gains — or worrying about the value of fiat money. 


Why we exclude them from our strategy: Despite the exciting narratives, crypto is not 
part of the recommended toolkit in this book’s system. There are a few key reasons: 


e Extreme volatility and unpredictability: Crypto prices can move 10%, 20%, 
even 50% in a single day based on little more than a tweet or a rumor. While 
stocks can be volatile too, crypto takes it to another level. The swings are so wild 
that it’s very difficult to apply our kind of reasoned scenario analysis. You could 
be absolutely right about the long-term value of a certain crypto project and still 
lose most of your investment in the short run because market sentiment turned 
or acrypto exchange got hacked. 


e Lack of fundamental anchors: When we analyze a stock, we have anchors like 
earnings, revenue, users, tangible assets, etc. Even if these are hard to predict, 
they at least provide some basis for thinking about what a company is worth. 
With many cryptocurrencies, especially smaller tokens, there’s often no clear 
intrinsic value or cash flow. It’s worth what people think it’s worth — which can be 
a shaky foundation. It often becomes a game of guessing other speculators’ 
psychology, which is not our focus. 


e Security and fraud risks: The crypto world has been rife with hacks, scams, and 
exchange failures. Entire trading platforms have collapsed, taking customer 
funds with them. Projects have turned out to be Ponzi schemes or have had fatal 
technical flaws. The regulatory environment is also uncertain — governments 
might crack down or restrict crypto activities, especially in a crisis scenario. 
These risks add another layer of uncertainty beyond normal market risk. 


e Disconnect from real-world events: Our investing method leans heavily on 
mapping real-world developments to market moves (for example, “a war breaks 
out, oil stocks rise”). With crypto, the link to real-world utilitarian value is often 
tenuous. Some cryptos try to tie to real assets (like stablecoins pegged to the 
dollar, or tokens backed by commodities), but many operate in a speculative 
vacuum. This doesn’t mean crypto is meaningless — it has its own internal 
dynamics — but those dynamics are not the focus of this book and are not as 
easily interpretable through our event-driven lens. 


Nir’s advice on crypto: If you personally are a big believer in crypto and hold some as a 
bet or hedge, treat it as completely separate from the money you are allocating to the 
strategies in this book. In other words, you might say: “I have $2k of Bitcoin that I’m 
keeping just in case it goes to the moon or in case fiat collapses, and I’m willing to lose 
it. Meanwhile, my other $8k I’m going to manage with stocks/ETFs/cash according to an 
event-driven plan.” That’s fine. What we don’t want is to make crypto a core part of 
scenario investing, because it encourages a casino mentality that is hard to combine 
with disciplined analysis. For novice investors especially, crypto can be a siren song — 
many have wrecked their finances chasing the next big coin. Our stance is simply: not 
needed, and not within our scope. You won’t see us recommending “buy XYZ coin” as 
part of a 6-month scenario trade. It’s outside the playbook for this book. 


Illiquid or Complex Assets 


The last category of things to avoid involves assets that are not easily tradable or are 
highly complex. These include: 


e Realestate deals that you can’t quickly sell: For example, buying a rental 
property, or joining a real estate crowdfunding project, or investing in a land 
deal. Real estate can be a great long-term investment, but it’s obviously not 
something you can sell on a day’s notice without potentially taking a hit. It’s also 
very location-specific and often involves larger sums of money (down payments, 
mortgages, etc.). 

e Private equity or venture capital: These are investments in private companies 
(not traded on stock exchanges). They usually require you to lock up money for 
years and are often only available to accredited (i.e., high-net-worth) investors. 
They carry a lot of company-specific risk and you typically can’t exit until some 
event like a buyout or IPO. 


e Collectibles and art: Some people invest in things like paintings, classic cars, 
rare wine, or even luxury watches. These can appreciate, but they have very 
limited markets (you need to find a buyer who values the item) and often it takes 
years to see significant gains — plus there are storage, insurance, and 
authenticity issues. If you needed cash quickly, how fast can you auction a 
painting or sell a coin collection? Not very. 


e Other esoteric products: This includes structured products sold by banks 
(complex instruments with derivatives embedded), certain insurance-linked 
investments, or annuities with complicated terms. These often tie up your 
money or have clauses that make it costly to exit early, and they can be very hard 
to understand fully. 


Why we avoid them: The common thread with all the above is that they don’t fit our 
liquidity and time horizon criteria. We want investments we can enter and exit 
relatively easily as scenarios unfold. If the world starts looking more dangerous or an 
opportunity arises elsewhere, we want to be nimble. With a house or a private startup 
investment, you’re basically stuck — those are commitments you can’t unwind just 
because you changed your 6-month outlook. 


Another reason is expertise and bandwidth. Each of those areas (real estate, private 
deals, art, etc.) is a specialized field. There are people who spend entire careers 
mastering them. Our typical reader doesn’t have the time or resources to dive into each 
of these while also learning to trade stocks in a crazy market. It’s better to keep things 
simple and clear. 


If you already own some illiquid assets: Many people do have these sorts of 
investments as part of their life. Maybe you own your home or a rental property. Maybe 
you have some equity in a friend’s startup, or a piece of art your family passed down. 
That’s perfectly fine. We’re not saying “sell everything and only hold stocks, ETFs, and 
cash.” The point is that our active investment strategy — the one we’re teaching in this 
book for navigating the end-of-world scenarios — does not rely on touching those 
assets. They’re outside the scope of what we actively manage or count on for short- 
term moves. If anything, think of them as part of your broader life situation or long-term 
backdrop. For instance, owning your home might give you personal security (a place to 
live no matter what), but it’s not liquid capital you can reallocate to a 3-month trade. 


Bottom line: To implement the strategy in this book, focus on assets that trade on 
public markets (or are akin to cash). That means mostly stocks and ETFs, with a dash of 
cash (or maybe a little gold) if needed. Steer clear of tying up your money in things you 
can’t get out of, and don’t complicate your plan with opaque financial products. 
Simplicity and flexibility are worth a lot in uncertain times. 


How “End of the World” Thinking Changes Traditional Advice 


At this point, you might notice that some of our recommendations differ from what 
you’d hear in a typical investing 101 seminar. That’s intentional —- our perspective is a bit 
unusual because we’re considering the possibility that the long term (decades out) 
might not play out as expected. Let’s take a moment to contrast traditional 
investment advice with our approach in this book: 


Traditional wisdom highlights: 


e “Invest for the long term.” The classic advice is that time in the market beats 
timing the market. You’ll hear that you should buy and hold for years or decades, 
because in the long run, markets tend to rise. Retirement planning often 
assumes a 30- or 40-year horizon, gradually shifting from stocks to bonds as one 
ages to reduce risk. 


e Diversify across asset classes. A usual portfolio might include a mix of stocks, 
bonds, maybe some real estate or international funds, sometimes a small 
allocation to alternatives like gold. The idea is to spread out risk so that no one 
asset class ruins you. 


e Adjust for risk tolerance and age. Younger people are told they can take more 
risk (more stocks, fewer bonds) since they have time to recover from downturns. 
Older folks approaching retirement are advised to shift toward stable income 
(bonds, dividend stocks, etc.), so their nest egg isn’t too volatile when they need 
it. 


Modest, steady returns. Mainstream advice often assumes relatively efficient 
markets and encourages Setting realistic expectations. For example, aiming to 
grow your money by perhaps 5—-8% per year on average, via broad index funds, is 
considered prudent. 


Our end-of-world influenced approach: 


“The long term might not be what it used to be.” If we truly face an Al-driven 
collapse or dramatic upheaval in the next few years, planning for 2050 in the 
markets may be moot. Our strategy compresses the horizon to what’s tangible 
and actionable now. We’re effectively saying: let’s make the most of the next few 
cycles (maybe the next few years at most) because beyond that, the visibility is 
too low or the world might be fundamentally different. 


Emphasis on agility over permanence. Instead of “buy and forget,” we’re 
advocating “stay alert and adapt.” We fully expect to shuffle the portfolio as 
scenarios change every few months. This isn’t frenetic day trading, but it’s also 
not passive holding. It’s a medium-term active approach. 


Favored asset classes. Notice we lean heavily on stocks and certain ETFs. 
Why? Because these are tied to real-economy developments and technologies 
that are either driving the change (like Al, automation, energy shifts) or reacting 
to it. We’ve de-emphasized bonds because they’re a bet on long-term stability, 
which we doubt we have. We’ve kept gold and cash mostly as defensive tools 
rather than core growth engines. 


Mentality: offense and defense. In our scenarios, sometimes the play is 
offensive (grab upside in a tech surge), sometimes it’s defensive (hunker down in 
cash or defensive stocks if a crash looms). Traditional advice often assumes a 
smoother ride and tries to balance risk for a long journey. We’re more willing to 
swing between risk-on and risk-off stances, because if the world’s ending 
(financially speaking), the regular rulebook is already out the window. 


No reliance on exotic instruments. Traditional finance, especially for 
sophisticated investors, might involve options strategies, leverage (like hedge 
funds use), or alternative assets to juice returns. We’ve intentionally stripped 
those out for clarity and because we think the environment’s volatility gives us 
enough action without adding nitroglycerin to the mix. 


A reassuring note: Does adopting this short-term, end-times strategy mean you 
abandon all hope for the future or for more conventional investing? Not at all. In fact, 
even if the absolute worst-case scenario doesn’t materialize on schedule, the skills and 
habits you’re developing here can still serve you well. You’re learning to think critically 
about how events impact markets, to be nimble, and to manage risk. Those are 
timeless investing skills. And you’re also learning capital preservation — avoiding 
pitfalls like overleveraging or chasing fads — which will protect you in any future. So, 
think of this approach as a kind of intensive course in strategic investing under 
uncertainty. If the world stabilizes and you later return to a traditional long-term plan, 
you'll do so with much sharper insight and caution, which is a good thing. 


Looking Ahead: Building Scenario Portfolios 


Now that we’ve identified our “tools” — the asset classes we’ll use (and those we won’t) 
—the next step is figuring out how to combine those tools for different situations. The 
final part of this book is all about scenario portfolios: showing what a portfolio might 
look like under various “flavors of doom” or alternative futures. 


For example, consider a few distinct scenarios: 


e Sudden Shock and Crash: Perhaps a major Al mishap or geopolitical event 
triggers a sharp market collapse. In such a scenario, you might imagine a 
portfolio that’s heavy on cash (for safety and to swoop in at a bottom), maybe a 
touch of gold, and only very selectively invested in a few stocks that could 
weather the storm or even benefit (for instance, companies providing critical 
infrastructure or defense technologies). 


e Slow Unraveling: Maybe the world doesn’t end overnight, but things slowly 
deteriorate - economies stagnate, debt loads weigh down growth, Al causes 
widespread job disruptions leading to social unrest. In a grinding bear market or 
stagflation scenario, the portfolio could lean toward things like defensive stocks 
(utilities, consumer staples - companies that sell things people need no matter 
what), value stocks with solid balance sheets, and perhaps some hard assets or 
inflation-protected assets. Essentially, this is about hunkering down for a long 
winter while still seeking pockets of opportunity. 


e Techno-Utopia (the Last Hurrah): It’s also possible we get a surprising period of 
stability or even euphoria — say Al actually boosts productivity massively in the 
short term, and for a brief time technology brings a boom rather than a bust. 
Markets could roar upward in a final blow-off top. In such a scenario, a more 
aggressive portfolio might shine — heavy on tech and Al-focused stocks or ETFs, 
perhaps some speculative plays (still within our no-leverage, no-crypto rules) to 
ride the wave. The idea would be to capitalize on a last upswing, all while 
keeping a careful eye for any cracks that signal the party is ending. 


These are just illustrative sketches. The next chapter will delve into a set of concrete 
scenario portfolios —- essentially case studies of how one might allocate among stocks, 
ETFs, and cash given a particular outlook. Each scenario will use the same building 
blocks we’ve discussed here, just mixed in different proportions or focused on different 
areas. 


The important thing is: by defining what we can own (stocks, ETFs, cash, maybe a small 
dose of bonds or gold) and what we avoid, we’ve actually simplified the daunting task of 
portfolio construction. Think of it like a chef preparing a menu in uncertain times — 
we’ve narrowed down the pantry to reliable ingredients. We won’t be using every exotic 
spice or risky wild mushroom; we’re sticking to the staples that we trust. Now our job is 
to combine those staples into dishes (portfolios) suited for each kind of feast or famine 
the future might serve. 


So take a breath —- we’re prepared with a solid toolkit. In the next chapter, we’ll put it all 
together and show sample “recipes” for portfolios on the brink of the end of the world. 
That’s where theory meets practice in a very tangible way. 


Summary and Homework 


Key Points Summary: 


Core asset classes for this strategy: Our primary toolkit consists of individual 
stocks, sector or broad-market ETFs, and cash or near-cash reserves. These 
assets are liquid, event-responsive, and suitable for a 3-6 month scenario 
approach. 


Peripheral (secondary) asset classes: We give a nod to bonds and 
gold/commodities, but we use them sparingly. Bonds don’t feature prominently 
because long-term debt bets clash with a short-term collapse mindset (though 
very short-term bonds or money-market funds can function as cash 
alternatives). Gold and other commodities can be a hedge or a specialized play if 
you’re knowledgeable, but they’re not at the core of our examples — they’re more 
like insurance or side bets. 


Avoided assets and tactics: The strategy deliberately excludes options and 
leverage (too risky and complex for our purposes), cryptocurrencies and 
speculative tokens (too volatile and not grounded in the kind of fundamental 
analysis we do), and illiquid or complex investments like private equity, real 
estate deals, or collectibles (not flexible enough for quick scenario shifts). These 
are outside the scope of what we do in this book. 


Rationale —- why this selection? Because our investing horizon is short and the 
future is highly uncertain, we prioritize assets that we can understand, track, and 
adjust quickly. Everything in our core toolkit is something you can move in and 
out of on short notice and analyze with real-world logic. The things we avoid tend 
to be those that could either lock us in, blow us up, or distract us from clear 
thinking. In an “end of the world” framework, simplicity, liquidity, and staying in 
control of your risk are worth more than exotic strategies. 


Not your grandma’s portfolio: Traditional advice gets tweaked here. We’re not 
automatically including the usual heavy bond allocation or assuming a 30-year 
timeline. That’s not because those things are never valid, but because we’re 
focusing on what to do if you don’t think you have a long and calm road ahead. 
Still, even in this approach, basic principles like diversification (e.g. using ETFs 
for broad exposure) and prudent risk management remain crucial —- we’re just 
adapting them to a more volatile, short-term context. 


Remember: Nothing in this chapter (or book) is personal financial advice. All 
investments carry risk, especially in turbulent times. Use this framework as a guide for 
thinking, but do your own due diligence and consider consulting a licensed financial 
professional for advice tailored to your situation. 


Homework - Applying This to Your Own Situation: 


Now that we’ve outlined the playing field, it’s time for a brief practical exercise. This will 
help you connect the chapter’s concepts to your personal finances: 


9. List Your Current (or Intended) Assets: Make a simple list of all the types of 


assets you Currently hold, or that you have been considering investing in. Be 


honest and comprehensive — include everything from your stock holdings to that 
crypto you dabbled in, to any real estate, bonds, etc. If you’re just starting out 
and mostly in cash, list that too. 

10. Categorize Each Asset Type: Go down your list and label each item as either 
Core, Peripheral, or Out of Scope based on the definitions in this chapter. 

11. Core = individual stocks, ETFs, cash (the main tools our system uses). 

12. Peripheral = bonds, gold, or other commodity plays (acknowledged in this book 
but not central to the strategy). 

13. Out of Scope = things like options, leveraged investments, crypto/speculative 
tokens, private illiquid assets, etc. (not used in our strategy). 

14. Reflect on the Non-Core Items: For anything you labeled “Peripheral” or “Out 
of Scope,” write one sentence about why you own it (or were considering it). 
Then add whether you plan to keep it as a separate side holding, reduce it, or 
eventually eliminate/simplify it. Example: “Crypto (Out of Scope): | own a small 
amount of Bitcoin as a speculative hedge. | will keep it for now but treat it as 
money | can afford to lose, separate from my main strategy.”* This step is just for 
your own clarity — it’s okay if you decide to keep some out-of-scope stuff; the 
goal is to be intentional about it. 

15. Define Your Go-Forward Toolkit: Write down a short statement for yourself: 
“For the purposes of my 3-6 month scenario investing system, my primary 
building blocks will be:___.” Fillin the blank with the asset classes you intend to 
focus on (e.g., “cash, broad market ETFs, plus a handful of individual tech and 
energy stocks”). This will serve as your personal guideline, keeping you aligned 
with the strategy as we move into scenario planning. 


By doing this homework, you'll have effectively customized the lessons of this chapter 
to your own life. You’ll know exactly what tools you’re going to use, and which ones 
you'll avoid, as you navigate whatever crazy turns the world might take in the coming 
months. That clarity is powerful — it will help you ignore the noise and stick to a plan. 


With your asset toolbox in hand, you’re ready to move on. In the next chapter, we’ll take 
that toolbox and build different “end of the world” portfolios, showing how you might 
combine assets for various scenarios. Get ready to see theory put into practice —- and 
remember, fortune favors the prepared mind (even at the end of the world). 


Chapter 12 — Scenario Portfolios: Different Flavors of 
Doom 


In this chapter, we step back from individual stock picks and short-term signals to look 
at the big picture of portfolio design under extreme future scenarios. Up to now, we’ve 
built a toolkit for analyzing investments: we’ve learned to read charts without getting 
lost in noise, extract key parameters from stock moves, layer on macro context 
(markets, interest rates, recessions, wars), dive into sectors with deep research, and 
even consult an LLM “council” of Als for diverse perspectives. We also defined a 
practical 3-6 month investment system and discussed which asset classes make 
sense (liquid stocks, ETFs, cash) and which to avoid (options, leverage, crypto, illiquid 
bets). 


Now it’s time to put it all together. This chapter introduces scenario portfolios — 
different ways to allocate your money depending on how the end of the world might play 
out. We don’t claim to know the future with certainty (neither humans nor Als can!), but 
we can prepare for a range of outcomes. Think of it this way: 


“If the future starts looking like this scenario, a portfolio like that would be 
prudent. If instead it looks like another scenario, a different portfolio might 
hold up better.” 


By the end of this chapter, you’ll understand four distinct scenarios for the near future 
(the “different flavors of doom”) and see illustrative \$10k portfolios for each one. 
These examples aren’t one-size-fits-all prescriptions — they’re guiding templates. You'll 
also learn how to choose a base scenario to guide your current portfolio, and how to 
tilt (adjust) your allocations as the world changes without whipsawing your strategy 
every day. The goalis to stay realistic, nimble, and prepared within the short horizon we 
have, given Nir’s view that an Al-driven collapse or radical upheaval is increasingly likely 
in the next few years. 


Before we dive in, one important note: This is not personalized financial advice. It’s a 
framework for thinking. All investing is risky and even a “doom-proof” plan can lose 
money (especially in true catastrophe). Our aim is to be less naive and more deliberate 
—to manage risk in unprecedented times, not to promise any guaranteed safe haven. 
With that in mind, let’s explore the four scenarios and their portfolios. 


Scenario A — Sudden Shock / Fast Crash 


Narrative of Scenario A 


In Scenario A, the world faces a sudden shock — a fast and brutal crash. Imagine an 
event that triggers global panic almost overnight. It could be a severe Al-related 
disaster, a massive cyberattack, an abrupt escalation of a war, or a financial system 
accident (for example, a major bank collapse or liquidity freeze). News breaks, fear 
spreads, and within days or weeks markets are in free-fall. This is the kind of scenario 
where major stock indices might plunge 20%, 30%, even 50% in a short span before 


anyone fully processes what’s happening. It’s 2008 or March 2020 on steroids — a flash 
of true panic. 


In this narrative, trust in institutions is shaken. Central banks and governments might 
respond with emergency measures (rate cuts, money printing, market closures), but 
confidence is so low that their actions have limited immediate effect. Everyone is 
scrambling for safety. Cash becomes king; investors sell first and ask questions later. 
There’s arush into the absolute safest assets: physical cash, short-term Treasury bills, 
perhaps gold or other traditional havens — anything to ride out the storm. In short, 
Scenario A is the “heart attack” version of collapse: quick, brutal, and indiscriminate in 
its initial damage. 


Market and Sector Behavior in a Fast Crash 


How do markets and sectors behave in such a sudden crash? Almost everything sells 
off hard in the initial phase. When fear is at its peak, even high-quality stocks get 
dumped because people need liquidity. Correlations between risky assets go to 1 - 
meaning diversification offers little short-term help as virtually all equities drop 
together. Volatility soars; the VIX (volatility index or “fear gauge”) spikes to extreme 
levels. You might see indiscriminate selling of stocks, corporate bonds, real estate — 
anything that’s not nailed down — as investors de-risk en masse. 


After the first phase of indiscriminate selling, some differentiation emerges. 
Historically, when the dust starts settling, certain sectors or companies recover faster 
or stabilize because they’re seen as essential or relatively resilient. In an Al-driven 
crisis or wartime scenario, for instance: - Essential infrastructure and utilities might 
rebound or at least fall less — people still need electricity, water, communications. - 
Defense and cybersecurity stocks could catch a bid if the crisis involves conflict or 
digital threats (governments suddenly spending to respond). - Key Al/automation 
enablers (think of companies providing critical Al infrastructure or robotics) might also 
recover if they’re needed to rebuild or adapt after the shock. 


However, in the middle of the crash, distinguishing winners from losers is tough. Even 
safe-haven assets that usually rise (like gold or government bonds) might temporarily 
dip as frantic investors sell whatever they can to raise cash. The hallmark of a fast crash 
is chaos and correlation: for a time, almost every risk asset is highly correlated — 
mostly downward. 


Portfolio Design Principles for Scenario A 


In a sudden shock scenario, the top priority is survival, not shooting the lights out. The 
guiding principles are capital preservation and optionality. You want to come through 
the crash intact and ready to seize opportunities once things bottom out, rather than 
stretching for the last bit of return before the crash or, worse, getting wiped out. 


Key design principles: - Hold more cash than usual: Cash (or near-cash instruments 
like treasury bills or money-market funds) provides stability and flexibility. In a crash, 
cash is not just a safety net for your portfolio’s value — it’s dry powder to deploy at lower 
prices if you have the nerve and foresight. In Scenario A, we significantly overweight 
cash compared to a normal environment. - Favor broad, high-quality exposure: Since 


it’s hard to guess which specific stocks will hold up, lean towards broad market ETFs or 
funds that cover large segments of the market (e.g. a total market index or S&P 500 
ETF), and towards sectors known for resilience (staples, utilities, healthcare). High- 
quality companies with strong balance sheets and essential businesses are preferable 
to speculative or highly leveraged companies. - Reduce risky and narrow bets: Avoid 
or minimize positions in the frothiest parts of the market — things like meme stocks, 
companies with no profits, or any asset that requires stable conditions to do well. This 
is not the time for leverage, derivatives, or exotic instruments. No options, no margin, 
no crypto gamble — these can implode during a sudden crash or even become illiquid. 
We want simple, solid assets. - Phased entry for new investments: If you are looking 
to buy into the market (for example, after an initial crash leg), do it gradually. In a fast 
crash, catching the exact bottom is almost impossible. Prices could fall far and then fall 
further. So you might deploy a bit of your cash at atime in stages (this is a form of 
dollar-cost averaging) rather than rushing in all at once. This way, if the market keeps 
dropping, you still have cash to buy at even lower prices. 


The overall mindset is defense first. You accept that in a sudden shock, you won’t 
make a killing on the upside; your win is not losing too much and being ready to act 
when clarity returns. It’s about outlasting the storm. 


Example Portfolio Allocation for Scenario A (Approximately \$10k) 


Let’s translate those principles into a hypothetical portfolio for a small investor with 
around \$10,000, geared for a fast-crash scenario. Remember, this is just an example 
to illustrate the idea — not a strict recipe. But it might look something like: 


e Cash or Near-Cash: ~50% — Roughly \$5,000 kept ultra-safe, e.g. in a savings 
account, money market fund, or short-term Treasury bills. This is your cushion 
against the storm and ammunition to invest later. Such a high cash allocation 
might normally drag down returns, but in a crash it can be a lifesaver and a 
source of psychological comfort. 


e Broad Market ETFs: ~30% — About \$3,000 in broad, diversified stock market 
exposure. For example, you could use a large-cap index ETF or an all-world stock 
ETF. This portion will certainly take a hit in a crash, but by being broad you 
reduce the risk of any single company blowing up your portfolio. It ensures you 
still have a foot in the market, in case the crash is followed by a swift recovery 
(you don’t want to be 100% on the sidelines if things rebound). 


e Defensive Sector Stocks/ETFs: ~15% — Maybe \$1,500 in sectors that tend to 
be more defensive. This could include a consumer staples ETF (companies that 
sell food, household goods, etc.), a utilities ETF, or a healthcare ETF - or a mix of 
a couple of these. These sectors usually fall less in panics because their 
earnings are steadier in bad times (people still buy soap and power, and get sick 
regardless of the economy). You might also include some “quality” stocks here 
— companies with strong finances and dividends. The goal is to cushion the 
downside a bit relative to the overall market. 

e Selective High-Conviction Stocks (Aggressive picks): ~5% — Around \$500 in 
one or two names that you highly believe in for the long run (perhaps critical Al or 
infrastructure players that could thrive post-crisis). This is optional - you could 


also keep this 5% in cash if you prefer maximum safety. But if you have a couple 
of positions you’ve researched deeply (using our 3-6 month system and LLM 
council) and you’re willing to weather wild swings, a small allocation here could 
pay off big in a recovery. The key is small. We’re talking a few hundred dollars out 
of \$10k — an amount that won’t jeopardize your finances if those stocks drop 
50% in the crash. Think of this like a venture bet: high risk, high reward, only with 
money you can afford to lose. 


Why such a setup? In a sudden crash, this portfolio is positioned to survive first, 
recover second. About half of it is safe (cash) or relatively safe (defensive sectors). The 
broad market piece will get hit, but not as hard as if you only held volatile tech stocks, 
and it ensures you participate in any eventual market recovery. The small high- 
conviction slice gives you upside if certain themes (like Al infrastructure) prove crucial 
in the aftermath, but it’s sized so that even if those picks implode, it doesn’t devastate 
your wealth. 


Crucially, this portfolio leaves you with flexibility. If the market crashes 40%, your 50% 
cash means you’ve only taken maybe a ~20% hit on your total portfolio (because your 
cash held steady, and your equities fell). You’re in a position to start buying while others 
are forced to sell. You can rebalance: perhaps use some of that cash to buy stocks 
when they’re much cheaper, moving toward a more aggressive stance once the 
scenario improves. In other words, you’re playing offense by first playing good 
defense. 


Adjustments if Scenario A Likelihood Changes 


Scenario A may or may not happen — it’s one possibility. How do you use this in 
practice? The idea is to tilt your portfolio toward this structure when you see signs 
that a sudden crash is becoming more likely, and tilt away if those signs recede. 


e If the world starts looking more like Scenario A: Suppose you observe 
increasing signals of an abrupt crisis - maybe Al news is getting dire (e.g. arogue 
Al incident), geopolitical tensions reach a boiling point, or credit markets start 
freezing. Your analysis (and perhaps an LLM council check-in) suggests a fast 
crash is more probable now than before. In that case, you’d move your portfolio 
incrementally toward the above allocation. For example, you might raise your 
cash percentage gradually by trimming some stock positions (taking profits or 
cutting losers) and not reinvesting yet. You might rotate some of your holdings 
into broader index funds or defensive sectors, reducing exposure to any single 
risky stock. Essentially, you batten down the hatches. You don’t necessarily go 
100% to the Scenario A portfolio overnight (unless the crisis is literally upon you, 
which is rare to time perfectly), but you start de-risking and increasing 
liquidity. 

e If things calm down (moving away from Scenario A): On the other hand, say a 
feared crisis passes without catastrophe — perhaps there was a tense standoff 
that gets resolved, or successful Al safety measures reduce immediate 
extinction risk. Volatility falls and confidence inches back. Then you can slowly 
redeploy some of that cash into riskier assets again. Maybe you increase your 


allocation to growth stocks or sector ETFs that you had cut back on, or add to 
equity positions with strong 3-6 month theses. The key is that you do this 
gradually and thoughtfully, guided by your system’s analysis — not just because 
the market went up for a week, but because the fundamental risk backdrop has 
genuinely improved. 


In practice, the shift between scenarios should be done with a cool head and a 
systematic approach (as we'll discuss later in the chapter). Scenario A’s portfolio is a 
bit like a crash helmet: you wear it when the ride looks truly dangerous, and you take it 
off a little when the road smooths out. 


Scenario B — Slow Bleed / Grinding Decline 
Narrative of Scenario B 


Not every “end of the world” happens with a bang; some feel like a long, drawn-out 
whimper. Scenario B envisions a slow bleed, a grinding decline of economic and social 
conditions rather than a single cataclysmic shock. In this world, there’s no headline- 
grabbing asteroid impact or Al turning evil overnight. Instead, it’s death by a thousand 
cuts: - Technological unemployment and inequality: Al and automation steadily 
erode job security for millions. Productivity might rise in a technical sense, but the gains 
aren’t widely shared. Many people feel left behind as their livelihoods slip away or 
stagnate. - Political and social unrest: Frustration grows with institutions that can’t 
seem to address these issues. We see periodic protests, increases in crime or civil 
unrest, maybe a rotating door of shaky governments. However, none of these events 
singularly topples the system; they just add stress. - Frequent mini-crises: Perhaps 
there are short recessions, banking scares, or geopolitical standoffs — but each time, 
the system muddles through, albeit a bit weaker. Confidence in the future slowly 
erodes. - Erosion of trust: In general, people and markets lose a little faith every day 
that tomorrow will be better. It’s not a dramatic apocalypse scene, but a pervasive 
gloom and decline in optimism. 


Economically, this scenario might mean low growth or stagnation. Corporate earnings 
stop growing much or even shrink in certain industries. There isn’t an obvious global 
collapse, but year after year, living standards feel a bit worse for the majority. In market 
terms, it’s like a slow leak in a tire rather than a blowout. 


The stock market in such a scenario might drift sideways or downward over an 
extended period. Imagine a chart that doesn’t crash violently, but each rally peters out 
and each dip goes a bit lower than the last. Investor sentiment remains cautious or 
bearish for years. Valuations (like price-to-earnings ratios) compress as investors are 
no longer willing to pay high premiums for growth that never materializes. Companies 
struggle to hit optimistic forecasts, leading to disappointing earnings seasons. It’s a bit 
like what Japan’s stock market saw in the 1990s-2000s after its asset bubble burst — 
decades of malaise - or the global markets in the 1970s stagflation era where inflation 
and unemployment were high but the world didn’t literally end overnight. 


Market and Sector Behavior in a Grinding Decline 


Ina slow-bleed scenario, not everything falls apart at once; instead, the weaknesses 
accumulate over time. Some patterns you might expect: - Cyclical and speculative 
stocks suffer: Companies that depend heavily on economic cycles (like luxury goods, 
tourism, construction, or banking) may underperform because the good times never 
fully return. Similarly, highly speculative sectors (like companies that need constant 
investor optimism or easy money — many early-stage techs or biotech with no profits, 
for example) struggle to find support as risk appetite stays low. - Highly leveraged 
businesses feel the pain: In a grinding downturn, debt can be deadly. Firms with big 
borrowings find it hard to grow their way out of debt, and if interest rates remain 
elevated or credit markets cautious, refinancing is tough. Some may survive but with 
stocks that languish; others could face restructuring. - Winners from automation and 
cost-cutting: On the other hand, certain companies might actually benefit from the 
trends. If Al and automation are advancing, firms that provide these technologies (Al 
software, robotics, cloud computing, etc.) could still see demand as other businesses 
and governments desperately seek efficiency. They might be relative bright spots in the 
market, even as overall sentiment is muted. Similarly, companies that successfully use 
automation to cut costs and maintain profitability can outshine peers. - Essential 
goods and defensive sectors hold steadier: When people feel poorer or uncertain, 
they still spend on necessities. Consumer staples (food, basic household items), 
utilities, and healthcare demand don’t disappear. These sectors often don’t soar, but 
their earnings are more reliable, so their stocks might gently grind flat or even up, while 
others grind down. Dividend-paying stocks with stable cash flows also become 
attractive for their income when capital gains are hard to find. - Moderate inflation or 
stagflation impacts: Depending on the economic backdrop, if this slow decline is 
accompanied by inflation (stagflation scenario), then commodities and energy might 
see periods of strength. If it’s more of a deflationary stagnation, cash and government 
bonds might do relatively well because prices of everything else fall. We won’t digress 
into macro details too much, but it’s worth noting you’d watch inflation, interest rates, 
and employment trends to fine-tune which sectors to favor. 


Overall, expect choppy, range-bound markets that frustrate both bulls and bears. 
Nothing goes to zero en masse, but traditional growth investments disappoint. It’s a 
climate of lowered expectations. 


Portfolio Design Principles for Scenario B 


Designing a portfolio for a slow grind downward means aiming for resilience and 
selective growth, with a balance between playing defense and not missing the few 
opportunities that do exist. Key principles include: 


e Emphasize quality and resilience: In a world where the tide is slowly going out, 
you want companies that can swim on their own. That means strong balance 
sheets (low debt), real earnings and cash flow, and products or services that 
people continue to pay for even when budgets are tight. “Quality” ETFs or funds 
(which weight companies by profitability and stability) could be a good core. 
Avoid firms that need constant hype or cheap credit to survive. 


e Focus on secular winners (especially from automation/Al): Even if the overall 
environment is tough, some sectors will have structural growth because they’re 
actually riding the cause of the turmoil. For example, if automation is causing job 
losses but boosting productivity, companies making the automation tools might 
see revenue growth. Al, robotics, certain tech infrastructure, perhaps electric 
vehicles or energy transition technologies — these could be secular themes that 
still advance amid a stagnant economy. However, be careful to avoid the most 
hyped and overpriced names in these areas; we want real winners, not just 
dreamers. 


e Include essentials and defensive plays: A portion of the portfolio should be in 
things that hold value in hard times. Utilities, consumer staples, healthcare, 
possibly some commodity or energy exposure if relevant — these can provide 
ballast. They might not skyrocket, but they can produce dividends or steady 
returns as people still need what they sell. 


e Maintain a moderate cash buffer: You don’t need as much cash as in the fast- 
crash scenario, because there isn’t one big buying-opportunity moment you’re 
waiting for. However, having some cash (or short-term safe assets) is useful. It 
cushions the portfolio if markets keep dripping downward, and it gives you the 
flexibility to buy something really attractive that might suddenly go on sale (for 
instance, if a good company’s stock plunges on a piece of bad news, you have 
funds to scoop it up). 


e Avoid deep cyclical bets and high leverage: You probably won’t want a lot of 
exposure to, say, pure-play luxury retailers, highly cyclical commodities (unless 
we are in an inflationary stagflation, which is a nuance), or heavily indebted 
companies. The risk/reward just isn’t great when the baseline is weak demand 
and tighter credit. Also avoid getting lured by “this stock is down 80%, it’s 
cheap!” unless you have strong conviction it’s a survivor; in a slow bleed, some 
seemingly cheap stocks can stay cheap or go to zero over time. 


In essence, the Scenario B portfolio is about durability. It might not perform 
spectacularly in any given month, but it aims to not let your wealth slowly erode 
alongside the world. You’re playing long-term survivor, positioning to still be standing 
(and even thriving relatively) after several years of tough conditions. 


Example Portfolio Allocation for Scenario B (Approximately \$10k) 


Let’s sketch an example \$10,000 allocation suited to a grinding decline scenario: 


e Cash or Cash Equivalents: ~25% — Perhaps \$2,500 held in cash or very low-risk 
instruments. This is a bit higher than normal times, reflecting the uncertain 
outlook, but not as high as in an imminent crash scenario. It’s enough to provide 
flexibility and safety without dragging too much on potential returns if the 
decline is milder than feared. 

e Broad Market / “Quality” ETFs: ~35% — Around \$3,500 in a broad stock market 
index fund or an ETF tilted toward high-quality companies. This serves as the 
core of your portfolio. It ensures diversification and that you’ll participate if the 
market does eke out gains or if it stabilizes. A quality tilt means you emphasize 


companies with solid earnings and low debt, which are precisely what you want 
in a slow downturn. 


e Thematic Growth (Automation/Al/Energy) Exposure: ~25% — About \$2,500 
dedicated to the sectors and themes that could still grow despite the gloom. 
This could be split among a couple of ETFs or a few stocks. For instance, you 
might put some into an Al & robotics ETF, some into a technology 
infrastructure fund (cloud computing, data centers), or an 
energy/renewables ETF if the transition is ongoing. You could also own one or 
two individual leaders in these fields (say a top semiconductor company 
enabling Al, or a dominant renewable energy firm), if you have done research and 
have conviction. The idea is to capture the secular winners that benefit from the 
very forces causing the world’s pain, but without going overboard on any single 
high-flyer or unproven startup. 


e Defensive/Dividend Stocks: ~15% — The remaining \$1,500 could go into 
defensive sector funds or reliable dividend-paying stocks. For example, a 
consumer staples ETF or a utilities ETF would fit here, or a basket of a few 
individual defensive stocks that are known for stable earnings and dividends. If 
you’re an income-oriented investor, you might choose a dividend aristocrats 
fund (companies with long histories of raising dividends). These holdings provide 
some return even if stock prices stagnate (thanks to the dividends) and tend to 
fall less if the overall market keeps sagging. 


Why this mix? It’s a balance between offense and defense appropriate for a slow 
decline. The portfolio isn’t as hunkered down as the fast-crash one; it assumes no 
immediate apocalypse, so it leans into some growth areas (Al, tech, etc.) to capture 
what upside is available. But it’s also prepared for tough times: nearly half of it (cash + 
defensives + quality core) is in very resilient assets. If the market indices were to 
gradually lose, say, 1% per month for a few years (a grinding bear market), a portfolio 
like this would likely lose less than that on average —- and might even gain in some 
portions — thus preserving capital better than a full equity exposure. And if there are 
occasional rallies (as often happen in long bear markets), your thematic and broad 
market pieces will participate enough that you don’t feel completely left out. 


Adjustments if Scenario B Likelihood Changes 


How do you manage your portfolio as the probability of a slow-bleed scenario changes 
over time? 


e When signs point to a grinding decline: You might notice economic data 
showing persistent weakness — e.g., unemployment creeping up month after 
month, corporate earnings growth stalling, consumer confidence low and not 
recovering. Maybe Al deployment news continues to scare people about jobs, 
and political tensions simmer without boiling over. If our LLM council and 
research point towards “no big crash, but no real recovery either,” that’s a cue to 
lean more into this Scenario B style. Concretely, you might gradually rotate some 
of your aggressive growth positions into more stable ones, increase your 
allocation to quality and defensive sectors, and keep a healthy chunk of your 
new savings in cash rather than immediately investing it. You might trim any 


holdings that seem especially vulnerable to prolonged weakness (for instance, a 
company that needs consumers to spend freely, or a highly leveraged player that 
could struggle if credit markets slowly tighten). 


e Ifthe slow bleed abates or a different scenario looms: Suppose instead that 
you start seeing signs of either a sharp crisis (which would push you more 
toward Scenario A) or conversely signs of real stabilization or boom (which 
would lead toward Scenario D, the surprisingly okay scenario). In that case, you 
would adjust away from the Scenario B mix. For example, if suddenly 
governments roll out big stimulus and Al is leading to a productivity boom (more 
optimistic scenario), you might decide to deploy more cash into growth assets, 
lighten up on defensives, and accept a bit more risk for more return — shifting 
toward Scenario D’s posture. Or if a crisis is brewing (moving toward A), you’d 
raise more cash and get even more defensive. The point is, Scenario B portfolio 
is sort of a “middle-of-the-road pessimism” stance. If the world tilts toward 
either extreme (fast crash or genuine recovery), you don’t stay static — you tilt 
your allocations accordingly. 


In a sentence: with Scenario B, you’re positioned for a tough grind, but you stay alert. If 
the plot of the movie changes, you rewrite the script for your portfolio before it’s too 
late. 


Scenario C — Chaotic Volatility / Whipsaw World 


Narrative of Scenario C 


Scenario C is a wild ride: a world of chaotic ups and downs, where extreme volatility is 
the norm. Picture an environment where every month (or week) brings a new shock and 
a new boom. It’s almost schizophrenic: one moment, doom seems Certain; the next, 
euphoria returns, only to swing back to panic. Why would this happen? Possibly 
because the world is grappling with powerful conflicting forces: - Breakneck 
technological change: Al advancements come in fits and starts — a breakthrough here, 
a scandal there. For example, a new Al system might dramatically boost some 
companies’ productivity (sending their stocks soaring), but then a different Al incident 
causes public outrage or fear (Sending markets tumbling). - Geopolitical flare-ups and 
cooldowns: Think of frequent regional conflicts or crises that flare up and then get 
patched over. A war threat causes a market drop; a sudden peace deal triggers a rally. 
Sanctions, trade wars, surprise alliances — it’s a constantly shifting chessboard. - 
Policy whiplash: Governments and central banks also swing between extremes — 
perhaps slashing interest rates one quarter (pushing asset prices up) only to hike them 
later when inflation jumps (pushing asset prices down). Or they introduce big 
regulations on tech one month, then subsidize the same tech the next. - Market 
sentiment overload: In the age of social media and instant information (and 
misinformation), sentiment can overshoot. Investors swing from fear of missing out 
(FOMO) to fear of losing everything, on headline news that comes rapid-fire. 


In short, the Scenario C world is unstable, but not one-directional. It’s neither a 
steady decline nor a one-off crash; it’s more like living in a perpetual storm where the 
wind keeps changing direction. For a historical taste, consider 2020: a sudden crash in 


March, a massive rebound by summer, then more volatility. Or parts of the late 2010s 
where trade war news caused big market moves up and down. Now multiply that 
intensity and frequency — that’s our whipsaw world. 


Psychologically, this scenario is very challenging for investors. The constant extreme 
moves can lead to bad decisions: chasing the rally when it’s almost over, panic-selling 
during the drop, then missing the next rebound. Many will feel whiplashed and 
exhausted. 


Market and Sector Behavior in a Whipsaw World 


Ina highly volatile, news-driven environment, you can expect: - High volatility across 
most asset classes: The VIX would routinely be elevated, and individual stocks swing 
widely. Correlations might shift rapidly - one week everything moves together (risk-off 
vs risk-on), another week it’s a stock-pickers market with huge dispersion. - Frequent 
rotations between sectors: One month tech and Al-related stocks rocket upwards on 
optimism, the next month they crash on fears of regulation or a tech backlash. 
Meanwhile, energy or defense stocks might spike when conflict fears rise, then drop 
when peace talks emerge. Essentially, leadership in the market keeps changing as 
narratives shift. - Opportunities and traps: Such volatility means there are often big 
winners and big losers in short periods. A nimble investor could, in theory, make great 
trades (buying after a crash, taking profit after a rally). But it’s extremely hard to do 
consistently without hindsight. For most, the risk is getting caught offside repeatedly. - 
Some sectors maintain an underlying uptrend: Even amid chaos, if we step back, we 
might see certain areas still trending up over the medium term. For example, 
companies tied to fundamental growth trends (Al infrastructure, cloud computing, 
cybersecurity, robotics, etc.) may be volatile but could still be net gainers over a year, 
because they have real demand supporting them. In contrast, sectors in secular 
decline (maybe traditional retail or certain industrials that are being automated away) 
might be volatile and in a downtrend overall. - Cash and safe assets as refuge during 
spikes of fear: In those moments when the pendulum swings to extreme fear, there will 
be rushes into cash, Treasury bills, or gold, which then reverse when fear subsides. So 
even the “safe” assets might bounce up and down somewhat, though less violently 
than stocks. 


In essence, everything is in motion, few things have a constant trend, and the market’s 
“mood” can flip on a dime based on the latest shock or surprise. 


Portfolio Design Principles for Scenario C 


Designing a portfolio for an environment of constant volatility is tricky —- you want to be 
able to handle swings without getting shaken out, and to benefit from the upside bursts 
without assuming they’ll last forever. Key principles include: 


e Stay diversified and balanced: This is not the time to have all your eggs in one 
basket or one theme, no matter how exciting. A mix of asset types and sectors is 
crucial because today’s star can be tomorrow’s laggard and vice versa. We want 
a core of stable holdings plus several satellite positions in different themes to 
capture upside. 


Don’t overreact to every move: Perhaps the most important (and hardest) 
principle is behavioral: resist the urge to trade on every piece of news. Ina 
whipsaw world, over-trading can kill your returns and psyche. Our 
recommended 3-6 month thesis horizon for positions (from Chapter 10) 
becomes a valuable discipline here — it forces you to ask “Is this new 
development truly altering my 3-6 month thesis, or is it just noise?” Often, you'll 
decide to stick to your plan despite short-term swings, unless something 
fundamentally changes. 


Use acore-satellite approach*: One way to handle volatility is to structure 
your portfolio into a *core portion that is fairly stable and always present, anda 
satellite portion that is more actively adjusted. The core might be broad index 
ETFs or very reliable stocks that you expect to hold through the turmoil. The 
satellite part can include thematic ETFs or stocks in those high-volatility, high- 
opportunity areas (Al, tech, etc.) with smaller position sizes. This way, the core 
keeps you anchored and participating in the overall market, while the satellites 
give you the potential to outperform if you ride a swing correctly — but if a 
satellite blows up, it’s a small part of your portfolio, not ruinous. 


Maintain moderate cash (liquidity): Similar to Scenario B, a whipsaw world 
justifies having some cash on hand. When volatility is extreme, having, say, 20% 
in cash means if markets plunge, you’re not 100% exposed and you can buy a 
dip; if markets spike irrationally, you’re not fully missing out either because you 
had core positions, and you can even take profits from some satellites and let 
cash build. Cash gives you the optionality to respond to swings rather than be 
forced to react. However, unlike Scenario A, you might not hold as much as 50% 
cash — that would drag too much when markets whip upward. It’s about finding a 
balance. 


Avoid complex leverage or timing bets: It might be tempting in a volatile 
market to try fancy trading strategies, like using options to bet on swings or 
leveraging up when you feel confident. Our advice is to avoid those in this 
scenario (really, in all scenarios for the readers of this book). High volatility can 
make options extremely expensive (implied volatility premium), and leverage 
can wipe you out on a single wrong-way swing. We’d rather structure the 
portfolio more simply and ride out volatility with less stress. 


In a sentence, the portfolio for Scenario C is built to be a volatility sponge — absorbing 
shocks, staying in the game, and opportunistically squeezing something out of the 
chaos, without blowing up. 


Example Portfolio Allocation for Scenario C (Approximately \$10k) 


Following those principles, an illustrative \$10,000 portfolio for a chaotic volatility 
scenario might look like: 


Cash: ~20% — Around \$2,000 in cash or ultra-safe assets. This is a cushion 
against volatility, an anchor for your emotions (Knowing not everything is at risk 
inthe market), and dry powder to trade or invest when opportunities arise after 
big swings. It’s a bit lower than in Scenario B because if the volatility has an 


overall sideways/slightly up bias, too much cash could cause you to lag. But it’s 
enough to matter. 


e Core Broad Market Holdings: ~35% — Roughly \$3,500 in broad, diversified 
equity exposure that you’re not touching often. This could be a broad index fund 
(like an S&P 500 or a global stock ETF). You might also split part of this into a 
low-volatility ETF or a balanced fund (though we aren’t focusing on bonds, 
some balanced funds have a mix of stocks and bonds which can smooth 
volatility). The idea is that this core is always invested, providing baseline growth 
if the world doesn’t actually end and ensuring you catch rebounds after sell-offs. 


e Thematic Satellite Bucket: ~40% — About \$4,000 spread across several volatile 
but promising themes or sectors. You might split this into, say, 4 chunks of 
\$1,000 each in different areas: 


e One chunk in an Al/Tech Infrastructure ETF or stock (e.g., companies providing 
cloud services, Al chips, data centers - likely to benefit from tech progress, but 
also subject to tech sector ups/downs). 


e One chunk in Robotics/Automation companies or an ETF, since automation 
could accelerate (volatile because they can soar on innovation news or drop on 
economic worries). 


e One chunk in EVs and Renewable Energy or perhaps Energy/Commodities 
more generally. This is partly a play on energy transition (which has growth 
potential) and partly a hedge that in chaotic times, commodities can swing 
strongly with geopolitics. 

e One chunk in Defense and Cybersecurity. In a whipsaw scenario, international 
conflicts and cyber threats are frequent, which could give defense stocks or 
cyber companies periodic boosts. 


Within this ~40% thematic bucket, diversify and keep position sizes modest. You’ve 
essentially made mini-portfolios in different high-volatility arenas. Any one theme could 
have a setback (for example, if a peace treaty is signed, defense stocks might dip, but 
maybe that coincides with tech rallying on good Al news). By spreading out, you’re not 
betting the farm on one storyline. - (Optional) Gold or Alternatives: ~5% — I’ll mention 
as an option: some investors in a chaotic world like to hold a small amount of a non- 
stock asset like gold or Bitcoin or other alternatives as a kind of “chaos hedge.” Our 
general stance in this book is cautious on crypto (so we wouldn’t suggest, say, 5% in 
Bitcoin unless you specifically want that and accept the risk), but a small gold or silver 
position, or a volatility ETF, could be considered by those who want an extra buffer. 
However, it’s optional and many readers may just stick to stocks, ETFs, and cash. If you 
skip this, you’d allocate that 5% extra across the other categories (or hold a bit more 
cash). We include this here just to acknowledge that in a whipsaw scenario, 
uncorrelated assets can help — but they come with their own risks and complexities. 


This Scenario C portfolio is actively managed in parts, but with a strong passive core. 
In practice, you might rebalance the thematic bucket more frequently — taking profits in 
one slice after a big run-up and perhaps reallocating to another slice that dipped. Or if 
one theme’s story deteriorates fundamentally, you might swap it out for a different 
theme that emerges (for example, if robotics completely saturates, and a new hot 
theme is, say, space technology and it fits the chaos narrative, you might rotate one slot
