into that). The core 35% and the cash 20% give you the stability to do this tinkering 
without your overall portfolio value gyrating as wildly as any single theme. 


Adjustments if Scenario C Likelihood Changes 


To use this scenario approach dynamically: 


e When the world is acting like a seesaw: If you are indeed in a period of 
frequent ups and downs - every few weeks there’s a scare then a surge — and you 
believe this is likely to persist, you’d maintain this general strategy. That means 
keeping that cash reserve, maintaining diversification, and not going all-in on any 
one outcome. You would also focus on process: regularly re-evaluating each 
position with your 3-6 month theses and using tools like the LLM council to 
separate genuine shifts from noise. For instance, if a sudden drop happens, 
you’d consult your research: is this drop giving me a chance to add toa 
fundamentally solid theme? Or is it the start of a real regime change? Having a 
council of Al opinions (Chapter 9) might help filter the barrage of news ina 
chaotic scenario. 


e If volatility subsides or explodes into something else: Volatility is cyclical. If it 
starts to calm down for genuine reasons (say international tensions ease for a 
sustained period, or the market kind of “gets used to” Al news so it’s less 
reactive), you might dial back the complexity. Perhaps you’d consolidate some 
of those thematic positions, or lower the cash drag if you feel more confident 
stability is returning (moving more toward Scenario D). Conversely, if the chaotic 
swings look like they’re about to break into a full-blown crisis (Scenario A) or if 
they settle into an ongoing downturn (Scenario B), you’d pivot accordingly. For 
example, if a whipsaw period suddenly lurches downward in a way that looks 
like the start of a real crash (maybe a credit event or a cascade of bad news), 
you’d increase cash, cut down some risk positions — essentially sliding into a 
more crash-proof posture. The beauty of having watched multiple scenarios is 
that you have a game plan for each and can morph from one to another over a 
few weeks or months, rather than reacting with no plan. 


In summary, the Scenario C approach is about staying agile but grounded. You accept 
that you can’t predict each swing, so you prepare for many and capitalize on some, 
while always preserving your core capital to fight another day. 


Scenario D — Partial Stabilization / “Surprisingly Okay for a While” 
Narrative of Scenario D 


Finally, let’s consider the “most optimistic” of our doom-flavored scenarios — which is 
to say, the least doom-y. Scenario D is one where, against many odds, things hold 
together better than expected, at least for some time. The world doesn’t exactly 
become paradise or solve allits issues, but we manage a period of partial 
stabilization: - Al Alignment and Regulation Efforts — Perhaps the world’s 
governments and tech companies get serious about Al safety. They implement some 
regulations, form international committees, and invest in alignment research. No one is 


claiming the long-term existential risk is solved, but the immediate uncontrolled Al race 
is slowed or made safer. This reduces the probability of an imminent Al catastrophe. - 
Geopolitical Cooling Off - Major powers, seeing the brink of disaster, pull back from 
the edge. We might not get world peace, but say conflicts don’t escalate further; maybe 
there are ceasefires or diplomatic breakthroughs that avoid the worst-case wars. - 
Economic Adjustments — The economy adapts to Al and automation somewhat 
productively. Yes, some jobs are lost, but new roles emerge or governments intervene 
with support. Productivity gains from Al actually start to show up in economic growth. 
Corporations find ways to use Al to improve profits and maybe even share some 
benefits (higher dividends, lower prices) that help consumers. - Surprising Resilience — 
Society, having been afraid of collapse, becomes more resilient and collaborative (at 
least temporarily). Think of how sometimes after a scare, humans band together or 
innovate out of fear. We might see rapid progress in renewable energy (to reduce 
resource conflicts), improvements in education (to reskill workers for an Al world), and 
so on. 


In this scenario, the world is still far from perfect — many underlying issues remain — but 
the timeline for doom gets pushed out. Instead of immediate collapse, we could get a 
few years of something resembling a boom, or at least a decent normalcy. A phrase like 
“surprisingly okay for now” captures it —- people are almost surprised that the worst 
hasn’t happened yet and in the meantime, some positive things are happening. 


For investors, Scenario D feels more like a classic bull market interlude, albeit with 
jitteriness. Markets could become risk-on, meaning investors are willing to buy stocks, 
especially in sectors with growth potential. If companies are benefiting from Al and 
productivity, earnings could rise and justify climbing stock prices. Confidence 
cautiously returns; interest rates might stabilize or even fall if inflation is under control, 
giving another boost to asset prices. There would still be volatility (the world’s not 
exactly tranquil), but generally, this scenario has the stock market trending upward or 
at least functioning normally, rather than collapsing. 


Market and Sector Behavior in a Stabilization Scenario 


If humanity gets a breather: - Growth sectors lead: Areas like technology, Al, 
automation, and renewable energy likely do very well because they have a double 
tailwind —- reduced existential fear and real economic benefit. Companies in these fields 
see strong demand, investments, and possibly supportive policies (like government 
spending on Al research or green infrastructure). - Corporate profits improve: If Al 
indeed boosts productivity, some companies could become more efficient and 
profitable, supporting their stock prices. Lower risk of immediate catastrophe also 
means investors apply higher valuation multiples (they’re willing to pay more for future 
earnings because they believe there will be a future). - Market breadth: Not just one or 
two stocks go up; there could be a broad rally. Even some “legacy” sectors might 
stabilize or bounce back as recession fears fade. For instance, banks and industrials do 
okay if the economy is chugging along with the help of tech. Consumer discretionary 
stocks (retail, travel, etc.) could recover if people feel a bit better about the future and 
spend some money. - Defensive assets lag: In a relative sense, safe-haven assets like 
certain bonds, gold, or defensive sectors (staples, utilities) might underperform growth 


sectors. They might still be positive, but if money is flowing into riskier bets, these 
become sources of funds. Investors rotate out of safety trades into more cyclical or 
growth-oriented trades. For example, high-dividend slow-growth stocks may lag the 
tech stocks in performance during this period. - Volatility moderates (somewhat): 
There might still be shocks (we haven’t eliminated Al risk, we’ve just managed it for 
now), so volatility wouldn’t vanish. But it could decrease compared to Scenario C. 
Markets might settle into a more typical pattern where big swings are less frequent, 
although given the overall context, we wouldn’t expect a 100% serene market. 


Think of Scenario D as akin to a “soft landing” or a postponed reckoning. Perhaps 
similar to periods like the mid-1990s after the Cold War ended — a sense of relief and an 
economic boom with the internet revolution, even though many feared chaos when the 
Soviet Union fell; or the post-2009 recovery when massive crises were averted by 
coordinated policy (though our scenario has more tech acceleration). Investors in these 
times can make solid gains, but should remain watchful for new risks on the horizon. 


Portfolio Design Principles for Scenario D 


In a scenario where things are surprisingly okay, the portfolio can afford to be more 
offensive than in the other scenarios — focused on capturing upside from growth trends 
-—while still retaining some caution because we know the world isn’t 100% safe. 
Principles: 


e Lean into secular growth opportunities: This is the time to overweight those 
exciting areas like Al, automation, robotics, clean energy, etc. If the doomsday 
clock is effectively slowed, these technologies may drive a lot of economic 
growth. Your portfolio should seek exposure to the sectors and companies that 
are innovating and expanding. We still do our due diligence (Chapter 8’s deep 
research on sectors and the LLM council to sanity-check hype), but in general, 
you’d allocate a larger share to these themes than you would in a darker 
scenario. 


e Maintain core diversification: Just because we’re more optimistic doesn’t 
mean put everything on red. You still keep a core of broad market or large-cap 
exposure, because maybe some traditional companies will do well too, and you 
don’t want to miss out or be too concentrated. Broad ETFs also protect you if 
you’re wrong about which specific themes will win. 


e Lower (but not zero) cash reserve: In an upswing scenario, too much cash can 
indeed “rob” you of returns (since cash yields might lag stock returns 
significantly if stocks are rising). So you can afford to hold less cash. However, 
we wouldn’t recommend zero — partly because having no liquidity at all is risky 
(something could still go wrong unexpectedly), and partly because even in good 
times, having some cash to take advantage of occasional pullbacks is useful. 
Also, your personal comfort matters: sleeping well at night knowing you have a 
bit of cash buffer is fine. 

e Watch valuations and avoid new bubbles: Optimism can breed excess. As you 
lean into growth, keep an eye on valuation — don’t just buy things at any price. 
Use the 3-6 month discipline: if a stock has run far beyond its fundamentals due 
to euphoria, be willing to trim or avoid it. In Scenario D, we assume rationality 


hasn’t completely left the building; if it starts to (like a wild speculative bubble 
forms), that might actually be a warning sign of a possible return to Scenario C or 
A if it pops. So enjoy the party, but don’t forget where the exits are, so to speak. 


Consider some international or broader exposure: If the stabilization is global, 
some emerging markets or international stocks might also do well (whereas in 
gloom scenarios, we might avoid certain regions). For instance, if Al is boosting 
productivity worldwide and conflicts are easing, countries in Asia or Europe 
could see strong growth too. So a bit of international diversification could be 
beneficial. Keep it to things you can easily invest in (international ETFs, etc., 
since we’re avoiding illiquid stuff). 


In summary, the Scenario D portfolio skews toward optimistic growth but with an 
underlying respect for risk (because we know from the book’s premise that the end-of- 
world concerns aren’t gone, just delayed). 


Example Portfolio Allocation for Scenario D (Approximately \$10k) 


Here’s an example of how a \$10,000 portfolio might be allocated if you believe we’re in 
a period of partial stabilization: 


Cash: ~10% — Only about \$1,000 in cash or equivalents. This is mainly an 
emergency/opportunity fund. It’s lower than in any other scenario because we 
don’t anticipate an immediate need to deploy a lot after a crash (no crash in this 
scenario) and we don’t fear constant drawdowns. However, that \$1k is still 
there if something suddenly changes or if a great buy-the-dip moment appears. 


Broad Market & Quality Core: ~35% — Around \$3,500 in broad index funds ora 
mix of broad market and a quality-tilted fund. This core holds the anchor of your 
portfolio in the general economic growth. In scenario D, broad indices likely do 
well (since many companies profit from the stability and tech gains). A quality tilt 
can ensure you still emphasize stronger companies, but you might even include 
some small-cap or growth index exposure here for more punch, as long as it’s 
diversified. 


High-Growth Thematic Exposure: ~50-55% — The majority, say \$5,000 to 
\$5,500, goes into the high-conviction growth themes. This could be subdivided 
into a few categories like: 


Al & Cloud: Significant investment in Al-related stocks or ETFs (semiconductors, 
software, cloud computing providers, etc.). These are the picks-and-shovels of 
the Al boom. 


Robotics & Automation: Companies that make robots, factory automation 
systems, Al-driven enterprise software — essentially those driving the 
productivity leap. 

Electric Vehicles & Clean Energy: If we have some breathing room, society will 
likely keep pushing the energy transition (plus it can be a source of growth 
investment). EV makers, battery tech, solar/wind power companies, etc., could 
be in this mix. 


Defense/Cyber (selectively): Interestingly, even in a stabilization scenario, 
defense and cybersecurity might continue to get funding — partly to ensure things 


stay stable. But this might be a smaller portion here compared to scenario C. It’s 
more of a hedge because even a “good” scenario D acknowledges ongoing 
serious problems. 


You don’t need to include every single theme — focus on 2-4 that you understand well 
and believe in. The key is that your portfolio is now oriented toward sectors of the 
future, under the assumption that we’ll have a future. This is the time you might take 
larger positions in individual superstar companies (if you have clear favorites from your 
research) because the risk of total market collapse is perceived lower. Still, any single 
stock should not be an outsized portion of your total - diversification within this growth 
basket is wise. - Minimal Defensive Allocation: ~0-5% — You might notice we didn’t 
explicitly allocate a chunk to staples/utilities in this scenario. That’s because ina 
genuinely improving environment, those are less attractive. However, if you personally 
like having something there (maybe a bit of dividend yield or just to cover bases), you 
could put perhaps 5% in a defensive ETF or a bond fund. It’s optional. Scenario D 
assumes you don’t need much in defensive positioning because the overall conditions 
are favorable. 


This portfolio is clearly the most aggressive of the four scenarios. It bets on humanity 
getting by for a while and focuses on growth. If markets indeed rally and technology- 
driven sectors thrive, such an allocation could outperform a cautious portfolio by a lot. 
Of course, if our assumption is wrong and a shock hits, this kind of portfolio would get 
hurt more than the others — which is why, in practice, you always maintain the flexibility 
to shift if needed. But while Scenario D holds, you are inclined to “make hay while the 
sun shines.” 


Adjustments if Scenario D Likelihood Changes 


Using scenario D in context means: 


e When stabilization seems to be happening: If multiple indicators start pointing 
toward improvement - e.g., no major crises for a while, constructive policy 
developments, bullish signals from the economy, positive results in Al alignment 
efforts —- you’d lean into this scenario. That means progressively tilting your 
portfolio to be more growth-heavy: deploying more of your idle cash into 
investments, increasing positions in tech and other favored sectors, and maybe 
taking some profits out of any hedges you had (like gold or excess defensives). 
You might also extend your investment horizon on some holdings — in a stable 
scenario, you might be comfortable holding certain stocks for a year or more, 
whereas in a doom scenario you might have only committed for a few months. 
The sense of urgency is less, though complacency must still be avoided. 


e If cracks start to appear again: We must be realistic - Scenario D could be 
temporary. If, for instance, a serious new conflict erupts, or a misstep in Al leads 
to ascare, or economic data suddenly worsen, you need to be ready to rotate 
out. That means reducing some of those aggressive allocations and raising cash 
or adding defensive positions before a full-blown reversal. Essentially, you’d 
start migrating the portfolio back toward whichever other scenario now seems 
likely - maybe back to C if volatility returns, or even to A if it looks dire. The 


benefit of having had a Scenario D period is hopefully your investments gained 
value, giving you a larger capital base to protect. Then you convert some of those 
gains into safer assets as storm clouds gather again. 


Think of Scenario D like the eye of a hurricane — a calm in the middle of turmoil. You 
enjoy the calm, but you don’t assume the storm is gone forever. You take advantage of 
good weather, but keep an umbrella handy. 


How to Use Scenarios in Practice: Base Case and Tilts 


We’ve outlined four distinct scenarios (A through D), each with a narrative and a model 
portfolio. But how do you actually use this framework as an investor navigating an 
uncertain future? The key is to identify which scenario (or mix of scenarios) the 
current environment most closely resembles, position your portfolio accordingly 
as a base, and then adjust (tilt) as new information emerges. 


1. Identify Your Current Base Scenario: 

Start by asking: “Which of these scenarios does today feel most like?” This requires 
synthesizing a lot of input - economic data, news, your sector analysis, maybe even gut 
feel. Our Chapter 10 system, including the LLM Council, can help here. For example, 
you might prompt your Al council with: “Given the latest news and data, does itseem 
like we’re headed for a sudden crash, a slow decline, wild volatility, or some 
stabilization?” You (and the council) might not agree 100%, but you’ll get a sense of 
probability for each scenario. Choose a base case for now - essentially, pick the 
scenario that you believe has the highest likelihood at present. 


Maybe the signs of late 2025 (as a hypothetical) show a lot of volatility but no clear 
trend, so you lean towards Scenario C as your base. Or perhaps things look eerily calm 
but with underlying tech progress, nudging you to Scenario D. Or you see multiple red 
flags in credit markets and geopolitics, making you opt for Scenario A preparations. 
There’s no one right answer; it’s an analytical judgment call. 


2. Construct Your Portfolio to Match that Scenario (Base Allocation): 

Using the examples we gave, or your own variation on them, structure your portfolio in 
line with that base scenario. This means deciding the allocations to cash vs stocks, 
defensive vs growth, broad vs thematic, etc., according to the principles of the 
scenario. In practice, if you already have a portfolio, this might mean making some 
trades to reach the desired mix. For instance, if you decide Scenario B (slow decline) is 
the base, you might raise a bit of cash, sell a couple of highly speculative positions, and 
buy some quality or defensive stocks/ETFs. You don’t have to be exact to the 
percentages we listed, but you use them as a guideline. The result is that your portfolio 
is now positioned for the world you think you’re in. 


3. Tilt Gradually, Don’t Flip Rashly: 

Life is dynamic. New information will come next week, next month. The idea is not to 
jump frantically from one scenario portfolio to another with every headline — that would 
be self-defeating. Instead, monitor the world and adjust in increments. Let’s say your 
base is Scenario B (slow bleed), but over a couple of months, you notice things are 
getting more volatile and unpredictable — edging towards Scenario C. You might then do 


a partial shift: increase your cash a bit (for safety and opportunistic buys), and perhaps 
add a new thematic position that thrives on volatility (like a defense stock), while 
keeping most of your holdings intact. You don’t throw out the whole playbook, you just 
tilt the angles. 


Similarly, if you were in Scenario C and it starts looking more like a real crash looming 
(Scenario A), you steadily cut down some of your more volatile positions and raise cash 
or buy protective broad ETFs. Or if you were in Scenario C and instead things calm and 
improve (Scenario D), you slowly deploy cash into more growth and trim the hedges. 


The mantra is “evolution, not revolution” when shifting scenarios. Markets often 
punish the hyper-reactive. By making gradual shifts, you avoid selling everything at the 
worst possible time or buying at the absolute peak because of a false alarm. You’re 
always somewhat diversified so that if you’re a bit off in your assessment, you won’t be 
completely wrong-footed. 


4. Set Reassessment Intervals: 

One practical tip: schedule regular check-ins with yourself (and your Al tools, if using) 
to formally reassess scenarios. It could be quarterly, or whenever a major event 
happens (whichever comes first). In that check-in, review key metrics and news: - Is the 
market trend and volatility aligning with my current scenario assumption? - Are there 
new threats or solutions on the horizon (Al news, political shifts) that change the 
probabilities? - How is my portfolio performing and would it hold up well if the 
alternative scenario happened instead? 


If you find that the world has shifted, you then plan out the tilts you’ll implement. This 
way, you’re proactive and systematic, rather than purely reactive. It also helps remove 
some emotion — you’re doing a scheduled review, not panicking in real-time at every 
Twitter alert. 


5. Avoid Whipsawing Yourself: 

Importantly, scenario-based investing is not about bouncing between fear and greed. 
It’s about having a mental model of possible futures and adapting as one becomes 
more likely than another. If you find you changed your scenario stance twice this week, 
something is off — either the world truly had two back-to-back huge developments 
(rare), or you are over-trading on noise. Use the tools from previous chapters: e.g., 
Chapter 9’s LLM Council can help filter sensational headlines from truly regime- 
changing information. Make sure any significant portfolio shift is justified by a clear 
change in the medium-term outlook, not just a blip. 


By approaching it this way, you essentially have a navigation system for your investing. 
The scenarios are like four possible maps; you start on the one that looks right, and if 
you realize you’re actually on a different terrain, you switch maps accordingly. But you 
always have a map —a plan -as opposed to wandering aimlessly. 


Integrating Scenario Thinking with the 3-6 Month Investment System 


You might be wondering how this high-level scenario approach meshes with the 
detailed 3-6 month stock-picking system we introduced earlier (Chapter 10). Let’s 
clarify that, because both are important: 


e Position-Level Analysis (Bottom-Up): Earlier chapters taught us to analyze 
individual investments: picking stocks or ETFs by studying their fundamentals, 
charts, sector outlook, and consulting the LLM Council for multiple viewpoints. 
For any given stock on your watchlist, you’d assess, “/s this a good position to 
hold for the next 3-6 months given what we know?” You’d consider factors like 
its recent big moves (Chap 6 insights), the macro backdrop (Chap 7), sector 
trends (Chap 8’s deep research), and get Al second opinions (Chap 9). The 
output might be a conviction level or a decision to buy, hold, or avoid that 
position. 


This remains crucial. Even in a scenario like A or D, you still want your specific holdings 
to be well-researched and justified. For example, if your Scenario C plan calls for 
owning a robotics ETF, you should still examine that ETF’s composition, the outlook for 
the robotics industry, etc. We don’t throw stock-picking principles out the window; we 
just apply them within the scenario context. 


e Portfolio-Level Design (Top-Down): What we’re adding with Chapter 12 is the 
view from above: given a scenario, how do we allocate among those individual 
positions and asset classes? This is about weighting and selection at a higher 
level. The scenario tells us what mix of assets we want (how much cash, how 
much broad market vs thematic, how defensive or aggressive to be). It provides 
a sort of risk-on vs risk-off dial setting for the portfolio as a whole. 


Think of it this way: the scenario context is the stage, and the individual positions 
are the actors. You still audition each actor carefully (with your analysis tools). But the 
type of play you’re casting for (tragedy, comedy, etc.) determines which actors you pick 
and how many of each role. In Scenario A’s tragedy, you want a lot of stoic, solid 
characters (cash, defensives) and only a few risky heroes. In Scenario D’s hopeful story, 
you cast more bold, growth-oriented stars. 


In practical steps when building your portfolio: 1. Decide scenario > set allocation 
targets (top-down). For example, decide “I’m in Scenario B mode, so | want ~30% cash, 
~40% broad ETFs, ~30% mixture of Al/automation and defensive stocks”. 2. Select 
specific investments for each part (bottom-up). Now choose which broad ETF (e.g., 
SPY or a total market fund?), which defensive stocks or ETF (maybe a utilities ETF ora 
couple of stable blue chips?), which Al/automation plays (maybe that robotics ETF or 
two specific tech stocks you researched). 3. Run each potential investment through 
your diligence process. Maybe you create a mini “dossier” (from Chapter 10 method) 
for each major holding, even if it’s an ETF or a stock, to confirm it’s a good choice in 
current conditions. 4. As time passes, manage at both levels: If a certain stock’s 
thesis breaks or it hits your price target, you might sell it— that’s a position-level 
decision. You’d then find a replacement that serves a similar role in the portfolio (or 
adjust weights a bit). Separately, if the whole scenario shifts, you adjust the top-down 
allocation targets, which then guides which types of positions to add or trim. 


This integrated approach helps avoid two extremes: the top-down only approach 
(where you might perfectly allocate by scenario but pick terrible stocks/ETFs to execute 
it), and the bottom-up only approach (where you might pick good stocks individually 
but end up with a portfolio that’s too risky or too conservative for the actual world 


because you didn’t see the big picture). By marrying them, you aim to hold strong 
individual positions that collectively make sense for the world’s regime. 


Let’s illustrate briefly. Suppose we’re in Scenario C (volatile whipsaw world). Top-down, 
you decide on 20% cash, 40% broad, 40% thematic split. Now bottom-up: for the broad 
part, you consider an S&P 500 ETF vs a total world ETF. You analyze if U.S. or 
international looks better (maybe the U.S. market has more of the Al winners, so you 
lean that way, for instance). For the thematic part, you list out candidates: say you 
identified a few ETFs (tech, defense, clean energy) and perhaps a couple of individual 
stocks (like a major Al chip maker). You then use your research method: check their 
recent performance, the fundamentals, maybe ask the LLM council, “Is this company 
likely to outperform in a high-volatility environment?” After that, you choose the ones 
you’re most confident in. You execute your plan, and you monitor both the positions 
(are they doing what you expected? any red flags?) and the scenario (is the world still in 
chaos or settling into a trend’). 


This way, scenarios provide a framework and guardrails for portfolio construction, while 
your 3-6 month system ensures you’re not just buying things blindly because they fit a 
category — you’re still insisting on sound reasoning for each investment. 


Warnings, Caveats, and Humility 


Before we close out the chapter with a summary and some homework, we need to 
underscore a few important warnings and disclaimers. This is especially important 
given the dire nature of our topic (“end of the world”) and the speculative element of 
scenario planning. 


1. Scenarios are Simplified Models: 

Reality doesn’t read our book or follow our four neatly defined paths. The future could 
combine elements of all scenarios, or switch between them abruptly in ways that catch 
everyone off guard. For example, we might see a slow decline (Scenario B) that 
suddenly triggers a fast crash (Scenario A), or a volatile period (C) that eventually 
resolves into a boom (D) before something else hits. The scenarios are meant to help 
you think and prepare, but they are not predictive certainties. Don’t fixate on them as 
literal forecasts. Use them as flexible guides. 


2. No Portfolio is Truly “Doom-Proof”: 

We can talk about being defensive or resilient, but the truth is, in a genuine global 
collapse scenario (let’s say a nuclear war or a permanent Al catastrophe), financial 
portfolios would be the least of our worries. Even short of that, there are extreme events 
that can overwhelm any strategy — governments can freeze markets, currencies can 
hyperinflate, banks can fail and prevent access to cash, etc. The point is, these 
portfolio plans improve your odds in most turbulent scenarios, but they don’t 
guarantee safety or profits. There is no magic asset allocation that wins no matter 
what. What we aim for is better odds and better decision-making, not invincibility. 


3. Emotional and Behavioral Challenges: 
It’s one thing to outline these moves in a book; it’s another to actually execute them in 
real life under stress. In a fast crash, can you hold your nerve and not panic sell at the 


bottom? In a whipsaw market, can you resist chasing fads? In a slow bleed, can you 
endure boredom and negativity without abandoning your plan? Be humble about the 
difficulty. Even professionals struggle with this. One reason we emphasize process (like 
the 3-6 month system and LLM council) is to create some rules and rational 
checkpoints to counteract emotional impulses. Still, recognize that you’re human 
(unless you’re an Al reading this, in which case, recognize humans are human!). Build 
habits that help — for instance, writing down your scenario thesis and intended actions 
can remind you, when the storm hits, what you planned to do. 


4. This is Not Personal Financial Advice: 

We said it earlier but it bears repeating as a disclaimer: the scenarios and portfolios 
discussed are general ideas, not tailored to your personal situation. Every investor 
has different risk tolerance, time horizons, financial needs, and alternatives. \$10k is 
just an example amount; you might be working with \$2k or \$200k or more — the 
percentages could still apply, but the stakes differ. We don’t know your personal 
situation, So we can’t tell you exactly what to do. If you’re unsure, consider consulting a 
licensed financial advisor, especially one open-minded about unconventional 
scenarios. Use this book to inform yourself, but make decisions that fit your life. 


5. Acknowledge Uncertainty and Stay Agile: 

Perhaps the biggest lesson to take is humility in the face of the unknown. Nir’s 
worldview (and by extension this book’s perspective) is that very hard times are likely 
near. But even Nir would agree that we could be wrong about specifics — maybe Al 
won’t collapse civilization in 2026, maybe something else entirely will shape the 2030s. 
The reason we plan for multiple scenarios is precisely because we don’t have a crystal 
ball. Always be willing to update your beliefs and strategy as evidence changes. Rigid 
certainty (“I know exactly what will happen and when!”) is dangerous in investing. 
Ironically, being too sure of doom can be as Costly as being too complacent — imagine 
sitting 100% in cash because you’re sure a crash will happen by June, but then it 
doesn’t and a bull market runs away. Better to have a plan for doom and a plan for less 
doom. 


In summary, stay humble, watchful, and adaptive. We are trying to navigate 
unprecedented territory. If you maintain a mindset of continuous learning and caution 
balanced with courage, you’ll be as prepared as one can reasonably be. 


Summary and Next Steps 


We’ve covered a lot in this pivotal chapter. Here are the key takeaways and a bit of 
“homework” to cement your understanding: 


Chapter 12 Summary: Scenario Portfolios 

- We identified four scenario “flavors of doom” for the near future: - Scenario A- 
Sudden Shock / Fast Crash: A rapid, fear-driven market collapse triggered by an acute 
crisis (Al disaster, war, financial panic). Everything plunges at once. Portfolio: 
Emphasize capital preservation — lots of cash (®40-50%), broad safe equity exposure 
(indexes, defensives ¥50%) and only a small slice for high-conviction bets. Goalis to 
survive the crash and have dry powder. - Scenario B - Slow Bleed / Grinding Decline: 
A prolonged period of stagnation and malaise (job automation pain, social unrest, weak 


growth) without a singular collapse event. Markets drift down or sideways over time. 
Portfolio: Emphasize quality and resilience - moderate cash (®20-30%), a core of 
broad/quality stocks (®30-40%), plus select exposure to automation/Al winners (®20- 
30%) and some defensives/dividend-payers (10-20%). Goal is to not bleed out with 
the market, and capture the few growth areas that still work. - Scenario C —- Chaotic 
Volatility / Whipsaw World: A tumultuous environment of constant mini-crises and 
relief rallies (extreme ups and downs). News and sentiment flip frequently. Portfolio: 
Emphasize balance and agility - hold some cash (®20%) and a stable core (®30-40% 
broad market), then diversify the rest (30-40%) into several volatile themes (Al, 
robotics, energy, defense, etc.) with small positions. Avoid leverage, don’t over-trade; 
use your core to anchor and satellites to play swings. Goal is to ride the storm without 
getting thrown off. - Scenario D - Partial Stabilization / “Surprisingly Okay”: A 
scenario where disaster is deferred (efforts on Al safety, geopolitical easing) and we get 
a period of growth or at least stability. Markets are more bullish and risk-on. Portfolio: 
Emphasize growth opportunities — lower cash (10-15%), solid core exposure (30-40% 
in broad market), and a larger allocation (®45-60%) to high-growth sectors (Al, tech, 
automation, EV/renewables, etc.). Still keep diversified and be ready to pull back if 
needed, but allow yourself to benefit from the boom. Goal is to participate in the upside 
while it lasts, without being reckless. - For each scenario, we described: - The narrative 
(what’s happening in the world). - Market/sector behaviors to expect. - Portfolio 
design principles to prioritize (e.g. preservation vs growth). - An illustrative \$10k 
portfolio allocation with percentages and rationale. - How to adjust (tilt) the portfolio 
if probability of that scenario rises or falls. - We stressed that these portfolios are 
examples. They are not personalized advice or guaranteed solutions, but rather 
starting points you can modify. - The chapter also explained how to use scenarios in 
real life: - Pick a base scenario that best fits the current climate. - Allocate your 
portfolio roughly according to that scenario’s plan. - Tilt, don’t flip: adjust gradually as 
new evidence shifts your view of which scenario is playing out. - Use a structured 
approach (e.g., quarterly reviews) and tools like the LLM Council to avoid knee-jerk 
reactions and filter noise. - We discussed integrating scenario thinking with the 3-6 
month investment system from earlier: - Scenario = top-down allocation framework 
(how much to each category). - 3-6 month research = bottom-up selection (which 
specific assets to hold). - Both work together: you still research each pick, and you 
make sure your overall mix fits the world’s situation. - Finally, we included important 
warnings: - Reality can defy scenarios (blend them or change suddenly). - No strategy is 
foolproof in extreme collapse (investing has inherent risk). - Emotions are tough — be 
humble and disciplined. - This is not individualized financial advice; it’s a general guide. 
- Stay ready to adapt; no one can predict the future with certainty, so keep learning and 
adjusting. 


Homework: Applying Scenario Thinking to Your Portfolio 

Now it’s your turn to put these ideas into practice. You don’t need actual money to do 
this homework — it can be a thought exercise or done on paper (though if you are 
investing real money, it’s directly applicable). Take some time to work through these 
steps: 


16. Choose Your Current Scenario: Based on everything you know and feel about 
the world right now, which of the four scenarios (A, B, C, or D) do you think we 


are closest to at this moment? Write a short paragraph explaining why you 
chose that scenario. What evidence or intuition led you there? (For example: “I 
think we’re in Scenario C (Whipsaw World) because every week there’s a big 
tech news that swings stocks, plus the ongoing trade tensions cause volatility. 
We’re not collapsing, but it’s very unstable...” etc.) 

17. Draft a Portfolio for That Scenario: Imagine you have \$10,000 (adjust the 
number up or down if you actually have more or less, the percentages matter 
more than the absolute dollars). Outline an ideal portfolio allocation for that 
money under the scenario you picked. Be specific: How much would you keep in 
cash? What broad market fund or ETF would you use, and what percentage? 
What thematic or sector investments would you include (and roughly how much 
in each)? Do you have any individual stocks you strongly want in there for that 
scenario? Essentially, write down a mini plan of how you’d allocate and why. 
(This is just for you — an exercise in structuring your thoughts. You can compare it 
with the examples in this chapter and see where you differ or agree.) 

18. Set a Scenario Switch Trigger (Plan B): Write a few “if-then” thoughts for how 
you would know your chosen scenario is no longer the right one and what you’d 
do about it. For instance: “If | see a sudden 20% market drop and central banks 
panicking, that means we’re shifting into Scenario A (Fast Crash), so | would 
move to raise more cash and add defensives immediately.” Or conversely: “If 
peace treaties are signed and Al labs all pause development for a while, that 
looks like Scenario D (Stabilization), so I’d start deploying more cash into growth 
ETFs.” Come up with at least one signpost for switching scenarios and what 
action you would take. This will train you to pre-plan your responses to major 
changes, rather than improvising under stress. 


Doing this homework will help solidify your understanding of scenario-based investing. 
It also personalizes the chapter’s lessons to your situation. Remember, the aim is not to 
be locked into one scenario forever, but to be ready for any of them. By explicitly 
thinking through “What if I’m wrong and it’s actually a different scenario?”, you’ll be 
much quicker to adapt when the time comes. 


With scenario portfolios in your toolkit, you are now equipped to face an uncertain 
future with a structured approach. You know how to change your sails depending on the 
wind, without losing sight of the shore (your long-term goals and the survival of your 
capital). 


In the next chapter, we will [transition to whatever the next chapter might be about, if 
applicable — since this is the final scenario design chapter, perhaps next is about 
execution, monitoring, or concluding thoughts]. For now, take a moment to digest these 
scenarios, do the homework, and consider discussing your plan with a trusted friend or 
advisor. Planning for the end of the world is heavy stuff, but by doing so, you’ve actually 
increased your chances of coming out okay on the other side. Good luck, stay vigilant, 
and stay flexible. The end (of the chapter) is just the beginning (of being a truly prepared 
investor). 


Chapter 13 — Case Studies: How the System Would 
Have Reacted 


Introduction: Learning from Real Scenarios 


Theory is useful, but seeing a system in action is invaluable. In this chapter, we walk 
through three real historical case studies to illustrate how our investment system 
(developed in Chapters 5-12) might have behaved. By applying the book’s methods to 
past events, we can examine when the system would have worked well, when it would 
have been uncertain, and when it might have struggled or stayed out. These examples 
make it clear that this approach is not a magic money machine — it’s a structured way to 
think causally about markets, with Al as a guide, not an oracle. We'll cover: 


e Case Study 1: Tesla & the Musk/Twitter Drama (an individual stock saga). 
e Case Study 2: The Al Chips Boom (a sector/thematic surge and bubble debate). 
e Case Study 3: The COVID-19 Crash & Aftermath (a macro shock and recovery). 


Each case study will follow a structured approach: a brief recap of what happened, how 
our system would prepare (using charts, parameters, and scenarios available at that 
time), key decision points with a simulated “LLM Council” of Al advisors, and an honest 
assessment of outcomes. 


Disclaimer: These case studies are illustrative thought experiments. We are 
applying our method retroactively with the benefit of hindsight (which we will 
try to mitigate by sticking to information that was knowable at the time). This is 
not a rigorous backtest or financial advice — it’s a narrative tool to see the 
system’s logic in action. Past events are used to train our thinking, not to 
claim we would have predicted them perfectly. Past performance is not 
indicative of future results. 


With that in mind, let’s dive into the first case. 


Case Study 1 — Tesla’s Wild Ride (2020-2023): From S&P Euphoria to 
Twitter Overhang 


Few stocks captured the market’s imagination in the 2020s like Tesla (TSLA). In a span 
of just a few years, Tesla went from an ambitious niche automaker to a trillion-dollar 
company, then faced steep drawdowns amid executive antics and rising competition. 
This case study examines how our system might have navigated Tesla’s extreme 
volatility and high-profile events — including inclusion in the S&P 500, Elon Musk’s 
stock sales (to fund his Twitter acquisition), and emerging competitive threats. 


1. Historical Recap: Tesla’s Soaring Rise and Bumpy Descent 


Time period: Late 2020 through late 2022 (with notes on 2023). In 2020, Tesla’s stock 
went on a spectacular run. It rallied roughly 700% in 2020 alone, turbocharged by 
excitement over electric vehicles (EVs), stock splits, and the news that it would join the 


S&P 500 index in December 2020[1]. On the day of its S&P 500 debut, Tesla’s stock 
closed at a record high (~$695/share pre-2021-split), after jumping ~70% in the month 
following the inclusion announcement([1]. This marked the peak of euphoria -— Tesla 
became the most valuable carmaker in the world and the largest-ever new entrant to 
the S&P 500, forcing index funds to buy $85 billion worth of its shares[2]. 


The next two years saw major reversals. In November 2021, CEO Elon Musk polled his 
Twitter followers asking if he should sell 10% of his Tesla stake. They voted yes, and 
Musk began offloading shares. The stunt immediately wiped about $60 billion off 
Tesla’s market cap as the stock fell ~5% in a day[3][4] (and it fell over 15% in the days 
around the poll{5]). By late 2021, Tesla had hit an all-time high around $1,200/share (on 
a split-adjusted basis), then started to slide. 


In 2022, a broader tech bear market and Musk’s distraction with acquiring Twitter took a 
heavy toll. Musk sold tens of billions of dollars worth of Tesla stock to finance the 
Twitter deal, despite repeatedly saying he was “done” selling[6][7]. By late 2022, Tesla’s 
stock had lost roughly half its value since Musk launched his Twitter bid in April[8] 
(in fact, from the November 2021 peak to the end of 2022, TSLA was down nearly 70%). 
Tesla’s plunge was fueled by fears that Musk’s attention (and capital) were diverted to 
Twitter, as well as rising competition and higher interest rates deflating high-growth 
stocks. 


2023 update: Tesla’s stock saw a strong rebound in the first half of 2023 (roughly 
doubling off the 2022 lows) as tech stocks recovered and optimism returned. However, 
competitive pressures have continued to mount. By late 2025, for example, Chinese EV 
maker BYD was outselling Tesla in some markets; Tesla’s sales in key European 
countries plunged in late 2025 (~20% down year-on-year in Germany) as it lost 
market share to Chinese rivals[9][10]. This underscores that Tesla’s story is ongoing — 
it’s not just a meme stock but a company facing real competitive and macro 
challenges. 


With that context, let’s see how our system would have approached Tesla’s wild ride, 
focusing on the period around late 2020 (surging optimism) through 2022 (harsh 
comedown). 


2. Preparing the Dossier: Tesla Ex Ante Analysis 


Imagine it’s late 2020, just before Tesla’s inclusion in the S&P 500. How would our 
system prepare to handle a stock like Tesla? 


e Chart Analysis (Chapter 5): Looking at a multi-year chart of Tesla in late 2020, 
an investor would see a near-vertical ascent. The stock had split 5-for-1 in August 
2020 and still kept roaring upwards. A physicist-minded view (per Chapter 5) 
would note the extraordinary momentum but also the physical reality that such 
exponential moves eventually encounter resistance (often in the form of profit- 
taking or news). We’d identify key past moves and inflection points: e.g. the early 
2020 COVID crash (Tesla fell sharply in March 2020 but rebounded), the battery 
day event in September 2020 (when Musk’s presentation initially disappointed 
and the stock dipped briefly), and the run into S&P inclusion. 


Company-Specific Parameters (Chapter 6): We build Tesla’s dossier of 
causally relevant parameters: 


Production & deliveries: Tesla’s quarterly production numbers and delivery 
growth were crucial. In late 2020, these were trending strongly upward (despite 
the pandemic) and the company had just reported consistent profits, a 
prerequisite for S&P 500 inclusion. 


Technology & products: The rollout of the Model 3 globally and plans for Model Y, 
plus Tesla’s advancements in battery tech and Autopilot, were bullish drivers. 
Any breakthroughs or delays here would be “flip factors.” 


CEO/Insider actions: Elon Musk’s behavior is a major parameter. His tweets 
and selling/buying of stock can sway Tesla massively. By late 2020, Musk hadn’t 
yet sold stock (in fact, he was famously not selling any shares for years), but this 
parameter would go from dormant to critically active by 2021 (when he began 
selling). 


Capital raises and inclusion: Tesla had taken advantage of the soaring stock to 
issue new shares (raising capital to fund growth). And S&P 500 inclusion itself 
was a one-time technical catalyst causing index funds to buy in[2]. We’d note 
that such technical surges can be double-edged -— they boost the stock into the 
event (as seen by +70% in a month/[1]), but often the enthusiasm can fade once 
the event passes. 


Competition: In 2020, the market narrative largely downplayed competition — 
Tesla was far ahead in EVs. But a diligent dossier would still list competition as a 
lurking factor (established automakers launching EV models, and emerging 
players especially in China). We now know, for instance, that by 2023-2025 
companies like BYD would seriously challenge Tesla[9], so competition is a 
parameter to keep on the radar even if it wasn’t top-of-mind in 2020’s hype. 


Macro and Sector Context (Chapters 7 & 8): At the end of 2020, the macro 
backdrop was unusual: the world was recovering from the initial COVID shock. 
Interest rates were near zero, and tech stocks were in a speculative fever. Our 
system’s macro layer would have identified a mostly bullish market regime 
(thanks to massive stimulus and no immediate recession in sight —- Scenario D, 
“partial stabilization,” in our terms). However, it would also flag extremely high 
valuations in growth stocks as a potential risk if interest rates were to rise (an 
early hint of Scenario B, “slow bleed,” that actually unfolded in 2022 when 
inflation and rate hikes arrived). For Tesla’s sector (EVs and clean tech): Deep 
Research (Chapter 8) would have noted that the EV revolution was a strong 
secular trend with policy support (e.g. many countries planning to phase out 
gasoline cars). Key sector flip factors included battery costs, regulatory 
incentives, oil prices, and consumer adoption rates. Overall, by end of 2020, the 
sector narrative was extremely optimistic — perhaps too optimistic — about 
Tesla’s dominance and growth. 


Avoiding Hindsight Bias: Crucially, as of late 2020 we would not know exactly 
when a pullback would happen. Our preparation would acknowledge that Tesla 
was ina highly non-linear, speculative phase. We might recall historical 


analogues (dot-com era high-fliers, etc.) to remind ourselves that trees don’t 
grow to the sky. We’d be ready for volatility. The dossier would explicitly state: 
“This stock can swing 30-50% on sentiment alone; position sizing and risk 
management are critical.” 


In summary, going into late 2020, our system would be excited about Tesla’s upside 
(strong trend, fundamental growth, Al/Autopilot optionality) but also cautious about the 
frothy sentiment and the outlier role of Musk’s behavior. We might tentatively classify 
the situation as Scenario D (partial stabilization with speculative excess) — markets are 
ok, Tesla’s story is great, but the speed of the rise suggests a flip to Scenario C or B 
(whipsaw or correction) could happen if sentiment shifts or if Musk does something 
unexpected. 


3. Key Decision Points and LLM Council Simulations 


Now let’s walk through three pivotal moments and imagine how we’d apply the 
method step-by-step at each decision point: 


Decision Point 1: Late December 2020 - Post S&P Inclusion Euphoria 


Situation: It’s just after Tesla’s inclusion in the S&P 500 (Dec 21, 2020). The stock 
touched record highs as index funds bought in. Everyone is talking about Tesla — it’s 
now worth over $600 billion, up 8-fold in a year. The news is mostly positive: EV demand 
is strong, Tesla had just delivered its first profitable year, and stimulus money is 
flowing. However, some whispers of concern exist: “Can this valuation be justified? Will 
there be a pullback now that the S&P inclusion is done?” By early January 2021, Tesla 
stock is extremely extended above its moving averages, a classic technical overbought 
condition. 


Dossier Highlights at the time: Company parameters were largely positive (delivery 
growth, tech edge), but one-time catalysts (like the inclusion) were now behind us. The 
macro was still risk-on (markets hitting all-time highs), though there were faint early 
signs of inflation picking up. Sector-wise, competition was still a minor story — for 
instance, NIO and other Chinese EV startups had seen their stocks surge too in 2020, 
suggesting EV enthusiasm was lifting all boats. The dossier would also note an 
upcoming flip factor: Q4 2020 earnings in a few weeks — would Tesla’s results live up to 
the hype? 


LLM Council Deliberation: We prompt our panel of Al advisors to assess Tesla in early 
January 2021, without knowledge of the future. They might respond as follows: 


e Advisor A (Bullish): “Tesla’s momentum is incredible and likely to continue near- 
term. The company is ahead of schedule on growth - we see rising deliveries and 
new factories coming online. With the S&P inclusion, more institutional money 
will hold the stock. Low interest rates also justify high growth valuations for now. 
| think a Scenario D (stabilization/uptrend) is still in play — any dips could be 
minor and met with eager buying. Perhaps maintain a position, maybe trim a 
little to lock some profit, but overall stay long.” 


e Advisor B (Neutral/Cautious): “Tesla is a great company, but the stock has run 
far ahead of fundamentals. After such a huge rally, a whipsaw (Scenario C) is 
quite possible — perhaps a 20% correction or a sideways period. We should not 
chase the stock at these highs. Musk’s unpredictable nature is a risk - who 
knows, he might do something like focus on another venture (little did we know: 
Twitter). My suggestion: take some profits here. We can love the company but 
reduce our position size to manage downside. Keep it on the watchlist for re- 
entry on a pullback or if new positive catalysts emerge (like a big earnings beat or 
new product announcement).” 


e Advisor C (Bearish): “The signs of a bubble are here. Tesla’s valuation in early 
2021 implies decades of flawless growth. The market is pricing perfection. 
Historically, when stocks go parabolic like this, a crash or at least a Scenario B 
slow bleed can follow if the macro shifts or if the narrative cracks. Remember, in 
a doom investing context we also worry about systemic issues — what if rising 
interest rates or some shock hits in 2021? Tesla could easily drop 30-50%. | 
would exit most or all of the position now. Better to miss out on some upside 
than to ride a huge drawdown.” 


Position Decision Logic: Hearing these perspectives, how would our system act? 
Likely with a balanced, risk-aware move: At this point, the system might trim the 
position significantly. For example, if we held, say, 10% of our portfolio in Tesla through 
2020 (benefiting from the run-up), we might sell enough to lock in a large gain, reducing 
it to perhaps a 2-3% position. We would not short the stock (that would violate our 
method of avoiding complex bets and not fighting the tape), but we recognize the 
asymmetric risk. Essentially, we’d play defense: raise cash from Tesla profits and 
redeploy some of it into other opportunities or hold it awaiting a clearer signal. The 
system’s strength here is humility - acknowledging that we don’t know if Tesla will keep 
roaring or pull back, but knowing we have a gain to protect. By trimming, we ensure that 
if Tesla keeps rising we still participate with a small stake, and if it falls, we’ve taken the 
lion’s share of profits off the table. 


Decision Point 2: November 2021 - Musk Starts Selling at the Peak 


Situation: Fast-forward to early November 2021. Tesla’s stock had been relatively 
range-bound for much of 2021, then broke out to new all-time highs around $1,200 in 
late October. The company had just reported strong Q3 2021 earnings and Hertz 
announced plans to buy 100,000 Teslas (sending the stock higher on that news). 
Optimism was back in full swing — until Elon Musk’s weekend Twitter poll on Nov 6, 
2021. He asked followers if he should sell 10% of his shares (worth roughly $20 billion). 
The majority said “Yes,” and within days Musk began selling. Tesla’s stock tumbled 
about 16% in the following week on fears of Musk offloading shares[5]. By Nov 8, it 
was already down ~5% intraday, and continued volatile swings thereafter. This is a 
classic company-specific shock: the CEO selling a large stake (for reasons ostensibly 
about taxes and Twitter polls) was notin any model’s forecast. 


Dossier Highlights at the time: Tesla’s fundamental trend was still strong - Q4 2021 
was expected to be another record quarter. However, our dossier’s insider action 
parameter would flash red. Musk selling was an unprecedented event for Tesla (he 


hadn’t sold significant shares since the company’s early days). Insider selling itself isn’t 
always bad (executives sell for many reasons), but such a public and large sale from 
Musk signaled potential trouble (or at least overvaluation in his eyes). The macro 
context by late 2021 was starting to shift: inflation had risen and the Fed was talking 
about tapering bond purchases, raising the specter of higher interest rates (a headwind 
for richly valued growth stocks in 2022). So our macro layer might be tilting from 
Scenario D towards a Scenario B (a slow bleed or correction coming), though the 
market was still near highs at that moment. Sector-wise, EV competition was heating 
up modestly — Ford’s Mustang Mach-E and other models were gaining traction, and 
Chinese EV makers were growing, hinting that Tesla’s dominance could eventually be 
challenged. But the immediate issue was Musk’s selling and what that implied (Does he 
know something? Is he just paying taxes? Will he come back?). 


LLM Council Deliberation: We convene the council in mid-November 2021, after 
Musk’s selling announcement and initial stock drop: 


e Advisor A (Bullish-ish): “This drop could be a buying opportunity. Yes, Musk 
selling is pressuring the stock, but he stated it’s to pay taxes and follow the poll - 
not because Tesla’s business is faltering. The fundamentals of Tesla remain 
strong; Q4 deliveries will likely be record-breaking. If the stock is down 15% from 
the peak, but the company outlook is intact, maybe Scenario C (whipsaw) is at 
play — a quick dip that could reverse. | would consider adding back some Tesla if 
we trimmed before, or at least hold what we have. Many long-term Tesla bulls 
might “buy the dip” here, assuming Musk’s sales are temporary.” 


e Advisor B (Moderate/Cautious): “Musk’s sale is indeed a fundamental change — 
it increases float and could weaken the stock’s momentum. We should heed the 
warning from our system’s parameters: a CEO selling a tenth of his stake is a big 
deal. It suggests that even Musk thinks taking some money off the table is wise. 
Coupled with the macro turning (Fed tightening coming), the risk is that this isn’t 
just a blip but the start of a Scenario B slow bleed in highly valued tech stocks. | 
advise continuing to stay mostly out or very light on Tesla. We can let the dust 
settle. If we still hold a small piece from before, it might be okay to keep a token 
position, but | wouldn’t bet big here until we see how the stock behaves post- 
Musk sales. Also, watch how the stock reacts around year-end and the next 
earnings — if it can’t rally despite good results, that’s a bad sign.” 


e Advisor C (Bearish): “This is likely the turning point. Musk’s behavior is a huge 
red flag about governance and focus. Additionally, | foresee more trouble: Musk’s 
poll hints at his fixation on Twitter/social media — indeed, it wouldn’t surprise me 
if he pursues something crazy like buying Twitter itself (we Als don’t know the 
future, but we gauge personalities!). The combination of insider selling, potential 
distraction of the CEO, and a looming macro tightening is screaming Scenario B 
or even a mini Scenario A (fast crash) for overvalued names. My stance: zero 
exposure to Tesla until it’s, say, 30-50% lower or the situation clarifies. Capital 
preservation is key.” 


Position Decision Logic: Our system’s likely move here would be defensive again. 
Given we had mostly trimmed in late 2020/early 2021, we might have only a small Tesla 


position left by Nov 2021. The prudent course now would be either to stay out (hold that 
small position or none at all) or if one is very nimble, perhaps do a short-term trade on 
the dip with tight risk limits. However, trading is not our main approach, and catching a 
falling knife is dangerous. The rational decision is to respect the parameter: CEO 
selling. Chapter 6 taught us that when real events like this occur, we update our thesis. 
Musk’s sale likely indicates the stock was overpriced (indeed it was, in hindsight). So 
the system would either exit completely or maintain only a token stake (maybe just 
enough to keep monitoring it). Crucially, the system at this point would shift Tesla into 
the “high-monitoring, not a core holding” list. We’d set alerts for key flip factors: e.g. if 
the stock falls to a strong support level or if we get news that Musk’s selling is done or if 
Q4 earnings blow out expectations, we might reconsider. Otherwise, we watch from the 
sidelines. 


This decision illustrates the system’s strength in avoiding major losses. By being mostly 
out of Tesla from late 2021 onward, an investor would avoid the brunt of the 2022 
collapse. 


Decision Point 3: Late 2022 - Twitter Chaos and a Potential Bottom 


Situation: By December 2022, the picture is grim. Tesla’s stock price has collapsed 
from about $400 (split-adjusted) in late 2021 to nearly $120 — a level not seen in over 
two years. The NASDAQ is in a bear market, and Tesla is underperforming even other 
tech stocks. Musk did end up buying Twitter in October 2022, and it’s going about as 
badly as one could imagine: he’s CEO of both companies, selling more Tesla shares to 
fund Twitter’s losses, and Tesla investors are furious that he’s not focused on the car 
business. News headlines chronicle Musk’s controversial moves at Twitter almost 
daily, while Tesla faces weakening demand in China and Europe (exacerbated by 
Musk’s tarnished personal brand and higher interest rates making car loans costlier). 
Many analysts have slashed price targets, some calling this “Musk’s Icarus moment.” 
Sentiment is extremely bearish. At the same time, valuation-wise, Tesla is now finally 
at more “normal” multiples (for the first time, it’s under 50x earnings, which for Tesla is 
almost cheap). Some contrarian investors are sniffing around: “Is this a generational 
buying opportunity or is it catching a falling knife?” 


Dossier Highlights at the time: The macro in late 2022 was in Scenario B—a slow 
bleed bear market, with recession fears for 2023, though inflation was showing signs of 
peaking. There was also the ongoing war in Ukraine and energy crisis dampening the 
global outlook (macro layers from Chapter 7 clearly negative). Tesla’s company- 
specific parameters were a mix of negatives and a few positives: Musk’s distraction 
(negative), recent price cuts Tesla implemented to support demand (negative signal on 
pricing power), but also the fact that Tesla’s earnings were still growing and the 
company had a robust balance sheet (positive fundamentals amid the chaos). Sector- 
wise, 2022 saw legacy automakers (GM, Ford, etc.) struggle and EV startups like Rivian 
and Lucid crash — not just Tesla. So the EV sector parameter indicated a cyclical 
downturn in enthusiasm. A key “flip factor” now would be Musk stepping aside from 
Twitter or refocusing on Tesla — any sign of that could flip sentiment fast. Another 
would be Tesla’s Q4 2022 delivery numbers (due in early January 2023) — a surprisingly 
strong or weak number could respectively restore confidence or confirm the worst 
fears. 


LLM Council Deliberation: We convene the council around Dec 2022 when Tesla is 
~$120: 


e Advisor A (Cautiously Bullish Contrarian): “Tesla’s stock has been crushed — 
down ~70% from its peak. Often, such extreme moves can overshoot on the 
downside. The business is still fundamentally profitable and growing (though 
slower). Musk has indicated he might find a new Twitter CEO, which could 
happen and remove a big overhang. If our portfolio has a high risk tolerance, this 
might be a time to dip a toe back in- perhaps Scenario C (whipsaw) is coming, 
where this steep drop is followed by a sharp relief rally. However, size small: 
maybe initiate a small long position, say 1-2% of the portfolio, as a speculative 
contrarian bet that in 6 months sentiment will be better. Use a stop or mental exit 
if the stock falls, say, another 20% from here. Essentially, start easing back in 
gradually.” 


e Advisor B (On the Fence): “It’s tempting to buy here, but catching bottoms is 
hard. The macro outlook for 2023 is still poor (possible recession, which could 
hurt car sales). Musk has not yet shown he can right the ship at Twitter or fully 
refocus — he hasn’t even named a new Twitter CEO yet (though he said he would). 
| lean towards Scenario B (continued bleed or sideways) until we see a 
catalyst: maybe wait for that flip factor like a clear change in Musk’s behavior or 
an uptick in demand indicators. It’s okay to be late to the bounce rather than 
early and wrong. I’d keep Tesla on a very tight watch. No big position now -— 
perhaps keep a token holding to align our interest in following it closely. If the 
stock stabilizes above a certain support level for a while, that might signal the 
bottoming. But discipline says wait for evidence of improvement.” 


e Advisor C (Still Bearish): “The old trading adage: “Never try to catch a falling 
knife.” Nothing fundamentally has changed yet — Musk is still juggling two 
companies, the economy is worsening, and competition in EVs will only intensify 
in 2023 (for instance, we know BYD, etc. are expanding globally). | fear Scenario 
A (fast crash) could still happen if, say, Tesla misses delivery targets badly or if 
Musk sells yet more shares. It could drop another 30-40% from here for all we 
know (perhaps not likely, but possible). The downside risk still looms. Therefore, | 
advocate staying mostly in cash regarding Tesla. We can survive without 
capturing every rebound. There will be a time to buy Tesla again, but the 
downtrend is intact until proven otherwise. Maybe consider other plays for now 
(e.g. broad market or other sectors) and revisit Tesla in a few months.” 


Position Decision Logic: In this scenario, our system is dealing with a potential turning 
point. This is often the toughest decision: when a once-loved stock has imploded, do 
you step in or stay away? The strength of our approach is that we don’t need to fully 
predict the turn; we can scale in gradually if conditions warrant. The likely action: 
remain very cautious, but prepared to re-engage. Practically, the system might do 
something like: “If we had zero Tesla by late 2022, maybe initiate a tiny starter position 
(for example 1% of portfolio) just to have skin in the game for a rebound scenario, but 
only with money we can afford to risk.” At the same time, set clear criteria for adding 
more: e.g., “If Tesla’s next earnings show resilient demand or if Musk announces a 


Twitter CEO and the stock reacts positively, then increase to 3% position,” etc. If those 
positive signals do not materialize and the stock keeps falling, that small position is our 
maximum loss exposure — we’d not add more until the thesis changes. 


In essence, the system errs on the side of not getting sucked into a value trap, but it 
also recognizes that after extreme moves, opportunities can arise. By using a stepwise 
approach (watchlist > tiny position > dossier review > council > then bigger position if 
confirmed, per Chapter 10’s process), we ensure we’re never betting the farm on 
catching a bottom. 


This balanced response shows the system’s limitations and strengths: we likely 
wouldn’t have bought at the absolute bottom (which turned out to be around $102 in 
January 2023, after which Tesla doubled over the next six months). We might have 
started buying back only after some confirmation — meaning maybe we re-entered 
around $150 or $180 in 2023, missing the lowest prices. That’s okay. The method 
prioritizes not losing big, over perfectly timing bottoms. We accept possibly “missing” 
the first part of a recovery in exchange for confidence that a real recovery is underway. 


4. Outcome and Reflections for Tesla Case 


What actually happened: After the late 2022 nadir, Tesla’s stock indeed rebounded 
strongly in the first half of 2023 (as Advisor A hoped). Partly this was due to a broad tech 
rally when the Federal Reserve slowed rate hikes, and partly due to Tesla’s own actions 
(they implemented price cuts which spurred volume, and Musk in late 2022 said he 
would find someone else to run Twitter, which gave investors hope). By mid-2023, Tesla 
was back around $250 (a significant recovery from $120). However, it remained volatile 
and below its 2021 highs. The system’s hypothetical cautious re-entry around early 
2023 would have meant we participated in some of that upside, though not all of it. 
Importantly, during the worst phase (2022), the system would have largely been out of 
the stock, thereby avoiding a catastrophic drawdown that many undisciplined 
investors suffered. 


Strengths shown: This case demonstrates the system’s ability to anchor decisions in 
real events and data. When Musk sold shares and later got distracted with Twitter, 
those were concrete events that our parameters flagged. We didn’t have to guess his 
motives perfectly; the very occurrence of those events was enough to act (reduce or 
exit). Likewise, relying on scenario planning, the system would have prepared for a 
macro downturn (rates rising) which helped justify getting out when things looked dicey. 
The LLM Council provided a cool-headed analysis at each turn, offering multiple 
perspectives instead of either mindless bullishness or panic. This would help a human 
investor resist both FOMO at the top and despair at the bottom. 


Limitations and misses: Of course, the system isn’t all-knowing. It likely would have 
trimmed or sold too early in some instances — for example, it might have sold a lot in 
early 2021 at ~$700, only to see Tesla run to $1,200 later that year (we would have 
missed that last leg of blow-off top). It also might have been slow to buy back in early 
2023, wanting confirmation and thus not catching the absolute lows. These are the 
trade-offs of a disciplined approach. We accept sometimes missing the extremes in 
order to capture the more predictable middle of a trend. Another limitation is that the 


system would have had a hard time quantifying something like “Twitter distraction” — 
it’s a qualitative factor. Here the human co-pilot (you, the reader) would add judgment, 
informed by the Al council and research, to say “this factor is materially bad.” 


Overall, in Tesla’s case, the system would have done its primary job: protect the 
investor from the stock’s worst periods while still allowing participation in its long- 
term growth story. We would have avoided blind hero-worship of Musk (the system is 
not swayed by cults of personality; it looks at causal impact), and equally avoided 
writing Tesla off entirely (keeping it on the watchlist for when conditions improved). 


Now, let’s move to the next case, which zooms out from a single stock to an entire 
industry boom and the question of bubble vs. reality. 


Case Study 2 — The Al Chips Boom (2022-2024): Hype or Lasting 
Trend? 


Our second case study examines the recent boom in Al semiconductor stocks — 
companies that make the GPUs and other chips powering the Al revolution. The poster 
child here is Nvidia (NVDA), which in 2023 became one of the world’s most valuable 
companies on the back of surging demand for Al model training. We’ll explore how the 
system would handle a sector-wide theme where excitement and fundamentals 
converged, raising the classic question: “Are we in a bubble?” This case will highlight 
how our Sector Deep Research (Chapter 8) and LLM Council (Chapter 9) help 
navigate booming but volatile trends. 


1. Historical Recap: Al Gold Rush in Chips 


Time period: roughly 2022 through mid-2024. In late 2022, breakthroughs in artificial 
intelligence — notably the public release of ChatGPT (November 2022) — kicked off a 
frenzy for Al infrastructure. It quickly became clear that training and running advanced 
Al models required massive numbers of specialized chips (GPUs, primarily from 
Nvidia). Demand from big tech firms and startups for these Al chips went through the 
roof in 2023. 


Key events and milestones: 


e October 2022: The U.S. government imposed export controls on the most 
advanced AI chips (like Nvidia’s A100) to China, fearing their use in military 
applications. Nvidia’s stock initially dipped on losing China sales, but this 
underscored how strategically critical these chips had become. 


e Late 2022: Early adopters (OpenAl, etc.) were buying up chips. Nvidia in late 
2022 was already guiding toward strong data center demand. But the stock 
market hadn’t fully reacted yet - NVDA shares were actually down ~50% in 2022 
(as part of the tech bear market). 


e Q1 2023 (Feb-Mar): Hype builds as ChatGPT’s success signals a coming Al arms 
race. Al-related stocks start climbing off their lows. 


e May 2023: The inflection point. Nvidia announced earnings and guidance that 
stunned Wall Street — it projected quarterly revenue over 50% above analyst 
estimates due to surging orders for Al chips[11][12]. This was described as 
“suidance for the ages.” Nvidia’s stock soared 24-28% in one day to a record 
high, adding about $200 billion in market cap[13]. By this point, Nvidia was 
approaching a $1 trillion valuation, and its success lifted the entire 
semiconductor sector. Other Al-exposed companies (like equipment makers, 
cloud providers, and rival chipmakers like AMD) also jumped. 


e Mid/Late 2023: The “Al boom” became the dominant market narrative. The 
NASDAQ’s gains in 2023 were heavily driven by Al hopes — Nvidia’s stock 
tripled in the first 8 months of 2023/14], and a handful of Al-related megacaps 
accounted for a disproportionate share of S&P 500 performance[15][16]. Every 
company was adding “Al” to their pitch. This was reminiscent of past tech 
manias (dot-com in 1999, crypto in 2017, etc.), but with a twist: Nvidia and 
friends were reporting real, growing earnings. This wasn’t all vaporware — the 
demand (at least for now) and profits were tangible, leading some to argue “this 
time it’s different.” 


e Late 2023 into 2024: Debate raged on whether this was an Al bubble. Some 
skeptics pointed out signs of excess: concentration of sales in a few customers, 
skyrocketing valuations, and the risk that the surge was driven by one-off capital 
spending that might not sustain. Indeed, even Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang 
addressed this — in late 2023 he said he doesn’t see an Al bubble from his 
vantage, pointing to widespread demand across cloud, enterprise, and edge 
applications[17]. Nonetheless, analysts cautioned that expectations might be 
too high: “The concern that Al infrastructure spending growth is not sustainable 
is not likely to ebb,” said one analyst in November 2025/18]. Through early 2024, 
Al chip stocks remained volatile but largely at elevated levels compared to pre- 
boom. There were a few air-pockets (e.g., when interest rates spiked or when 
rumors of GPU oversupply emerged, Nvidia’s stock would dip), but no 
catastrophic crash as of mid-2024. 


In short, the Al chips case is one of big upside followed by uncertainty. It’s an ongoing 
story: by 2025, Nvidia had firmly joined the ranks of trillion-dollar tech giants, but 
questions remained about cyclicality and competition (AMD, and efforts by Google, 
Amazon, and Chinese firms to develop their own Al chips). 


Now, let’s consider how our system would engage with this theme as it unfolded, 
treating it as a sector play (with Nvidia as a key stock, but not the only one). 


2. Preparation: Building the Al Chips Sector Thesis 


Back in, say, early 2022, how would we have prepared for the possibility of an Al chip 
boom? This requires some foresight, but remember we had Deep Research reports on 
many tech themes. We likely had our eye on the semiconductor industry and the 
emerging trend of Al acceleration. 


Sector Deep Research (Chapter 8): We would compile a dossier on Al 
Infrastructure & Chips. Key fundamental parameters might include: 


Cloud capital expenditure (capex): How much are companies like Google, 
Microsoft, Amazon investing in data centers and Al hardware? In 2022, trends 
showed cloud capex rising. A flip factor: any sign of capex cuts or delays. 


Al adoption metrics: e.g. number of Al models being developed, enterprise Al 
spending growth. By late 2021, there were already signs (GPT-3’s buzz in 2020, 
etc.) that a breakthrough in usefulness could dramatically boost these metrics. 


Supply constraints: The semiconductor industry was just emerging from the 
2021 chip shortage. We’d note that advanced chips (like Nvidia’s leading GPUs) 
couldn’t be produced infinitely -TSMC fabs had long lead times, etc. A 
parameter here: wafer capacity for advanced nodes, which if maxed out could 
constrain supply and drive prices higher (good for supplier profits in short-term). 


Competition and substitution: Nvidia was dominant in Al chips, but AMD was 
fielding GPUs too, and other approaches (like Google’s TPU, or smaller startups 
with Al accelerators) were in the mix. We’d mark down that if Nvidia’s moat held, 
they’d reap most gains, but if alternatives proved effective, the spoils might 
spread or Nvidia’s pricing might weaken. 

Geopolitics: The dossier would highlight the US-China tech tensions — export 
controls (as indeed happened in 2022) could be a flip factor. Also potential 
subsidies or support (U.S. CHIPS Act in 2022, etc. could boost domestic 
capacity). 

Valuations: As of early 2022, Nvidia stock had a very high P/E (it had boomed in 
2021 on metaverse and gaming hype, then fell in late 2021/early 2022). We’d be 
mindful that it wasn’t “cheap”, so this would require growth to justify. 


Chart/Macro (Ch. 5 & 7): In early 2022, the macro environment was turning 
negative (inflation, Fed hawkishness — Scenario B approaching). Indeed, Nvidia’s 
stock in H1 2022 was falling with the market. Our system, noticing the macro 
headwinds, might have been defensive on tech generally in 2022. But deep 
research might have kept a watch on Al chips as a theme likely to rebound 
strongly once the macro stabilized. We’d likely place Nvidia and perhaps an ETF 
like SOXX or SMH (semiconductor ETFs) on our watchlist, with notes like: “Big 
upside potential if Al soending accelerates; near-term risk from Fed tightening.” 


Scenario Planning: What scenario would we ascribe to an Al boom? Possibly a 
Scenario C (whipsaw) where macro is weak (normally bad for semis, which are 
cyclical) but a secular story causes a divergent rally in a subset of stocks. 
Alternatively, if the Fed pivoted and economy avoided recession, we could get 
Scenario D (stabilization) that allows the secular trend to flourish. We’d consider 
a bull case scenario: “Al adoption accelerates faster than expected, Nvidia’s 
earnings explode, stocks rally.” And a bear case scenario: “Al proves over-hyped 
or spending gets cut in a recession, stocks slump back.” We might also map a 
bubble scenario akin to Scenario A, albeit in a specific sector: “exuberance 
pushes stocks up 5x and then they crash 80% later.” This has precedent in semis 
(e.g., late 1990s tech bubble saw Intel and others rocket up then crash). 


Armed with this preparation, we wouldn’t necessarily predict the exact May 2023 Nvidia 
event, but we’d recognize the pieces in place. By early 2023, when ChatGPT mania hit 
headlines, our watchlist would flash that “hey, our Al chip thesis might be activating.” 
We’d then proceed to decision-making. 


3. Decision Points and Applying the System 


We’ll focus on two critical junctures for the Al chip boom: (A) the onset of euphoria 
(around spring 2023 when things really took off), and (B) after the boom — assessing 
bubble risk (say late 2023 or early 2024 when the debate about sustainability peaked). 


Decision Point A: May 2023 - The Big Bang Quarter 


Situation: It’s late May 2023. Nvidia has just reported its shocking guidance: demand 
from cloud giants and others for its Al chips is off the charts. The stock jumped ~25% in 
a day[13], and is now about double where it was at the start of 2023. The whole market
