is abuzz about Al. Other chip stocks (AMD, TSMC, etc.) and Al beneficiaries (like cloud 
computing ETFs, robotics, etc.) are also rising. We, as aninvestor, need to decide: Do 
we jump on this speeding train, and if so, how? 


Remember, just a few months ago (late 2022) the macro was bad and semis were ina 
slump. Now suddenly, despite still iffy macro, Al has injected optimism. This feels like a 
regime change for the sector. 


Dossier Check at that time: Our updated dossier for Nvidia/Al chips in May 2023 
shows: - Earnings momentum turned hugely positive (Nvidia’s revenue forecast blew 
past estimates/11][12]). This triggers a parameter saying “fundamental breakthrough — 
adjust projections upward.” - Valuation is high but arguably justified by the growth 
(forward estimates are all being revised up by analysts). - Macro: The overall market 
was recovering in Spring 2023 (the S&P 500 was climbing, Scenario D slowly emerging 
as the Fed talked about pausing rate hikes). However, inflation was still above target, so 
not completely out of woods. But clearly the market was willing to reward growth again. 
- Competition: AMD was announcing its next-gen Al chips (MI300 series), but they were 
not yet proven — Nvidia still had ~90% market share in Al accelerators. So the 
competitive threat parameter remained low in the immediate term. - Council of Als 
specialty: This is where we might even incorporate an LLM’s insight as a domain 
expert. For instance, ask one advisor to role-play as a tech industry analyst. They might 
note how generative Al could drive years of investment, not just a one-quarter blip, 
comparing it to historical shifts like the rise of the internet (which massively increased 
demand for networking equipment in the 90s). 


LLM Council Deliberation (May 2023): 


e Advisor 1 (Enthusiastic Technologist — Bullish): “The Al demand here is real 
and sizable. This isn’t based on hope of future users —- companies are literally 
placing orders for $10B+ of chips. It’s like a gold rush for whoever supplies the 
‘picks and shovels’ (the GPUs). We likely are at the early stage of a multi-year 
adoption curve for Al. Nvidia’s CEO even said GPUs are harder to get than drugs 
right now[19]. This suggests pricing power and full order books for the 
foreseeable future. My view: Scenario D (sustained uptrend) — we have a new 


secular driver. Sure, the stock doubled, but earnings justification also jumped. 
We should establish a position in this theme. Perhaps buy a basket: e.g., some 
NVDA stock for pure exposure, and maybe an ETF like SMH (semiconductor ETF) 
to capture broader upside while diversifying. Size it moderately (not too small, 
because we believe in the story, but not too huge, because volatility is high —- say 
a 5% portfolio allocation between NVDA and the ETF).” 


Advisor 2 (Valuation-Conscious Analyst — Moderate): “| agree the story is great, 
but we must be careful of the price we pay. Nvidia now trades at an extremely 
high valuation (P/E has skyrocketed on forward numbers, although forward E is 
also rising). Historically, even the best tech stocks have had vicious corrections. | 
recall the dot-com era: Cisco was the picks-and-shovels of the internet, had 
amazing earnings growth in the 90s, but still crashed in 2000 because 
expectations overshot. We might be in an echo of that. | lean that we should 
invest, but keep the position relatively small and tightly monitored. Call it 
Scenario C: huge whipsaw potential. This could run higher in 2023, but we 
should set a rule —- for example, if NVDA runs another 50% beyond its 
fundamentals, maybe trim; or if competitive news hits, reassess. A possible 
approach: take a 3% position now via an Al-focused ETF or mix of NVDA + AMD, 
and plan to add on dips or on confirmation of continued growth next quarter. But 
have an exit plan in case this turns bubble-like.” 


Advisor 3 (Skeptic —- Bubble Worrier): “This feels very frothy. The speed of the 
stock moves is worrisome — 25% in a day for a $700B company is not normal. It 
suggests a feeding frenzy. Often such frenzies end poorly. Yes, demand is real 
now, but we should consider that big cloud companies sometimes double order 
then pause (the infamous semiconductor cycle). What if everyone bought GPUs 
in 2023 and then in 2024 orders dry up? Also, macro risk: if inflation flares or 
recession hits, some Al projects might be delayed. There’s a non-zero chance 
this is a mini-bubble that could pop if any bad news hits (e.g., stricter export 
bans, or Al regulatory issues, or simply growth not meeting hyper-expectations 
next quarter). | lean toward Scenario B or even an Al-specific bubble scenario: 
the stocks could give back a lot of gains. My advice: if we want exposure, keep it 
very small, or even wait for a better entry after a correction. No shame in 
missing some upside if downside risk is high. We can also play indirectly (maybe 
invest in more diversified tech ETFs that have some NVDA but won’t blow up if 
NVDA corrects).” 


Position Decision Logic: Given these views, the system would likely take a measured 
long position in the Al chips theme post-Nvidia’s blowout quarter, but with an 
understanding that it’s chasing momentum and thus risk is higher. Concretely, we 
might decide: 


Buy: Allocate perhaps ~5% of the portfolio to the theme. For example, put 3% 
into a broad semiconductor ETF (SMH or SOXxX) to get diversified exposure 
(which includes Nvidia, AMD, TSMC, etc.), and 2% directly into Nvidia stock (to 
overweight the main player). This way, if Nvidia continues to outperform, we 
benefit, but if one company stumbles, the ETF cushions somewhat. Alternatively, 


if a specific Al-focused ETF exists (some thematic ETFs cover Al 
hardware/software), we could use that. 


e Risk Management: We immediately set some guardrails. For instance, decide 
that if Nvidia’s stock falls by more than, say, 20% from its new high, we’ll review 
or cut the position (that would indicate either the whole market rolling over or 
specific bad news). Also, we won’t allow this position to grow beyond a certain 
size without rebalancing — e.g., if our 5% allocation grows to 10% because the 
stocks soar, we might trim back to lock in profit (the system doesn’t aim to 
diamond-hand everything; it respects valuation and concentration risks). 


e Monitoring: The dossier is updated with new flip factors: e.g., watch Nvidia’s 
next earnings (Aug 2023) closely — if they confirm the growth again, great; if they 
disappoint, that’s a possible exit signal. Also, watch news on chip supply (any 
sign that supply is catching up might cap Nvidia’s pricing) and Al demand 
anecdotes (are more companies adopting or do we hear of Al training 
saturation?). 


The decision here captures the system’s agility: we missed the absolute bottom (Nvidia 
was far cheaper in Oct 2022), but we are willing to buy when a causal inflection justifies 
it- even at higher prices - because new information has changed the game. The system 
is also humble enough to scale in rather than bet everything at once. 


Decision Point B: Late 2023/Early 2024 - Bubble or Business? 


Situation: Some months later, say December 2023 or January 2024. Nvidia had another 
strong earnings in August 2023, and the stock hit a fresh high around that time. By late 
2023, it’s trading somewhat off the peak (perhaps it’s oscillating), and the initial 
euphoria has tempered. Now the market is trying to discern 2024 demand. Will Al 
spending keep expanding exponentially, or will it plateau? The term “Al bubble” is being 
thrown around in financial media and even by officials. For instance, the Bank of 
England had commented on elevated tech valuations, and numerous think pieces 
compare the Al boom to past bubbles[20][21]. Meanwhile, on the ground, big tech 
firms are announcing massive Al investments (which is bullish), but also some are 
delaying other capex to focus on Al (could mean a finite budget). 


Our position from mid-2023 is in profit, but not without volatility. We need to decide 
whether to hold, increase, or reduce exposure going into 2024. 


Updated Dossier: By late 2023, some things have changed: - Customer 
concentration: Nvidia’s results showed a large part of sales concentrated in a few big 
buyers (e.g., US cloud titans). Our parameter notes that four customers made up ~60% 
of Nvidia’s sales[22]- which is a risk if any pause orders. - Inventories and supply: 
Nvidia started increasing supply hugely, and even engaging in some unusual practices 
(like renting cloud capacity itself to meet demand[23]). This could imply they see 
demand as so high they'll even finance it, but skeptics say it might be hiding oversupply 
or “circular” financing (Nvidia investing in startups that buy Nvidia chips, etc.)[24]. - 
Competition moves: By Q4 2023, AMD launched its MI300 Al chips; some big players 
like Microsoft hinted at exploring alternative chips (including in-house). Also, the U.S. 


imposed even tighter chip export rules to China in Oct 2023 (forcing Nvidia to offer 
downgraded versions for that market). These competitive and regulatory angles re-enter 
the risk calculation. - Macro: The global economy in late 2023 had mixed signals — 
inflation was down from peak, but interest rates were high, and many expected at least 
a mild recession in 2024. If a recession hits, companies might slow Al spend 
temporarily. That leans toward caution. 


This is a classic monitor and adjust moment. 
LLM Council Deliberation (late 2023): 


e Advisor A (Still Bullish, Long-Term): “We’re just getting started. Think of how 
much compute will be needed as every company and industry implements Al - 
this could be a decade-long tailwind. Near term, even if there’s some lumpiness, 
Nvidia’s ecosystem (CUDA software, etc.) gives it a moat; it’s not easy for 
customers to switch to AMD or custom chips overnight[25]. | suspect 2024 will 
still surprise to the upside in demand. Even if the stock is expensive, it staying 
expensive and growing into it is a plausible scenario (like Amazon in the 2010s 
always looked expensive but kept growing). So I’d maintain our position, maybe 
even add on dips. Scenario D still for me - any macro recession would likely be 
met with more stimulus (which could actually boost tech again). Unless we see a 
clear sign of oversupply or a big tech firm reporting they overbought GPUs, I’d 
ride this trend.” 


e Advisor B (Pragmatic): “I’m optimistic on Al long-term, but the stock has priced 
ina lot. The council’s own research flags issues: customer concentration and 
potential “double ordering” risk[26][27]. What if these cloud companies pause 
their buying after building enough capacity? We could see a few quarters of 
slower growth, and the market might not like that given high expectations. Also, 
rising competition — maybe not a threat to Nvidia’s dominance yet, but enough to 
prevent valuations from expanding further. | lean that we should trim some 
profits here. Keep, say, half the position, but take the other half off the table. 
That way, if the boom continues, we still benefit, but if a correction comes, we 
have cash ready to buy the next dip. Essentially shift from offense to a bit more 
defense as the easy money from the early rally is made. Our scenario is 
somewhere between C and B- could be choppy or moderately down for a while.” 


e Advisor C (Worried): “The bubble signs are all present: media hype, enormous 
one-day market cap moves, retail pouring into Al-themed ETFs, and insiders 
possibly selling (I’d check if any execs sold shares — often they do after big run- 
ups). The fact that even Al company CEOs are trying to downplay bubble 
fears[28][17] suggests they know expectations are dangerously high. One 
misstep and the stocks could tumble 30-50%. We’ve made a good profit; the 
prudent move is to lock in most of it. Maybe leave a token amount (like 1% 
position) just so we’re not completely out (in case it truly keeps roaring), but 
otherwise take profits. If it is indeed a secular trend, we will find other ways to 
ride it over time (maybe on a pullback or via more diversified plays). Our scenario 
now is a potential mini Scenario A if the bubble pops, or at least Scenario B (a 
long grind down from peak valuations). Better safe than sorry.” 


Position Decision Logic: We synthesize these views with our own risk appetite. 
Suppose our portfolio is already up nicely for the year thanks in part to Al stocks —- we 
might decide to de-risk a bit going into 2024. 


For example, if we had 5% allocated, perhaps we cut it to 2% by selling some Nvidia 
and ETF holdings. We realize gains and rebalance. This doesn’t mean we’re abandoning 
the theme -— it stays in our watchlist and core theses — but we acknowledge that the 
easy early phase of the trend is over, and now we’re in a more uncertain middle 
phase. 


We’d also document what would make us scale back up or down further: - Scale back 
up if, say, a major dip occurs but fundamentals still look good (e.g., if NVDA fell 30% on 
general market correction but we see no change in Al adoption trajectory, we might buy 
that dip). - Scale down (even exit) if evidence of a bust emerges (e.g., Nvidia’s quarterly 
growth suddenly stalls or inventory builds up, or a recession drastically cuts IT 
spending). 


This dynamic management reflects the system’s adaptive scenario approach. Initially, 
in early 2023, the scenario shifted bullish so we increased exposure. By late 2023, the 
scenario maybe shifts toward potential downturn or at least higher uncertainty, so we 
reduce exposure. We’re not married to a position — we follow the evidence. 


4. Outcome and Lessons for Al Chips Case 


As of the latest data (through 2024), what happened? Nvidia did continue to post strong 
results through 2023 (with some quarterly volatility), and the stock remained high, 
albeit with swings. There were a few dips — e.g., in late 2023, NVDA pulled back ~15- 
20% from its high on profit-taking and rate worries, but then it rallied again on strong 
earnings or Al news. No catastrophic bust occurred in 2024 (unlike the dot-com era 
where many high-fliers imploded); however, the debate on sustainability remained 
open. 


System Performance: Our system in this scenario would have captured a significant 
portion of the upside (we got in after confirmation, rode the wave, and likely took some 
profit near highs). We might have missed the last bit of the rally if it continued after 
trimming, but that’s a fine trade-off for risk management. Importantly, if a crash had 
happened, we would have avoided the full brunt by not being overexposed at the peak. 
So either way, we come out with gains and relatively controlled risk. 


Key Takeaways: 


e This case highlights how Sector Deep Research pays off. We had pre-identified 
the potential for Al hardware demand. When real-world events validated it 
(ChatGPT’s success, Nvidia’s orders), we were ready to act swiftly. Many 
investors only react after a narrative is obvious; our method tries to anticipate 
plausible narratives so we’re not caught flat-footed. 


e l|talso showcases the role of the LLM Council in tempering hype. In a boom, 
there’s a temptation to throw caution aside (“this will grow forever!”). Having 
multiple Al advisors, including skeptical ones, helps reinforce discipline. For 
example, recalling historical analogues (dot-com picks-and-shovels) is exactly 


the kind of insight an Al might bring up[20], reminding us that even “real” trends 
can have overvalued phases. 


e The importance of position sizing shines here too. Because we never bet, say, 
50% of the portfolio on Al chips, a worst-case scenario (even a 50% drop in those 
stocks) would be a manageable hit to our overall portfolio. This prevents any 
single thesis from sinking us. 


e Another subtle lesson: taking profits is okay. Our system doesn’t equate selling 
with abandoning conviction; it treats it as reloading. In a fast-moving sector, 
volatility can be a friend — by trimming high and potentially adding back on dips, 
we actually capitalize on whipsaws rather than dread them. 


Finally, this case affirms that our system is not about being permanently bullish or 
bearish — it shifts stance with the data. We were cautious on semis in 2022 (rightly so, 
as they fell), bullish in mid-2023 when the trend turned, and cautious again when things 
looked stretched. This flexibility is what we want when “investing in the end of the 
world” — the ability to adapt to swift changes, driven by evidence and multiple 
viewpoints. 


Having examined a single stock drama and a sector boom, our last case goes full 
macro: the COVID crash, an event that truly felt like the end of the world for a while. 


Case Study 3 —- The COVID-19 Crash & Aftermath (2020): Navigating a 
Once-in-a-Century Shock 


Our final case study puts the system to the test against an extreme macro shock: the 
rapid onset of the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020, the fastest bear market in modern 
history, followed by an extraordinary recovery fueled by unprecedented stimulus and 
technological adaptation. This scenario will illustrate how our approach would handle a 
sudden Scenario A (fast crash) and then transition through whipsaw to partial 
stabilization as conditions evolved. It’s a perfect showcase for the scenario portfolios 
from Chapter 12, and how an investor might move from one to another as reality 
unfolds. 


1. Historical Recap: From Pandemic Panic to Rapid Rebound 


Time period: February 2020 through mid-2021 (the crash and the first year of recovery). 


In late 2019, COVID-19 was a mere news blip of a virus in Wuhan, China. By January 
2020, cases were spreading internationally, but markets largely shrugged it off, hitting 
all-time highs by mid-February. Then, seemingly overnight, sentiment flipped: 


e Late Feb to March 2020 - The Crash: As outbreaks exploded in Italy, Iran, and 
elsewhere, and the realization set in that a global pandemic was at hand, 
financial markets collapsed with shocking speed. From February 19, 2020 to 
March 23, 2020, the S&P 500 plunged 34%[29], wiping out three years of gains in 
about a month[30]. It was the fastest 30% drawdown ever for stocks. Volatility 
spiked to crisis levels. Virtually all risk assets fell together: stocks, oil (which 
plummeted as travel froze; at one point WTI oil prices even went negative briefly 


in April), credit spreads blew out, etc. Certain industries were decimated — 
airlines, hotels, cruise lines, anything travel or leisure related, as global travel 
ground to a halt and economies entered lockdowns. By late March, markets were 
in full panic, pricing in a severe global recession or depression. 


Policy & Bottom - Late March 2020: Governments and central banks responded 
with massive intervention. The U.S. Federal Reserve on March 15 cut rates to 
zero and launched unlimited quantitative easing. On March 23 (the very day of 
the stock market bottom), the Fed announced extraordinary measures to 
backstop corporate credit. Around the same time, fiscal authorities rolled out 
huge relief packages (e.g., the $2+ trillion CARES Act in the U.S.). These actions 
created a backstop and helped sentiment bottom out. The S&P 500 began rising 
after March 23, marking the end of the bear market just one month after it 


began[31][32]. 


April 2020 to mid-2021 - The Rebound and New Trends: What followed was 
equally astonishing. Far from a languishing recovery, markets rocketed higher. 
The S&P 500 regained its pre-pandemic high by August 2020 and ended 2020 at 
new records[32]. The Nasdaq did even better, as technology and “stay-at- 
home” stocks soared. Companies like Amazon, Apple, Microsoft, Zoom, Netflix, 
etc., saw huge gains as their businesses actually benefited from lockdowns (e- 
commerce, cloud services, remote work software usage exploded). For example, 
from the March lows to end of 2020: Amazon’s stock nearly doubled (Amazon’s 
sales were up 40% in Q2 2020 and its stock was ~97% above the March low by 
August[33]), Apple’s stock also roughly doubled (and the company hit a $2 trillion 
market cap by August 2020, with shares +124% from the bottom[34]), and 
Zoom’s stock skyrocketed (up several-fold, from ~$70 pre-pandemic to over 
$300 by late 2020[35]). By contrast, old-economy sectors lagged: energy stocks, 
banks, and travel-related companies recovered more slowly and in some cases 
remained deeply depressed through 2020. 


This divergence created a K-shaped market. Overall indices did great, but the winners 
were concentrated in tech and defensive sectors, while many other stocks were flat or 
still down. For instance, small businesses and traditional retail, hospitality, etc., 
suffered even as NASDAQ soared[36][37]. 


Late 2020 - Vaccine News and Rotation: In November 2020, vaccines were 
announced (Pfizer, Moderna breakthroughs). This led to a market rotation: 
beaten-down cyclicals (like airlines, banks, energy) jumped on hopes of 
reopening, while some “stay-at-home” winners paused. But the broad market 
still climbed, anticipating a strong recovery in 2021 with the pandemic’s end in 
sight. 


Summing up: a fast crash (Feb-Mar 2020) followed by a whipsaw rally (Apr—Jun 2020) 
and then a partial stabilization/uptrend supported by stimulus and adaptation (rest of 
2020 into 2021). It was a chaotic period where flexibility and discipline were essential - 
exactly the kind of environment our system is built for. 


2. Preparation: Could We Have Seen it Coming? 


Hindsight is 20/20 - very few saw a pandemic coming in 2020, and certainly not the 
speed of it. However, let’s see what parts of our system could have prepared us, at 
least conceptually: 


e Historical risk awareness (Chapter 7 macro): We might have noted that 
pandemics, though rare, have happened before and can be massively disruptive. 
Historical data shows pandemics like the 1918 flu killed tens of millions[38], and 
even more recent ones (1957, 1968 influenzas) killed 1-4 million each[39][40]. 
Public health experts had long warned of a pandemic being a question of “when, 
not if.” Our scenario planning in Chapter 12 may have included a ScenarioA 
(fast crash) due to a global pandemic as a tail risk scenario. Indeed, the 
frequency chart of pandemics shows they occur periodically[41][42], and 
globalization/air travel means modern pandemics can spread exponentially 
fast[43]. If we were particularly astute (or following someone like Bill Gates who 
gave a famous 2015 TED talk on pandemic risk), we might have had pandemic 
crash as a low-probability, high-impact scenario in our playbook. 


e Watchlist in early 2020: As COVID news started in Jan-Feb 2020, we would add 
related items to our dossier: e.g., tracking case counts, noting how China locked 
down Wuhan (an unprecedented step that hinted at severity). However, we must 
admit most investors (likely including our system) didn’t grasp how bad it would 
get until very late. What matters is what we do once the evidence hits tipping 
point. 


e Portfolio prior to crash: Coming into 2020, the economy was pretty strong, 
markets at highs, but there were some caution signs (valuations stretched, etc.). 
Nir’s worldview (the book’s premise) was that a collapse is likely near-term — 
though the cause wasn’t specified as a pandemic, a pandemic fit the general 
idea of a major disruption. So it’s possible our recommended baseline portfolio 
inJan 2020 was moderately tilted to defensive just in case (maybe a bit more 
cash than usual, some gold or defensive stocks, etc.). But let’s say we were 
roughly in a standard allocation with some tech, some cyclicals, etc., and 
needed to react to the sudden shock. 


3. Decision Phases and System Response 


We’Il break this case into phases rather than one-off points, because it was a rolling 
crisis: 


Phase 1: February—-Mid March 2020 - The Waterfall Decline 


Moment of recognition: Suppose it’s the last week of February 2020. The market is 
down ~10% from its peak (that happened in just 6 trading days[44], the quickest 
correction ever at the time). Italy has locked down Lombardy, and disturbing headlines 
of overwhelmed hospitals are coming in. Our stops/trailing signals on charts are likely 
triggered (stocks breaking below moving averages violently). We realize this could be 


not just a dip but something much worse - essentially Scenario A: a fast crash 
triggered by a global crisis. 


Council deliberation at the start of crash: At this point, our LLM council would almost 
unanimously focus on risk management: - They’d note that this shock is non-financial in 
origin but has huge real economy impact. Factories in China were idled, global supply 
chains disrupted — manufacturing PMIs were plunging. Advisor voices might say: “This is 
exactly the kind of exogenous shock that justifies going to Scenario A playbook: raise 
cash, reduce exposure, seek safety until clarity emerges.” - Perhaps one contrarian Al 
might say “buy the dip” (there are always those voices, referencing SARS 2003 which 
only caused a brief market blip), but given the rapid global spread, most would advise 
caution. 


System actions in crash: According to Chapter 12’s scenario portfolios, for Scenario A 
(fast crash) the guidance is to preserve capital. That means: - Sell down equities 
aggressively: We’d cut a lot of our stock positions, especially any cyclicals or high-beta 
stocks, aS soon as we recognize this could be an uncontrolled cascade. By mid-March, 
even if we were a bit late, we definitely move to maximum defensive. We might hold 
only some robust large-cap stocks or none at all temporarily. - Shift to cash or near- 
cash: likely moving a substantial portion (maybe 50% or more) to cash or Treasury-bill 
funds. In March 2020, even bonds were volatile (except treasuries), so cash was king. - 
We avoid panic selling of everything at any price (we’re systematic, not emotional), but 
our framework gives us permission to go to high cash because the parameters are 
overwhelmingly bad: a once-in-a-century pandemic is clearly a game changer. - 
Perhaps we also allocate a bit to hedges: maybe gold or treasury bonds (though even 
gold dipped in March initially due to liquidity scramble, it rose after). - We definitely 
avoid any leverage or bottom-fishing in things like airlines yet — too early. 


By the market bottom in late March, our portfolio might look relatively conservative: 
Let’s say 60-70% cash, 20-30% in some broad market ETF (if we didn’t sell absolutely 
everything) or some defensive equities (like consumer staples, which fell less), and 
maybe a small 5-10% in “hedges” (gold, or just more cash equivalent). This means we 
likely suffered a loss but much smaller than the market. If the market fell 34%, maybe 
our portfolio is down on the order of 10-15% thanks to quick action. (Exact numbers 
vary, but definitely we aimed to avoid the full brunt). 


One might ask: if we sold stocks at, say, a 15-20% decline, didn’t we “lock in a loss”? 
Yes, but that’s okay because we prevented further 15%+ of downfall. The system’s 
priority in a fast crash is survival. 


Phase 2: Late March-April 2020 - Inflection and Beginning of Whipsaw 


Situation: It’s the end of March. Markets are extremely volatile but off the very lows, 
thanks to Fed intervention. Italy’s curve might be peaking, China’s outbreak was 
brought under control by lockdowns, but the virus is spreading rapidly in the US and 
elsewhere. Economic data is terrible (unemployment claims spiking). However, you 
also see the tremendous firepower of monetary and fiscal responses. The S&P has 
bounced about 15% from the low by mid-April (but is still well below February levels). 


This is tricky: is ita sucker’s rally or the start of recovery? Our system must avoid two 
traps: re-entering too early (if it’s a false dawn) or staying out too long (and missing the 
bulk of recovery). We handle this with our staged approach. 


Council deliberation as of early April 2020: Likely split opinions: - Some advisors say: 
“We’ve probably seen the worst. The market tends to bottom when news is darkest. 
With all this stimulus and the Fed essentially backstopping things, the scenario might 
be shifting to Scenario C (whipsaw) — sharp down then sharp up. Tech and healthcare 
sectors may even thrive in this environment. We should carefully start getting back in.” - 
Others caution: “The virus is still not under control, entire economies are shut. Earnings 
will be horrendous for at least a couple quarters. We could retest lows if there’s a 
second wave or if stimulus isn’t enough. Maybe this is a dead-cat bounce. So Scenario 
A could morph into a drawn-out Scenario B (a slow grind recession market) — don’t rush 
in too fast.” 


System actions in early recovery: We compromise by gradually scaling in, guided by 
data: - We identify which areas have clear positive causal drivers now: obviously, 
anything to do with digital life is a winner. So we might start small new positions in, say, 
a technology or NASDAQ-100 ETF, or specific strong companies (Amazon, Microsoft, 
etc.), anticipating they’ll do well with everyone stuck at home. Our Deep Research on 
cloud computing, e-commerce, etc., would highlight these as relative winners. Indeed, 
by June 2020 Amazon was up ~40% above Jan levels[33], and Zoom was up several- 
fold[35]. The system likely would have caught at least some of that by focusing on 
causal logic: “Lockdowns => more online activity => invest in the companies enabling 
that.” - We also consider broad market indices (S&P 500) but perhaps lean towards 
quality/growth bias given the nature of this downturn (it hurt old economy more). So 
maybe we favor the Nasdaq or S&P 500 “low-vol” or “quality” ETF instead of equal- 
weight. - We keep a lot of cash initially. Maybe from 70% cash we go to 50% cash, 
deploying 20% into those opportunities gradually over April/May 2020. - Importantly, we 
set flip factor triggers: for instance, “If new cases resurge or if credit markets seize up 
again, pause purchases or even trim.” Alternatively, “If the S&P breaks back above its 
200-day moving avg and economic data shows improvement, that’s a sign to add more 
risk.” Essentially, we’re data-dependent. 


By summer 2020, as the trend solidified upward (helped by stimulus checks, Fed 
support, and partial reopening), our system would increase exposure further. Possibly, 
by July 2020, we’re back to, say, 70% equities (with a heavy tech tilt) and 30% cash. We 
might also have taken special opportunities: e.g., in Chapter 8 research we might recall 
renewable energy or health tech as themes — COVID could accelerate those 
(renewables got a boost from green recovery plans, etc.). So some thematic plays might 
be considered. 


Phase 3: Late 2020 - Adapting to the New Normal (Partial Stabilization) 


Situation: Toward late 2020, markets had largely recovered. Our portfolio likely is doing 
well, possibly even higher than pre-COVID if we overweighted the big tech winners. But 
now we face a different question: is the rally overdone? Will there be a second crash 
(some feared a W-shaped recovery)? 


In September 2020, there was atech sell-off (Nasdaq fell ~10% after a huge run). Also, 
the virus was still rampant in fall 2020 (a second wave hit Europe/US). Yet stimulus was 
ongoing and vaccines were on the horizon. 


System approach: By this time, we’ve likely moved from Scenario C (whipsaw 
recovery) to Scenario D (partial stabilization) outlook: The world hasn’t ended; we’ll 
survive albeit with damage, and markets function albeit with volatility. We would: - 
Rebalance: possibly trim some of the big winners (they’d grown a lot). For example, if 
Apple or Amazon doubled and became outsized positions, we’d trim to manage risk. - 
Rotate modestly: With vaccines likely, we might start nibbling at some cyclicals that 
could benefit from reopening. E.g., maybe add a bit of an industrials or travel ETF or 
quality names like Disney (which was hurt but would recover). Our council would weigh 
the likelihood of a successful vaccine rollout - by November when Pfizer announced 
results, that became more certain[45]. - Portfolio by end of 2020: Perhaps something 
like 60% stocks (broadly diversified but tech-heavy tilt), 20% sector/specific thematic 
bets (like small positions in beaten-down cyclicals or extra in tech winners), and 20% 
cash remaining as dry powder or for safety against unexpected turns. 


Throughout, we maintain the monitoring routine: checking macro indicators (yield 
curve had steepened after the Fed cuts, suggesting recovery), policy changes, virus 
trends, etc. We’d also by now integrate what we learned: pandemics can mutate — news 
of new strains (Alpha, Delta, etc., in 2021) would be new flip factors to watch. 


4. Outcome and Honest Assessment for COVID Case 


Looking back: If one had executed this kind of strategy, how would it have gone? - We 
likely outperformed the market on the way down (by losing much less in Q1 2020). 
That capital preservation meant we had more to deploy at lows. - We might have 
underperformed slightly on the way up if we were too cautious initially and didn’t go 
“all-in” at the bottom (which we wouldn’t; we scale in gradually). But we still captured a 
good portion of the rebound, especially by focusing on the big winners of the new 
normal. By end of 2020, we probably achieved solid positive returns while the average 
60/40 investor might have been just back to breakeven or so. - We avoided blowing up 
in speculative traps. For example, our system likely wouldn’t gamble on bankrupt 
companies or failing industries just because they’re “cheap.” We’d need causal 
reason. Airlines, for one, were cheap but without a vaccine some might have gone 
bankrupt. We probably largely avoided them until late 2020 when prospects improved. 
So we didn’t catch the exact bottom of airlines or cruise stocks, but that’s a risk we 
traded off. - Emotionally, an investor using our system might have felt comfort in having 
a plan (scenarios) during the chaos, which in reality was terrifying for many. By focusing 
on process (e.g., “The council says raise cash, the dossier says these sectors are 
resilient”), one could act when many froze. 


Limitations: Let’s acknowledge a possible critique: Would our system really sellin late 
Feb/March 2020? Some argue one should never sellin a crash, just hold or buy more. 
Our approach did call for selling early in the crash — that saved us; but if someone only 
realized late and sold at bottom, that’s bad. The system’s advantage is the early 
warning via scenario triggers (once Italy locked down and market broke support, that’s a 
regime change signal). So ideally we act before the absolute bottom. If executed 


correctly, great; if someone delayed, they might’ve sold at a low and missed some 
rebound. The system tries to minimize such timing mistakes by continual monitoring 
and not being complacent (“ignore noise” from Chapter 5 doesn’t mean ignore a once- 
in-a-century event!). It pays attention to causal reality. 


Another limitation: we possibly re-entered too timidly and missed some upside in April. 
For example, some aggressive investors who bought heaps of SPY calls at the bottom 
made a killing - our more measured approach wouldn’t do that (no options, no 
leverage). We’re willing to miss the heroic trades in exchange for steady, lower-risk 
gains. That’s a conscious choice given we prioritize not blowing up. 


Key lessons reinforced: - Scenarios save the day: Having pre-defined playbooks for a 
fast crash and whipsaw helped guide actions under extreme stress. - Parameters > 
Emotions: We sold not because we felt fear, but because objective triggers (pandemic 
spread, market free-fall, liquidity issues) hit our criteria. And we bought not because of 
greed, but because we saw concrete signs of stabilization (stimulus working, 
adaptation happening). - Adaptability: The situation evolved rapidly (from collapse to 
boom in months). Our system’s iterative council reviews and dossier updates allowed 
us to pivot from defense to offense relatively quickly. Many investors failed to re-enter 
after selling due to fear. Our structured approach of gradually scaling back in as 
evidence improved mitigates the paralysis that can occur after a traumatic sell-off. 


Having now walked through three very different cases — a single-stock rollercoaster, a 
sector mania, and a global crash —- we can distill some overarching lessons. 


Synthesis: Lessons from the Case Studies 


Across these case studies, several common themes and lessons emerge about how 
our system behaves: 


1. The Power of Parameters and Causal Thinking 


In each scenario, focusing on specific causal factors proved crucial. For Tesla, 
tracking Musk’s actions (insider selling, distractions) and real-world events (index 
inclusion, competition) gave clear signals to adjust positions. In the Al chips case, 
monitoring actual earnings and demand data (not just stock price) informed when to 
ride the wave and when to be cautious. During COVID, hard data like infection rates, 
lockdown policies, and policy responses drove our scenario shifts. 


Lesson: Grounding decisions in real events and quantifiable parameters helps cut 
through noise. It prevents both irrational exuberance and undue pessimism by 
constantly asking “what’s really happening here?” Rather than reacting to price alone, 
we react to why price is moving. This keeps our decision-making anchored in reality, 
which is especially valuable when narratives get out of hand (as in bubbles or panics). 


2. LLM Council: Diversity of Views, Not Oracles 


Our use of a council of Al advisors demonstrated its worth by exposing different facets 
of each situation. The council provided bullish optimism, cautious realism, and bearish 
risk-awareness in turn. Importantly, the council didn’t “predict the future” with 


certainty — instead, it painted scenarios (“if X, then Y”) which helped us prepare for 
multiple outcomes. 


Lesson: Embracing multiple perspectives guards against our human biases. It’s like 
having a team of experts in your ear — one might remind you of historical analogies (e.g., 
past bubbles), another focuses on the immediate data, another on long-term potential. 
This approach leads to more balanced decisions. In practice, it means we rarely go “all- 
in” on one extreme view; we size positions for a range of possible futures. The Al 
advisors also bring up considerations a single person might miss, improving the breadth 
of analysis. 


3. The Discipline of Position Sizing and Risk Management 


In all three cases, position sizing was a hero. We often started with small positions and 
only scaled up when confidence (and data) increased. We trimmed or exited when risk 
rose beyond our comfort. By doing so, the system avoided catastrophic losses even if a 
thesis went wrong or was early. For example, we didn’t stubbornly hold a large Tesla 
stake through 2022’s carnage, and we didn’t bet the farm on Al chips in case it was a 
bubble. 


Lesson: How much you invest is as important as what you invest in. The system’s 
method of using small/medium/large position sizing (Chapter 10) and having portfolio- 
level rules (e.g., not more than X% in one theme) proved effective. It creates a safety 
margin. It also acknowledges an important truth: we will be wrong sometimes. By 
never over-sizing, any single mistake won’t knock us out of the game. Surviving to play 
another day is the first rule of long-term investing, especially in a potential end-of-world 
scenario. 


4. Knowing When to Stay Out (or Sell) Is As Important As When to Get In 


There is a tendency in investing to think one must always be doing something — always 
invested, always trading. These case studies show that sometimes the best decision is 
to step aside or reduce exposure when uncertainty is too high or risk/reward too poor. 
We saw this when the system chose not to chase Tesla at absurd heights, or to largely 
exit during COVID’s onset. Conversely, we also saw that once conditions improved, the 
system was willing to get back in. 


Lesson: Patience and timing — not in the sense of pinpoint precision, but in the sense 
of waiting for the odds to be in your favor — are key. The system essentially practices 
strategic patience: it can sit on cash when appropriate (even if that’s hard 
psychologically), and it can re-deploy that cash swiftly when the time is right. This is not 
market timing in the day-trading sense; it’s scenario-based allocation. It requires 
humility to sometimes say “I don’t know what’s going on here, so I’ll reduce risk until | 
do.” As demonstrated, that humility can save a portfolio. 


5. Flexibility and Adaptation to New Information 


In each case, our outlook shifted as new information emerged. We didn’t marry one 
hypothesis (“Tesla will always go up” or “Alis a bubble” or “the pandemic recovery will 


be slow”) regardless of contrary evidence. Instead, we updated dossiers, and 
sometimes reversed course — from seller to buyer or vice versa. 


Lesson: Rigid thinking is dangerous, especially in a world moving as fast as ours. A 
strength of combining human judgment with Al input is that Als can rapidly process new 
data and flag when the regime might be changing. Our system encourages revisiting 
assumptions frequently. Chapter 9’s council method explicitly fosters that by re-asking 
questions at key intervals and expecting different answers if facts have changed. In 
practice, an investor must be willing to let go of yesterday’s story and position for 
today’s reality (or tomorrow’s possible realities). This adaptive mindset is perhaps the 
most valuable skillin an era of constant disruption. 


6. Emotional Detachment and Process Over Prognostication 


Another thread through these stories is how the system helps manage emotions. Many 
investors got swept up in Tesla’s hype or despair; many either overhyped or completely 
dismissed Al chips; and during COVID, many panicked or froze. By relying on a clear 
process (dossiers, councils, scenarios, size rules), our system acts almost like an 
emotional circuit breaker. It replaces knee-jerk reactions with considered steps. 


Lesson: Having a process to follow in good times and bad instills a sort of investor 
calm. You won’t eliminate anxiety (we’re human, after all), but you channel it into 
analysis (“What do the indicators say? What’s my plan for Scenario X vs Y?”) instead of 
into rash trades. This tilt from reactive to proactive behavior is a huge edge. It means 
when others are panicking, you’re executing your pre-thought-out risk plan; when 
others are FOMOing at tops, you’re sticking to your discipline about valuation and size. 


7. No Magic, Just a Better Toolkit 


Perhaps the most important overarching lesson: this system is not magic. It didn’t 
predict these case outcomes with 100% accuracy, nor did it capture every dollar of 
profit. What it did do was provide a structured, research-backed way to navigate them. 
In each case, there were misses (trimming Tesla too early, underestimating how fast 
Nvidia might rise, being a bit slow to re-buy post-COVID bottom). But those misses are 
acceptable because they came from a prudent approach. We’re playing a game of 
probabilities, not certainties. The goal is to have more successes than failures and to 
have failures be small. 


If you compare using the system versus a naive approach (or no plan at all), the 
difference is striking. A naive investor might have: held Tesla all the way up and down 
(round-tripping gains), ignored Nvidia until late then chased at top, or sold everything in 
March 2020 and never re-entered — outcomes that either lost money or missed 
opportunity. The system user, while not perfect, likely ended each scenario in a decent 
place: Tesla case, preserved a lot of gains; Al case, made a good profit with 
manageable risk; COVID case, protected downside and got back into the upside. 


In summary, the case studies validate the core idea of the book: structured, causal, 
Al-augmented thinking can improve investment decision quality in turbulent times. 
There are no guarantees — luck and randomness still play a role — but you tilt the odds in 
your favor. 


Practicing on the Past: How to Train Yourself with Case Studies 


Having seen the system in action on historical episodes, you might be thinking: “This 
makes sense in hindsight, but could | do it in real time? How do! train myself to use this 
method effectively?” One excellent way is to practice on past case studies yourself. 
Treat it like a simulation exercise or war-game for investors. Here’s how you can do it: 


1. Pick a Historical Episode 


Choose an event or period you remember or find interesting. It could be one of the 
cases we discussed or something else entirely — e.g., the 2008 financial crisis, the dot- 
com bubble of 1999-2000, the rise of Bitcoin, a specific stock’s saga like Apple in the 
2000s - whatever. Ideally, pick something where you can gather data (news, charts) and 
that had significant decision points. 


2. Gather the Data and Context 


Do a bit of research as if you were living just before that scenario unfolded. Find: - Price 
charts (5-year or max) leading up to the event. - Major news headlines and fundamental 
metrics from that time (e.g., in 2008, what were people saying about housing? In dot- 
com 1999, what were Cisco’s valuations?). - For macro events, note the economic 
indicators (rates, inflation, etc. at the time). 


3. Build a Mock Dossier 


Write down the key parameters that, in hindsight, one should have watched. For 
example, if doing 2008: - Mortgage delinquencies, bank leverage, credit default swap 
spreads, etc. If doing dot-com: - Nasdaq index valuation, rate of IPOs, any earnings or 
lack thereof in the sector. If doing a company: - Its revenue growth, competition, any big 
product announcements or setbacks. 


Also list what the prevalent narrative was then. (Often, you’ll find the narrative at the 
peak was ridiculously optimistic, and at the bottom, overly pessimistic.) 


4. Role-Play with an LLM Council 


Now, using a tool like ChatGPT (or any Al model you have access to), prompt it to 
imagine it is at that time without knowledge of the future. This is important to avoid 
hindsight bias. For example: “/t is June 2000, the Nasdaq has dropped a bit from its high 
but many think it’s a pullback. Here are some facts... How would different analysts view 
the situation?” Have it produce a bull and bear case from that time’s perspective. 


You can even take on roles yourself: Write down what you might have thought then, and 
then what a skeptic might have said. The goalis to surface the assumptions people had 
versus how reality played out. 


5. Make Simulated Decisions 


Following the structure we used: say it’s your money in that historical moment. Write 
out: - What scenario you’d assign (A, B, C, or D from our framework). - What moves you 


would make (e.g., “Il would sell X and buy Y, or hold Z”). - Crucially, why - tie it to the 
parameters (e.g., “Because credit spreads are widening and that’s a red flag, I’d reduce 
exposure,” or “PC sales are booming, and Microsoft looks undervalued relative to that, 
sol’d go long MSFT”). 


6. Fast-Forward and Analyze 


Then reveal (or recall) what actually happened after. Compare it to your simulated 
actions: - Did your decision avoid a loss or capture a gain? If not, was it because of a 
wrong assumption or a missed parameter? - Were there signals that, in retrospect, 
were key but you ignored or didn’t have? (This can highlight improvements to your 
dossier checklist for next time.) 


If you got it right, was it luck or based on solid reasoning that would generalize? 


The aim here is not to cherry-pick being right; it’s to train your pattern recognition and 
refine the system. The more historical cycles you walk through, the more you’ ll start to 
see parallels to today’s situations (for instance, recognizing “this feels a bit like 2000 
dot-com” or “these conditions echo 2019 pre-COVID”). 


7. Embrace Humility in Practice 


When doing these exercises, you’ll inevitably find some scenarios where even with 
hindsight, it’s hard to know what you should have done. That’s okay. A core part of our 
philosophy is acknowledging uncertainty. Through practice, you get comfortable with 
the idea that sometimes the right move is to do nothing or to take a very cautious 
stance. Not every environment offers a clear high-probability bet — and it’s fine to say 
“Vd stay mostly in cash here and wait.” 


By practicing on past episodes, you also learn to forgive yourself for not nailing tops or 
bottoms. For instance, in our COVID case, nobody buys exactly at the bottom — your 
simulated exercise will show you that missing the bottom by even months still yielded 
great returns if you had a solid process. 


8. Develop Your Own Playbook 


Finally, use these practice sessions to jot down your own heuristics or rules of thumb. 
Over time, you might create personal checklists like: - “If | ever see X (e.g., a stock 


doubling in a month on hype), | will always at least trim my position.” - “If Y happens 
(e.g., yield curve inverts and credit spreads blow out), move to Scenario A playbook 
ASAP.” - “Sector Z usually leads the recovery in this kind of crisis,” etc. 


These become part of your internalized system. Essentially, you’re training your 
intuition in parallel with the explicit framework. 


Remember: The goal of practice is not to prove you would have been a genius investor 
in the past. It’s to make you a better investor going forward by learning from the past. 
Each case study is like a vaccination shot — exposing you to a bit of the disease (market 
chaos) so you build immunity to panic or euphoria when it happens for real. 


Conclusion: No Perfection, But Progress 


Through these case studies, we’ve seen that our system is not a crystal ball —- it won’t 
catch every top or bottom, and it will sometimes be early, sometimes late. But 
importantly, it tends to be directionally right on the big moves and, above all, it keeps 
us out of serious trouble. That is the essence of “investing in the end of the world”: to 
navigate through unprecedented events without assuming we can predict them with 
certainty, but with the confidence that we have a method to respond to whatever 
comes. 


Final Disclaimer: These case studies were illustrative retrospectives, not guarantees of 
how we or you would have acted in real time. Hindsight bias is real; we’ve tried to 
mitigate it by sticking to information available at the time in each scenario, but 
inevitably it’s easier to sound smart after the fact. The value in doing this, however, is in 
training ourselves to think in the structured way during the next crisis or boom. Past 
performance is not indicative of future results — but past learning can be indicative of 
future preparedness. 


In the next and final chapter, we’ll summarize the entire system and discuss how you 
can apply it going forward into an uncertain future. If the world is indeed headed for 
some form of collapse, at least you’ll approach it with a clearer mind and a robust 
process. As these case studies hopefully showed, that can make all the difference 
between panic and purpose.
