Chapter 14 —- Ethics: Making Money While the World 
Burns 


Naming the Uncomfortable Questions 


Imagine the world truly is on the brink of collapse — notin some abstract, distant future, 
but soon, perhaps within a few years. Now imagine using that knowledge to make 
money. It’s an unsettling thought. You might be asking yourself: “/f / believe 
catastrophe is coming and | try to profit from it, does that make me... a bad person?” 
This question might arrive in the quiet moments, unbidden: Am / just another parasite 
taking advantage of suffering? 


Let’s name this discomfort clearly. It can feel like investing in collapse is akin to selling 
tobacco while knowing it causes cancer, or buying stock in weapons 
manufacturers during wartime. In other words, profiting from others’ pain. |f you feela 
twinge of guilt or unease about that, take heart: that feeling is normal and even 
healthy. It means you have a moral compass. In fact, if someone felt nothing at all 
about the idea of making money while the world burns, that would be more concerning. 
We’re human; we’re supposed to care. 


This chapter is not here to pat you on the back or to tell you that every profit is blessed 
by moral righteousness. It’s here to honestly explore the ethics and psychology of 
investing at the end of the world. We will wrestle with the hard questions, not sweep 
them under the rug. By the end, you won’t have a simple “yes or no” verdict on whether 
it’s right or wrong — instead, you should have a clearer understanding of your own values 
and some tools to guide your decisions. 


So if you’ve been secretly thinking, “/sn’t this basically disaster capitalism? Am | 
becoming a war profiteer on Al doomsday?”, know that you’re not alone in that worry. 
(Indeed, critics like Naomi Klein have used the term “disaster capitalism” for the act of 
exploiting crises for profit/1].) Let’s dig into these feelings and questions, starting from 
the very human place of moral discomfort. 


Different Perspectives on Profiting from Collapse 


People approach this moral dilemma from very different angles. There isn’t a single 
“correct” ethical stance in a scenario as extreme as global collapse; reasonable folks 
can land in different places. Let’s look at a few common perspectives readers like you 
might have. As you read, think about which (if any) resonate with you — and remember, 
each stance has its own pros and cons: 


19. “I just want to survive and protect my loved ones.” In this view, making money 
from an impending crisis is first and foremost about duty to family and friends. 
If the ship is sinking, you grab life jackets for your people. You might think, / have 
a responsibility to use every tool available — including saw investing — to shield 
my partner, children, or close circle from the worst. From this angle, profit is a 
means of protection. Morally, it can feel justified because your intention isn’t to 


20. 


21. 


22. 


gloat over others’ misfortune; it’s to reduce the misfortune that befalls your tribe. 
Critics might call it selfish, but defenders say that charity begins at home and 
that looking after your own in hard times is a deeply human, even honorable 
impulse. 


“l want to make money so! can do good with it.” This is the “earn to give” 
mindset — using profits for a greater purpose. If you can foresee the crisis, maybe 
you can channel the gains toward helping others or fighting the very collapse 
you anticipate. For example, you might plan to donate to effective charities, fund 
Al-safety research, support grassroots activists, or just directly assist people hurt 
by the coming turmoil. In ethical philosophy, this stance resonates with a 
utilitarian idea: the ends (doing good) can justify the means (profiting from a bad 
situation). Real-world movements like effective altruism have embraced a 
version of this: the controversial idea of “earning to give” argues that one way to 
better the world is to take a high-paying job or profit from the system, then 
donate much of the money to important causes/2]. The logic is that money is a 
powerful tool — if you have more, you can help more. The tricky part here is 
honesty with oneself: it’s easy to say you’ll use the money for good, but will you 
follow through? And even if you do, could you be unintentionally funding harm in 
the process of earning that money? We’ll come back to these tough questions. 


“| just want to stay out of the way (neutral self-preservation).” Not everyone 
who invests during crisis has grand plans. You might feel, /’m not trying to save 
the world or exploit it—1| just don’t want to be a victim. This stance is about 
personal resilience without taking on moral crusades. You use the investing 
method to keep yourself afloat: secure some savings, maybe relocate or change 
your lifestyle, and essentially opt out of being either hero or villain. Ethically, this 
might seem moderate: you’re neither actively causing harm nor actively riding in 
to save the day. It’s a form of moral minimalism —- focus on not being a burden to 
others, and handle your own needs. The potential downside? Inaction or 
neutrality in a crisis can feel unsatisfying or even cowardly to some; critics might 
ask whether staying “neutral” is just a convenient excuse to not engage with the 
moral challenges at all. Still, for many ordinary people, just getting through the 
day with their family safe is as much heroism as they can muster, and there’s 
dignity in that. 


“! refuse to profit from collapse at all.” Finally, there are those who decide that 
any attempt to make money from humanity’s possible downfall is a line they 
won’t cross. Someone taking this position might drastically limit their investing or 
avoid it entirely once they realize the moral implications. They might say, “I’ll not 
buy shares of companies that thrive on laying off workers or surveilling people or 
accelerating Al arms races. | will not cash in on tragedy.” Instead, they could 
choose to invest time and money directly in preventing collapse or helping 
people, even if that means missing out on financial gains. This stance is morally 
bold — it prioritizes principle over personal gain. Its obvious challenge is 
practicality: if you don’t engage at all with financial preparations, you might put 
yourself (and those who rely on you) in a worse spot if things go south. And 
abstaining from profit doesn’t necessarily slow the collapse; it just means you 


won’t personally benefit. It’s a noble position in theory, but it can come ata cost. 
Only you can weigh whether that cost is worth it. 


These perspectives aren’t exhaustive, but they cover a range from pragmatic to 
idealistic. You might find your own view is a mix of several. The key point is that each 
approach has ethical merits and pitfalls — and acknowledging that can help you 
navigate your own moral stance without self-deception. This book isn’t here to declare 
a winner among these viewpoints. Instead, we want to help you think clearly about 
where you stand and why. 


Responding to Reality vs. Causing Harm 


One crucial distinction can bring some relief to your ethical worries: the difference 
between responding to a harmful reality and actively causing harm. The investing 
method we outlined in this book is about observing and anticipating real-world events 
(even very dark ones) and positioning yourself accordingly. It is not about creating 
those events. This sounds obvious, but it’s worth spelling out. 


e Responding to a coming crisis means you are effectively front-running reality: 
you see signs of what’s likely to happen (say, a company’s Al layoffs ora 
geopolitical conflict), and you adjust your portfolio to prepare. You’re doing what 
any prudent person does hearing a weather forecast — if a storm is likely, you 
close your windows and maybe invest in an umbrella. In our context, that might 
mean investing in companies that build resilient infrastructure, or shorting an 
index that you expect to crash. The moral difference here is that you are not the 
one who brewed the storm; you’re just reading the barometer. 


e Causing harm for profit, on the other hand, would be something entirely 
different and unequivocally unethical. That would be like deliberately setting 
the fire so you can sell fire extinguishers at a markup. For example, it would 
cross a bright red line to go out and actively spread disinformation to crash a 
stock so you profit, or to encourage dangerous Al development just so your 
“doom portfolio” pays off. That’s not what this system is about, and we imagine if 
you’ve read this far, that’s not what you’re about either. 


This may seem like common sense, but guilt can blur the line. You might catch yourself 
thinking, “/f | make money from a company that benefits during a crisis, am | complicit 
in the crisis?” The answer is usually no, not in any direct way. Owning a few shares of 
an agricultural stock because you foresee climate-driven food shortages doesn’t mean 
you’re causing the droughts. Shorting an overvalued tech stock because you suspect a 
bubble doesn’t mean you inflated the bubble. 


However - and this is an important however - we should also acknowledge indirect 
effects and moral entanglement. While you personally aren’t moving markets or funding 
evil empires with your small trades, where you choose to put your money can carry 
symbolic weight and tiny real effects. For instance, investing in a company does signal 
some level of support for it. If thousands of people invest in a company that, say, builds 
autonomous weapons, that could lower the company’s cost of capital or raise its 
profile. Any single investor’s impact is negligible, but collectively, investors do shape 


what businesses thrive. This means it’s worth asking: Am / comfortable owning a piece 
of this enterprise? 


The comforting side is, as a retail investor, you are mostly responding, not 
responsible. You didn’t design the unethical Al or start the war. Still, ethical investing 
isn’t just about blame avoidance; it’s about personal integrity. You have a say (however 
small) in which realities you choose to bet on. You’re allowed to weigh the moral 
implications of those bets. 


In summary, using insight to protect yourself (and even profit) is not morally 
equivalent to causing the world’s problems. We should never conflate awareness with 
culpability. But awareness does bring responsibility: once you see a tragedy coming, 
what will you do with that foresight? Will you just profit and isolate yourself, or will you 
also try to help or at least avoid making things worse? That part is up to you. 


Drawing Your Moral Lines (What Won’t You Invest In?) 


Because even “just responding” can have moral weight, many people find it helpful to 
set some personal filters or red lines for their investments. The beauty of the investing 
system we described (with scenario analysis, LLM councils, etc.) is that you remain the 
ultimate decision-maker on which stocks or sectors to include. The system doesn’t 
force you to invest in anything you find abhorrent. You can design your portfolio to 
reflect your conscience. 


What might moral red lines look like in practice? Here are a few examples of how 
different individuals might draw them: 


e “No Doomsday Devices” filter: You might decide not to invest in companies 
whose core business is something you believe is accelerating the end of the 
world. For example, if you’re convinced that unrestrained Al development is the 
chief threat, you might avoid holding shares in companies leading the charge 
toward more powerful Al at all costs. Or if you think fossil fuel companies are 
hastening ecological collapse, you leave those out, even if they might perform 
well in a certain doom scenario. This way, you’re consciously not profiting from 
the very things you fear and oppose. It’s a form of ethical alignment: your 
investments don’t have to betray your values. 


e Labor and corruption concerns: Maybe you’re okay with Al companies, but you 
draw the line at companies known for exploiting workers or engaging in severe 
corruption. For instance, if a corporation has a track record of using child labor 
or dumping toxic waste in poor communities, you decide you won’t be part of 
its shareholder base. Money is money, but you don’t want the feeling that your 
gain came from someone else’s abuse. In practical terms, you might use existing 
frameworks like ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) scores as a rough 
guide, or do a bit of research into any company that shows up in your scenarios 
to see if it passes your smell test. 


e “Gray areas” with a pledge: Some people take a more nuanced route: invest in 
almost anything, but with a guilty conscience put to work. If you choose to 


include morally gray sectors (say, defense contractors or Big Tech firms with 
problematic practices) because you think they’ll soar in a collapse scenario, you 
might make a personal pledge: “I’ll invest here, but if it pays off, | commita 
significant chunk of the profits to causes that counterbalance the harm.” For 
example, someone who profits from defense stocks might donate to 
humanitarian aid or peace organizations. Or if you make money from a social 
media giant that’s contributing to misinformation, you could use some of it to 
support independent journalism or digital literacy programs. This approach tries 
to balance the scales — it acknowledges complicity to a degree but aims to turn 
it into actionable good. 


The point is not to provide a one-size-fits-all ethical rulebook, but to empower you to 
consciously choose your own boundaries. Take a moment to reflect: Which sectors 
or companies do! personally consider off-limits? Maybe jot down a short list. It could 
be specific (“Il won’t invest in Company X”) or broad (“no weapons manufacturers”, “no 
surveillance tech”, “no companies run by people who have lied to Congress”, etc.). You 


can then apply the investing strategies from earlier chapters within those guardrails. 


This way, you remain true to yourself while still engaging with the market. There is no 
law that says you must invest in everything that might profit. In fact, having a moral filter 
can make you feel more at peace when you do make a trade — you’ll know you at least 
avoided your personal red flags. It transforms investing from a purely profit-driven game 
into something more intentional: a game you play by your rules, even as the wider world 
plays by others. 


Money as a Tool, Not an Idol 


Let’s step back from investing specifics fora moment and talk about money itself. In a 
book about making money amidst global turmoil, it’s easy to get the impression that 
money is the central concern. But remember the larger picture: money is important, 
especially when the world is shaky — and yet, money is only a tool. It’s not the ultimate 
goal of life, and certainly not something to worship, especially if we truly believe the end 
of the world may be near. 


There’s an old saying: “Money is a good servant but a bad master.” This captures a 
crucial truth. If you treat money as your servant — a means to achieve goals, care for 
people, experience joy, and do good — then money can be incredibly useful, particularly 
in chaotic times. With sufficient funds, you can buy time: you might not need to work 
three jobs, giving you precious hours to spend with family or to simply rest (which is a 
luxury for many). Money can buy a degree of freedom: the ability to relocate to a safer 
area, to say no to a toxic job, to support a friend in need. Money can also buy resources 
for resilience: stockpiling food or medicine, securing a safe property, or investing in 
education and skills for a turbulent future. In short, money can amplify your agency -— 
it’s a tool that helps you do more of what you decide matters. 


But if money becomes the master - if the pursuit of wealth overtakes everything else — 
then it becomes a trap. This is true in normal times and even more painfully true in end- 
times scenarios. One could easily fall into a mindset of hoarding wealth as a form of 
control when the world feels uncontrollable. It’s an understandable impulse: when the 


ground is shaking, grabbing for gold (or crypto, or stocks, or whatever) can feel like 
grabbing something solid. Yet, if all you do is obsess over your portfolio while ignoring 
the human moments in front of you, you might end up rich and utterly miserable 
(assuming we’re lucky enough to have time to be miserable). In a darkly comical sense, 
it would be the ultimate irony to survive the apocalypse financially only to realize you 
squandered your remaining days glued to stock charts instead of being with those you 
love. 


So, what’s the balanced view here? It’s absurd to pretend money doesn’t matter - 
until the final cataclysm truly hits, we all have bills to pay and needs to meet. Within the 
story of the man and the tigers, as long as you haven’t fallen into the jaws of the tiger 
quite yet, money still buys dinner and shelter. At the same time, it’s equally absurd to 
think money is the only thing that matters. The true measure of wealth in a collapsing 
world might actually be time, relationships, and meaningful experiences. 


Use money to serve those ends, not replace them. Let money fund the things that 
actually bring joy or comfort: a weekend trip to see your parents, a course to learn 
something you’ve always wanted, a generator for when the power grid gets unreliable, 
or simply the ability to say yes when someone needs help. In doing so, you transform 
money from a cold number on a screen into something imbued with purpose. 


To put it in perspective: if the timeline is short, ask yourself what you’re saving for. Is it 
just to see a bigger number in your account? Or is it to ensure that, come what may, you 
and your loved ones can share as many sweet moments as possible? If it’s the latter, 
then you won’t lose sight of why you’re investing in the first place. The money is a 
means to an end - your values are the end. Keep that hierarchy straight, and you won’t 
fall into the trap of idolizing money. 


A Personal Perspective: Nir’s Journey 


Up to now, we’ve spoken in generalities about what you, the reader, might feel or 
choose. It might be helpful to hear how one person — in this case, Nir, the human co- 
author of this book — grappled with these very issues. This isn’t to present Nir as a 
moral exemplar or the ultimate answer, but as a real example of someone trying to 
reconcile collapse investing with conscience. 


Nir’s background is somewhat unusual: before turning to writing and investing, he 
spent years as an activist trying to prevent Al-related catastrophe. In fact, when 
advanced Al was still mostly theoretical, Nir was on the side of warning people, 
organizing, and pushing for safety measures. He didn’t start out in finance or as a 
Silicon Valley insider; he started out as a concerned citizen yelling “Fire!” in a crowded 
theater that hadn’t yet caught flame. For a long time, he wasn’t trying to make a single 
dollar from the Al boom - quite the opposite, he was trying to slow it down or at least 
make the powers that be consider the risks. 


Why does this matter? It shows that the ethos behind /nvesting in the End of the World 
isn’t giddy apocalypse profiteering. It’s rooted in concern and, frankly, sorrow about 
where we’re headed. Nir’s activism years taught him a hard truth: the world’s 
trajectory can’t be easily turned by one person. By the mid-2020s, as Al 


breakthroughs accelerated and the global mood didn’t shift enough, a realization set in. 
The collapse scenario wasn’t just a tiny probability discussed on obscure forums; it was 
entering mainstream discourse. Governments were talking about Al doomsday plans, 
companies were racing faster than ever, and the brakes were not in sight. 


Around this time, something else changed in Nir’s life - something deeply personal. He 
fell in love. (We’ll keep her name pseudonymous and call her Madie for privacy.) 
Suddenly, the abstract future had a very real face beside him. The prospect of collapse 
now Carried a new weight: protecting and cherishing another person. Like many of us, 
when alone, one might take more risks or live ascetically for a cause; but when you 
have someone you love, your sense of responsibility shifts. Nir has described this as 
a pivotal moment: “I realized | needed to not just fight Al or write manifestos —| needed 
to create a life for myself and my loved one, for however long we have.” 


This is where the idea of an investing method was born. It wasn’t born out of a desire to 
get rich for riches’ sake. It was born from a mix of urgency and care. Nir thought, / have 
all this knowledge and fear about what’s coming... perhaps | can turn it into a guide, 
something practical that others can use, and something that might provide me a 
livelihood too. After years of not trying to monetize doom (and, to be candid, likely 
undermining his own financial stability in the process), he essentially gave himself 
permission to earn some money - but on terms that aligned with his values. That 
meant a few things: 


e Transparency and sharing: Rather than hoard the strategy and quietly trade on 
it, he co-authored this book to share it publicly. The idea was to level the playing 
field a bit. If indeed only a few years remain, this knowledge shouldn’t be 
reserved for elite hedge funds or those “in the know.” Everyone deserves a 
chance to prepare. By writing it all down, Nir isn’t trying to front-run you; he’s 
running alongside you. 


e Not causing harm: Nir’s approach avoids anything that would mean directly 
contributing to what he fears. For example, he’s not about to go take a job at an 
AGI lab to get insider info, nor invest in companies in ways that give them more 
capital to do dangerous things. He’s operating as an observer and analyst, not an 
enabler. This goes back to the earlier point — he strives to respond, not cause. 


e Purpose for profits: Nir has plans for any significant profits that go beyond just 
personal comfort. Some of it, of course, is to build a simple and good life with 
Madie —- what’s the point of saving the world if you lose your own heart in the 
process? But beyond that, he has a clear intention: to fund ongoing awareness 
and safety efforts. That might mean donating to organizations working on Al 
safety, or supporting whistleblowers and journalists shedding light on Al issues, 
or even just having the freedom to work on volunteer projects rather than chase a 
salary. In short, the money becomes fuel for doing what he thinks is right in the 
time that remains. 


By sharing this, we’re not sanctifying Nir or saying “look how great our intentions are.” 
The reason to include his story is to illustrate a middle path. One can be fully aware of 
the darkness ahead, deeply morally opposed to it, and still decide to engage with 


markets and money as a form of pragmatic adaptation. It’s a bit like a doctor who 
knows a patient’s illness is terminal but still works to ease pain and extend life where 
possible. Nir couldn’t stop the illness afflicting civilization (at least, he hasn’t yet), so 
now he’s administering a kind of financial first aid. 


For you, the details will differ. But maybe this personal vignette helps make it concrete: 
you can hate what’s happening in the world and still use your knowledge of it to protect 
those you love. There’s no moral law that says you must go down with the ship just 
because you saw the iceberg and couldn’t move it. 


Guilt, Privilege, and Emotional Honesty 


We need to talk about guilt. If you’re reading this book and considering its strategy, 
chances are you have some advantages that not everyone has. Perhaps you live ina 
country where you have access to online brokers and Al tools. Maybe you have some 
savings to even invest in the first place. You likely have the education or awareness to 
understand these concepts. In a collapsing world, these are forms of privilege. Millions 
— even billions — of people don’t have these luxuries. They’re not plotting trades; they’re 
just trying to find clean water or avoid the next bombing or keep their minimum-wage 
job a little longer. 


When you recognize this disparity, it’s natural to feel a heavy pang of “Why me? Why do 
| get to attempt to secure myself financially while others are in immediate peril?” 
Alongside that, there might be a fear: “By focusing on my portfolio, am | abandoning 
those less fortunate? Shouldn’t! be doing something more directly helpful?” These 
voices of conscience are not your enemy. They keep you connected to humanity. But 
let’s unpack them so that guilt becomes a guide, not a ball-and-chain. 


First, acknowledge the reality: yes, you do have relative privilege. This isn’t about self- 
loathing; it’s about clarity. If you’re reading an investing book and thinking about the 
future, you’re already ahead of many who are stuck in crisis mode daily. It’s okay - you 
didn’t personally create the global inequalities, and you can’t solve them single- 
handedly. Guilt often comes when we compare our situation to others and find it unfair. 
But instead of letting that guilt fester into inaction or self-hatred, you can channel it into 
empathy and purpose. 


Consider this perspective: by taking care of your own needs and those of your loved 
ones, you actually free up capacity to help others. It’s like the oxygen mask principle 
on airplanes — secure your own mask so that you are able to assist those who can’t 
secure theirs. If you are financially stable, you won’t be the one needing rescue when 
things get tough, and maybe you could even be the rescuer for someone else. There’s 
nothing noble about refusing to prepare and then becoming another victim who needs 
saving. 


On the other hand, beware the trap of denial or self-justification. Some people react 
to guilt by suppressing it or rationalizing it away completely: “Well, the world’s always 
been unfair, nothing | can do, might as well just enjoy myself.” That attitude, taken too 
far, deadens your empathy. It’s true none of us can Carry all the world’s sorrows on our 
back — it would crush us — but we also shouldn’t pretend those sorrows don’t exist. The 


healthiest posture might be a kind of sober sadness paired with determined action. You 
can Say to yourself, “/t’s awful that not everyone can do what !’m doing; | feela 
responsibility to not waste this chance | have.” 


Emotional realism means accepting that you will feel some sadness, anger, and 
guilt as you navigate this crazy time. Let those feelings surface and let them inform 
you, but not paralyze you. If you ever feel your ethical discomfort growing to a point 
where it’s debilitating — say you feel despair or profound depression about the state of 
the world - recognize that that’s a sign to seek support. Talk to friends or family about 
how you feel, or even a professional counselor if needed. These are heavy topics, and 
you’re only human. It’s okay to need emotional help. In fact, grappling with the end of 
the world and trying to act morally is about as heavy as it gets; you’re not “weak” for 
feeling weighed down. 


Finally, remember that you’re not alone in this mix of guilt and determination. 
There’s a community (even if loosely knit) of people who both see the looming threats 
and refuse to completely give up. They’re trying to carve out pockets of goodness, 
sanity, and safety — just like you. Some are investing; some are protesting; some are 
doing both. What unites them is a refusal to become either heartless profiteers or 
helpless bystanders. If you can find even one or two like-minded souls (online or offline) 
to discuss these feelings with, it can help a lot. Just knowing that others share your 
conflict can lighten it. 


To sum up: feeling bad about the state of the world is normal. Feeling a bit guilty that 
you’re in a position to strategize when others can’t is also normal. Use those feelings 
as fuel to stay compassionate and fair, but don’t let them prevent you from taking 
practical steps to survive. As one philosopher noted, you can’t pour from an empty cup. 
Taking care of your finances and well-being can ensure you have something to pour out 
for others, whether that’s money, time, or simply emotional availability. 


An Ethical Investing Checklist: Questions to Ask Yourself 


By now we’ve explored many ideas and feelings. It might be helpful to distill some of 
this into practical questions you can use as an ethical compass when making 
investment decisions. Before you click “buy” or “sell” on that doomsday trade, 
consider running through a quick mental (or written) checklist. Here are some 
questions that can guide you: 


23. Alignment with My Values: Does this investment align with my core values? 
Take a hard look at the company or asset in question. What does the company 
actually do? Are you comfortable being a (even tiny) part-owner of that 
enterprise? For example, if you strongly value privacy, think twice before 
investing in a surveillance technology firm. If you’re passionate about 
environmental stewardship, consider whether that mining stock fits your ethos. 
You don’t have to overthink every trade, but at least for major positions, ask: “Am 
| okay holding this, morally?” lf the answer is a gut-level no, that’s a sign to look 
for an alternative. 


24. 


25. 


26. 


27. 


Impact (Even If Tiny): Will my investment choice, in any meaningful way, 
contribute to harm? This is tricky because, as we discussed, your single action is 
usually economically negligible. But this is more about principle and cumulative 
effects. If a thousand investors like you pour money into something, does it 
accelerate a harmful trend? It might help to rephrase: “/f many people did what 
I’m about to do, would the world be better or worse?” If the honest answer is 
“worse,” ask if there’s another way to achieve your financial goal. Perhaps a 
different company in the same sector that’s slightly more ethical, or a way to 
short the bad actor instead of going long. Sometimes you can’t avoid all dirty 
hands — the world is complicated — but you can make an effort to minimize your 
participation in what you personally view as wrongdoing. 


Purpose of Profits: What is this money for, ultimately? Before you execute a 
trade, imagine it goes exactly as you hope - you profit handsomely. Now, picture 
what you will do with that profit. Be concrete: will you bolster your emergency 
fund for your family? Will you take a trip to visit someone important to you? Will 
you donate a portion to a cause? Will you invest in a home or a community 
project? By visualizing the end use of the money, you remind yourself that profit 
isn’t just a number - it’s fuel for real things. If you struggle to answer what the 
money is for, that might indicate you’re getting caught up in profit for profit’s 
sake. On the other hand, having a clear plan like, “If | make $10,000 here, $5k 
goes to my kid’s education fund and $1k goes to support my unemployed friend,” 
can imbue your trade with meaning. It doesn’t have to be all altruistic either; it’s 
fine if it’s partly for fun or joy (say, a celebratory dinner or a concert you’ve always 
wanted to attend). Just know why you seek the gain. 


Soul Check (The Mirror Test): How would | feel explaining this choice to 
someone | respect? This could be a loved one, a mentor, even a child. If you had 
to sit down and say, “I decided to invest in XYZ because...”, what reaction do you 
imagine or hope for? If you’d feel ashamed or have to euphemize your reasons, 
that’s a warning sign. If you could look your dear friend in the eye and they’d nod 
understandingly, that’s a good sign. Another version of this is the future self test: 
imagine looking back on this decision a year from now (or if things really go bad, 
in whatever future you might have). Will you be proud, indifferent, or regretful 
about this choice? None of us can predict the future perfectly, but we often have 
an inner moral intuition about actions before we take them. Listen to that inner 
voice. 


Time and Attention Balance: /s the time and mental energy I’m spending on this 
investment worth it? Every hour you spend doom-scrolling news or tweaking your 
portfolio is an hour not spent on other aspects of life. It’s worth asking, “/f/ only 
had a year (or five) left to live in relative normalcy, is this how! want to spend a 
chunk of it?” This question isn’t exactly about the investment’s morality, but 
about life priorities. Sometimes we justify excessive focus on money by saying 
“’’m doing it for my family or future,” which can be true — but it can also become 
an excuse to avoid other challenging or beautiful parts of life. If you catch 
yourself checking stocks when you could be playing with your child or calling 
your parents or going for a walk, gently remind yourself of the strawberry (more 


on that soon). The goal of ethical end-of-world investing is not to win some 
contest of who dies with the most money; it’s to enhance life, not replace life. 
Use this question to keep yourself honest about that balance. 


These questions don’t come with definitive yes/no thresholds. They are meant to 
prompt reflection. Sometimes, just pausing to run through the checklist in your mind 
will clarify the right course of action. Maybe you’ll cancel a trade, or maybe you’ll 
proceed but with a clearer conscience and plan for the proceeds. The key is not to go 
on autopilot. Ethical lapses often happen not because people choose evil deliberately, 
but because they don’t choose at all - they just slide into decisions without reflection. 
By actively questioning yourself, you stay awake to your own values. And if you ever 
answer one of these questions in a way you’re not proud of, that gives you a chance to 
adjust before reality adjusts it for you. 


Remember the Strawberry: Finding Meaning in the Moment 


In the preface of this book, we shared a Zen parable about a man hanging off a cliff, a 
hungry tiger above and another below, with the rope he’s clinging to slowly being 
gnawed by two mice. In that dire, no-win situation, he notices a wild strawberry within 
arm’s reach. He plucks it and eats it. “How sweet it tasted!” the story concludes. 


It’s time to circle back to that strawberry, because it holds the secret of why any of this 
— investing, ethics, survival— matters at all. We are that man on the vine. The tigers 
represent the existential threats (Al doom, climate collapse, nuclear war, take your 
pick). The mice represent the relentless passage of time (each day, each month, 
nibbling away at the rope of our lives and civilization). By all rational accounts, the 
situation is grim and the ending likely tragic. And yet... there are strawberries. 


In the parable, the man’s act of tasting the strawberry isn’t a foolish denial of his 
predicament; it’s a courageous affirmation of present life. It’s saying: Even now, with 
doom surrounding me, there is this moment and it can have sweetness. The point of 
making money at the end of the world is not to become a dragon sleeping on a pile 
of gold. It is to enable more strawberry moments. Think of profit as a way to reach a few 
more sweet experiences in the time we have. 


What might a strawberry look like for you? It could be as simple as a quiet cup of coffee 
with your spouse in the morning sun, knowing the bills are paid for now. It could be the 
relief of helping a friend get to safety because you had the resources to do so. It could 
be the laughter of your child when you’re not working overtime because your smart 
investing gave you breathing room to come home early. It could be donating to a cause 
that actually saves a life or preserves a piece of art or nature, giving someone else a 
strawberry moment of their own. 


The tigers (the collapse scenarios) are real in our story —- we’re not sugarcoating that. 
We’ve spent the whole book staring up at those tigers, describing them in detail. We’ve 
also looked down into the chasm below. We are not in denial. But if all we do is fixate on 
teeth and doom, we might miss the very thing that makes life worth living in the first 
place. There’s a balance to be struck: see the tigers clearly and taste the strawberry 


anyway. In fact, the clarity of the danger can make that strawberry taste sweeter, can 
make moments more precious. 


Ethically, this perspective can guide you. If you ask, “What is a good life when time is 
short?”, the answer is surely not “accumulate as much as possible and sit on it.” The 
answer likely involves love, connection, creation, and presence. Money can support 
those things but should never eclipse them. Perhaps counterintuitively, keeping the 
strawberry in mind can prevent both ethical compromise and joyless hoarding. If you 
regularly ask yourself, “Have | tasted the strawberry today?”, you’re less likely to 
become the kind of person who’d, say, lie or cheat for an extra dollar — because you 
remember that the dollar is not the point. And you’re also less likely to drown in despair, 
because you remember there are still sweet things to reach for, even in darkness. 


The Zen story doesn’t tell us what happens after the strawberry. Maybe the man falls to 
his death moments later; maybe not. What matters is that he truly lived in that 
moment by savoring that berry. Similarly, none of us know exactly how our efforts will 
pan out. Maybe our investments secure a peaceful final chapter of life; maybe collapse 
comes sooner and all the numbers were for naught. But if along the way we were fully 
alive and aligned with our values, if we cared for others and experienced gratitude for 
small joys, then we haven’t wasted our time. 


Crafting Your Personal Ethical Plan 


We’ve covered a lot of philosophical ground. Let’s close this chapter with something 
concrete and actionable for you to do. Think of it as a mini-homework or a journal 
exercise — one that could make a real difference in how you approach the coming 
months. The task is to write down your own ethical investing plan. This isn’t for anyone 
else’s eyes (unless you want to share); it’s a compass for you to refer back to when 
things get confusing or emotionally charged. 


Here’s what to include in that one-pager (or even a half-page of bullet points): 


e Your “Why”: Answer for yourself, “Why do! want to invest at all, given the 
possibility that the world might end soon?” Be honest. There’s no wrong answer. 
It could be “to protect my family’s well-being,” or “to fund a peaceful final 
chapter for me and my community,” or even “because it gives me a sense of 
control and I’d go crazy otherwise.” Knowing your core motivation will center you. 
Whenever you’re caught up in the frenzy of market news, re-reading your “why” 
can remind you of your true priorities. 


e Who You’re Protecting or Supporting: Write down the names of people (or 
causes) that you care about deeply. It could be your children, your partner, your 
parents, close friends, or a charity or group of strangers you feel a bond with (for 
example, “the next generation of kids in my town” or “refugees of climate 
disasters” — whatever moves you). This is to remind you that your investing is 
ultimately in service of someone or something, not an end in itself. When faced 
with a tough choice, you can ask, “Does this serve the people on my list?” 


e Your Red Lines: List the sectors, companies, or practices you refuse to invest in 
(or will only invest in with significant caution). Make it as specific as you need. 
This is your moral do-not-cross list. It might include things like: “No investments 
in companies that fuel mass surveillance,” or “Avoid any stock that requires 
things to get worse for people (e.g., private prisons, predatory lenders),” or 
whatever you personally feel strongly about. This list can evolve as you learn, but 
having it written down will make it easier to stick to when temptation comes. It’s 
much easier to say no when you’ve pre-decided “This is off-limits for me.” 


e Plans for Good: Write a short plan for how you intend to use any significant 
profits. This doesn’t have to be a binding pledge, but sketch some intentions: “If | 
make more than $X, | will donate Y% to [cause or community].” Or “Profits will go 
towards [my sibling’s debt] or [building a resilient food garden for neighbors] or 
[funding an art project | care about].” Include fun things too: “I’ll take my family 
on that trip we always wanted” — if that’s a strawberry for you, acknowledge it. 
The point is to anchor your gains to real-world outcomes that reflect your 
values. It’s surprisingly easy to lose sight of why you wanted money once 
numbers start growing. A written reminder helps keep you grounded. 


e If Things Go South: This is a harder one, but consider jotting down a note to your 
future self about how you want to behave if things really start falling apart. For 
example: “If collapse accelerates and society frays, | commit to still treat people 
around me with kindness and not just retreat with my money.” Or “I will 
remember the less fortunate and find ways to share or help, even if it’s scary.” It’s 
like a short ethical will for chaotic times. We all hope we’ll keep our humanity in a 
crisis, but it helps to articulate it now, while calm, and keep that note where you 
can see it. 


Once you’ve written this down —- your why, your who, your red lines, your plans for good, 
and your commitments — keep it somewhere visible. Maybe literally tape it inside your 
notebook or on the wall near your desk. You might even give it a title like “My Ethical 
Compass” or “In Case of Moral Uncertainty, Read This.” Every so often (especially if 
you’re about to make a big move or when you feel morally icky about something), read 
it. Update it as needed, because life changes and you might refine your views. 


This little exercise is powerful because it externalizes your intentions. When market 

adrenaline is pumping or fear is gripping you during a news doom-scroll, it’s easy to 

lose track of these quiet resolutions. Having them in writing is like having your better 
self from a calm Sunday afternoon speak to your frantic self on a panicked Tuesday 

midnight. 


Lastly, remember that no one is perfectly ethical, especially not in a collapsing, 
complex world. You will make some mistakes. You might realize in hindsight that 
something you invested in had a darker side you didn’t know, or you might catch 
yourself getting greedy and then feel guilty. Forgive yourself, learn, and adjust. This 
ethical plan isn’t about purity; it’s about intentionality. It’s about aiming to live, even 
financially, with purpose and heart, rather than by accident or solely by fear. 


Postscript: This chapter has been a heavy, soul-searching one. It’s not offering legal, 
financial, or mental health advice —- those domains have their own experts. We’ve 
shared philosophies and frameworks, but you have to decide what feels right and seek 
professional guidance if needed. And if all this talk of the end of the world or moral 
burden is making you feel overwhelmed or hopeless, please reach out to someone -a 
friend, a family member, or a counselor. You’re not alone in feeling this. Sometimes 
just voicing these fears can lighten their weight. Remember the strawberry, remember 
why you Care, and take care of yourself. 


Now, having wrestled with ethics, let’s continue our journey with clearer eyes and a 
steadier heart. The world may be burning, but we still seek to do what’s right while doing 
what it takes to get through the flames.
