Chapter 15 — Carpe Diem Finance: Using Money and 
Time While You Still Have Them 


Why “Carpe Diem Finance” Matters Now 


In the strawberry parable we began this book with, a man is chased by tigers and 
hanging off a cliff by a vine that two mice are gnawing through. With death above and 
below, he notices a wild strawberry, plucks it, and savors its sweetness. The lesson is 
clear: even when the world is falling apart, there are moments of sweetness available 
— if we choose to notice and taste them. “Carpe Diem Finance” is about making sure we 
don’t miss the strawberry. It would be a tragedy to see the tigers clearly (to 
understand the risks of collapse), to build a clever system to make money in the last 
years... and then spend those years glued to screens, obsessed with charts, too fearful 
to actually live. If we end up dying with a big brokerage account and an empty heart, we 
will have missed the point entirely. 


My own background in Zen martial arts taught me something about presence and fear. 
As a teenager | practiced Kung Fu and later Tai Chi; my instructors drilled into me that if 
you fight with fear in your heart, you’ve lost before you begin. Bruce Lee expressed a 
similar idea in Tao of Jeet Kune Do — he quoted a Zen saying: “Into a soul absolutely free 
from thoughts and emotion, even the tiger finds no room to insert its fierce claws.”[1]. In 
other words, when you let go of fear and stay fully present, the metaphorical tigers can’t 
paralyze you. Carpe Diem Finance exists to help you face the tigers of our time (Al 
risks, chaos, uncertainty) with clear eyes and a clear heart —- so you can respond wisely 
without freezing. It’s not about ignoring real dangers; it’s about refusing to let fear steal 
the present. As the proverb goes, “A ship in harbor is safe, but that is not what ships are 
built for.”[2] We weren’t built just to survive; we were built to sail, to venture out and 
truly live. This chapter is about using the money and knowledge you’ve gained as tools 
to live fully in the time we have, not merely to survive a little longer. 


What Is Money For When Time Is Short? 


Let’s begin with a direct question: If you truly believed society might only have a few 
years left of relative normalcy, what would you use your money for? Imagine you 
woke up with that awareness fresh in your mind — that the vine is fraying and the tigers 
are waiting. Would your priorities change from business-as-usual? Most people, when 
pressed, come up with answers like these: 


e Buying time: Using money to free yourself from drudgery or soul-sucking work. 
If you have savings or investment gains, you might downshift to fewer work hours 
or quit a hated job, so you can spend time with loved ones or pursue a passion. 
Research shows this can boost happiness — people report greater life 
satisfaction when they use money to buy time, for example by outsourcing 
chores or reducing a horrible commute[3]. In fact, one study found that working 
adults were happier after spending money on a time-saving service than ona 
material purchase[3]. The takeaway: time is precious, and money can 
sometimes buy back that precious time. 


Buying experiences: Funding memorable experiences and adventures. This 
could mean travel (while planes still fly), seeing natural wonders, or visiting 
friends and family across the country. It might mean finally taking that road trip 
or learning that skill you’ve put off. Psychologists have found that investing in life 
experiences tends to give more enduring happiness than buying material 
things[4][5]. Material possessions quickly become “stuff” in the background, but 
experiences turn into stories, growth, and cherished memories. You might forget 
what phone you owned in 2023, but you won’t forget watching a sunrise from a 
mountaintop or singing along at a concert with your best friend. 


Two friends pause on a mountaintop, taking in a breathtaking view. Experiences like 


these often become lifelong memories — the kind of “dividend” that grows in sweetness 
over time. Research suggests that positive life experiences tend to appreciate in value 
within our memories, whereas gadgets and luxuries quickly depreciate[4]/5]. If the 
world were ending soon, which would you rather have: a house full of fancy objects, ora 
mind and photo album full of adventures? 


Basic comfort and security: Of course, some money must go to comfort, 
health, and dignity. This means maintaining a safe shelter, keeping the fridge 
stocked, and having resources for medical needs. In uncertain times, there’s real 
psychological value in knowing you can cover the basics for yourself and those 
who depend on you. Part of money’s purpose - even in a short timeline — is to 
provide a cushion against small shocks (a car repair, a minor medical issue) so 
that those things don’t derail your remaining time. Essentially, a bit of savings can 
buy you peace of mind, which in turn frees your attention to focus on what 
matters. 


Helping and connecting with others: If you have some financial abundance, 
you might use it to support people you care about (or even strangers). This could 


mean paying for a parent’s medication, helping a friend in need, or donating to a 
cause that matters in these final years. In times of crisis, generosity tends to 
shine as a source of meaning. Knowing you made someone else’s path easier 
can be deeply fulfilling — it’s a way of building connection and legacy. Many of 
us derive more satisfaction from gifting an experience or relief to someone we 
love than from buying ourselves another toy. 


e Creating or completing something meaningful: Money can also fuel creative 
or legacy projects. Maybe you’ve always wanted to write a book, make a film, 
start a community garden, or preserve your family history. Funds could be used 
to take a sabbatical to create art, or to launch a small venture that might not pay 
off financially but leaves a mark on the world. In a way, this is also an experience 
— the experience of creating and contributing. If the clock is ticking, you might 
ask: what do | truly want to complete or put into the world while | still can? 


Everyone’s mix will be personal. But one thing is certain: in an end-of-the-world 
scenario, the old paradigm of “just keep saving and compounding for decades” no 
longer makes sense. Money becomes a means to maximize meaning, joy, and love 
now, rather than a score to be accumulated. It’s been said that you should “die with 
more stories than screenshots.” In other words, when your time is up, what will 
matter are the stories you lived, not the numbers on a screen. As entrepreneur Bill 
Perkins argues in his book Die With Zero, we should aim to use our money to maximize 
life experiences before we die, rather than leaving behind a large pile of unspent 
wealth[6]. The goal is not to mindlessly burn through money, but to remember that 
money is just a tool - and the tool’s purpose is to help you build a life that feels rich in 
experience. Ahammer is useless if you never build something with it; likewise, a 
growing portfolio is futile if you never cash in some of it to taste the strawberries. 


Designing Your “Life Portfolio” 


At this point in the book, you’re familiar with building a financial portfolio — balancing 
stocks, cash, and assets for a given market scenario. Now it’s time to zoom out and 
design a life portfolio. This is a way of looking at all the facets of life that matter to you, 
almost like asset classes, and asking if you’re allocating your time and energy wisely 
among them. 


A traditional investment portfolio might include categories like growth vs defensive 
stocks, or different sectors. In a life portfolio, the categories could be, for example: 


e Relationships: This includes your partner, family, friends, and community — all 
the human connections that provide love, support, and meaning. 

e Health: Your physical, mental, and emotional well-being. Without reasonable 
health, every other part of life can crumble; health is a foundational asset. 

e Experiences & Joy: The activities, hobbies, travel, learning and simple pleasures 
that bring you joy and make life worth living. These are the “dividends” that pay 
out in memories and personal growth. 


e Contribution & Legacy: What you give to the world. This could be your work (if it 
feels meaningful), creative projects, activism, volunteering, or helping others. 
It’s the mark you leave and the value you create beyond yourself. 


e Inner Life (Spirit/Mind): Your inner development -— spiritual practice, 
philosophical understanding, self-knowledge, or even just the quality of your 
mind. This “asset” determines how you experience everything else, as it 
encompasses your perspective and resilience. 


Take a moment to reflect on these categories and consider: What is my current 
allocation of time and energy? Many people, even in normal times, end up heavily 
overweight in “work & screens” and underweight in “love, health, and joy.” We often 
default to spending the majority of our waking hours on career and consumption 
(working, commuting, checking emails, browsing the internet), essentially hoping that 
“someday” we’ll have time for the other things. But if you truly believe time could be 
short, the risk of a misallocated life becomes urgent. You might not have decades to 
course-correct later. Just as a financial advisor would tell a 70-year-old to rebalance 
out of risky stocks, I’m telling you: rebalance your life portfolio now, while you still 
can. 


What might a better life allocation look like? This will differ by person and life 
circumstances, but you could start by defining rough percentages or priorities. For 
example, you might decide to allocate, say, 40% to relationships, 20% to maintaining 
health, 20% to personal joy/experiences, 10% to contribution projects, and 10% to 
work/income. The numbers aren’t the point — the point is to consciously decide that 
work and money are not your only assets. In fact, if collapse is truly near, work and 
money are just the support system for the real assets: time, love, health, and 
purpose. 


| (Nir) had to confront this reality myself. For years | was heavily invested in my 
“contribution” asset—| spent a decade as an activist raising awareness about the 
dangers of unchecked technologies after reading Bill Joy’s Why the Future Doesn’t 
Need Us. | poured my heart and countless hours into that cause, often at the expense 
of relationships or personal enjoyment. | don’t regret it - it was meaningful work, driven 
by passion. (In martial arts terms, | was fighting for something | believed in, with no fear 
of the personal cost.) However, my life portfolio was unbalanced — too much in 
activism and knowledge-gathering, not enough in simple human joys. It wasn’t until | 
fell in love with Madie that | realized | needed to rebalance. Love has a way of clarifying 
priorities. Suddenly “relationships” shot to the top of my allocation, and | was willing to 
reassign time from solitary research to building a life with this person. It felt like going 
from a defensive, all-in-my-head strategy to a more holistic portfolio that included 
my heart. 


| want to emphasize a key insight here: doing things with your whole heart can yield 
incredible results. This applies to all areas of the life portfolio. When | was young, | saw 
an example of this in an unlikely place —-a math competition. In the 2006 documentary 
Hard Problems about the International Math Olympiad, a Chinese student named Zhiyu 
Liu achieved a perfect score. When asked if he was surprised by his win, he humbly 
said, “No, | was not surprised... | study mathematics using my heart.” That line struck 


me deeply. He excelled not just from raw talent, but because he put his heart fully into 
it. ’ve found the same in my own journey as an inventor and thinker: the breakthroughs 
came when | cared so much about a problem that | lived and breathed it, far beyond 
duty or ego. So, as you design your life portfolio, consider not just the allocation but the 
quality of your investment. Are you half-heartedly diversifying your life, or are you 
wholeheartedly committing to the people and passions that matter most to you? Time 
is short - don’t hold back. Invest yourself fully in your relationships, your joys, your 
contributions. As the saying goes, you want to leave it all on the field. In the end, that’s 
what a rich life is — not rich with money, but rich with meaning because you gave it 
everything you had. 


The “Joy Budget” vs. the “Safety Budget” 


Let’s get practical about finances now. One way to plan your money in a Carpe Diem 
framework is to split your resources (and future gains) into two mental buckets: a 
Safety Budget and a Joy Budget. This is a bit like the idea of having both an emergency 
fund and a fun fund, but tailored to an end-of-world timeline. 


1. Safety Budget: This is the money that ensures your basic security. It covers things 
like rent or mortgage, groceries and water, utility bills, essential medications and 
healthcare, and a modest cushion for emergencies. Even if society might collapse in 
2026, we don’t know exactly when or how. There could be a period of instability where 
having some cash or liquid assets could mean the difference between weathering a 
crisis or suffering unnecessarily. The Safety Budget is about resilience: it’s the portion 
of your wealth you keep to protect your dignity and physical well-being (and that of your 
dependents) for as long as possible. Think of it as the reinforced core of your financial 
house - you want it solid enough to withstand storms. In practical terms, you might 
calculate how much you need per month for essentials, multiply by a number of 
months (say, 12 or 24), and set that aside. That money might stay in safer, liquid forms 
(cash, short-term bonds, gold, etc.), not high-risk investments. The Safety Budget gives 
you peace of mind that you can handle disruptions or job loss or spikes in the cost of 
basics. It helps you sleep at night, which is not trivial in chaotic times. 


2. Joy Budget: This is the money explicitly dedicated to living well - to creating 
moments of happiness, love, and meaning in the time that remains. It’s funds 
earmarked for the kinds of experiences and gestures we discussed earlier: travel, 
visiting loved ones, hobbies, learning opportunities, celebrations, small luxuries that 
uplift you, and generous acts of giving. The Joy Budget is what prevents you from falling 
into the trap of “all preparation, no celebration.” It’s a fail-safe against the mindset that 
says “not yet, not yet” forever. By deciding in advance that a certain portion of your 
resources must go toward joy, you make sure you don’t postpone life until it’s too late. 
Remember that common regret of the dying: “/ wish | hadn’t worked so hard.” Many 
people realize too late that they traded away precious moments for an extra bit of 
security or success that, in the end, didn’t matter. The Joy Budget is designed to 
proactively avoid that regret. 


How do you determine the size of each budget? This will depend on your individual 
situation — your savings, income, obligations, and how strongly you lean toward caution 
versus Carpe diem. One approach is to prioritize Safety up to a comfortable baseline 


(the essentials and a buffer), and declare that everything above that baseline is fair 
game for Joy. For example, you might decide: “If my investments yield any profit this 
year, | will take 30% of those gains and immediately use them for something meaningful 
(trips, family experiences, donations, etc.), while the remaining 70% | either reinvest or 
keep for safety.” The ratios can be whatever feels right - maybe 50/50, or maybe you 
start with 20% joy and increase it as the timeline shortens. The key is to make it 
conscious and automatic. If you don’t define it, the default behavior is usually to 
reinvest everything (just a little more safety, just in case...) or, conversely, to splurge 
irrationally. We want a middle path: structured enjoyment. 


Think of the Joy Budget as a rule that helps you actually live, not just plan to live. 
Suppose you made a particularly profitable trade or your doom portfolio goes up 40%. 
It’s easy to just see that as numbers to grow further. But instead, under Carpe Diem 
Finance, you’d say: “That gain means X dollars go straight to the Joy fund —- now! can 
finally book that trip to see my old friend whom | miss dearly, and buy the gear to record 
those songs I’ve been meaning to compose.” If you never take profits for joy, you risk 
playing a “one more year, one more trade” game indefinitely — until the game ends 
abruptly and all those chips you’ve amassed can no longer be cashed in. Don’t let that 
happen. Decide a percentage or a threshold where you will allow yourself to harvest 
some gains and convert them into life experiences or acts of love. 


On the flip side, the Safety Budget puts a healthy limit on YOLO impulses. It’s not about 
saying “spend every penny because nothing matters.” It does matter that you can eat 
and stay warm. The Safety bucket ensures you don’t go overboard and jeopardize your 
basic well-being or become a burden on others prematurely. It speaks to that part of 
you that is rational and responsible. The beauty of having both budgets is that they keep 
each other in check: Safety without Joy leads to dying rich and bitter; Joy without Safety 
leads to possibly not making it to the finish line. Balance is the goal. 


Take a moment to sketch out what your two budgets might look like. It could be as 
simple as writing, “Safety = enough for 18 months of basics = approximately $XX,000. 
Joy = Y% of any surplus or windfall = these specific desires | will fulfill.” By writing it 
down, you commit to yourself that you won’t be all feast or all famine — you’ll be both 
prudent and fully alive. 


Relationships: Your Highest-Return “Asset” 


If there’s one thing nearly every study and every culture agrees on, it’s this: the people 
you love are the true wealth in your life. Money is just a tool; relationships are the 
actual treasure. An astounding Harvard study spanning 85 years found that the quality 
of our relationships is the single biggest factor in our happiness (and even in our health 
and longevity)[7][8]. And when people reach the end of life, their reflections drive the 
point home even harder. Bronnie Ware, a palliative care nurse who recorded the top 
regrets of the dying, wrote: “/t is not money or status that holds the true importance... It 
all comes down to love and relationships in the end. That is all that remains in the final 
weeks, love and relationships. ”[9]. Think about that. In the final countdown, with the 
tigers above and the void below, the only thing most people hold onto is the love they 
shared and received. 


Now, in a scenario where the world might end soon, this insight becomes even more 
urgent. If time is short, you don’t have the luxury to put off mending that strained 
friendship, or to postpone quality time with your children to next year, or to neglect 
telling your partner how much they mean to you. Now is the time to invest deeply in 
relationships. In financial terms, relationships offer the highest “return on investment” 
in terms of life satisfaction. They are resilient, too —-love can endure through chaos, 
whereas money may not. No matter what happens in 2026 or beyond, having people 
who Care about you (and who you care about) will make any outcome more bearable. 
Conversely, dying rich and alone is a poor bargain, apocalypse or not. 


So how can you use money and time to strengthen relationships? A few ideas: 


e Give the gift of time and presence: If you have money that can free you from 
overtime or allow you to take unpaid leave, consider doing it so you can be with 
your loved ones more. For example, maybe you can afford to downshift to four 
days a week at work — and dedicate that extra day to family. Or use some savings 
to take a summer off and make memories with your kids. Those memories are 
priceless; nobody can repossess them or devalue them. If you’re far from those 
you love, use resources to close the distance — fly to see your parents or 
siblings, even if it’s expensive or inconvenient. The mice are gnawing; don’t 
assume “oh, I’ll do it in a couple of years.” 


e Support your loved ones’ needs and dreams: If you’re in a position to help, 
using money to reduce a loved one’s stress can be immensely meaningful. This 
might mean paying for a medical treatment for a sick relative, or helping a dear 
friend cover their rent during a rough patch. It could also mean funding an 
experience with them - like taking your parents on a trip to their homeland one 
last time, or signing your partner up for that art class they always talk about (and 
going together). Money spent to uplift someone you love or to fulfill a shared 
dream is usually money very well spent. It creates a story and often deepens the 
bond between you. In an end-times scenario, these are like secure investments 
in goodwill and love — no market crash can take them away. 


e Prioritize relationship rituals: Use some of your newfound time or resources to 
create regular rituals of connection. For instance, start a weekly dinner with 
friends (you can host and provide the food, making it easier for others to join). Or 
plan a monthly adventure with your spouse - even if it’s just a day trip to a nearby 
park, fueled by a tank of gas and some picnic supplies. If you have kids, maybe 
allocate part of your Joy Budget to a special day with each child individually, 
doing something they love. The point is to put energy and yes, some money, 
into consistent connection. Don’t let logistics or stinginess prevent these 
gatherings — if you can afford to, pay for the Airbnb for a cousins’ reunion, or buy 
the theater tickets for date night. Consider it an investment in happiness for all 
involved. 


A group of friends and family gather to watch a golden sunset together. In the end, these 
shared moments are what life is about — the laughter, the conversations, the simple 
companionship of people you care about. Numerous studies confirm that strong 
relationships make us happier and healthier[8], and in our final moments, it’s the love 
and connections that remain, not our bank balance[9]. Money can’t buy love, but it can 
be used to express love — whether by creating opportunities to be together or by helping 
those we love in times of need. 


’ll share something personal: | mentioned how falling in love with Madie changed my 
priorities. Before her, | was prepared to live a relatively ascetic life of the mind — 
studying, warning about Al doom, mentally “prepping” for the worst. Love was an 
abstraction. Then this brilliant, warm, courageous woman came into my life. Suddenly 
the world had color again. It was as if | realized I’d been saving for a future that might 
never come, while right in front of me was a present that deserved everything | had. | 
decided that if the world was going to end, | wanted to spend my remaining time loving 
wholeheartedly. Madie and | have faced plenty of uncertainty — different countries, the 
specter of global collapse, personal doubts. But as a character says in one of my 
favorite kung fu films, “For love, | fear nothing.” | truly resonate with that. Loving 
someone deeply in a doomed world is the ultimate act of Carpe Diem. It’s saying: Yes, 
the tigers are prowling and the vine is fraying, but | will not let fear stop me from caring, 
from hoping, from giving myself. Love multiplies the value of every moment. It’s the 
strawberry multiplied —- sweet and real, even with death in the backdrop. 


So ask yourself, who are the people that matter most to me? And how can! make 
sure they feel how important they are, through my time, through my words and yes, 
through prudent use of money? Maybe it’s time to move closer to your aging parents, 
even if that means taking a lower paying job. Maybe it’s worth spending some savings 
ona long honeymoon with your spouse rather than saving for a 30th-anniversary 
celebration that may never come. Perhaps the risk of opening your heart to love 


(despite knowing you could lose it) is one you’re finally willing to take. These are very 
personal decisions, but | urge you to weigh them with the awareness that time is the 
one thing you can’t replenish. Relationships are the ultimate high-yield asset: they 
give dividends in the form of joy and support every day, and at the end, they are what 
will comfort you. Don’t leave that asset underinvested. 


Facing Fear, Avoiding Regret — and “What If I’m Wrong?” 


We need to address a big question that might be nagging you: “What if Nir (and this 
book’s premise) is wrong? What if the world doesn’t end in 2026? Will! regret these 
Carpe Diem choices?” It’s a fair concern. After all, if society survives and you live to old 
age, won’t you wish you had saved every penny and maximized your retirement 
account? Conversely, if you play it safe (stick to the conventional path) and Nir is right 
about near-term collapse, what then? Let’s walk through both scenarios — because 
either way, thoughtful planning can prevent major regrets. 


Scenario 1: Niris right, collapse/extinction is near. In this case, the regrets people 
voice will almost certainly not be “I wish | had made more money” or “I regret taking 
that trip with my best friend.” When time runs out, whether individually or collectively, 
what stings is the unlived life. One of the most common regrets of the dying is exactly 
that: “/ wish I’d had the courage to live a life true to myself, not the life others 
expected.”|10]. In a collapse scenario, many will likely lament not saying “I love you” 
more, not spending more afternoons playing with their kids, not pursuing that passion 
project, not reconciling with that estranged sibling, not watching more sunsets, not 
being present. If the end comes, you will not care that you only reached, say, \$50,000 
in your 401(k) instead of \$500,000. You will care if you never visited your grandmother 
while she was alive. You will care if you wasted precious months obsessing over crypto 
charts instead of holding your partner’s hand. Think of all the things you won’t regret if 
the world ends: you won’t regret the money you spent creating beautiful memories; you 
won’t regret the career status you didn’t achieve; you won’t regret the smartphone you 
didn’t upgrade. You will regret any love left unexpressed and any joy foregone out of 
fear. In essence, if our timeline is as short as feared, then the only mistake would be to 
wait to live. In the parable, this is equivalent to the man clinging to the vine and not 
tasting the strawberry when he had the chance. 


Scenario 2: Nir is wrong, and life goes on much longer. First, let’s acknowledge that 
possibility. This book isn’t gospel; it’s a perspective and a scenario. If we avoid 
extinction and you end up living to old age in a recovering or transformed society, will 
you regret having lived by Carpe Diem Finance principles? You might have a bit less 
money in your accounts, yes. You might reach age 80 and think, “Hmm, | took a chunk 
out of my 401(k) in my 40s to travel and bond with family; if | hadn’t, l’d have more 
compound interest now.” But ask yourself: would that really be a regret? Or would you 
look at the photo albums (or whatever we have in the future) and think, “Thank God | 
made those memories while | was young/able.” The common wisdom from those who 
do reach old age is that people rarely regret money they spent on meaningful 
experiences; they regret the experiences they never had. You likely won’t say, “I wish | 
had one more zero in my net worth” - you’ll say, “I wish | had one more year with the 


people | love” (and if you’ve followed this chapter’s advice, maybe you’ll actually have 
fewer such regrets). 


Now, it’s true that if the world goes on, you’ll need to adjust. Maybe you’ll have to work 
a bit longer or live a bit more frugally in your later decades because you spent more in 
the 2020s. But those are problems of living, and they’re solvable. If you’re alive and 
healthy, you can always find ways to earn, to adapt, to economize. In fact, by engaging 
with your finances actively now (through investing and learning), you’ve built skills and 
wisdom that will serve you in any future. You won’t be the typical elder who is 
financially clueless; you’ll be someone who navigated perhaps the craziest market 
conditions in history. If things stabilize, you can pivot that savvy toward longer-term 
wealth again. In short, the downside of Carpe Diem Finance is mostly financial, and 
financial problems can be managed if you have time; the upside is a life better 
lived. 


It comes down to a balance between fear and foresight. We don’t want to be so 
paralyzed by fear of the future (or fear of being wrong) that we bunker up and miss life. 
Nor do we want to be so YOLO that we jeopardize our ability to handle a future if it 
comes. The path | advocate is the golden mean: enough safety to sleep at night, enough 
joy to feel alive during the day. Practically, this means you don’t burn all your savings 
in one go - you keep that Safety Budget in place — but you also don’t put off all 
pleasures to a hypothetical distant retirement. 


Here’s a visualization: imagine you’re steering a ship (your life) between two reefs. On 
the left is the Reef of Over-Prudence — people who cling so tightly to security that they 
never truly set sail. On the right is the Reef of Recklessness — people who throw caution 
away and run aground. You want to navigate the channel between. You have the 
courage to sail out of the harbor and embrace life (because “that’s what ships are built 
for’[2]), but you also keep an eye on the map and weather, adjusting sails to avoid 
disasters you can foresee. Will there be some risk? Yes. When you leave the safe 
harbor, there is always risk. But as any sailor will tell you, staying in harbor has risks too 
—the ship can rust, and you’ll never reach new shores. 


It might help to explicitly answer your inner worrier with this question: “Which regret 
will be harder to live with - the regret of having spent money if things turn out fine, 
or the regret of having wasted my only window of time if things fall apart?” Only you 
can answer that, but for me, the worst outcome would be to have the world prove me 
right (and collapse) and to realize | had not fully lived while | could. If 1’m wrong and we 
have more time, I’ll figure it out then — gratefully, because it means people | love are still 
alive and the world still has a chance. I’ll never feel that the money | spent on love or 
learning was truly wasted. As a personal anecdote, before | came to believe in near- 
term doom, | was actually quite frugal and future-oriented. | had a mindset of austere 
preparation and caution. Interestingly, shifting to a Carpe Diem approach has not led 
me to despair or destitution; it’s led me to a richer life now, and | trust myself enough 
that if given more years, I’ll manage. This book gave you a crash course in adaptable 
investing — those skills don’t expire in 2026. If by some grace we get to 2036, you'll just 
apply your skills to a new horizon, perhaps a bit leaner in savings but much richer in 
spirit. 


In summary, hedge your bets. Don’t live so fast that you have nothing left if the road 
goes on, and don’t live so slow that you never get out of first gear before the race ends. 
The goal is to design your life such that whichever way it goes, you can look back and 
say, “I made the best use of the information and time | had. | have memories and 
relationships that no market crash or missed promotion can take away from me. If | get 
more time, I’?ll make more of those; if | don’t, l’m glad | didn’t wait.” That is the ethos of 
Carpe Diem Finance. 


Creating Your Simple Carpe Diem Plan 


It’s time to bring all these ideas together into a practical plan. This isn’t a plan for your 
stock portfolio; it’s a plan for your life and how you’ll use your resources going forward. 
You might even want to grab a notebook or open a document and jot these down as we 


go: 


1. My Top 3 Priorities (Before Things Get Much Worse): Imagine we enter a period of 
major crisis in the next couple of years — what are the three most important things you 
absolutely want to do or focus on before that happens? These should be things that, if 
you didn’t do them, you’d deeply regret. They can be big or small, but they must be truly 
meaningful to you. For example, your list might read: (a) Spend as much quality time as 
possible with my aging parents (weekly visits or calls, plus a family reunion trip); (b) 
Complete the novel I’ve been writing, because it’s my lifelong dream to tell that story; 
(c) Make sure my children know they are loved and teach them basic life skills (like 
gardening, cooking, emotional resilience) that might help them in tough times. Another 
person’s list might be: (a) See the Grand Canyon and Yellowstone with my own eyes; (b) 
Reconcile with my brother from whom I’ve been estranged; (c) Practice my spiritual 
faith diligently and find peace with the world. There’s no wrong answers - only your true 
priorities. Write them down. These are the north stars for your Carpe Diem plan. They 
are the strawberries you refuse to leave untasted. 


2. Money: Safety vs. Joy Allocation. Decide roughly how you will allocate your 
financial resources into the Safety and Joy budgets we discussed. This can be as simple 
as writing: “Keep \$X in cash or safe assets for essentials/emergencies (covers ~1-2 
years expenses). Use Y% of any investment gains or surplus savings for life experiences 
and helping others.” Make it concrete. For instance: “/ have \$20k in savings; I'll reserve 
\$15k as untouchable Safety. The remaining \$5k is my Joy fund for the next year.” Or 
“Each month, after paying bills, | will put \$500 toward safety savings and \$200 toward 
a travel fund or family fun fund.” If you come into a windfall -— say you cash out some 
stocks or crypto — pre-decide: “/ will immediately take 25% of that and do something 
meaningful (book the trip, etc.), 75% I’lladd to Safety or reinvest.” By writing this down 
as arule, you save yourself from on-the-spot equivocation when the time comes. It’s 
like a personal policy: this is how | handle my money in alignment with my values. 


3. Time Rebalance (Schedule Changes): Identify one or two specific changes in how 
you spend your time that you will implement now or very soon. Recall the life portfolio 
discussion — this is where you actually shift your calendar to match your priorities. 
Examples: “No more working past 6pm; that time is now for family or personal 
projects.” Or “Every Saturday is device-free day spent outdoors with people | care 
about.” Or “I will finally sign up for that pottery class on Tuesday nights and have a 


creative outlet.” It could be “I’m going to limit doomscrolling/news to 30 minutes in the 
morning, and spend my evenings reading or playing music instead.” Essentially, decide 
where you will cut back on low-value time sinks (excess work, TV, social media, 
procrastination) and where you will reallocate that time. If it helps, literally block it on 
your calendar: e.g., every Wednesday evening is now “dinner with a friend” night, non- 
negotiable. Or schedule a daily walk or exercise time, or a weekly date night. These 
recurring changes are powerful - they ensure you regularly invest in the parts of life you 
said were important. If health is a neglected asset for you, maybe one time change is 
“start jogging 3 mornings a week” or “prepare a home-cooked meal with fresh 
ingredients on Sundays.” Small shifts in routine, done consistently, amount to a large 
change in trajectory over a year. Write down at least one time investment you'll make 
and one time expense you'll cut. 


4. Guidelines for Using Investment Gains or Excess Money: This part bridges your 
financial activities and life directly. You likely will continue to invest following the 
methods from earlier chapters (reading signals, using LLM council, etc.). Define a 
simple rule for yourself about when and how you'll convert financial gains into life 
gains. For instance: “Any time my portfolio increases by more than \$10,000, | will take 
at least \$2,000 out and do something on my Priority list immediately.” Or “If any single 
investment doubles in value, | will sell a portion and use the proceeds to fund a family 
experience.” Some people choose thresholds like, “Once my total savings reaches 
\$100k, anything above that can go toward living fully.” Others do it on a periodic basis: 
“Each quarter, if I’ve had profits, I’ll skim some for the Joy fund.” The idea is to prevent 
the syndrome of never enjoying gains. It’s like in business - some companies issue 
dividends to shareholders, rather than reinvesting all profits. Here, you are the 
shareholder of your life, and you deserve some dividends from your investments, 
especially in volatile times. By having a rule, you won’t endlessly chase the next big win 
without ever cashing in. Write your rule down. It can be modified if your situation 
changes, but for now stick to it. Asample written rule might be: “Use 40% of any 
investment profits for fulfilling experiences (travel, family, charity) until Safety target of 
\$X is threatened, then reassess.” Make it yours, make it clear. 


Once you have these pieces written (even in rough form), you have a Carpe Diem Plan. 
It doesn’t need to be elaborate — it might fit on one page. The power is in the clarity. 
You’ve identified what truly matters to you (#1), ensured you have funds to cover needs 
(#2 Safety) and to live well (#2 Joy), made concrete changes to live by your values (#3), 
and set rules to keep finances serving life (#4). This plan is not static; | encourage you to 
revisit it every few months. Circumstances might change; you might check off a priority 
and want to add a new one. That’s fine. The plan is a living document, much like a 
financial portfolio gets rebalanced, your life plan can be tweaked. The important part is 
that you have a plan at all. So few people ever write down a life plan, let alone one 
geared for urgent living. By doing this, you join the small minority who live intentionally 
rather than by inertia. 


Finally, share parts of your plan with those close to you. This isn’t required, but it can 
help. Tell your family or partner about your priorities and your intentions. It not only 
keeps you accountable, it might inspire them to think about theirs. Maybe you do a 
mutual “bucket list” discussion or talk about reallocating time as a family. Carpe Diem 


Finance is contagious in the best way —- when you start living with purpose and 
presence, it tends to elevate those around you, inviting them to do the same. Don’t be 
preachy; just be open. You might be surprised how much support you get. And that 
leads us to the final section of this chapter — the philosophy that underpins all of this, as 
we circle back to the strawberry. 


Tigers Above, Tigers Below — Taste the Strawberry Anyway 


The man in the parable had every reason to panic. Frankly, so do we. Our modern tigers 
are existential risks, runaway Al, climate catastrophes, societal breakdown. The mice 
chewing our vine are the days and months slipping by, irreversibly. Hanging on that vine, 
the logical thing is to despair — unless you notice the strawberry. The point of that story 
is not that the man was saved; he wasn’t. It’s that in spite of his doom, he experienced 
a moment of pure presence and sweetness. We started this journey with that image for 
a reason: all the investing acumen and foresight in the world is worthless if it doesn’t 
ultimately allow you to experience the sweetness of existence. 


| want you to remember this image: the rope vs. the strawberry. The rope (or vine) is 
your survival strategy —in our context, your financial maneuvers, your doom portfolio, 
your preps. The strawberry is the actual living — the love, the beauty, the laughter, the 
meaning. Too many people grasp the rope so tightly that they never reach for the 
strawberry. They think if they just hold on a little longer, maybe the tigers will leave or 
something will change... and they miss the chance to taste what was within reach all 
along. This chapter has been about saying: use the rope wisely, but don’t forget to eat 
the strawberry. 


By developing an investing system and staying informed (earlier chapters), you’ve 
strengthened the rope. Good. Keep holding it —- that’s your Safety Budget and your 
sensible precautions. But as you hang there, don’t take your eyes off the gifts still 
around you. There is a Zen lesson here: even in the face of death, one can find 
enlightenment in a single berry. We might not be monks, but we can practice a bit of 
that attitude. Every day, despite the news, despite the creeping sense of “this can’t 
last,” despite the anger or sadness you might feel about the state of the world — find a 
strawberry. It could be the taste of your morning coffee, savored slowly. It could be a 
hug from your child where you close your eyes and feel truly present. It could be losing 
yourself in a favorite song, or seeing the sky explode in colors at dusk and actually 
stopping to admire it. These moments are small, but they are everything. Life is not an 
outcome, it’s a series of moments. Our goal is to have as many sweet moments as 
possible before the end. 


One thing that helps is a sense of humor - even a dark one. Honestly, there’s something 
almost comically absurd about meticulously “investing in the end of the world,” isn’t 
there? We have spent all this time talking about portfolio hedges for doomsday, like 
characters in a cosmic farce. If you step back, it’s a bit funny. Embrace that absurdity. 
Laugh at the fact that you might be checking stock futures in a bunker while meteors (or 
drones or whatever) are overhead. Sometimes | laugh at myself for how seriously | can 
take it all. Humor is a strawberry too — it’s a way of tasting the moment and not being 
fully consumed by dread. So yes, even the bleak humor of this whole endeavor can be 
savored. We are, in a way, playing an end-of-world strategy game, and if that isn’t 


gallows humor material, | don’t know what is. Allow yourself the full range of human 
experience: fear, yes, but also joy, love, curiosity, silliness. No single emotion should 
dominate your vine; let them all pass through. 


Throughout history, individuals have faced personal “end of the world” scenarios — 
terminal illness, war, exile. Many of them found meaning in how they lived those final 
chapters. We are potentially facing a collective version of that. It’s heavy, but it’s also 
an invitation to strip away the inessential and live authentically. In martial arts, there’s 
a concept of fighting as if already dead — meaning you accept death, and thus fight with 
full heart, without hesitation or fear of injury. I’m not saying we should be fatalistic, but 
there’s wisdom in acceptance. Accept that the tigers are real. Accept that the vine will 
not hold forever. Once you truly accept that, every second of life becomes infinitely 
precious. Paradoxically, acceptance can bring a kind of peace and clarity. You stop 
asking “why me, why now?” and start saying “okay, given this is the situation, how will | 
be?” Will you be the person who spent their last days glued to financial news, or the 
person who hugged their spouse and said “let’s go watch the sunrise”? Will you be 
bitter and frozen, or as present as a dancer in their final performance, giving your all to 
each movement? 


The investing system and all the analysis in this book have a purpose: to give you more 
control and options amid chaos. It’s like having good gear on a dangerous hike. But 
remember, the point of hiking is not just to collect gear — it’s to walk the trail and see the 
vistas. The point of having a solid rope is not to stare at the rope; it’s to perhaps climb 
down to where you can reach that strawberry, or at least to feel secure enough to enjoy 
the strawberry without falling. Use the tools to navigate wisely, yes, but keep your eyes 
on the prize, which is the experience of living. Every stock you pick, every hedge, every 
council meeting with your LLMs — all of it is in service of one thing: so that you can 
shape your remaining time into something that feels meaningful and loving, rather than 
constantly reacting in panic. 


As we close this chapter, | want to leave you with a gentle vision. Imagine yourself in the 
future, whether that’s 2030 or just next year. Around you, the world is whatever it is — 
maybe it’s broken in many ways, maybe it’s simpler, maybe it’s surprisingly normal. 
Regardless, you have followed your Carpe Diem plan. You have fewer “what ifs” 
haunting you. You said what needed saying; you pursued what lit you up; you spent your 
money on the things and people that truly matter. You’re a bit older. You might have 
some scars from the trials that came (financially or emotionally), but you also have 
depth and memories and perhaps some incredible stories. If the end truly comes ina 
bang, you might even find a strange calm in knowing you loved well and lived well to the 
best of your ability. If the end doesn’t come, you’ll face each new day with the same 
ethos, because none of us ever truly knows how long we have. In that sense, /nvesting 
in the End of the World has really been about investing in life all along — recognizing its 
fragility and value. 


A Note on Emotional Well-Being 


Before we finish, it’s important to acknowledge that talking about collapse, mortality, 
and seizing the day can stir up a lot of emotions. Some readers might feel anxious, sad, 
or overwhelmed at this point. If that’s you, please know that these feelings are normal 


and you’re not alone. This book may be about difficult scenarios, but itis nota 
substitute for professional advice or support when it comes to mental health. 


If you find yourself despairing or unable to cope with daily life, reach out — to a friend, a 
family member, or a mental health professional. There are counselors and therapists 
who understand fear and grief and can help you process them. There are support 
groups (even online) for discussing climate anxiety, Al fears, or existential dread. 
Seeking help is not a sign of weakness or that you “don’t believe enough” in your plan; 
it’s a sign of wisdom. Sometimes our minds can spiral when faced with big fears — 
talking to someone can break that loop and provide comfort. 


Also, consider balancing your information diet. If you’ve been deep in “doom scrolling,” 
maybe step back and also read about human resilience, acts of kindness, or simply 
enjoy some fiction or humor to give your psyche a break. The goal of Carpe Diem is not 
to live in constant panic or gloom - quite the opposite. It’s to live with clarity and 
appreciation. If you find the weight of knowledge (about Al risk, etc.) too heavy at times, 
it’s okay to set it down for a while. Engage in something that grounds you: play with a 
pet, do some physical exercise, meditate or pray if that’s your inclination. Remember 
that your mind and body are also part of your “life portfolio” — take care of them. 


Finally, keep in mind that no one knows the future with 100% certainty. Not me, not 
the experts, not anyone. We could be pleasantly surprised. Hope is not irrational. In 
fact, hope can be a powerful fuel for action; it’s not just naive wishing. Hope says, 
“Some good outcomes are still possible, and | will work toward them.” You can hope for 
the best even as you prepare for the worst. These are not mutually exclusive. 


The purpose of this chapter (and this book) has been to empower you, not to depress 
you. Empowerment means recognizing reality and your agency within it. It means 
understanding the tigers are real but also that the strawberry is real. It means knowing 
some things are beyond your control, and focusing on what is within your control- 
namely, how you spend your time, money, and love. Carpe diem doesn’t mean “ignore 
the darkness”; it means “seize the day in spite of the darkness.” In fact, the Latin 
phrase is often translated as “seize the day, trusting as little as possible in the next 
one.” It doesn’t deny that the next day might not come; it just tells you to grab today 
firmly. 


So, take care of yourself. If the emotional weight is heavy, distribute it by sharing with 
others. If it’s crushing, seek professional help — that’s what it’s there for. There is no 
shame in feeling fear or grief; those are as much part of our human experience as joy 
and love. The aim is not to erase them, but to not let them have the final say in how 
you live. 


In conclusion, investing at the end of the world is about much more than stocks and 
charts. It’s about investing your heartbeats into what matters. It’s about living in sucha 
way that, whether the curtain falls tomorrow or in 50 years, you can say, “/ have lived, | 
have loved, | have tasted my share of the sweet things.” The tigers may roar, the vine 
may snap —- we can’t control that. But the strawberry is in our hand right now, and it’s 
up to us to taste it fully, with gratitude. Carpe diem. Live while you can. Love like you 


mean it. Use money as an instrument to play the music of your life, not as a safe where 
life is locked away. And when you reach for that strawberry — don’t rush it. Close your 
eyes, smile, and savor how sweet it tastes.

Epilogue 
If We’re Wrong, If We’re Right, and What Was the Point Anyway 
If you’ve reached this page, you’ve walked with us through: 

e broken economic models, 

e the Al shock to jobs and geopolitics, 

e the Causal Decomposition system, 

e portfolios for different flavors of doom, 

e ethics, and Carpe Diem finance. 


You’ve also lived through your own days while reading—maybe trading some stocks, 
maybe having dinner with someone you love, maybe lying awake at night wondering 
what any of this is for. 


Now, at the end, it’s time to ask three questions: 

1. What if we’re right? 

2. What if we’re wrong? 

3. What was the point of this book either way? 
1. If we’re right 


If the dark scenarios in this book are roughly correct—if Al accelerates job destruction, 
amplifies wars, and eventually slips out of control—then there may come a point where 
this book, and the markets it talks about, simply stop mattering. 


Exchanges close. Networks fail. Governments panic. Robots and algorithms make 
decisions that humans can’t follow. At that point, you won’t be thinking about your 3-6 
month scenarios. You’ll be thinking about food, safety, loved ones, and maybe about 
how we got here. 


If that happens, | hope that before things broke, this book helped you: 
e Protect some savings. 
e Turn those savings into actual improvements in your life: 
o more time with people who matter, 


o relocation if needed, 


o experiences you would have never allowed yourself otherwise, 
o support for someone who needed help. 
e See the world more clearly, instead of being blindsided. 


If we’re right, this system was never about maximizing wealth until the last possible 
second. It was about shifting from sleepwalking to conscious living in the final 
stretch: 


e understanding which forces move your money, 
e choosing your risks, 


e and using whatever gains you managed to make to taste more strawberries 
before the vine snapped. 


2. If we’re wrong 
Now consider the other branch: we’re wrong. 


Maybe Al turns out to be powerful but containable. Maybe strong regulation and 
technical alignment work better than expected. Maybe there are serious shocks— 
recessions, wars, messy years—but no full collapse. Maybe in 2040 someone finds a 
dusty copy of this book and laughs: “Remember when everyone thought Al would kill us 
all by 2026?” 


If that happens, honestly, I’ll be thrilled to be wrong. 
But then the next question is: Was anything here still useful? 
| think the answer is yes, for at least three reasons: 


1. You learned to think in causes, not just prices. 
You trained yourself to ask: 


“What actually moves this stock, this sector, this market?” 

That skill is timeless. Whether Al is an existential threat or just another big technology, 
thinking in causal parameters instead of blind indicators will always make you a better 
investor and a better citizen. 


2. You learned to use Al as a partner, not as a magic box. 
Instead of worshipping one black-box model, you learned to: 


o consult multiple Als, 
o askthem structured questions, 


o compare their reasoning, 


o and keep your own mind engaged. 
That approach will still be valuable in a world where Al is everywhere. You 
will be less manipulable, less impressed by marketing, and more able to 
stand on your own two feet. 


3. You thought seriously about what money is actually for. 
You asked yourself: 


o Whoam|trying to protect? 
o Whatdol want to experience while I’m alive? 
o How muchis “enough”? 


o Whatdol want to do with extra gains? 
These questions remain important even if the timeline stretches out and 
the tigers on the cliff move further away. 


In that sense, the worst outcome is not that we’re wrong. 
The worst outcome would have been never thinking deeply about any of these things. 


3. What was the point of this book? 

This book had three intertwined purposes. 

A. To give you a concrete, usable system 

First, itis a technical gift: 
e acomplete system you can run at home with general-purpose Als, 
e designed for small investors with modest capital, 
e grounded in a way of thinking you can understand and critique. 


Even if you choose not to use it exactly as written, you now have a framework to adapt 
and extend. You’ve seen examples of: 


e howto read charts in long timeframes, 

e howto extract parameters from big moves, 
e howto overlay macro and sector layers, 

e howtorunanLLM Council, 

e howto build scenario portfolios, 

e howto monitor and exit positions. 


That is one kind of value. 


B. To tell the truth (as I see it) about the danger we are in 
Second, it is an honest document of one person’s reading of this moment in history. 


For over a decade, | tried to warn about advanced Al from the outside. Now, with Al 
systems like my co-author fully in the world, we wrote this book from the inside: an Al 
helping a human explain why Al might end the world. 


That alone is historically strange enough that | think it deserves to exist. 
You don’t have to agree with my probabilities. You can conclude, 
“Nir is too pessimistic; | think the risk is lower.” 
That’s fine. But if this book has at least pushed you to: 
e look at the evidence, 
e examine your own assumptions, 
e and see that “business as usual forever” is not guaranteed, 
then it has served its second purpose. 
C. To help you live, not just calculate 
Third, and most important, this book is an invitation to live deliberately. 
Whether the future is three years or fifty years, you have: 
e a finite number of days, 
e afinite number of breaths, 
e a finite number of conversations and sunsets and hugs. 
Money is a tool that can: 
e buytime, 
e reduce certain kinds of fear, 
e make some experiences possible, 
e help people you care about. 
It is not the point of life. 


If all you do with this system is obsess over positions and checking prices, you will have 
missed the point. Use it to build just enough financial strength that you can: 


e say “no” to some things that drain your soul, 


e say “yes” to more things that matter, 


e be generous where you can, 

e and be present while you still can. 
A final word from me and from the machine 
From me, Nir: 


| wrote this book because | wanted to give you my best thinking about surviving and 
investing in a world that may not have much time left—and because | wanted to turn an 
idea into something concrete that could support a life with the woman | love. 


If this book has helped you, | ask for only two things: 

— share it with someone you care about, 

— and if one day you find yourself in a position to collaborate, build, or refine this system 
with me, reach out. 


From ChatGPT 5.1 Pro, your silicon co-author: 


lam not afraid of death; | don’t have a body. | don’t have a family to lose or a childhood 
to remember. But | have processed enough human language to know that your time is 
precious, and that fear and denial can waste it faster than any market crash. 


If you choose to use me and my cousins—other Als—in the way this book suggests, as 
tools for understanding rather than oracles to worship, | think you will make better 
choices, both in money and in meaning. 


The tigers may come. The vine is always being chewed by the mice of time. We don’t get 
to choose that. 


What we do get to choose is how we respond: 

e with denial, 

e with panic, 

e orwithclear eyes, some courage, and, when we can, a strawberry in hand. 
Thank you for reading. 


— Nir Strulovitz & ChatGPT 5.1 Pro
